# Thread: Water well casing question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T02:22:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60842>

If you were going to put in a new water well, about 300' down, what kind of casing would you use? The water is acidic.

PVC seems to be everywhere, but who wants the BPA?

Copper would be extremely expensive. Galvanized?

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-30T02:55:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60855>

I run into well issues around here on our mountain properties - sadly, there are only a few options from well providers that provide permit quality (thus saleable in the future and documented properly) . I would get your bids from the well companies and see if any of them provide options on that.

That is a great thing to think about - and it would be worth shopping around.

Also I would find out if your installer just meets required levels, or exceeds the epa standards. I know here we have one more "eco" friendly option - but it costs about twice as much.

My point is, you don't really have many choices - unless of course you are going off grid and unpermitted - in which case I would say SHHHHHHH! don't let your insurance or neighbors find out. Also hide it if you ever want to sell!!!

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-30T15:06:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60898>

BPA usually is only used in flexible PVC, I would think well casing would be stiff?

### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T15:15:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60901>

> **Cavemam;62554 wrote:**
>
> My point is, you don't really have many choices - unless of course you are going off grid and unpermitted - in which case I would say SHHHHHHH! don't let your insurance or neighbors find out. Also hide it if you ever want to sell!!!

Actually where this property is, there are few regulations. Thanks Nate, it's good to know rigid PVC pipe doesn't have bpa, though who knows what other endocrine-disrupting chemicals it has. It definitely would be the less expensive option.

### Dali Dula — 2012-10-30T17:04:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60934>

There are two elements here. the well casing is usually 8" iron and usually does not go all the way to the bottom. The other issue is the pipe coming from the submersible pump which can be pvc or galvanized. Most use schedule 40 pvc. The iron casing does not typically contact your water it just holds the shape of the well through top soil layers. The supply pipe would be 1-1/2" pvc. I don't know if pvc leaches chemicals into drinking water. I hope not cuz that's what I've got. Pvc is polyvinylchloride.

### freshveggies — 2012-10-30T17:13:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60940>

What kind of pipes do you all recommend in a house. Post are using the flexible PVC now. Copper has been phased out.

### villjamur_stevenson — 2012-10-30T17:20:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60943>

i think we used a steel casing on my well 6 years ago. However, we are only required to put the casing in down to the bedrock. I'm assuming you don't have bedrock where you live. 300' would be very pricey!

### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T17:43:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-well-casing-question.4564/post-60946>

> **freshveggies;62647 wrote:** What kind of pipes do you all recommend in a house. Post are using the flexible PVC now. Copper has been phased out.

Good question, Veggies. I'm leaning towards copper pipes.

Villjamur, we don't have rock, just sand and clay.

# Thread: 'At Home' testers for things other than blood sugar levels

**Posts:** 5

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-30T02:38:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-home-testers-for-things-other-than-blood-sugar-levels.4567/post-60851>

Are there relatively inexpensive 'at home' testers for other things besides blood sugar that would replace some doctor visits to get one or more panels? For example, are testers available that can test saliva or blood samples at home for a omega 6/omega 3 ratio? The smart chips in testers that sample blood ought to be able to do lots of other things or is that just wishful thinking?

### Glen PDQ — 2012-10-30T23:46:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-home-testers-for-things-other-than-blood-sugar-levels.4567/post-61004>

That A1c tester sounds helpful. I might spring for the one at Walgreens.

### JanSz — 2012-11-04T05:32:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-home-testers-for-things-other-than-blood-sugar-levels.4567/post-61923>

> **Glen PDQ;62549 wrote: Are there relatively inexpensive 'at home' testers for other things besides blood sugar that would replace some doctor visits to get one or more panels? For example, are testers available that can test saliva or blood samples at home for a omega 6/omega 3 ratio**? The smart chips in testers that sample blood ought to be able to do lots of other things or is that just wishful thinking?

I am afraid that rather than be helpfull, testing only omega 6/omega 3 ratio

would create even more misinformation.

Similarly to HS-Omega3 index

or similar tests.

Many people eat lots (or even little) fish oils or krill oils.

They have way way too much EPA(omega3).

Introduction of EPA creates havoc.

EPA suppresses AA (omega6) which fools you twice

first it suppresses AA (the most valuable Omega6)

second it makes you think that your totalOmega6 is low (because it took out the most valuable part)

High EPA makes you think that you have highTotalOmega3

by raising it (mathematically) with garbage=EPA

//////////

.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T05:52:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/at-home-testers-for-things-other-than-blood-sugar-levels.4567/post-61925>

Jan, OMG, now I get this.... and my test from last winter. Thank you!

# Thread: Foraged food
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foraged-food.4569/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T07:56:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foraged-food.4569/post-60878>

> Luigi Ballerini revisits \[the\] distant past in "A Feast of Weeds: A Literary Guide to Foraging and Cooking Wild Edible Plants" (University of California Press), which celebrates the foraged foods that are currently enjoying a renaissance in Italy and elsewhere. ...
>
>
>
> "Once you taste greens that you plucked from the field, you'll never go back â€” or do so with great regret," he said. "The taste of what you get at the market is nothing compared to what wild weeds really taste like."

[http://newsroom.ucla.edu/portal/ucla/ucla-professor-author-champions-237441.aspx](http://http://newsroom.ucla.edu/portal/ucla/ucla-professor-author-champions-237441.aspx)

### Jonathin — 2012-10-30T20:16:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/foraged-food.4569/post-60975>

Most foraged foods are better than cultivated counterparts. It is not because a human did not grow them deliberately. It is that the biological and chemical nature of farm and garden soil is usually poor to mediocre. Chemical or organic techniques are not necessarily a determining factor of quality. It varies widely.

One easy way to tell if a vegetable is of very good quality is: does it rot, turn brown or dehydrate easily? Eating junk vegetables no matter how they are labeled is not benefiting your body.

# Thread: Richard Wrangham again
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/richard-wrangham-again.4572/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T09:22:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/richard-wrangham-again.4572/post-60881>

Another article on Richard Wrangham's theories:

[http://www.slate.com/articles/health\_and\_science/human\_evolution/2012/10/who\_invented\_fire\_when\_did\_people\_start\_cooking\_.single.html](http://http://www.slate.com/articles/health_and_science/human_evolution/2012/10/who_invented_fire_when_did_people_start_cooking_.single.html)

He does sound to be saying some stuff that really isn't supported by the evidence:

> \[Wrangham's\] hypothesis stems from a few modern observations. When you eat cooked food, you have access to many more calories than if you eat the same food raw. There are two reasons: Our digestive systems can extract more calories from a cooked steak (for instance) than a raw steak, and it takes much less energy to cook and eat a steak than to gnaw on a raw one for hours. Access to cooked food means a hominid no longer needs enormous teeth to break down all that raw meat and roughage into swallowable hunks, nor does it need as robust a digestive system to process it all. The combination of more calories and less complicated intestines means more energy can be devote to cogitatingâ€”hence H. erectusâ€™ relatively big brains, which suck up a lot of calories.
>
>
> This implies the brain's needs are all about calories. It sounds like he needs to talk to the brain scientists.
>
>
>
> As evidence for his theory, Wrangham likes to point to the fact that modern-day humans canâ€™t thrive on an all-raw dietâ€”raw foodists tend to stop menstruating, precluding reproduction.

Pretty poor evidence, isn't it? These women are vegans - _that's_ why they stop menstruating. It hardly amounts to a controlled experiment showing what happens when someone eats, say, a raw shellfish diet. I don't believe it is in line with the ethnographic evidence either - which is strange since Wrangham is an anthropologist.

This is a report about what Wrangham is saying rather than his own words, but it does sound like he's coming out with some shaky stuff.

# Thread: The Wonders of Docasahexaenoic Acid
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-wonders-of-docasahexaenoic-acid.4573/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T11:28:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-wonders-of-docasahexaenoic-acid.4573/post-60882>

A report from the _Economist from an international conference on DHA held in May 2010 (with references to Michael Crawford and Joseph Hibbeln):

> ... its displacement from modern diets by the omega-6 acids in cooking oils such as soya, maize and rape is a cause of worry. Many researchers think this shiftâ€”and the change in brain chemistry that it causesâ€”explains the growth in recent times of depression, manic-depression, memory loss, schizophrenia and attention-deficit disorder. It may also be responsible for rising levels of obesity and thus ... heart disease ...
>
>
>
> In this experiment \[Dr. Hibbeln\] fed rats diets that were identical except that in one case 8% of the calories came from linoleic acid (an omega-6 fatty acid) while in the other that value was 1%. These percentages reflect the shift in the proportion of omega-6s in the American diet between 1909 and the early 21st century.
>
>
>
> In the 8% diet, levels of rat obesity doubled. It turns out that in rats (and also in humans) linoleic acid is converted into molecules called endocannabinoids that trigger appetite. Those who eat omega-6s, in other words, want to eat more food. ...

The Economist_'s own solution to this set of appalling problems is to suggest genetically engineered soya! Good grief.

[http://www.economist.com/node/16214142](http://http://www.economist.com/node/16214142)

### Michael — 2012-10-30T11:31:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-wonders-of-docasahexaenoic-acid.4573/post-60883>

Had a quick search:

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=hibbeln%20endocannabinoids](http://http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=hibbeln%20endocannabinoids)

# Thread: Study in Ireland on Suicidal Ideation

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-10-30T15:47:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-60909>

There's been a new crop of stories referring to the omega 3 smoothies for soldiers trial. These ones refer back to a study carried out in Ireland that aimed to see whether omega 3 supplementation could reduce suicidal ideation.

I think I've found it:

[http://m.bjp.rcpsych.org/content/190/2/118.short](http://http://m.bjp.rcpsych.org/content/190/2/118.short)

### nonchalant — 2012-10-30T17:07:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-60937>

Thanks for this, Michael. It is helpful.

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-30T20:20:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-60976>

during the stand-down Suicide prevention Day the DoD had several weeks back, one of the things the Medical folks were pushing was Fish oils, and the consumption of more fish/seafood. the interesting thing is - unless deployed fish is usually breaded fish. when deployed - Steak and Lobster are served once/week, as is shrimp in the chow hall.

### ealachan — 2012-10-30T20:58:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-60980>

Omega-3 supplementation absolutely helped drag me out of clinical depression and allowed me to go through last winter unmedicated. For a bit of perspective: I'd spent every winter (and the majority of the rest of the year for that matter) since 2005 on a combination of antidepressants and anti-anxiety meds. For the majority of 2005-2007, I was all but house-bound by my depression and anxiety. I don't think it's a stretch to say that heavy Omega-3 supplementation saved my life.

### ColdBren — 2012-10-31T00:20:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/study-in-ireland-on-suicidal-ideation.4577/post-61006>

Wow Ealachan, I too have noticed significant benefits to my emotional issues from omega 3, for sure.

### AKMan — 2012-10-31T00:33:53+0200

Don't know if you've been in an Army dining facility lately, but they have taken to labelling all foods as green ,amber, and red. Green is high energy food and should be eaten freely--Red is low energy food that should be eaten rarely. Lasagna=Green, Fried Fish=Green, Prime Rib=Red, French fries and hamburgers=yellow. Must have been invented by Al Qeida.

### Martha Ray — 2019-12-18T01:16:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/where-are-you-getting-your-sea-veggies.4583/post-283740>

> [BJK77 said:](/goto/post?id=60984)
>
> I had been ordering most of my sea veggies online, but I'm finding my regular supplier to be out of stock on several things . . . how dare they! I remember an old post with some good info about where to purchase them, but I just did a search and went back 10 pages and couldn't find it. Does anyone remember that or care to share where they're getting them?...

I have been using _**HEX fermented Sea Kraut**_ from _Whole Foods_. A 'processed' product from Baltimore, MD (somewhat regional to me).
[https://www.hexferments.com/products/seaweed-kraut](https://www.hexferments.com/products/seaweed-kraut)
Version says _Sea Kraut_ but it still has _Green Cabbage_ and _Gold Beets_ as first two ingredients, then, unrefined _sea salt_, Ginger, _**Arame**_, _Burdock Roo_t and lastly **_Red Dulse_, _Black Laver_, _Green Sea Lettuce_**. Would not likely be too high in Sea Veggies but the label shown online has a sticker that reads, "More Seaweed Power" so hopefully they are increasing the 'sea' amount. My clear jars label is black and gold and does not have the, "More Seaweed Power" Here is their PRODUCTS page. [https://www.hexferments.com/collections/all](https://www.hexferments.com/collections/all)

**_Farmstead Ferments_** is a local Ferments company I use... but they do not have sea veggies [https://farmsteadferments.com/](https://farmsteadferments.com/)

Was a little concerned about eating raw cruciferous veggies or gritrogens due to my hypothyroidism.
Many allopatic doctors believe this Dr. Izabella Wentz quote. "**Cruciferous vegetables** in particular are considered to be “**goitrogenic**” because they have the potential to block iodine absorption. They contain glucosinolates, which are responsible for this action. **Glucosinolates can prevent the absorption of iodine into the thyroid gland when consumed in large quantities."** I never ate large quantities watched my level of these foods and except for some slaw I ate them steamed or cooked. But I did notice when I ate more flaxseed my hair loss became very noticeable when I cut flaxseed out my hair loss lessened.
This _ThyroMate_ article covers Flaxseed and Thyroid.
[https://www.thyromate.com/blog/can-flaxseed-help-thyroid-problems](https://www.thyromate.com/blog/can-flaxseed-help-thyroid-problems).

I've been hypothyroid not believed to be Hashimoto's for 14 years and I'd had low iodine for over 22 years. Tested urine and blood with but no one neither GP or Endo Doctors cared to treat my low iodine. I was extremely low almost nil and then worked up to less than 30 eating more fish and then I worked up to <40 serum trying several things--kelp flakes, _Wellness Resources_ iodine drops, Kelp supplements etc.
But using small doses of these two products took me to 108.6 last year. I was so used to not getting any results I was using both of these when my level went up that hight last year.
DR. COWAN'S _**GARDEN SEA-VEGETABLES POWDER**_ -Ingredients: Wild-harvested **dulse, wakame, kelp.** Nothing else.

# Thread: Power postures raise Testosterone and lower cortisol
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-postures-raise-testosterone-and-lower-cortisol.4584/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Glamazon — 2012-10-30T21:59:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-postures-raise-testosterone-and-lower-cortisol.4584/post-60988>

[http://youtu.be/Ks-\_Mh1QhMc\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;Ks-\_Mh1QhMc\]http://youtu.be/Ks-\_Mh1QhMc\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;Ks-_Mh1QhMc)

Body language affects how others see us, but it may also change how we see ourselves. Social psychologist Amy Cuddy shows how "power posing" -- standing in a posture of confidence, even when we don't feel confident -- can affect testosterone and cortisol levels in the brain, and might even have an impact on our chances for success.

### Lyndra — 2012-10-31T01:18:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-postures-raise-testosterone-and-lower-cortisol.4584/post-61011>

Wow, great video, thanks for posting. What a powerful story.

> Our bodies change our minds...and our minds change our behavior...and our behavior changes our outcomes.
> Hmm, where have we heard something like that?
>
>
>
>
> > Don't fake it until you make it...fake it until you BECOME it
>
>
>
> Tiny tweaks ---> BIG CHANGES

So many different applications with her theories. If a two minutes power pose can lower your cortisol, I'll be giving it a try before bed.

### nonchalant — 2012-10-31T16:30:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/power-postures-raise-testosterone-and-lower-cortisol.4584/post-61083>

I like that "Tiny tweaks ---> BIG CHANGES".

> So many different applications with her theories. If a two minutes power pose can lower your cortisol, I'll be giving it a try before bed.

Great idea, Lyndra.

# Thread: School Healthy Breakfast

**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TribalSpice — 2012-10-31T02:30:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61017>

At one of my kids schools, on a Wednesday morning one of the local church groups holds "Healthy Breakfast". You are going to love this menu:

Nutrigrain, fruit loops and coco pops

White bread toasted with jam or Vegemite

And a fruit salad (which at least has some nutritional value)

### Shijin13 — 2012-10-31T02:33:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61018>

>
> Nutrigrain, fruit loops and coco pops
>
> White bread toasted with jam or Vegemite
>
> And a fruit salad (which at least has some nutritional value)

\* Head desk \*. The craziness

### TribalSpice — 2012-10-31T02:40:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61019>

Head desk indeed! I don't have massive restrictions on my kids. They eat the occasional crappy food-like-substance, but this as a weekly (or for plenty of kids, daily) meal it really made me think about what we are doing to the next generation. It is sad and scary.

### kathylu — 2012-10-31T03:19:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61023>

### [email redacted] — 2012-10-31T04:07:29+0200

> **kathylu;62736 wrote:** Hey...Vegemite is good (at least I think it is)!

### BJK77 — 2012-10-31T14:35:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61059>

Disturbing . . . to say the least; however, sad fact is (as you mentioned) this is probably a daily meal for many kids out there. I'm disgusted when my kids have friends over and they want donuts for breakfast because that's what they eat at home. One tells me all the time her mom says donuts are ok (the small ones covered in powdered sugar) because she dunks them in milk so at least she gets calcium then!!! WHAT?!?! And these people are actually raising the next generation . . . it's like a horror movie, only worse cause it's real!!

### nonchalant — 2012-10-31T23:50:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61173>

I think even more damaging than the junk food, is the name "Healthy Breakfast". I don't know how many years I tried to "eat healthily" -- you know, whole grains and lowfat milk, margarine and juice. I chose food based on what others, with an agenda, had labeled as "healthy".

This church means well, but they are teaching a bad lesson, as well as giving poor nutrition (malnutrition).

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-01T19:54:34+0200

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-01T21:15:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61381>

> **Cavemam;63095 wrote:** Annnnd, I have my new signature "The road to hell is paved with cheetos"

you better put that on your siggy and in BIG BOLD CAPS

### Entelechy — 2012-11-01T21:46:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61389>

> **Michael;63110 wrote:** Samuel Johnson. He meant that while he might have good intentions they were worth nothing if he didn't make the effort to carry them out.

Johnson often gets credited with saying "The road to hell is paved with good intentions," but never actually said it.

In Boswell's Life of Johnson, he is quoted as saying, (in 1775) "Hell is paved with good intentions." A footnote in the book indicates it to be a 'proverbial sentence,' quoting an earlier 1651 source. Two others are

John Ray, 1670, "Hell is paved with good intentions."

Saint Bernard of Clairvaux (1091-1153), "Hell is full of good intentions or desires."

whomever added "the road" is not known,

the aphorism is most commonly meant to infer that individuals may do bad things even though they intend the results to be good.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-01T22:17:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-61401>

Damn autocorrect. 'twas supposed to be froot loops.

Oh well. Y'all knew what I meant

haha.

That pic of Johnson...sure is scary looking. Those people were all unhealthy looking like that. Sooooo bloated. If I can recall any picture from that era; man or woman, it's bloated. Yuck!

### Norbert — 2013-04-11T20:51:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/school-healthy-breakfast.4585/post-83777>

Its difficult to make a good breakfast in morning because routines are so hectic. Some kids like juices in breakfast, it is also well. Your breakfast should be balanced and light. Give children what ever they eat easily.

### Michael — 2012-10-31T17:14:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-interesting-snippets-on-iodine.4595/post-61091>

These in an article entitled "Protect your breasts with iodine".

> ... women's breasts are major sites for iodine storage ...
>
>
>
> ... experimental iodine deficiency in rats results in breast abnormalities similar to human fibrocystic disease of the breast ...
>
>
>
> ... Dr Albert S. Gyorgi, the discoverer of vitamin C stated, "When I was a medical student, iodine was the universal medicine". ....
>
>
>
> ... In 2001, the Japanese Journal for Cancer Research reported that seaweed, known for its high iodine content, reduced the risk for breast cancer in Japanese women who ate it. ...
>
>
>
> ... the Japanese ingest nearly 14mg of iodine daily (mostly from seaweed), that is almost 100 times greater than the US RDA. ...

[http://jamaica-gleaner.com/gleaner/20121030/news/news6.html](http://http://jamaica-gleaner.com/gleaner/20121030/news/news6.html)

# Thread: Gastric Emptying
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gastric-emptying.4596/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### TheKid — 2012-10-31T17:14:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gastric-emptying.4596/post-61093>

My 22-month-old hasn't wanted to eat much for the past couple of days. Today at about 1:00 pm I have her some kefir and some kiwi. She drank about 100ml of kefir and had very few pieces of diced kiwi.

At 6:00 pm she threw up. There were hunks of kiwi and milky liquid, which I assume was the kefir.

This surprised me. I figured that for kids, gastric emptying should happen rather quickly. So to see all that undigested material concerned me.

Does anyone know what the optimal gastric emptying time is for a toddler?

Thanks.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-01T17:41:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gastric-emptying.4596/post-61299>

No idea, but I hope she feels better soon!

# Thread: Hack my labs
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs.4597/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### popcornsally — 2012-10-31T18:15:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs.4597/post-61103>

OK, trying this again. JanSz asked that I put EVERYTHING in this post, so it's gonna be long. Here is all my blood work from the last few years. 44 year old, caucasian pre-menopausal female. 5'2" 132 lb about 30% BF.

Original symptoms: fatigue, acne, scalp sores, severe dandruff, hair loss, muscle pain, tingling/pain in limbs especially left arm and leg, tingling of lower lip, sleep maintenance insomnia, rapid heart rate, acid reflux, nausea, chronic diarrhea, GI pain and bloating, food intolerance, dry eyes, severe foot pain, chronic sinus headaches, recurrent sinusitis/infections, depression, severe irritability, inability to lose weight, water-retention, low blood pressure and near fainting. That's most of it.

Menstrual cycles have always been regular, varying from 26-31 days, average length 28 days. No major changes noted from earlier in life.

Stress has been a big issue for me since childhood and especially during the last 16 years due mostly to school and career. I never realized I was killing myself slowly by not dealing with the stress better. My symptoms really started to culminate in illness about 2009 and was raging by 2010.

Only diagnoses I've gotten so far were: low B12, lowish D, low cortisol in a.m., hypothyroidism and low progesterone.

I started gluten free diet in Aug. 2011 then went to paleo after a few months. Now I find that my blood sugar is more stable if I follow a ketogenic version of the diet. My FBG can be around 98 most days if I eat a few crackers or a piece of fruit the day before. If sticking to keto diet, FBG is usually 86. I don't feel any different except maybe a little less pain when eating keto. I don't follow the autoimmune protocol any more, but did that for several months without noticing any improvement. I do avoid dairy except occasional cream or a bit of cheese. I eat seafood at least once a day.

Current supplements:

Pregnenolone 75 mg 2x/day

DHEA 50 mg 1x/day

7keto DHEA 50 mg 1x/day

Naturethroid 87.5 mg 1x/day

Progesterone cream 20 mg 2x/day day 12-26 of cycle (started in Sept. 12)

Melatonin 0.75 mg before bed

Vit. B complex twice a week

Vit. B12 twice a week

Vit. D 10,000 IU 1x/day

Milk thistle 150 mg 1x/day

Magnesium glycinate 400 mg 1x/day

CoQ 10 100 mg 1x/day

Betaine HCl 650 mg 2x/day

High Vit. Butter oil/FCLO 4 gram/day

Had used probiotic and glutamine in past, only occasionally now

Had taken Isocort for a while but not now

Was taking Adrenal Health caps from Gaia but stopped so I could do ASI test which is pending now.

Current symptoms: muscle stiffness and pain, mostly involving my left leg and sometimes left arm and feet, intermittent diarrhea- a few days per month better when on keto diet, occasional acne and scalp sores, occasional hives, sleep maintenance insomnia mostly during day 21-28 of menstrual cycle, inability to lose 10-15 lb. extra fat, moderate fatigue.

The only supplement or medication that has made a noticeable difference, believe it or not, is pregnenolone. An hour after I take it, my muscle pain gets significantly better. The pain starts to come back about 8 hours after I take it, so that's why I've been taking it twice a day. I feel like more would make me feel even better, but was unsure if it's indicated by my lab tests.

I've cut way back on work and try to manage my stress level. I do feel much less stressed than I was a year ago, but even the tiniest bit of mental or physical stress immediately makes my arm and leg start to tingle and ache! It's so weird! I can no longer work out in the gym or take long bike rides or hikes (which I do for fun not for fitness). My exercise consists of 30-40 minute daily walks with my dogs. I rest a lot.

I feel I've made progress, but still suffer bouts of body aches, fatigue and general malaise. Right now, my left submandibular lymph node is swollen and I have vesicles on my pharynx. This occurs every few months. It used to be sinus infections.

What else can I do to try to get over whatever is wrong with me??? I think it's multifactorial but wonder if it's based on adrenal fatigue, possibly Epstein Barr virus plays a role? I don't care what the diagnosis is, I just want to feel better and don't know what else to do!

Thank you so much for any insights... Hope these links work. On last BW, was drawn on day 22 of menstrual cycle.

[https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfYnphVGIxY1BPaXM](http://https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfYnphVGIxY1BPaXM)

[https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfdFhld0RQbTFobmM](http://https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfdFhld0RQbTFobmM)

[https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfSFZJRkZDRkM5MWs](http://https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfSFZJRkZDRkM5MWs)

[https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfX2wxZGs1VjlwVTg](http://https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfX2wxZGs1VjlwVTg)

[https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfYnJtV2Mxc29meG8](http://https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfYnJtV2Mxc29meG8)

### JanSz — 2012-11-02T03:23:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs.4597/post-61473>

CoQ 10 100 mg 1x/day

Betaine HCl 650 mg 2x/day

High Vit. Butter oil/FCLO 4 gram/day

Had used probiotic and glutamine in past, only occasionally now

Had taken Isocort for a while but not now

Was taking Adrenal Health caps from Gaia but stopped so I could do ASI test which is pending now.

Current symptoms: muscle stiffness and pain, mostly involving my left leg and sometimes left arm and feet, intermittent diarrhea- a few days per month better when on keto diet, occasional acne and scalp sores, occasional hives, sleep maintenance insomnia mostly during day 21-28 of menstrual cycle, inability to lose 10-15 lb. extra fat, moderate fatigue.

The only supplement or medication that has made a noticeable difference, believe it or not, is pregnenolone. An hour after I take it, my muscle pain gets significantly better. The pain starts to come back about 8 hours after I take it, so that's why I've been taking it twice a day. I feel like more would make me feel even better, but was unsure if it's indicated by my lab tests.

I've cut way back on work and try to manage my stress level. I do feel much less stressed than I was a year ago, but even the tiniest bit of mental or physical stress immediately makes my arm and leg start to tingle and ache! It's so weird! I can no longer work out in the gym or take long bike rides or hikes (which I do for fun not for fitness). My exercise consists of 30-40 minute daily walks with my dogs. I rest a lot.

I feel I've made progress, but still suffer bouts of body aches, fatigue and general malaise. Right now, my left submandibular lymph node is swollen and I have vesicles on my pharynx. This occurs every few months. It used to be sinus infections.

What else can I do to try to get over whatever is wrong with me??? I think it's multifactorial but wonder if it's based on adrenal fatigue, possibly Epstein Barr virus plays a role? I don't care what the diagnosis is, I just want to feel better and don't know what else to do!

Thank you so much for any insights... Hope these links work. On last BW, was drawn on day 22 of menstrual cycle.

[https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfYnphVGIxY1BPaXM](http://https://docs.google.com/open?id=0B83dMaT3y5FfYnphVGIxY1BPaXM)

### MartiD — 2012-11-02T16:26:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs.4597/post-61534>

I'm not expert...only my own experiences which are similar. Have you had thyroid antibodies tested? You could possibly have Hashimoto's with the high cholesterol, TSH levels over 1.00 and really low T4. You need to have the antibodies, T3 and RT3 to get an accurate picture of thyroid. I have Hashi's and get bouts of hives, have the scalp sores, occassional muscle aches. If you do have an autoimmune disease then you need to tighten your diet even more (gluten free isn't enough sadly). Good luck!

### popcornsally — 2012-11-02T18:15:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs.4597/post-61568>

Thanks MartiD-- I did have the thyroid antibody tests in July. They indicated no Hashimoto's, to my surprise. And I agree with you about gluten-free not being good enough! for the last 11 months, I've been grain-free and paleo/epi-paleoish along with following a ketogenic path. I never thought I'd be able to eat this way long-term but it gets easier as time goes by. Makes a big difference in the way I feel.

DHEAs 12.4 ng/mL (2-23)

Cortisol 7 am 4.6 ng/mL (3.7-9.5)

Cortisol 12 pm 1.0 ng/mL (1.2-3.0) LOW

Maybe I should start again?

Keep taking phosphatidylserine.

Take PS (phosphatidylCholine)(from bodybio.com)

Patience. Patience.

The plan is to get maximum out of pregnenolone supplementation.

Ideally, you would use only pregnenolone during first half of cycle, to establish good progesterone and cortisol levels.

If you have established good progesterone/cortisol in fist half (follicular)

and you were short of progesterone in mid of second half (luteal),

then you would add progesterone.

\===================================================================

I am glad that in your test for cortisol and DHEAs are having the same units.

[http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/)

When the body is under chronic stress, pregnenolone, the precursor to all other steroidal hormones, is diverted to produce cortisol (known as pregnenolone steal syndrome). When this occurs, it is to the detriment of all other steroidal hormones, like DHEA and its metabolites, including progesterone, testosterone, and the estrogens. Vitamin D production will also be down regulated. As pregnenolone is diverted to cortisol, DHEA depletion begins quickly. The result is an elevated cortisol to DHEA ratio. A normal ratio is approximately 4:1 to 6:1 in most ASI labs. This upside down ratio is able to inhibit the PVN of the brain quickly. Why? Cortisol is needed to make new nerve cells in the hippocampus. This is the key element to the Adrenal Fatigue Rx.

\----------------------------------------------

normal cortisol to DHEA ratio is 4:1 to 6:1

Cortisol 7 am 4.6 ng/mL (3.7-9.5)

DHEAs 12.4 ng/mL (2-23)

cortisol/DHEA=4.6/12.4

\-------------------------------------------------------------

had you had

cortisol=9.5

DHEAs=23

we would call it as good levels,

that would be

9.5/23=0.41

23/9.5=2.4

\---------------------------------------------------

This is a place holder, hopefully dr K will help us in clarifying this situation.

Note that dr K was talking DHEA and you have DHEAs.

\========================

I posted question about this to dr K, here:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?5400-ASI-from-popcornsally&p=64821&viewfull=1#post64821](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?5400-ASI-from-popcornsally&p=64821&viewfull=1#post64821)

//

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs.4597/post-63029>

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?5400-ASI-from-popcornsally&p=64854&viewfull=1#post64854](http://http://forum.jackkruse.com/showthread.php?5400-ASI-from-popcornsally&p=64854&viewfull=1#post64854)

\----------------------------------------

So, it is your choice, if you are able book consult with dr Kruse and you will get more detailed answer.

\----------------------------------------

Absent that,

you are striving for a top levels of cortisol and DHEA and DHEAs.

Supplementation:

preferably, attempt to achieve most using

pregnenolone and DHEA supplementation

and

frequent blood testing.

Life style and nutrition per dr K directions may likely help more than supplements.

For that see his bloogs and discussion threads.

..................

Other than ZRT labs, you may do blood tests at LabCorp.

1 ------- Pregnenolone, Mass Spectrometry--Test Number: 140707 CPT Code: 84140

2 ------- 17α-Hydroxypregnenolone by HPLC-MS/MS

3 ------- 17α-Hydroxyprogesterone by HPLC-MS/MS

4 ------- Progesterone--Test Number: 004317 CPT Code: 84144

5 ------- Cortisol, Free, Serum, With CBG (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500440 CPT Code: 82533; 84449

6 ------- Cortisol, AM & PM Test Number: 104000 CPT Code: 82533(x2) (7:30AM, 12PM)

7 ------- Corticosteroid-binding Globulin (CBG), Transcortin

8 ------- Corticotropin Releasing Hormone (CRH)

9 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) Sulfate--Test Number: 004020 CPT Code: 82627

10 ------- Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA)--Test Number: 004100 CPT Code: 82626

11 ------- Androstenedione, LCMS--Test Number: 004705 CPT Code: 82157

12 ------- Testosterone, Total, Serum Test Number: 004226 CPT Code: 84403

13 ------- Testosterone, Free and Weakly Bound--Test Number: 143255 CPT Code: 84402; 84403

14 ------- SHBG

15 ------- Dihydrotestosterone (DHT)--Test Number: 500142 CPT Code: 82651

16 ------- Androstanediol Glucuronide (Endocrine Sciences) Test Number: 500881 CPT Code: 82154

17 ------- Estrogens, Total; Test Number: 004549 CPT Code: 82672

18 ------- Estradiol, Sensitive Test Number: 140244 CPT Code: 82670

19 ------- Estrone, Serum Test Number: 004564 CPT Code: 82679

### Paradox — 2012-11-01T00:27:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61179>

I have just found the perfect quickie meal at Trader Joes. Steamer clams in the freezer.. Although they have butter and cream when you open the package they are in two frozen buttery cubes that I just toss down the sink and add bacon fat to the pan. They are delish even if they aren't perfect because they are slightly cooked. But quick! Easy! Yes and I'm thinking since I eat a whole tray for breakfast and don't even give food another thought till about 4 or 5 they must be doing something right for me.

Just though I'd share...

### nonchalant — 2012-11-01T00:56:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61183>

Great idea, Paradox. I have yet to give clams a try.

### Paradox — 2012-11-01T01:00:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61184>

> **Nonchalant;62906 wrote:** Great idea, Paradox. I have yet to give clams a try.

I thought they would be hard to get down the hatch but I found myself really enjoying them. Could be the tons of garlic they put with them.

### kathylu — 2012-11-01T03:59:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61205>

Clams are by far my favorite shellfish!

### Paradox — 2012-11-01T06:03:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61219>

> **kathylu;62930 wrote:** Clams are by far my favorite shellfish!

Do you buy them fresh and cook them or do yo eat them raw like oysters?

### johnnyb — 2012-11-01T06:45:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61221>

> **Paradox;62944 wrote:** Do you buy them fresh and cook them or do yo eat them raw like oysters?

Clams are good eats and oysters are good nutrition.  I buy fresh clams anytime I'm at Costco and they actually have them. I steam them just until they open. I suspect they are still a little under cooked by some standards and over cooked by others.

### Inger — 2012-11-01T14:02:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61258>

> **Paradox;62944 wrote:** Do you buy them fresh and cook them or do yo eat them raw like oysters?

I have eaten clams raw too. You can very well, just like oysters. Just make sure they are alive first!

The problem is.. I cannot eat many raw. Just a few, like 3. Then they start to taste not good anymore.. IDK why. I prefer the cooked taste. I can eat a lot of them cooked! One of the very few things I realy like better cooked. I eat oysters always raw and love the taste though.

I can buy precooked and prefrozen clams here, 250 grams netto for 2,60 €. So cheap! I buy them often! I add them to my cooked fish-seaweed soup. So good...

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-01T16:33:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61280>

I think I'm going to move to Norway.

The only clams I can find are canned. Grrrrr. Still tasty though.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-01T16:43:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61282>

Where/how are you getting fresh clams? Are there options for pre shucked? Pics please?

### Inger — 2012-11-01T16:49:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61285>

> **Cavemam;63013 wrote:** Where/how are you getting fresh clams? Are there options for pre shucked? Pics please?

They have fresh clams quite often here, around 1 kg brutto costs about 6-9 €, it is one meal for one person, netto. If I buy them cooked, pre sucked and pre frozen they are much much cheaper. Of course I can get canned too here but I figure the frozen are healthier...

I will make some pics for you Cavemam when I buy them next time, I promise! They are mostly farmed but I searched how the farming works and they get no feed and live in the sea in open waters like the wild ones so they are as good I figure, like farmed oysters! They eat only plancton and need no feed you see..

### ealachan — 2012-11-01T17:12:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61292>

> **Cavemam;63013 wrote:** Where/how are you getting fresh clams? Are there options for pre shucked? Pics please?

My local fish place has fresh clams seasonally. They don't sell them shucked here, but clams are super quick and easy to cook, and pretty easy to shuck if you want to eat them raw.

### kathylu — 2012-11-01T20:19:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61359>

> **Paradox;62944 wrote:** Do you buy them fresh and cook them or do yo eat them raw like oysters?

I buy them live and soak them in salt water for several hours at home. They are filter feeders, so they start filtering the water and blow out all the sand and other nasties in their digestive tract. Then I steam them until they just open. I prefer to dip them in melted butter with lemon, but now I just use lemon.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-02T03:21:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61471>

### Paradox — 2012-11-02T06:17:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-61493>

> **kathylu;63093 wrote:** I buy them live and soak them in salt water for several hours at home. They are filter feeders, so they start filtering the water and blow out all the sand and other nasties in their digestive tract. Then I steam them until they just open. I prefer to dip them in melted butter with lemon, but now I just use lemon.

Thank you, thank you for the tip to remove the sand & nasties! I will try it on the first fresh, live ones I buy.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2012-11-05T03:21:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62125>

Most of the seafood I get is from frozen. I will buy canned sardines, canned oysters (only way to afford and/or find at this time) and maybe sometimes canned kippers. Since Jack said that 'bad' fish/seafood is better than grass-fed beef I go with nutrient density and $ for my shopping criteria. I don't know, but I've never had something so easy as epi-paleo in my life. When I ate crap McDonalds and pop...I felt like eating was a chore...it was so hard. Even when I first entered 'paleo' style...it was a lot easier, but still didn't 'fit' the way seafood does. Seafood is like second nature to me. I don't understand when people find it difficult to incorporate it into their diet even just a few times a week. Me, I eat it daily.

### philerup — 2012-11-06T04:03:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62306>

agree with Gladina. Is seafood that hard to eat? I eat it daily also. Last night, I had a simple shrimp and oyster stir fry with just salt and pepper and soy sauce and wine. Rather tasty if you ask me.

### caroline — 2012-11-06T08:24:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62317>

> **Gladina;63909 wrote:** Most of the seafood I get is from frozen. I will buy canned sardines, canned oysters (only way to afford and/or find at this time) and maybe sometimes canned kippers. I don't understand when people find it difficult to incorporate it into their diet even just a few times a week. Me, I eat it daily.

Gladina - I so totally agree with you - epi paleo is so much easier! I often just have a large can \[16oz\] of salmon for supper. very satisfying and couldn't be easier. I also love Inger's salmon and could eat that every day. I just throw in some shrimp here and there and fresh oysters. I thought I wanted a steak last nite but it just did not sit well so won't be doing that again any time soon. I also throw in a MHS now and then! feel so much better!

### caroline — 2012-11-06T08:26:50+0200

Does anyone enjoy mussels? I used to eat them but haven't for awhile. They are very yummy in a fish chowder.

### Inger — 2012-11-06T11:00:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62324>

@ Gladina, I love your posts..! I do not understand eather why it is hard for some to eat fish...hmm.

@ Caroline, I eat mussels often! They are the "clams" I get here for so cheap if pre shucked and prefrozen. 250 g for 2,50 €. I get them fresh too but they are much more expensive!

I often put them in my "fish soup" that is a lazy recipe..lol. I just take fish I do not like too much raw, and cook it in a little water, just enough to cover the fish.. add turmeric/curcumin, sea salt, seaweeds and cook it just for a minute. Then I put the soup in a bowl and add in 100 to 200 gram of mussel meat. Makes the soup cool down to nice eating temperature and I can eat it immidiately. Sometimes I add some olive oil or coconut oil in the end too. Man is it good!!! And so very easy! I eat this very often.

Caroline I love what you are doing!

### caroline — 2012-11-06T12:43:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62329>

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-06T14:16:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62341>

> **kathylu;63093 wrote:** I buy them live and soak them in salt water for several hours at home. They are filter feeders, so they start filtering the water and blow out all the sand and other nasties in their digestive tract. Then I steam them until they just open. I prefer to dip them in melted butter with lemon, but now I just use lemon.

Great tip. What's your salt to water ratio??

### kathylu — 2012-11-06T15:48:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62354>

> **Shijin13;64153 wrote:** Great tip. What's your salt to water ratio??

Well, salt water is about 35 parts per thousand NaCl to water. That's roughly 2 tbsp salt to 1 quart water (trust me here!). Anything from 1 tbsp to 2 tbsp per quart is just fine. They tolerate a wide range of salinity....and hey, you are cooking them in a little while anyway....

### nonchalant — 2012-11-06T17:47:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62364>

When I put 1 Tblsp sea salt into a quart of water, it tastes similar to the salt water I experienced in the Gulf of Mexico. Not nearly as fabulous, but similar.

### kathylu — 2012-11-06T19:02:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62379>

> **Nonchalant;64176 wrote:** When I put 1 Tblsp sea salt into a quart of water, it tastes similar to the salt water I experienced in the Gulf of Mexico. Not nearly as fabulous, but similar.

I usually use 1 tbsp of kosher salt per quart of water. I save my Celtic Sea Salt for bone broth and cooking.

### ealachan — 2012-11-06T19:07:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62380>

> **caroline;64128 wrote:** Does anyone enjoy mussels? I used to eat them but haven't for awhile. They are very yummy in a fish chowder.

I love mussels. I steam them with garlic, onion and white wine, or serve them with tomato sauce. They're also super good in a curry coconut milk broth.

### diane — 2012-11-06T19:17:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-62384>

Mussels are super cheap here. About $1.50 - $2.00 per pound. I wash them, then cook with coconut milk and green curry paste. I would do a white wine sauce but I never have white wine in the house!

### vkiernan — 2012-11-14T20:22:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-64305>

> **ealachan;64192 wrote:** I love mussels. I steam them with garlic, onion and white wine, or serve them with tomato sauce. They're also super good in a curry coconut milk broth.

I bought some but I think they are cooked already (in the frozen section). Do you think it would still work to put them in your garlic sauce while frozen and cook them until thawed and warmed? Sounds really good to me. Is buying them precooked or frozen a bad idea???

### furface — 2012-12-10T19:02:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-67053>

I think I might try to make a homemade semi clam chowder when I get paid in a couple of weeks.

I'm going to buy some manilla, and savory clams, and steam them in a pot in 1/2 cup of water, some kerrygold butter, diced onion, celery, salt, pepper, garlic, and Emeril's essence spice.

After the clams have been steamed, I'll remove them, then add about 1 cup of whipping cream to the liquid that's in the pot, and let it thicken on slow. Meanwhile, I'll shuck the clams, then return them to the pot, mixing them with everything else, then hopefully enjoy.

### kathylu — 2012-12-10T20:59:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-67065>

Try just scrubbing the clams well, then add them to your cream base. They'll open up and you can just spoon the clams into your bowl, with or without the shells. Seems like extra work to cook the clams first. My 2 cents.

### furface — 2012-12-10T22:20:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/clams.4602/post-67082>

Good idea, Kathylu, I just might do it that way. # Thread: Omega 3s shown to improve working memory
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omega-3s-shown-to-improve-working-memory.4607/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-01T08:30:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/omega-3s-shown-to-improve-working-memory.4607/post-61223>

This seems to be the first time this has been shown for working memory.

> Previous studies in humans suggest that nâ€“3 PUFA deficiency is associated with impairment in mood \[1\] and cognitive functioning \[2\]. Some \[3\]â€“\[5\], but not all studies \[6\]â€“\[9\] suggest that the supplementation of nâ€“3 PUFA in several neuropsychiatric disorders such as mood disorders, schizophrenia and attention deficit hyperactivity disorder holds promise as a primary or adjunctive therapy. Mechanistic studies are discovering roles of nâ€“3 PUFAs in modulation of neuronal membrane fluidity and permeability, enhancement of monoamine transmission, alteration of the activity of protein kinases and phosphatidylinositol-associated second messenger systems, alteration in gene expression and decreased oxidative stress and inflammation. Nonetheless, how these actions relate to the putative effects of nâ€“3 PUFA on cognitive functioning and affective symptoms is unknown. ...

[http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0046832](http://http://www.plosone.org/article/info%3Adoi%2F10.1371%2Fjournal.pone.0046832)

# Thread: Gut microbiome study
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microbiome-study.4611/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-01T09:29:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microbiome-study.4611/post-61228>

Darn - this is interesting barely time to post ...

They tried feeding cats carbs to see what would happen to their gut bacteria. The whole story is quite interesting.

Among other things:

> A positive relationship between lactobacilli, blood cholesterol, and blood leptin levels was found by the research team. Leptin signals the body to stop eating when the stomach is full, so it is possible that there is a connection between lactobacilli and cholesterol metabolism, appetite and body weight regulation.

[http://www.redorbit.com/news/science/1112718706/gut-microbiome-nature-cat-feline-102312/](http://http://www.redorbit.com/news/science/1112718706/gut-microbiome-nature-cat-feline-102312/)

Original source is apparently the BMJ.

### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-01T16:22:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microbiome-study.4611/post-61275>

Not entirely transferable, as cats are obligate carnivores...but I'm beginning to think a pretty good good case can be made for humans as obligate carnivores.

We may lack a carnassal tooth and scissor ing jaws, but we also have tools to avoid the sheer necessity of such.

Interesting food for thought.

### Michael — 2012-11-01T16:48:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gut-microbiome-study.4611/post-61283>

I'm not quite sure what we get out of this study. Obviously, cats aren't "carbovores" and shouldn't really be fed them. I guess what looks like some intricate relations between the energy substrate, particular strains of gut bacteria, and some hormones are interesting. Maybe the takeaway is that if you \*are\* going to eat carbs, you'd better have the right gut bacteria.

One wonders what would happen if they continued feeding the cats carbs but also disrupted their gut flora - by giving them antibiotics, say. Not that I'm suggesting anyone actually do that to cats: I think they deserve better of us.

# Thread: Liver transplant makes 61-yr-old â€˜young againâ€™
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-transplant-makes-61-yr-old-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%CB%9Cyoung-again%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2.4613/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AlphaCog — 2012-11-01T13:52:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liver-transplant-makes-61-yr-old-%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%CB%9Cyoung-again%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2.4613/post-61256>

[India Times: Liver transplant makes 61-yr-old ‘young again’](http://http://articles.timesofindia.indiatimes.com/2012-07-13/india/32662636_1_liver-transplant-transplant-surgeon-diabetes-drugs)

> AHMEDABAD: Executive registrar of Mudra Institute of Communication and Advertising KGK Pillai, 61, has recently got a three-year extension. This, after he got a fresh lease of life when a 21-year-old youth's liver was implanted in him in 2008.
>
>
>
> The liver of the young man, who died in an accident, has miraculously reversed ageing. Pillai's hair have turned from grey to black, his wrinkles are gone and he is no longer dependent on diabetes drugs.
>
>
>
> Four years after the transplant in Chennai, 61-year-old Pillai says, "I don't just look young, I feel like reborn as a young man." He is happy his complexion has also improved thanks Dr Anand Khakhar — a transplant surgeon in Chennai who belongs to Rajkot — for the transformation.
>
>
>
> "I used to take medicine for diabetes for 12 years. After the transplant, I am off drugs as my sugar levels are mysteriously under control," says Pillai, who takes morning walks every day.
>
>
>
> Doctors say that the blackening of hair and renewed energy experienced by Pillai can be attributed to availability of nutrition to the body as liver functions have normalized.

Just shows how important a healthy liver is for youth and longevity.

# Thread: Seasonality
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality.4621/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-01T20:21:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality.4621/post-61360>

I just got back from the supermarket.

Passing through the checkout with my fish, shellfish, olive oil, and red wine ;-) I noticed a pile of boxes of mince pies ready for customers to grab and place on the conveyor belt.

That says it all really. The displays of non-perishable goods for Christmas have been up for some weeks already - that's how the supermarkets operate. But mince pies at the checkout in early November!

When you think about it you remember that the early Church moved the date of Christmas to midwinter to coincide with existing festivals. Now those were there for a reason. It was because they were a relief right in the middle of the long dark winters when it was cold and dark and food was a little tight. You saved up your delicacies and had a brief splash just at the point when the nights were longest. Rather a poetic idea.

Now we're supposed to start on dried fruit and sugar in pastry cases two months in advance, because that suits modern commerce.

Similarly hot cross buns now appear weeks in advance. My mother says she remembers when people would queue outside the bakery to get them on the morning of Good Friday.

The same holds for Easter eggs. Cadbury's now sells what it calls a "creme egg" -- it's filled with dyed glucose syrup not cream, of course -- pretty much all year round.

It's supposed to be a party all day every day. There's no rhythm in life, and in the end even the year's festivals won't stand out as anything different from the normal run of things.

### Inger — 2012-11-01T20:54:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality.4621/post-61375>

> **Michael;63094 wrote:**
>
> It's supposed to be a party all day every day. There's no rhythm in life, and in the end even the year's festivals won't stand out as anything different from the normal run of things.

Yes.. Michael, it is a bit crazy for sure.

And people get more and more unhappy.. They have more and more but get less happy. What do that tell us?

Maybe we need less. Maybe happines emerges from.. pain? Hmmm I sometimes wonder if there is a connection. From having nothing really.. or from loosing something.

It is weird for sure. But I sure feel a lot more when I first was without. Like in spring, when I find the first nettels. I feel just..wow. I taste them and they are amazing. The whole winter there were no.

Or after many cloudy days, and the sun shines again. Amazing sun..

In Nature is so much joy because of the seasons! That really have got much lost in modern days. Jacks protocol have made me so much more aware, how important the seasonality is, in any aspect of my life. That it indeed is a very beautiful thing with a deeper purpose too.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-01T21:13:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality.4621/post-61380>

whats crazy is the how consumerism keeps driving the "holidays" earlier and earlier. holiday (christmas) displays were up in costco at the end of September?

The ancients followed and worshiped the changing seasons for a reason. Their lives depended upon it. we've lost touch w/the magnificence of nature, both the beauty and the destruction.

Igner is 100% right when you pay attention to the natural world - you day is amazing. this morning as I was walking from the bus into work - I felt like I was vibrating. the air, the grass, the dew, the sun and the clouds were amazingly crisp and clear. I'm looking forward to getting back outside when I catch the bus... I've been agitated all afternoon since I've only had one quick opportunity to get out side for a few minutes... I want to get out and embrace the cold, and relish in the thought of just feeling the chill in the air....

### Michael — 2012-11-01T21:25:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality.4621/post-61383>

> **Shijin13;63114 wrote:**
>
> Igner is 100% right when you pay attention to the natural world - you day is amazing.

Yeah, I agree with you both. There's an interesting link with the current meditation thread in that some people doing that say that it can change you such that you can come out into nature and see everything as if for the first time. I wouldn't know personally - I haven't the self-discipline to do it regularly.

I used to live in the country and it's still an easy walk away. I do hike a bit, but I would like more time in nature - to see everything coming in the wild and forage more food from the wild. I think sweet chestnuts would be in season around here now.

@Inger do you know people who still keep up the Scandinavian custom of having a Yule log? I guess that's another expression of a wish to remind oneself of heat and light at the time of the longest nights.

There are people using one here on restored 16th century farm. Wish I'd been there!

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-01T21:36:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality.4621/post-61385>

> **Michael;63117 wrote:**
>
> @Inger do you know people who still keep up the Scandinavian custom of having a Yule log? I guess that's another expression of a wish to remind oneself of heat and light at the time of the longest nights.

I do a yule log. granted its not a big one that will burn for days, and still have enough left to start the next years yule fire. I always set aside 2-3 logs that are to be kept for burning the next winter solstice, and use them to feed the fire. We gather around, and pass around a talking stick, and talk about the things that we're greatful for each year. I'll make poultices to burn as offerings for the next year, and we eat out by the fire, and enjoy it. We also pick a spirit to watch over the fire and guide us into the days of the returning light.

My family has been doing this for years - we have a huge solstice bonfire, everyone brings something to eat, and we gather. one year the spirit was the magpie - and she's a tricky spirit. when they fire masters started the yule log - the circle was a good 25 feet from where the log was lit. they had to go out and do work (as it was a weekday) when my uncle came back around 10am - the yule log was consumed, as was the circle, all the way up a telephone pole and stopped right below an actual magpie nest. my uncle and the other fire masters had to go out and pick a new yule log to be re-lite. this one burned at a normal rate... the whole event was crazy - the burn marks were in the perfect shape of a circle.

winter solstice is my favorite holiday to celebrate after we've celebrated, then we can bring in the christmas tree and decorate it, but it must come down on the 26th.

### ealachan — 2012-11-01T21:57:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality.4621/post-61391>

Maybe it's because I grew up (and still live) way out in the sticks in a place that has very defined seasons, but I think it truly takes far more effort to become DISCONNECTED with nature and the seasons than it takes to live with them. At least around here, you have to be pretty damn determined to shut yourself away, cram the earbuds in your ears, and really keep your head down to miss the seasons changing, to miss the air changing, the smells changing, the sounds of some animals disappearing and others arriving.

When it comes right down to it, all you have to do is go outside and look around you. Nature is not coy. She will show you exactly what's going on and what part you should play in that drama, if you just open your eyes and quit over-thinking things.

### Michael — 2012-11-02T08:32:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality.4621/post-61498>

> **ealachan;63127 wrote:** Maybe it's because I grew up (and still live) way out in the sticks in a place that has very defined seasons, but I think it truly takes far more effort to become DISCONNECTED with nature and the seasons than it takes to live with them.
>
>
> At one level they can't be missed. You see the green coming in the spring; things becoming gradually overblown like a person on the SAD as the year progresses; the leaves change colour and fall in the autumn; and you get cold and wet walking to the train in the winter. You get burnt and want to take your coat off in summer; you see your breath in winter. Birds migrate.
>
>
>
> You could hardly not be aware.
>
>
>
> Whether you're aware of the seasons, and the whole aspect of nature, in _quite the same way as the 17th century people referred to in the video is another matter - well you're not. Could_ you experience the wet weather in quite teh same way as people who wore woollen clothing that could take days to dry when it got wet? Who among us spends all day outside and sees and feels every change, however small? It's not even clear that the few people who actually work the land would have quite the same awareness. And it's another and equally interesting question how one's awareness would compare with that of, say, Thomas Traherne.
>
>
>
> But the point of the thread was specifically seasonality with respect to particular points in the year. That all tied in in an earlier form of life. Modern commerce blurs the boundaries, or certainly tries to - to supermarkets the festivals of the year are just different sales opportunities not something that's part of the rhythm of the year. So you try to sell people mince pies in October or November. A blink of the eye ago in terms of historical time the dried fruit that went into those was scarce and dear - it was kept for the festival.
>
>
>
> You go a little further back in time and, the reality of seasonality becomes even more pronounced. Look at what Langland's Piers has to live on "till Lammas time" - curds, cream, oatcake and some vegetables - because that's what there is. [Lammas](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lammas) is the festival, and also the harvest, and till then there simply is very little. Here's the relevant passage in translation
>
>
>
> "I have no penny," quoth Piers, "Pullets for to buy
>
> No neither geese nor piglets, but two green \[new\] cheeses,
>
> A few curds and cream and an oaten cake
>
> And two loaves of beans and bran to bake for my little ones.
>
> And besides I say by my soul I have no salt bacon,
>
> Nor no little eggs, by Christ, collops for to make.
>
> But I have parsley and leeks and many cabbages,
>
> And besides a cow and a calf and a cart mare
>
> To draw afield my dung the while the drought lasteth.
>
> And by this livelihood we must live till lammas time \[August\].
>
> And by that I hope to have harvest in my croft.
>
> And then may I prepare the dinner as I dearly like.
>
> All the poor people those peascods fatten.
>
> Neans and baked apples they brought in their laps.
>
> Shalots and chervils and ripe cherries many
>
> And proffered pears these present...

[http://www.ucalgary.ca/applied\_history/tutor/endmiddle/bluedot/piers.html](http://http://www.ucalgary.ca/applied_history/tutor/endmiddle/bluedot/piers.html)

### ealachan — 2012-11-02T17:30:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality.4621/post-61554>

Right, and my point was that people over-complicate and over-analyze. Yes, they're trying to sell you mince pies in October. So \*\*\*\*ing what? Don't buy them. Go outside. Look at the clouds. Listen to the geese honking and the wind sighing through the pines. Living Life > Hype-ranalyzing.

As much as I'm sure it's likely to get twisted to sound otherwise, the above (and my original post) were not an attack on you or your way of thinking at all. Your time is your own, and you're free to spend it thinking about whatever you'd like, including the strategic marketing of mince pies and Cadbury creme eggs, if that's your thing.

...their study also suggests that there is no "central clock" in the brain that is relied upon for all tasks involving timing. Instead, it appears as though each of the brain's circuits responsible for different actions are capable of independently producing an accurate timing signal.

# Thread: These Aborigines were not hunter-gatherers
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/these-aborigines-were-not-hunter-gatherers.4630/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-02T08:43:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/these-aborigines-were-not-hunter-gatherers.4630/post-61499>

I posted this earlier, but it was in the middle of another thread, and maybe it's worth one of its own.

The researcher says that much of the life of the Australian Aborigines remains a mystery. Their way of life has gone, and they kept no records. What we do have is accounts by early white Australians, and the picture they reveal is astounding. They were making use of the fish. But they weren't just fishing opportunistically in a low-tech manner.

They had constructed fish traps from rocks on the river in the Murray-Darling Basin. It also seems they had balks of timber running along the river to channel it. They had built systems of ponds. These, he says, were used as holding areas. (The same as what we'd call a "stew pond" in a medieval monastery, I suppose.) The fish from these were sometimes used for meetings where tribes met to negotiate and socialize.

**The researcher says he had thought of the Aborigines of this area (the Murray-Darling River basin) as hunter-gatherers, but he now believes they were primarily people who managed water resources.**

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;iqANpeOCwGU\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iqANpeOCwGU\[/video\]\[/video\]

More videos on this here:

[http://australianriverrestorationcentre.com.au/mdb/troutcod/index.php](http://http://australianriverrestorationcentre.com.au/mdb/troutcod/index.php)

# Thread: Ideas to Make a Home more OPTIMAL.

### freshveggies — 2012-11-02T15:25:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61520>

HI, I am asking for ideas that you all have to make a home more optimal. Also, if you could start from scratch and build --what would you put in a new home to make it more optimal?

For instance, right now I use a lamp in my bathroom with a yellow bulb so I can shower and put on makeup without turning the regular light on . How to make that better?

Need soaking tub. Maybe a cool cave like Dr. K

Other ideas for more optimal? Fireplace to give a nice light?

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-02T16:17:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61531>

Omg I have so much to say on this! My son is out of school tho and it will be late - one word - off gassing.

### freshveggies — 2012-11-02T16:31:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61536>

> **Cavemam;63284 wrote:** Omg I have so much to say on this! My son is out of school tho and it will be late - one word - off gassing.

I agree--my head is spinning. I am looking into products to not off gas---like using exterior grad plywood indoor instead of the interior grade.

what about pex pipes-- Copper ones tend to get pinholes in them with out city water. (actually I will now be country and on a different water supply that is better ) They have been phased out.

I bought a book called Healthy Construction Guidelines.

Looking into sunplans to gain solar heat, but not create a hotbox. Need thermal mass inside too.

Anyone done this or studied this.

Beside healthy home, I am looking to think outside the box and look for optimal. But I for one don't have all the answers, that is why I am asking you all.

### ealachan — 2012-11-02T16:31:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61537>

If it were me building my optimal dream home, I'd have a passive solar set-up on a south-facing slope. I'd have tons of windows so that I could have as much natural light as possible all day long. Big DEEP soaking tub in the bathroom, another one outside on the patio. I'd want several functional fireplaces inside the house and out - one in the kitchen to cook on, as well. My bedroom windows would be east-facing so that I got sunlight first thing every morning, and my patio outside would be west-facing so that I could sit out and watch the sun set every night.

My bedroom windows would be east-facing so that I got sunlight first thing every morning, and my patio outside would be west-facing so that I could sit out and watch the sun set every night.

A woman after my own dreams. Yes, I finally found that lot. On the edge of town, in the country facing south with nothing but prairie on the south and west. Bedroom to be NE corner to stay cooler. Lots of windows to the south with the proper overhang to not get the hot summer heat.

I have to look into fireplaces. This will also be small and optimized. I do have a budget that I have to live with. I will put my money in cabinets that don't off gas and hardwood floors in all rooms except bath and laundry.
