### nonchalant — 2012-11-02T17:37:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61555>

> **MartiD;63295 wrote:** Hmmm....optimal bathroom. How about an icemaker attached to the bathtub? LOL

EXCELLENT idea!

### Jonathin — 2012-11-02T20:18:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61594>

The issue of air quality can be largely solved by using an air exchanger. They have been in use in Japan for some time. They are a fairly small unit that exchanges outside air for inside air and conserves most of the heat. Ideally, my best options are to use [faswall](http://http://faswall.com/) construction, natural clay plaster on the inside, poured adobe floor and integral rocket wood stove plus passive solar design and real lumber where necessary. And a nature-based water treatment system that uses biology and movement.

Another concern is the ambient EMF radiation especially in higher population areas. If you think light is bad for us, we are exposed to about 200 million times the levels due to manmade EMF transmitting devices (according to an expert that I have confidence in). Obviously it comes from outside but like pollution the inside levels can be worse - cell phone, wifi, electric wiring. Something to consider. Lifestyle choices are a big issue.

### freshveggies — 2012-11-02T20:32:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ideas-to-make-a-home-more-optimal.4634/post-61598>

> **Jonathin;63352 wrote:** The issue of air quality can be largely solved by using an air exchanger. They have been in use in Japan for some time. They are a fairly small unit that exchanges outside air for inside air and conserves most of the heat. Ideally, my best options are to use [faswall](http://http://faswall.com/) construction, natural clay plaster on the inside, poured adobe floor and integral rocket wood stove plus passive solar design and real lumber where necessary. And a nature-based water treatment system that uses biology and movement.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Another concern is the ambient EMF radiation especially in higher population areas. If you think light is bad for us, we are exposed to about 200 million times the levels due to manmade EMF transmitting devices (according to an expert that I have confidence in). Obviously it comes from outside but like pollution the inside levels can be worse - cell phone, wifi, electric wiring. Something to consider. Lifestyle choices are a big issue.

Thank you Jonathin. I have read about the plaster inside. Your design sounds good. I will be looking into air exchangers, but I know there are different kinds and I need to figure some of that out. I do think that the architect with sunplans helps with that too.

I am very much into reducing emf's as I am a very sensitive person. No wifi in my house. I will be further away from neighbors in the new location so that will be better. Do you now about how to lower the emf's coming from house wiring. I have read about putting the wires in a pipe in the bedroom and or using romex wires?

# Thread: seasonality and sex
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-sex.4638/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-02T16:28:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-sex.4638/post-61535>

I was thinking about the way in which a sense of the seasons must have pervaded people's consciousness in the past - and also how seasonality affected people's behaviour perhaps mostly unconsiously.

Anyway, I remembered how in _Kabloona_ the entire Eskimo village, to the anthropologist's amazement, was seized with something like a fever of sexual feeling.

I think one might speculate that the seasons had a bigger effect on Arctic dwellers, because day-length changes so much there. Then, all of a sudden, I remembered that line from Locksley Hall - that spring was when

> a young man's fancies lightly turn to love

http://www.ficml.org/jemimap/voy/colony/Locksley\_Hall.html

Presumably, Tennyson couldn't have used that line, if it had sounded ridiculous to his contemporaries.

Human fertility may be year-round but one suspects that interest may have waxed and waned somewhat around the year in the past. After all, why dance round a maypole in May rather than February - besides the fact that February would be a bit cold for that?

It also occurs to me that this is another area of human life that commerce has tried to annex. Sex is one of the main things used to advertise products. (The other, of course, is snobbery.)

### AKMan — 2012-11-02T18:03:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seasonality-and-sex.4638/post-61565>

It would make sense for babies to be born in March when fresh food is available for nursing mothers. That would make June the optimal month to 'fancy love'.

# Thread: epi-paleo in popular culture... movies, documentaries, music and books
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-in-popular-culture-movies-documentaries-music-and-books.4639/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Entelechy — 2012-11-02T16:34:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-in-popular-culture-movies-documentaries-music-and-books.4639/post-61538>

As quality input is required for change,

I thought a thread to capture peoples favourite examples could act as a resource.

Movie.

[The Snow Walker](http://http://www.imdb.com/title/tt0337721/),

the female star,Annabella Piugattuk, is a real Inuk, who was raised in the traditional culture.

there are scenes of eating a still wriggling raw fish, eating fat and bits of meat while butchering a caribou....

the movie shows the deep connection with nature that allows them to survive in the barren tundra.

for those who prefer text, the book the film is based on is called "Walk Well, My Brother" by Farley Mowat.

Farley has written numerous books about the northern Canada such as People of the Deer, Lost in the Barrens, and Tundra: Selections from the Great Accounts of Arctic Land Voyages. I've not read a Mowat book I've not gotten something out of.

# Thread: Placeboâ€™s Effect May Depend on Your Genes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/placebo%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-effect-may-depend-on-your-genes.4641/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jonathin — 2012-11-02T20:30:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/placebo%C3%A2%E2%82%AC%E2%84%A2s-effect-may-depend-on-your-genes.4641/post-61596>

This adds another dimension to the process of manifesting self-change.

\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_\_

from: [http://deskarati.com/2012/10/26/placebos-effect-may-depend-on-your-genes/#more-24199](http://http://deskarati.com/2012/10/26/placebos-effect-may-depend-on-your-genes/#more-24199)

**Placeboâ€™s Effect May Depend on Your Genes**

Posted on October 26, 2012 by Deskarati

Your response to placebos, or dummy medicine, may depend on your genes, according to a new study. People with a gene variant that codes for higher levels of the brain chemical dopamine respond better to placebos than those with the low-dopamine version. The findings, reported online Oct. 23 in the journal PLoS One, could help researchers design medical studies that distinguish the placebo response from the underlying effect of a medicine â€” the real aim of drug trials.

â€œThis is a possible way to discern who is going to be a placebo responder or nonresponder in a clinical trial,â€￾ said study co-author Kathryn Hall of Beth Israel Deaconess Medical Center in Brookline, Mass.

People report feeling better after receiving a placebo, such as a sugar pill or fake treatment, for conditions ranging from chronic pain to Parkinsonâ€™s disease. But only some patients respond strongly, and thereâ€™s no way to predict who will improve on a placebo. A few studies have provided clues. Differences in versions of the catechol-O-methyltransferase (COMT) gene, which determines levels of dopamine in the brainâ€™s prefrontal cortex, are linked to differences in reward-seeking and pain perception. People with the high-dopamine version, or allele, of the COMT gene feel pain more acutely and seek rewards more strongly than those who have the low-dopamine copy.

That led the researchers to wonder whether the gene modulates placebo response. To find out, Hall and her colleagues analyzed DNA from 104 patients with irritable bowel syndrome who were randomized to one of three groups: One was told they were on the waiting list for treatment, another received a placebo in the form of seemingly real, curt acupuncture, and the third group received fake acupuncture from a caring, warm practitioner who looked patients in the eye, asked about their progress, and even touched them lightly, Hall told LiveScience.

Patients with the high-dopamine version of the gene felt slightly better after seeing the curt, all-business health-care provider that gave placebo acupuncture. But they were six times as likely to say their symptoms improved with a caring practitioner as those with the low-dopamine gene, who didnâ€™t improve much in any group. The findings suggest that medical studies called clinical trials could identify treatment versus placebo effect by grouping patients by gene variant, Hall said. Knowing up front the level of placebo effect for a clinical trial could reduce the cost of the trial significantly by using fewer participants, for instance, she said.

People with the high-dopamine allele of the gene may do well on the placebo with the nurturing treatment because they are generally more attuned to their environments, said University of Michigan psychiatrist Jon-Kar Zubieta, who was not involved in the study.

â€œIt speaks about an interaction between the environment and the gene,â€￾ Zubieta said. â€œItâ€™s very possible that individuals with this allele are more able to process those positive environmental cues.â€￾ Via LiveScience

# Thread: That NDE
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 13
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T08:57:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-61725>

A (hostile) column on it I stumbled across. I don't know it adds anything, but it finishes with a Zen story that has all the characteristic pithiness of the genre:

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/9598971/Is-the-afterlife-full-of-fluffy-clouds-and-angels.html](http://http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/9598971/Is-the-afterlife-full-of-fluffy-clouds-and-angels.html)

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-11-05T06:33:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62150>

A sceptic about afterlife, but not a dead sceptic. I believe this is one of those stories you chose to believe or not. This guy was trying to make the point for non-believers.

Whether you believe in the afterlife or a life force that survives the mortal body the Doctor's experience is facinating.

What does come next?

### Michael — 2012-11-05T08:55:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62159>

> **ljbprrfmof;63948 wrote:** A sceptic about afterlife, but not a dead sceptic. I believe this is one of those stories you chose to believe or not. This guy was trying to make the point for non-believers.
>
>
>
> Whether you believe in the afterlife or a life force that survives the mortal body the Doctor's experience is facinating.
>
>
>
> What does come next?
>
>
> There's no need for anyone to take the NDE as the man who experienced it took it, as has been said here before. However, that column was written with a sneer, wasn't it? And that seems a bit unnecessary. I didn't think he added anything of moment, but I liked the Zen story at the end, and that's mainly why I posted it.
>
>
>
> Here is that columnist, saying at length in his appallingly superior manner would could be said in half-a-dozen words:
>
>
>
> They are nearly always pleasant and deeply reassuring in a saccharin-soaked way. Lots of spinning down warm, dark tunnels to the sound of celestial music; lots of trips along country lanes lined with hedges, towards the light of a welcoming cottage at the end of the road; lots of tumbling down Alice-in-Wonderland rabbit holes, but without the damaging effects of gravity.

In point of fact, there are negative NDEs as well. That's kind of interesting. I guess it might show our consciousness, or perhaps I should say unconscious, might be as ready to accuse as soothe us.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T02:22:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62467>

As I recollect the story, the physician was not religious, at least prior to the NDE.

### ljbprrfmof — 2012-11-07T13:26:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62501>

I believe that is correct.

### caroline — 2012-11-07T13:49:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62502>

love the zen story at the end too

### kathylu — 2012-11-07T18:19:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62575>

I am happy for him. It must be wonderful to look forward to the certainty of a blissful eternity after death. I just see a long sleep.

### ealachan — 2012-11-07T18:36:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62580>

> **kathylu;64414 wrote:** I am happy for him. It must be wonderful to look forward to the certainty of a blissful eternity after death. I just see a long sleep.

I see becoming worm fodder. Worms gotta eat, too...

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-07T18:54:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62589>

I hope I'm non-toxic enough for some worms to want to eat me when i die!

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-07T18:55:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62590>

I've seen the other side. It was as my MIL was dying. It was totally completely unexpected. We had driven 2days to get to Boise to be with her at the end. I walked into her room and ... the walls disappeared and I "saw" a vast open area with clusters of people (lots and lots of people) standing around. They appeared as tapered columns of golden light. I looked to my left and saw one standing near my MIL's bed and I knew instantly it was her late husband.

My only comment at the time was a spontaneous "Wow there's a lot of people in this room!"

### kathylu — 2012-11-07T19:08:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62592>

> **DarleenMB;64429 wrote:** I've seen the other side. It was as my MIL was dying. It was totally completely unexpected. We had driven 2days to get to Boise to be with her at the end. I walked into her room and ... the walls disappeared and I "saw" a vast open area with clusters of people (lots and lots of people) standing around. They appeared as tapered columns of golden light. I looked to my left and saw one standing near my MIL's bed and I knew instantly it was her late husband.
>
>
>
> My only comment at the time was a spontaneous "Wow there's a lot of people in this room!"

As I said, I really hope so...

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-07T19:12:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62593>

> **DarleenMB;64429 wrote:** "Wow there's a lot of people in this room!"

This is actually a common comment by people who are near death. I find it absolutely fascinating that you experienced this and you were amongst the living!

### Michael — 2012-11-07T19:48:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/that-nde.4650/post-62605>

> **Nonchalant;64282 wrote:** As I recollect the story, the physician was not religious, at least prior to the NDE.
>
>
> Yes and no.
>
>
>
> Although I considered myself a faithful Christian, I was so more in name than in actual belief.

[http://www.thedailybeast.com/newsweek/2012/10/07/proof-of-heaven-a-doctor-s-experience-with-the-afterlife.html](http://http://www.thedailybeast.com/newsweek/2012/10/07/proof-of-heaven-a-doctor-s-experience-with-the-afterlife.html)

It seems the experience, whatever it was, deepened his belief. AFAIK, that's not unusual.

What did seem special about this one was that "the neocortex, was inactivated". Later Jack dropped a possible explanation for that. So I guess that put us back to square one: neither more (nor less) remarkable than any other experience of the sort. In retrospect, I guess that would be so.

To be honest, although I did love the Zen story otherwise the columnist in _Telegraph did rub me up the wrong way. I think he thinks he knows and I don't think he can. What the subject says sounds sentimental to the columnist, but then if it did have some content worth knowing, what's to say _this_ man can transmit that ... or even that it can be put into words? When I read William James's the Varieties of Religious Experience_ I remember being somewhat amused by his recounting the story of a Nonconformist preacher shouting, "Woe to the bloody city of Lichfield!" because as he approached Lichfield he saw red everywhere. That really does sound like something organic. But ruling out every story that doesn't fit a preconceived view of things? There are some remarkable stories, for example, from remote Scottish islands of the "second sight" - someone might walk into a pub and see a man covered with water and fall into a faint; the man would be drowned shortly after.

# Thread: Video: Aboriginal Eel Farming
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T09:20:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61728>

This video is only here thanks to Coriander who pointed me to it in an earlier thread.

(Requires the Adobe Flash player.)

This is another video that seems worth a thread of its own.

It's another reminder that if we simply guess what aboriginal peoples did to feed themselves - they "must have" done this or that - we can miss the mark by a long way.

This video refers to people who are now mostly vanished. But for the work of these archaeologists we might never have known about these sophisticated techniques:

> The Lake Condah region in south-west Victoria shows evidence of a network of channels and rock structures used by the Indigenous inhabitants for trapping eels. **If the dating of the fish traps is correct, they would be some of the most ancient structures of this type in existence.** The Budj Bim National Heritage Landscape, which includes Lake Condah, was included in the National Heritage List in 2004, and World Heritage listing is being sought. ...
>
>
> [http://www.abceducation.net.au/videolibrary/view/indigenous-eel-farming-50](http://http://www.abceducation.net.au/videolibrary/view/indigenous-eel-farming-50)
>
>
>
> Radiocarbon dating of marsh sediment by Monash University scientists indicated that the fish traps may be up to 6,800 years old. Evidence that the inhabitants had smoked the eels came from an analysis of sediments taken from beneath hollow trees by Australian National University scientists using mass spectroscopy and gas chromatography.

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-03T14:51:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61753>

### Kiera2 — 2012-11-04T02:58:37+0200

### Michael — 2012-11-04T09:22:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61932>

Rick Stein reckons smoked eel would have to be in the world's top ten foods:

> ... the skin peeled off at the last minute and the fillet thinly sliced and served with a warm potato salad, a few dandelion leaves and some snipped chives.
>
>
> An old Cockney favourite was jellied eels.
>
>
>
> Stein gives a recipe for that, based on some he had in Leigh-on-Sea in Essex:
>
>
>
> **Serves 4-6
>
> 900 g (2 lb) eels, killed and skinned
>
> Pared zest and juice of 1 lemon
>
> 3 bay leaves
>
> 4 cloves
>
> 8 black peppercorns
>
> 4 teaspoons salt
>
> 1 small bunch curly parsley, chopped
>
>
>
> Method**
>
> Cut the spines out from the top and bottom edge of the eels, then cut the eels into 4 cm (1 1/2 inch) pieces. Put the pieces in a large saucepan with the lemon zest, juice, bay leaves, cloves, peppercorns and salt. Add enough cold water just to cover, then bring to the boil and simmer for 20 minutes. Transfer the eels and their cooking liquor to a bowl and leave to cool.
>
>
>
> Stir in the chopped parsley and divide the mixture between 4-6 small pots. Cover and chill until the jelly has set, then serve.

Rick Stein's _Seafood Lover's Guide_, a journey round the coast of Britain and Ireland.

http://www.amazon.com/gp/aw/d/0563488719

### diane — 2012-11-04T13:23:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video-aboriginal-eel-farming.4651/post-61941>

I love ungainly which is BBQ eel sushi. I don't think I've seen eel raw in sushi restaurants - just BBQ.

# Thread: Interesting podcast on foods that cause AI problems
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-podcast-on-foods-that-cause-ai-problems.4653/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T10:24:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-podcast-on-foods-that-cause-ai-problems.4653/post-61731>

This is a podcast where author J. J. Virgin is interviewed about her new book, which advises cutting out the 7 foods to which people are most commonly sensitive. This she says she's found frequently results in weight loss as people's inflammation drops.

Anyway, I almost didn't listen. You lnow what it's like with these podcasts. Someone's got a booklaunch, and they've obviously been phoning round, and they're on Jimmy's show and Abel's ... and Dr. Lo's ... and everyone else's in the ancestral health space. However, I did listen, and \*this\* version of the interview was different and interesting.

As well as the usual, the interviewee told a story about her son, who'd just been struck by a hit-and-run driver, leaving him with multiple fractures and brain damage. In the hospital they told her he would die. They said his heart needed blood-thinners, but if they gave them the bleeding from his brain would kill him. They said there were few places in the country that could do anything, but if he were airlifted \*that\* could kill him. So she asked them what would happen if he were \*not\* airlifted, and they said he'd die. She said: "Why are we standing aroundn then?" and began ringing hospitals herself.

The boy was flown to the best hospital in the country for this sort of thing. The surgeon came out, took her hand, and said, "I've got it in hand. Your son will be all right." He did come round and is now recovering.

She said if she hadn't been in possession of her full energies she doesn't know how she could have coped. This is the point of not eating all the processed crap - not weight-loss, but being able to be there and functioning whatever life throws at you.

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/11/02/the-virgin-diet-w-jj-virgin](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/11/02/the-virgin-diet-w-jj-virgin)

### Inger — 2012-11-03T12:05:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-podcast-on-foods-that-cause-ai-problems.4653/post-61738>

> She said if she hadn't been in possession of her full energies she doesn't know how she could have coped. This is the point of not eating all the processed crap - not weight-loss, but being able to be there and functioning whatever life throws at you.

This.

Thank you Michael! Now I need to go and listen to the podcast!

### caroline — 2012-11-06T02:12:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-podcast-on-foods-that-cause-ai-problems.4653/post-62292>

Thanks Michael - that was very interesting.

# Thread: Seaweed smoothies
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T15:31:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61758>

What are people doing in this line?

I tried this:

1 can organic coconut milk (said 400 ml)

About 1/4 cup drained, pre-soaked seaweed

1 tablespoon cocoa powder

1 teaspoon raw honey

A few drops vanilla extract

A pinch cinnamon

A pinch chilli

Just like Inger said, you don't taste the seaweed. What I did taste most was the coconut - which I don't actually like much. It probably didn't need the honey, as there was no sense of needing sweetening. (I kind of like the idea of the honey, though, since it seems reductive to ban it as a "sugar" when there are supposedly literally hundreds of compounds in it.)

I guess something like this could be quite healing - except the chilli perhaps.

Anyone tried any other combinations?

### Optimalbound — 2012-11-03T20:10:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61821>

Thanks for sharing, Michael. What kind of seaweed did you use?

### Glamazon — 2012-11-03T20:44:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61828>

I find most veggie tastes disappear in a smoothie if I put enough cocoa or berries or both in it. Maybe a slight grass aftertaste. I just use water.

### Michael — 2012-11-04T09:38:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61933>

> **Glamazon;63599 wrote:** I find most veggie tastes disappear in a smoothie if I put enough cocoa or berries or both in it. Maybe a slight grass aftertaste. I just use water.

I think I'd have probably preferred just water.

@OptimalBound This packet was a pretty much "let's see" choice from a bewildering variety in a good health-food shop. It was sold under the Clearspring label. I think that's a Welsh company, but the sea vegetables seem to have been wild-harvested off the French coast - says "Product of France". It's dulse, sea lettuce, and nori.

I didn't realize till I opened the packet, but they're shredded really fine with the idea that you can sprinkle them over a salad without needing to soak them.

### Optimalbound — 2012-11-04T10:56:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-61939>

Thanks, Michael. I've found some old recepies with bladderwrack that I intend to try out. Wild bladderwrack grows all along the coast where I live. Might try it in a smoothie, also. Will report back if I find anything interesting.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-06T18:27:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-62371>

I was thinking of going a different path with some seaweed.... when I get a new blender (mine broke).

Seaweed

Bone broth

Coconut Oil

Cultured Coconut Milk

sauteed leeks + garlic

\+ oregano and/or rosemary and/or cilantro, or whatever else I have from the garden or on hand.

Black pepper and maybe some cumin

I'm thinking it would come out tasting a lot like a cream of leek soup....

drink it when I'm taking my curcurmin....

Or is it only a "smoothie" if it's sweet or sweetish.... I don't know.

### Inger — 2012-11-06T20:16:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-smoothies.4657/post-62395>

RiverNeighbour, I think that is an excellent idea!!! I will try this out too. Look, I have been drinking plain seaweed smoothies now, without anything just soaked seaweeds and water, because I thought it tasted so delish by itself.. but now I want to optimize it further, with some superhealthy spices, onion/garlic etc.

Like a salty soup! Could be cold or warm. Wow.. now you got my brain running..lol I need to experiment with this tomorrow for sure!

# Thread: Minnow trap demonstrated by Jack Hargreaves
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minnow-trap-demonstrated-by-jack-hargreaves.4659/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T17:48:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minnow-trap-demonstrated-by-jack-hargreaves.4659/post-61789>

This is man was brought up in the country with animals, qualified as a veterinary surgeon, and then went into radio and TV in the early days. He tried to document the ways of the old way of life in the country that he'd known as a boy before it finally disappeared.

Here he is showing a "minnow trap" he bought in France, where he says he reckoned people were still taking pains to get their own dinners. He thinks they probably used it catch fish like whitebait. He also shows a wicker crayfish trap.

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;\_sGRHPGHSYg\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=\_sGRHPGHSYg\[/video\]\[/video\]

Most of the old film with Jack in was thought to have been lost or destroyed, but some has turned up now. He can be found in short clips on YouTube showing country cider-making, butchering, bantam-keeping, and so on.

[http://www.youtube.com/results?search\_query=jack+hargreaves](http://http://www.youtube.com/results?search_query=jack+hargreaves)

# Thread: Time for LHT?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-lht.4662/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### FarmersDaughter — 2012-11-03T20:50:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/time-for-lht.4662/post-61829>

Been doing some thinking and realized that the large majority of LS signs are here.

Sleep is awesome, even with a puppy that must go out 1-2x a night- wake easily in the am and feel ready to rule the world.

Pants are fitting way differently, scale is down only three pounds, but I look as though I've lost ten.

Much calmer, more serene, aside from random CT induced weirdness

Sweating was never really an issue for me...was always pretty well under control.

Hunger is under control...I still BAB, but I don't care to eat at all after about noon.

No cravings to speak of, unless you count salmon as a craving!

**Body temp is pretty consistently 99+...isn't that supposed to drop?**

Quit wearing makeup aside for lipstick and mascara...don't need it except from CT-acne, which is like clockwork these last couple weeks.

Getting fairly cold adapted, warm feels off to me, warm beverages feel out of kilter...like warm water tastes in summer.

I'm having the urge to LHT. But i dont want to screw it up. But everyone crows about a returning libido as a major LS sign....No sign of a libido here.

More Testing is on the list but as I've been out of work this year more than I've been employed and the doc I went to favors 24 hour urinary labs over serum and, well, I blew my test money before I started. Nothing to do about it now but save up and start over, although I did find I am way low on progesterone and have skewed 2/16 est ratios. So not totally wasted, just mostly.

Should I go ahead with LHT or wait just a bit? I'm over thinking this, I suspect, but Any insight is much appreciated!


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Pine kernels
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-03T21:39:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-61837>

I bought a packet of organic pine kernels today. Not sure what to do with them yet, but it seemed like a nice change.

I just looked at the side of the packet, and it says they're from China! Long way for pine kernels to come. I wonder who certifies them. The Chinese may say they're organic, but then _they_ say they're wanted in Tibet.

I'm thinking sprinkle on salads. I think you can make pesto with them, too.

### Endless — 2012-11-04T23:23:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62092>

Are those the same as pine nuts? I've used them toasted on salads, they're yummy. REALLY high in O6.

### Michael — 2012-11-05T09:06:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62161>

I think it would be the same thing under another name.

### Michael — 2012-11-05T09:12:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62163>

> This is a classic Venetian dish which has been given to me by our Editor, Sarah Randell. Sfogi (sole) is pronounced ‘sforgey’. It is a lovely sweet and sour combination. ...

### Endless — 2012-11-05T09:39:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62168>

I guess use will depend on ones omega 3/6 ratio, but I have always bought fresh nuts and seeds at a fall dale and made mixed roasted nuts containing walnuts, almonds, pecans, pine nuts, pumpkin seeds, sunflower seeds and macadamias. I add some garlic powder and tamari soy sauce and they are to die for. Looking for a new soy-free flavor panel, suggestions welcomed!

### Inger — 2012-11-06T11:16:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62325>

I have been eating around 200 grams wild Siberian "pine nuts" (Cedar/Cedrus) / day, the last 4 days. I just eat them plain, they are totally delicious!

Sometimes I have sprinkeled them on salads too. But to me they are so delicious I want to enjoy them just plain.

I usually munch on some whole organic cucumber at the same time..

### Michael — 2012-11-06T11:56:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62326>

200 g?

_\[Exit Inger, pursued by a squirrel\]_

[http://www.opensourceshakespeare.org/views/plays/play\_view.php?WorkID=winterstale&Act=3&Scene=3&Scope=scene](http://http://www.opensourceshakespeare.org/views/plays/play_view.php?WorkID=winterstale&Act=3&Scene=3&Scope=scene)

### Michael — 2012-11-06T12:02:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62327>

Here's a recipe for pesto:

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-06T15:40:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/pine-kernels.4664/post-62353>

Absolutely toast them. if you like them raw you will Love them toasted. Dry pan no oil medium heat until they start to brown and smoke. When I was a vegetarian they were my go to bacon replacement. Now I eat tons of bacon, but i stil enjoy toasted pine nuts. They were a staple of the southwest native americans untill the white man came and cut down pinon forests.

# Thread: off the wall but....  Milking cows and Fishoil supps
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/off-the-wall-but-milking-cows-and-fishoil-supps.4665/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-11-03T21:53:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/off-the-wall-but-milking-cows-and-fishoil-supps.4665/post-61840>

Dietary Fish Oil Supplements Modify Ruminal Biohydrogenation, Alter the Flow of Fatty Acids at the Omasum, and Induce Changes in the Ruminal Butyrivibrio Population in Lactating Cows1,2,3

[http://jn.nutrition.org/content/early/2012/06/26/jn.112.158576.abstract](http://http://jn.nutrition.org/content/early/2012/06/26/jn.112.158576.abstract)

> Four lactating cows fitted with ruminal cannulae and fed a grass silage-based diet were used in a 4 Ã— 4 Latin square with 28-d periods to investigate the effects of incremental dietary fish oil (FO) supplementation (0, 75, 150, or 300 g/d) on the flow of fatty acids at the omasum and **populations of rumen bacteria capable of biohydrogenation. **FO decreased silage intake and ruminal volatile fatty acid concentrations and promoted an increase in molar butyrate and propionate proportions at the expense of acetate.** Extensive ruminal biohydrogenation of 20:5(n-3) and 22:6(n-3) resulted in corresponding increases in numerous 20- and 22-carbon unsaturated fatty acids at the omasum. Omasal flow of several 20-, 21-, and 22-carbon all-cis (n-3) PUFA exceeded the intake from FO. Supplements of FO also induced a dose-dependent decrease in 18:0 and increased trans 18:1 and trans 18:2 flow at the omasum. Trans-11 was the major 18:1 intermediate in digesta, while FO induced quadratic increases in trans-10 18:1 flow, reaching a maximum of 300 g/d. FO had no substantial influence on omasal flow of CLA. Results suggest that one or more fatty acids in FO inhibit the reduction of trans-18:1 and trans-18:2 intermediates by ruminal microorganisms. qPCR based on 16S rRNA genes in omasal digesta indicated that key Butyrivibrio spp. declined linearly in response to FO. Dose-dependent increases in ruminal outflow of biohydrogenation intermediates containing one or more trans double bonds in response to FO has major implications for host metabolism and the nutritional quality of ruminant foods.**

### chocolate — 2012-11-03T21:57:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/off-the-wall-but-milking-cows-and-fishoil-supps.4665/post-61841>

# Thread: Whaling in Arctic Prehistory
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whaling-in-arctic-prehistory.4669/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-04T10:15:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/whaling-in-arctic-prehistory.4669/post-61936>

Here's another article from _Current World Archaeology. I used to subscribe to their sister-magazine _Current Archaeology_, but there are so many calls on one's money... I've never taken Current World Archaeology_.

This story is from May 2008.

> Members of a Russian-American team investigating the early history of whaling have made a spectacular find at Unâ€™enâ€™en, near the modern whaling village of Nunligran, on Russiaâ€™s Chukotka Peninsula. Right at the end of their 2007 season they uncovered an ivory carving, approximately 0.5m in length, engraved with scenes of men in umiaks (wooden framed boats covered in seal or walrus hide) harpooning whales. The carving was found sealed by the roof timbers of a collapsed structure that has been carbon dated to 1,000 BC. ...
>
>
>
> The US Co-Director, Daniel Odess of the University of Alaska Museum of the North, said **â€˜The importance of whaling in Arctic prehistory is now becoming clear. Prehistoric settlements were situated and defended specifically so that people could hunt whalesâ€™.**

So there we are: there were settlements near Bering Strait specifically for the purpose of whaling a millenium before Christ (and doubtless before that).

[http://www.world-archaeology.com/news/whaling-in-arctic-prehistory/](http://http://www.world-archaeology.com/news/whaling-in-arctic-prehistory/)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### chocolate — 2012-11-04T20:55:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/vitamin-c-and-b-12-i-cant-find-a-good-study-just-blurbs-dsplaces-b-12.4680/post-62067>

[http://curezone.com/forums/am.asp?i=1530791](http://http://curezone.com/forums/am.asp?i=1530791)

> Excessive vitamin C intake is also known to displace vitamin B12 from the body. Vitamin B12 is essential for the maturation of blood cells. Deficiencies of B12 lead to a problem known as macrocytic anemia. This condition leads to the formation of abnormally large red blood cells, with impaired ability to carry oxygen. Decreased oxygen levels may cause fatigue, muscle weakness, shortness of breath, and possibly heart arrhythmias.

If anyone knows of a good study... please share

# Thread: Growing Pains....  and the Gut
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T22:17:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62079>

My daughter is having growing pains. Specifically pains in her legs in the middle of the night. I'm convinced that it is gut related.... She does have gut issues and as we work on them I notice that the growing pains come when something impacts the gut situation (like eating a food that she's sensitive to).

Anyone have any thoughts / resources / ideas? My initial research hasn't really found anything useful.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-04T22:29:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62080>

My daughter used to have this, too. I didn't notice any correlation with food (but I wasn't paying very good attention back then). She recently had 'sore legs' again (same spot as you mention) and I rubbed magnesium oil over them. This stopped the pain. In her case, I think it was likely related to magnesium deficiency - and this recent 'test' somewhat confirms that. It's not directly gut related, but magnesium is certainly a building block to health, gut included.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-04T22:58:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62089>

This makes sense... Did it relieve the pain immediately?

I just got some minerals to add to her water figuring that minerals might be an issue. I should see about some magnesium oil too....

### kathylu — 2012-11-05T00:54:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62099>

> **RiverNeighbor;63866 wrote:** This makes sense... Did it relieve the pain immediately?
>
>
>
> I just got some minerals to add to her water figuring that minerals might be an issue. I should see about some magnesium oil too....

Don't waste money on Mg oil. Buy a bag of Epsom salts and dissolve some in warm water until it won't dissolve any more (forming a saturated solution). Use it just like Mg oil (which is a saturated solution of Mg chloride). With epsom salts you get sulfur too (magnesium sulfate)!

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-05T01:35:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62105>

> **kathylu;63877 wrote:** Don't waste money on Mg oil. Buy a bag of Epsom salts and dissolve some in warm water until it won't dissolve any more (forming a saturated solution). Use it just like Mg oil (which is a saturated solution of Mg chloride). With epsom salts you get sulfur too (magnesium sulfate)!

I couldn't actually afford 'real' mag oil. What I did was make it from nigari crystals the way Dali Dula suggested. I know there is more in there than just mg, but don't know what the content of the other minerals is.

Yes, RN, the pain subsided quite quickly. The growing pains stopped totally once we started supplementing with oral mg every night, too.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-05T05:27:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/growing-pains-and-the-gut.4681/post-62145>

Thanks ladies!

I already give her epsom salt baths..... Tomorrow I'll try some concentrated epsom salt water and do a wrap on her. I'll tell her it's her princess spa day or something. Maybe I'll do it and get my son to do it too. Put our swimsuits on and do it in the yard....

I actually tried giving her some of my Mg tonight (disolved in water)... but she spit it out. She's just not old enough to be able to do it because it might help.

We also tried some Mg Oil spray (it wasn't expensive here.... ), but she went into an itching frenzy.

I was able to get lots of broth and probiotic foods into her today so hopefully we'll get back on track working on her gut and we'll get this resolved.

# Thread: Shellfish Midden

### Michael — 2012-11-05T09:02:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/shellfish-midden.4688/post-62160>

New Zealand.

Kind of fun - for no particular reason, except that it again reminds us that there are huge piles of shells around:

> The discovery of a cluster of pipi shells has halted a million-dollar roading project.
>
>
>
> Archaeologists and iwi representatives have inspected the midden of shellfish remains, which were found in the side of a hill about 3km from McLaren Falls Rd.
>
>
>
> The cluster of shells was found about a month ago by contractors who were undertaking work to make McLaren Falls Rd wider and safer for vehicles.
>
>
>
> The finding has set back the completion of the project, angering some residents.
>
>
> [http://www.bayofplentytimes.co.nz/news/delays-after-shells-found-in-bank-anger-residents/1576692/](http://http://www.bayofplentytimes.co.nz/news/delays-after-shells-found-in-bank-anger-residents/1576692/)
>
>
>
> I guess one could drive the road through there without delay, forget one saw the shells, and go back to eating a McDonalds in a bun with fries and a coke.
>
>
>
>
>
> ... the average American eats 10 times more omega-6 fatty acids than omega-3, and as a consequence, the United States has the highest rate of obesity in the world. New Zealand also has a high rate of obesity with one in three people overweight, and one in four adults obese.

[http://www.voxy.co.nz/health/us-expert-urges-omega-3-testing/5/139234](http://http://www.voxy.co.nz/health/us-expert-urges-omega-3-testing/5/139234)

# Thread: Low carb diets rocked in the Stone Age
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-diets-rocked-in-the-stone-age.4689/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T09:09:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-carb-diets-rocked-in-the-stone-age.4689/post-62162>

This story is about a year old, but anyway ...

They found what's been found before: that the Neolithic in Northern Europe did _not_ mean everyone suddenly eating a lot of grain. In this case they looked at residues on pottery:

> Analyses of 6,000-year-old pottery reveal the culinary habits of Neolithic residents of todayâ€™s Denmark and Germanyâ€™s Baltic coast.
>
>
>
> Traces of meals show a surprising penchant for fish, shellfish and marine mammals, also after plant cultivation and animal husbandry became well established in the region.
>
>
>
> Transcribed to current fads the finds indicate that Stone Age people retained a lifestyle that continued to include a low carbohydrate diet based on hunting, fishing and gathering.

[http://sciencenordic.com/low-carb-diets-rocked-stone-age](http://http://sciencenordic.com/low-carb-diets-rocked-stone-age)

# Thread: Giant clams
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/giant-clams.4691/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T12:39:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/giant-clams.4691/post-62177>

Again, not a new story - but definitely an interesting one:

**Current Biology, Volume 18, Issue 17, 1349-1354, 9 September 2008

> Giant clams are among the most spectacular but also the most endangered marine invertebrates. Their large size and easy accessibility has caused overfishing and collapse of the natural stocks in many places and local extinction in some of the species \[1,2\]. The diversity of giant clams is extremely low because of reliction in this Tethyan group \[3,4\]. The latest additions of living species date back almost two decades \[5,6,7\], fixing the number of extant Tridacna at seven species \[3\]. Here, we report the discovery of a new species of giant clam: Tridacna costata sp. nov. features characteristic shells with pronounced vertical folds, is genetically distinct, and shows an earlier and abbreviated reproduction than its Red Sea congeners. This species represents less than 1% of the present stocks but up to >80% of the fossil shells. The decline in proportion and shell size (20) indicates overharvesting \[8\] dating back to the early human occupation of the Red Sea >125,000 years ago \[9\]. This earliest depletion reported so far of a shallow-water megafaunal invertebrate has important ramifications for human dispersal out of Africa \[10\].

**

> Its oversight in one of the best-investigated reef provinces \[11,12,13\] illustrates the dearth of knowledge on marine biodiversity.

[http://www.cell.com/current-biology/retrieve/pii/S096098220800969X](http://http://www.cell.com/current-biology/retrieve/pii/S096098220800969X)

These would be easily harvested from shallow water, and would provide plenty of nutrition - they really are pretty large. This is a species of Tridacna costata:

# Thread: Tiny ocean microbes don't just wait for food, they go after it
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-ocean-microbes-dont-just-wait-for-food-they-go-after-it.4692/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T15:12:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tiny-ocean-microbes-dont-just-wait-for-food-they-go-after-it.4692/post-62197>

> A life spent drifting in the currents, eating whatever food they bump into, was once the presumed fate of most microbes living in the ocean.
>
>
>
> But researchers are starting to re-imagine the lives of these tiny microorganisms, which are half the size (or smaller!) of red blood cells. New evidence indicates many ocean microbes are active swimmers, following chemical trails toward food "hotspots." ...

[http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/49648845/ns/technology\_and\_science-science/](http://http://www.msnbc.msn.com/id/49648845/ns/technology_and_science-science/)

# Thread: Fat cat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T17:53:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62212>

A literal one â€¦

Milo had to be put on a diet because he got jammed in the cat-flap:

> "He does want food quite a lot. You have to not give in to him because he will pester you. But he goes out more now and heâ€™s much more active."

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

[http://www.malverngazette.co.uk/news/10023966.Fat\_cat\_becomes\_a\_streamline\_feline/](http://http://www.malverngazette.co.uk/news/10023966.Fat_cat_becomes_a_streamline_feline/)

I guess this is interesting, because (I assume) Milo won't have been overdoing the carbs. It really does seem to be a case of his eating too much. I guess if we really understood why he was pestering for more food when he'd already eaten we'd know something worth knowing.

### Endless — 2012-11-05T18:42:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62215>

Cats are very easily addicted, and cat food manufacturers take full advantage of this! They can even become addicted to the shape of their kibble! One of my cats was amazingly difficult to transition to a raw food diet and off of his beloved dry food. When he got a chance to indulge he would literally gorge himself until he vomited and then eat more. He became overweight on kibble. All my cats are now on raw and they no longer overeat.

### Michael — 2012-11-05T19:34:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62221>

> **Endless;64016 wrote:** Cats are very easily addicted, and cat food manufacturers take full advantage of this! They can even become addicted to the shape of their kibble! One of my cats was amazingly difficult to transition to a raw food diet and off of his beloved dry food. When he got a chance to indulge he would literally gorge himself until he vomited and then eat more. He became overweight on kibble. All my cats are now on raw and they no longer overeat.

Thank you. That's interesting.

I'm deeply sceptical of the "hyperpalatibility" theory of obesity ... and yet it seems to explain what you've seen with your cat. I'd love to know what Milo was eating. Only a few years ago, it was all tinned meat. Now I suppose dried biscuits are a fair slice of the market. No can to open, and it wouldn't smell. Was that what he got? It's a shame we don't know.

I suppose if he's on the dried food we have to ask what they're putting in it and whether they're using cereals as a cheap filler. So is it the "palatability" of the cat food, or is it carbs in it? But the interesting thing is that Milo was begging for more and your cat would "gorge himself until he vomited and then eat more". That does sound more like an addiction scenario.

Here was I wondering whether it was the food (in one way or another) or maybe disruption of sleep and untimely electric light - or not getting cold enough in winter ...

You reckon the manufacturers deliberately spike the cat food then, working on the basis that, although the cat's not buying, so that that might not seem to be worth it, the owner probably can't resist it's importunity?

### diane — 2012-11-05T21:41:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62248>

> **Michael;64024 wrote:** You reckon the manufacturers deliberately spike the cat food then, working on the basis that, although the cat's not buying, so that that might not seem to be worth it, the owner probably can't resist it's importunity?

Yes - because owners feel good when their pets are excited and eat their food quickly. Then they'll buy it again because their pet likes it. I trust very few pet food manufacturers - and a lot of this is based on discussions and information from people who have been selling petfood for many years.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-05T21:56:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62254>

+1, definitely our experience with our cats!

Two years ago one of our cats was hit by a car. When she was able to walk again, she refused the dry kibble and started stealing raw meat from the dog's bowl. She had lost half her body weight and it was touch and go. We had never fed her raw meat. It was like some sort of survival instinct kicking in. She started gaining her weight back, her coat went full and shiny (it had been falling out) and the broken pelvis stopped limiting her mobility.

What was equally amazing was that we decided to follow her cue and feed all the cats raw meat. The other two (who were chubby and big kibble-overeaters) initially refused to eat it. For several days they meowed like crazy and got really cranky. Finally, they ate. In the course of the following year, they both lost heaps of weight.

So... the same diet caused the two overweight to drop weight and the one underweight to gain. They are all three at a healthy weight now and their raw diet persists. Now they fight over who is first to the bowl!

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-05T22:09:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62255>

I loved my cats. So much I calorie restricted them with only the best of foods. They did not go outside and risk death or disease. They got plenty of exercise. Both were potty trained (seriously), but able to protest that procedure by peeing on my laundry when I wasn't looking.

So, when we got to year 20 and had super old cats that threw up all the time and were super moody and frankly annoying - we were starting to wonder if their time was here. They were profoundly cantankerous. Waking me up multiple times a night, killer staring the baby, meowing in the most obnoxious way.

I had those cats since COLLEGE. They had moved from Arkansas to San Diego to Boston to Houston to Colorado. They were long lived and energizer bunny.

As the years went on, cat number two made it to 23 - we just put him down this year, and the doc had many suggestions of alternatives to that. I just wasn't going to give meds to a 23 yr old cat.

The last 2 years were a misery for all - hubby and I anxiously looked for signs that the devils housecat was near then end. As we know, sleep is important and we were not gettin it per kitty.

Probably why this year is my best health year - the darn cat finally departed.

I love cats, the next one I get eat's what he wants in the quantity he wants and if it's cat-crack tasty so be it. He will run outside and suppliment with self provided critters. Most likely he will be one fat, happy short lived thing. And that is the way I want it.

I wonder if my son will take that approach with me. . . "JUST GIVE HER MORE WINE AND CHEETOS - SHE LOVES IT!"

\*\*\*if you think I'm a shameful example and cruel terrible animal rights violating person - I suggest you calorie restrict your cats and do all you can for their health. Note, there is a bonus - less food means less litter cleaning. Please call me when they hit the vomit stage so I can enjoy paying it forward.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-06T00:08:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62272>

> **Cavemam;64061 wrote:**
>
> So, when we got to year 20 and had super old cats that threw up all the time and were super moody and frankly annoying - we were starting to wonder if their time was here. They were profoundly cantankerous. Waking me up multiple times a night, killer staring the baby, meowing in the most obnoxious way.
>
> The last 2 years were a misery for all - hubby and I anxiously looked for signs that the devils housecat was near then end. As we know, sleep is important and we were not gettin it per kitty.

Cavemam, I had a similar experience with two cats. One was convinced that for 20 years we had been trying to kill her. She seldom relaxed in our presence. We fed them dry food, mostly. The older they got, the stranger they got. I feel bad now, knowing that they were suffering from malnutrition. Guess dressed-up corn only goes so far for an obligate carnivore.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-06T00:15:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62273>

> **Nonchalant;64079 wrote:** Cavemam, I had a similar experience with two cats. One was convinced that for 20 years we had been trying to kill her. She seldom relaxed in our presence. We fed them dry food, mostly. The older they got, the stranger they got. I feel bad now, knowing that they were suffering from malnutrition. Guess dressed-up corn only goes so far for an obligate carnivore.

Yea, I think back when I first got them. . .20+ yrs ago science diet was the be all end all on feeding. And the dry-only mantra. What I find fascinating, and wonder how it applies to us. . . .they were very long lived and never sick. We didn't have to do one real vet visit their entire lives. . just the shots before airplane rides.

They for sure got stranger as older, I was frankly scared of the last one a little bit. The last year he was just a sack of bones and he could sneak up and you wouldn't realize he was on you. Then he would just stare.

Not all the years are good ones! We might take care to remember that for ourselves.

### Michael — 2012-11-06T09:17:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62321>

> **diane;64054 wrote:** Yes - because owners feel good when their pets are excited and eat their food quickly. Then they'll buy it again because their pet likes it. I trust very few pet food manufacturers - and a lot of this is based on discussions and information from people who have been selling petfood for many years.

It's about time that someone did an expose of what pet food manufacturers are up to then.

It would certainly make entertaining prime-time TV, although some stations might not want to broadcast it on account they might lose commercials for pet food.

Some background talking about Pottenger's experiments; add lots of shots of overweight animals, and point out that doesn't occur in the wild; reveal what's actually in the food. Throw in a bit of Tom Naughton style humour. You'd have a winner.

If that is what's going on what it amounts to cheating people out of money - creating a situation by chemical means where people end up buying more of something than they need - and knowing they've been fleeced and manipulated is something people cannot stand.

It's also interesting to know that zoos that have fed gorillas on "gorilla biscuits" instead of real food have ended up with sick and overweight gorillas:

[http://blog.cleveland.com/metro/2011/07/new\_diet\_for\_apes\_at\_cleveland.html](http://http://blog.cleveland.com/metro/2011/07/new_diet_for_apes_at_cleveland.html)

### Inger — 2012-11-07T09:37:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62494>

So what did this story tells us, if we really listen?

Maybe we should eat more raw too...

I have a 13 yo cat and I feed her mostly raw (the occasional canned tuna when I had no time to go shopping for her, and precooked shrimps wich are her favorit food) and 100% carnivore. She is an amazing cat. Very clever, very healthy.

Anyone that struggles with overweight.. start to eat raw. I bet you will loose the weight fast...!

(This is also my own Nr1.)

### Michael — 2012-11-07T12:26:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62498>

The U.S. has hit back with its own fat cat story:

[http://www.newsday.com/news/nation/fat-cat-skinny-drops-weight-down-to-37-pounds-1.4193836](http://http://www.newsday.com/news/nation/fat-cat-skinny-drops-weight-down-to-37-pounds-1.4193836)

The Aussies need to find a overweight feline and put him on the news now.

### Michael — 2012-11-07T12:44:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62500>

Why is this not a surprise?

[http://www.abc.net.au/news/2008-04-04/fat-cats-and-dogs-as-obesity-spreads/2393546](http://http://www.abc.net.au/news/2008-04-04/fat-cats-and-dogs-as-obesity-spreads/2393546)

Apparently, Oz has laid claim to the fattest cat with one clocking in at 47 lbs.

I see a pattern here. These cats need to be put on the Dean Ornish diet and made to jog. p-)

Isn't it interesting once you stop thinking you know the answer

calories in >>>

calories out >>>

low fat

cardio exercise

... and start looking in unusual places?

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-07T17:52:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62554>

Dont forget to feed your cat when he'd normally be hunting....

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-07T18:05:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62563>

> **RiverNeighbor;64392 wrote:** Dont forget to feed your cat when he'd normally be hunting....

Or don't. I'm firmly under the idea that a short lived cat is best for all!!!

### Inger — 2012-11-07T19:21:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62597>

> **Cavemam;64402 wrote:** Or don't. I'm firmly under the idea that a short lived cat is best for all!!!

Yeah.. I totally agree.. if you feed your cat something else than a fresh carnivore diet..lol

When cats get weird foods they get weird for sure. My cat was having pains when peeing when I got her 5 years back. She was fed catfood from the store by her previous owner. I started barfing her and soon there was no pain at all anymore when going to the toilet. She is the most amazing cat now. So wild.. so clever! And full of love and peace. She always wants me to go early to bed, and wants me to wake up early too..lol. It is like she knows my needs.. She loves to look into the sun too with me together. IDK, I will be sad for sure when she is gone..

Cats need to have access to fresh grass too BTW. At least once in a while.

### Linz — 2012-11-08T14:28:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62865>

Thirty years ago in the UK 'least cost' feeding was heavily emphasised in agricultural education and computer programmes were rapidly developing to reduce costs further and further. Most farm animals don't need to live long lives so the health problems weren't obvious (and what harm could it do to us?) and farmers' concerns that ingredients were not being declared - just general energy and protein levels and some nutrient content - were ignored.

Now read the ingredients on any can or packet of food for our carnivorous pets that we hope will live long and healthy lives. If only it was raw v. cooked...... how much meat, bone etc of any description is included? And back to least cost, most milk, most meat. How much was included in our poor herbivores' diets when extra protein could increase output?

I sent a letter to a major animal food company questioning how the ingredients were likely to affect my cat's health. Their response, a voucher entitling me to several free cans!!

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-08T15:40:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fat-cat.4693/post-62875>

I lost my kitty Zeke two weeks ago. I was just thinking about his mom this morning and how it was really amazing to watch her hunt birds and mice (farm cat) and how sometimes she felt like dropping one off on the doorstep for us. One time I had been fishing in the creek and caught a carp. I brought it up to throw on the compost heap and she brought her litter of kittens (a litter before Zeke's) and you should have seen them all work on eating that fish!

# Thread: Hack these three labs, please?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### maggie — 2012-11-05T19:04:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62216>

Would really appreciate any/all comments on the following:

Pregnenolone, MS 81/ng/dL (Range: Adults:

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-05T19:45:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62223>

The pregnenolone range from your lab has probably adjusted for your age. Usually down as you get older. I think Dzugan uses a range that is more useful to feeling good and for your body. Preg is the precursor to a lot of other hormones down the chain.

Your FT3 is super duper low. You poor thing, you must be exhausted all the time. Basically with a ratio of 14.2 your body is converting the FT4 into rT3 instead of FT3. You also may not have enough FT4 in your system but w/o labs that is a little harder to determine. You definitely have something going on with your thyroid. I would highly suggest autoimmune thyroid antibodies testing to see if you have Hashimotos (TPO & TgAB).

These labs as they are only tell you bits and pieces of the story. In your situation, I would guess there is more going on than just what is showing up here. More labs are probably necessary. I hope that helps you a little....

### maggie — 2012-11-05T20:22:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62227>

Thanks very much, Patty Cakes. It makes a lot of sense that the lab would adjust for age (I'm 70). Dzugan of course doesn't do that.

These are labs I had done after a consult last week with Dr. K, so they're not the only ones. If you think I look exhausted from the T3 levels, you shoulda seen the cortisol and melatonin numbers! Completely in the toilet. The thyroid antibodies looked OK, but there may be something brewing with dairy, so you're right, there's more to the story--probably hashi's which wasn't revealed by the thyroid antibodies because I haven't been eating grain for 5+ years.

I had these additional thyroid labs done because the doc thought that my very high cholesterol would be related to reverse T3 + low free T3 preventing cholesterol conversion to pregnenolone. Looks like the doc hit a home run here, as he did with the Adrenal Stress Index + melatonin test. Sheesh, what did we do before Dr K?

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-05T20:29:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62229>

I know, right? Gotta love it when you find something that works, like this amazing full body investigation we are all doing.

Sounds like you are on your way to figuring it out and now you know what to work on ..... I hope you have a good doc who will recognize what is going on. You'll feel like your 50 when you get this all worked out!!! Good luck!

### vkiernan — 2012-11-05T20:45:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62234>

> **Patty Cakes;64026 wrote:** Your FT3 is super duper low. You poor thing, you must be exhausted all the time. Basically with a ratio of 14.2 your body is converting the FT4 into rT3 instead of FT3. You also may not have enough FT4 in your system but w/o labs that is a little harder to determine. You definitely have something going on with your thyroid. I would highly suggest autoimmune thyroid antibodies testing to see if you have Hashimotos (TPO & TgAB)

Patty, Please help me understand if her t4 is converting to rt3, why isn't that number high? In fact, it is very low. Is her body able to rid itself of it? What am I missing here? Thanks much.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-05T20:53:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62237>

She probably doesn't have much FT4 to convert in the first place. At least that was what I was thinking originally. She is probably super low on all her thyroid hormones. I think it is about balance between all of the thyroid entities. But it does show her body is still converting improperly. At least that is my interpretation. Does that sound right?

Sorry Maggie about talking about you in the third person on your thread.

### maggie — 2012-11-05T21:01:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62239>

Talk on! I don't mind at all, and I appreciate the input.

I had more complete labs done a month ago, but I think that they were skewed because I took all my supps about 2 hours before the blood draw (as suggested by LEF). T4 came back within range, but T3 was high--out of range, so I dropped down 1/4 grain. I probably should have had the whole panel redone this time, but wasn't thinking about T4... just wanted the t3/rt3 ratio. darn.

Reason I think last month's numbers on the blood test were skewed is that DHEA-S came back on that one over 1000, while on the saliva test for the ASI, DHEA\_S came in low. I also took oral DHEA in the am before the blood test.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-05T21:09:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62240>

I think T3 peaks in your system around 1.5-2 hours after ingestion and then starts to drop off. SO you probably tested when it was at it's highest. In my opinion, FT3 testing is difficult when you are taking T3. I take my last T3 at around 3 pm (sometimes I add a dab later). I tested the following morning at 7:30 and I was around 2.2. But I hadn't had any T3 in my system for over 12 hours and I think it only lasts around 8 hours so it should be low. But like how you tested it should be high.

I wonder if anyone knows the best time to test FT3 when taking T3?????? I have read that it's a bugger to figure that out and may be an individual thing based on one's own system and that a person should go by how they feel.

### maggie — 2012-11-05T21:13:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-these-three-labs-please.4694/post-62242>

Aaah, love all this good information. Thank you. Should have mentioned that I take Armour, but had just started a compounded form, and I'm sure I was absorbing the granules in the gel capsule a lot better/faster than the armour tablet. At least I felt that way right after I switched to the compounded. So I think you're right that taking it at 6 would have poked my levels up by a little after 8.

# Thread: Chinese building massive DHA facility
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-05T19:51:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62225>

> A microalgae DHA production project was carried out by Qingdao Langyatai (Group) Co Ltd and Qingdao Institute of Bioenergy and Bioprocess Technology, and launched in Jiaonan to extract DHA from microalgae.
>
>
>
> It is located in Jiaonan Seaside Industrial Park covering an area of 2.67 hectare, the overall floorage of which is about 11, 940 square meters. With proprietary intellectual property rights, it is mapped out to achieve the DHA production scale of 1000-t/a and turn into the largest national microalgae DHA production base in three years.
>
>
>
> DHA is one of the major components of the human brain, which can be extracted from ocean fish or microorganism with DHA, such as microalgae. As the main DHA source in the past, producing DHA from fish oil is simple and cheap. It faces a difficult position due to water pollution and fishery resources depletion. Ocean microalgae is the major substitution of marine fish; therefore it has a broad market for the future.

[http://qingdao.chinadaily.com.cn/2012-10/26/content\_15850378.htm](http://http://qingdao.chinadaily.com.cn/2012-10/26/content_15850378.htm)

### ruby — 2012-11-06T06:29:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62316>

They expect DHA to be a money spinner. Problem is, as with loads of things in China, to milk this cow dry corners will be cut, for sure.

Funny thing, though, China is always quoted by the pro-carb brigade as the crowning proof that rice and grains are innocuous if not downright essential for health. But in my experience, the Chinese always consider rice and grain products "fillers", something to have at the end of the meal to top up real food in case it was not enough. Scoffing on rice has always been a financial resources issue not a dietary/health choice issue.

### Michael — 2012-11-06T09:02:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62319>

Maybe it'll be going in the rations of the Red Army. LOL

### Michael — 2012-11-06T15:35:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62352>

I shouldn't make jokes, should I?

The Chinese Army is reporting the US DOD trial - and doubtless looking on with interest:

[http://www.chnarmy.com/html/2012-10/29886.html](http://http://www.chnarmy.com/html/2012-10/29886.html)

### ruby — 2012-11-07T01:52:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-building-massive-dha-facility.4695/post-62455>
