# Thread: The 7th most important thing about... everything
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Fitness
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Barry — 2012-11-06T01:12:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/post-62285>

I've been busy running and sorting out my career (more on that to come), but this post is a little different. I think it applies to most people, it applies to me, and I'm sure it applies to Jack

Its summed up here by Steve Jobs

â€œYour time is limited, so don't waste it living someone else's life. Don't be trapped by dogma - which is living with the results of other people's thinking. Don't let the noise of other's opinions drown out your own inner voice. And most important, have the courage to follow your heart and intuition. They somehow already know what you truly want to become. Everything else is secondary."

To put this into more perspective, consider what you read here on this forum and what I have already posted about my sport and training. Health is ultimately about happiness. Your work and life dictates that happiness. It doesn't matter about how many kilo's of fish you had last month or how many grammes of carbs you had. Like I said before, I'm all about the nutrition, but I;ve realized that nutrition is only an "add-on" to your general make up. In other words, nutrition doesn't control your existence. Your brain controls your existence. How you think and what you think, and more importantly, the manipulation of your thoughts are governed by how you live. If you live a life of discontentment, stress etc.... then nothing else really matters. You are destined to be unhappy.

So when it comes to work and living and pursuits, follow your instinct. Don't let the noise of so called "know it all" paleo experts and personal trainers tell you what you should do. You need to tell you what YOU like doing. It doesn't matter if that means working in some low paid unusual job, and it doesn't matter if that means doing some sort of exercise that doesn't conform. What matters is that you are following your own inner voice and truly believing and enjoying how you live and what you do. You see, people want you to be like them and to stay that way. They don't like when you do things differently and it works. So don't rely on the opinion of others and doubt yourself. Make it on your own.

"Beyond the walls of intelligence, life is defined"

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-06T13:54:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/post-62340>

I stumbled into what I'm good at w/regards to work. It was pure luck that I happens to meet a distinguished leader in logistics in the fall of my senior yr. I went back after Thanksgiving break and changed my career selection to transportation, logistics, logistics planning & supply. I was 1of 2 classmates that got their 1st choice! The other guy got a pilot slot I became a transportation & logistics plans officer. About 9 yrs ago I wasn't doing what I loved and I was miserable. It took the death if my grandfather suddenly to jolt me out of what I was doing. 2 months later I began working with my current company doing logistic analysis and planning. I'm very fortunate that I get to do what I love every day. I'm one of very few women who have carved out a niche in logistics.

What's amazing is how much I'm improved my performance living an optimal life. I rarely have the days where my job sucks -and when I do I get over it rather quickly b/c those little annoyances don't matter. I'm sharp and in focus every day. I'm faster at completing analysis - such that my co-workers ask me how I do it - I point to my lunch of seafood.

Its amazing how things fall into alignment when your working from a place of being optimal.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T01:57:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/post-62459>

> **Barry;64092 wrote:**
>
> You see, people want you to be like them and to stay that way. They don't like when you do things differently and it works. So don't rely on the opinion of others and doubt yourself. Make it on your own.

Thank you for this, Barry. I know you'll find yourself in the career you want.

### caroline — 2012-11-11T07:35:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-7th-most-important-thing-about-everything.4699/post-63548>

Thanks Barry for posting that. I was lucky enough to work at something I loved\[and was really good at\] - with the person I loved for 35 years. Doesn't get much better than that I think.

# Thread: my right gets colder than my left hand

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-right-gets-colder-than-my-left-hand.4700/post-62291>

say, this strange experience i have been noticing. my right hand gets colder than my left hand.

this is nothing to do with CT. when im in my room and it is cold, my right hand gets cold faster than my left.

actually my whole body is okay and remains in perfect sync in terms of feeling cold, just that my right hand feels colder.

i have to get a light partial glove so my right hand doesnt get too cold.

can anybody explain if anything is wrong or is this normal? i am left handed by the way.

### Inger — 2012-11-07T23:33:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-right-gets-colder-than-my-left-hand.4700/post-62691>

Danny, I have no clue.. but I just wanted to share, my right hand is colder then my left right now, too.., I am right handed though.

I would say, do not worry.  I have recognized that the right part of my body is the weaker one anyways. I do not know why. Maybe it has something to do about Yin/Yang? One part is Yin and the other part Yang and what is weaker is even suffering more..? No idea..

### x_danny_x — 2012-11-08T01:14:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-right-gets-colder-than-my-left-hand.4700/post-62738>

Okay, that is good to hear. I thought I had a thyroid issue or something. I was like "oh man, another thing that is broken"

Good thing that you mentioned that your right hand is colder than your left.....since you are super healthy then I guess I can put that to side.

### Inger — 2012-11-08T01:26:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-right-gets-colder-than-my-left-hand.4700/post-62753>

You know Danny, I am far from Optimal...lol.

But I have no fear, because I do the right things, and then I know sooner or later everything will be just fine. The body needs time to heal for sure, just keep doing the good stuff and the issues will disappear!

### x_danny_x — 2012-11-08T01:49:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-right-gets-colder-than-my-left-hand.4700/post-62766>

i am broken all over after reading some fo the threads and the Brain Guts. i think i need a whole year of CT to get back to normal.

that gymnastic girl with her broken back/disc gives me a lot of hope. CT helped heal her back to compete in the London Olympics.

[http://ancestralliving.squarespace.com/blog/2012/9/2/australian-olympic-gymnast-with-broken-back-competed-in-lond.html](http://http://ancestralliving.squarespace.com/blog/2012/9/2/australian-olympic-gymnast-with-broken-back-competed-in-lond.html)

### Inger — 2012-11-08T01:56:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-right-gets-colder-than-my-left-hand.4700/post-62771>

Danny.. I'll tell you a secret. I give myself more than a year to heal... I own it to myself. Because I am worth it. Everyone is.

It does not even matter if it takes 5 years! Only the direction matters.

You will do it Danny.

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-09T15:44:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-right-gets-colder-than-my-left-hand.4700/post-63113>

Traditonal Chinese Medicine says that for every year of illness it takes one month of healing. Since most of us have been sick for decades...just do the math. I figure I'm looking at about 3-4 yrs. I am 18 mo paleo,1 year leptin reset , 9-10 mo. doing CT and 4+ mo. epi paleo. It has been a positive trend since I started. You will start to feel better and that is the instant gratification. Healing takes time.

# Thread: Endangered River Turtle's Genes Reveal Ancient Influence of Maya Indians
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endangered-river-turtles-genes-reveal-ancient-influence-of-maya-indians.4703/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-06T16:21:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/endangered-river-turtles-genes-reveal-ancient-influence-of-maya-indians.4703/post-62357>

From August 2011

> A genetic study focusing on the Central American river turtle (Dermatemys mawii) recently turned up surprising results for a team of Smithsonian scientists involved in the conservation of this critically endangered species. Small tissue samples collected from 238 wild turtles at 15 different locations across their range in Southern Mexico, Belize and Guatemala revealed a "surprising lack" of genetic structure, the scientists write in a recent paper in the journal Conservation Genetics. ...
>
>
>
> "We were expecting to find a different genetic lineage in each drainage basin," explains the paper's main author ... "Instead, we found the mixing of lineages. ... Despite appearing isolated, the genetic data showed the different turtle populations had been in close contact for years.
>
>
>
> "But how?" the researchers wondered.
>
>
>
> The best possible explanation, GonzÃ¡lez-Porter and her colleagues say, is that for centuries humans have been bringing them together. The turtles have been used as food, in trade and in rituals for millennia, widely transported and customarily kept in holding ponds till they were needed.
>
>
>
> "For centuries, this species has been part of the diet of the Mayans and other indigenous people who lived in its historic distribution range," the scientists point out in their paper. "D. mawii was a very important source of animal protein for the ancient Mayans of the Peten (Preclassic period 800-400 B.C.)â€¦. And it is possible that these turtles were part of the diet of the Olmec culture more than 3,000 years ago." ...
>
>
> [http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/07/110722112106.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2011/07/110722112106.htm)
>
>
>
> Interestingly, the turtle seems to have been seen as sacred:
>
>
>
> !e turtle carapace scene shows a youthful
>
> deity figure splitting out of a sea shell on
>
> a plate. !is resurrection from a giant turtle
>
> carapace is one of the truly important moments
>
> in Mayan cosmology.

[www.maya-archaeology.org/...archaeology.../30\_Sacred\_turtles\_in\_Mayan\_art\_and\_iconography\_Revue\_Magazine-Guatemala-August-2011.pdf](http://http://www.maya-archaeology.org/...archaeology.../30_Sacred_turtles_in_Mayan_art_and_iconography_Revue_Magazine-Guatemala-August-2011.pdf)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: New excavations will reveal diet of Skokomish, S'Klallam and Suquamish tribes
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-excavations-will-reveal-diet-of-skokomish-sklallam-and-suquamish-tribes.4705/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-06T17:12:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-excavations-will-reveal-diet-of-skokomish-sklallam-and-suquamish-tribes.4705/post-62361>

That should be an interesting report when it comes out. It should be no surprise that these people were exploiting both land and water resources - exactly what remains to be revealed:

> "A shell midden is really refuse accumulations from people living there, so there is a lot of information in those deposits about their diets â€” fish bones, deer bones, elk bones, seals, things like that," Lewarch said.
>
>
>
> He called it a time capsule that goes back 700 to 800 years. ...
>
>
>
> "It's concrete evidence that is expressed today orally in the traditions of the tribes that use Hood Canal. We know people hunted and fished and collected shellfish. This can actually tell us what species they collected and what kind of fish they fished for."

[http://www.kitsapsun.com/news/2012/nov/04/shell-midden-could-affirm-tribes-stories-of-hood/](http://http://www.kitsapsun.com/news/2012/nov/04/shell-midden-could-affirm-tribes-stories-of-hood/)

# Thread: Video:
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video.4708/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-06T20:58:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/video.4708/post-62404>

Video:

> Caves in South Africa reveal remnants of a nutrient-rich, seafood diet that my have contributed to early man's cognitive ability.
>
>
> [http://video.nationalgeographic.com/video/news/ng-today/112811-prehistoric-caves-ngtoday/](http://http://video.nationalgeographic.com/video/news/ng-today/112811-prehistoric-caves-ngtoday/)
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Discoveries in South Africa are important in this area. See:
>
>
>
>
> > Over the last forty years, South Africa has played a prominent role in recording the earliest evidence of shellfish in and substantial expansion of the early human diets as shown by several Middle Stone Age (MSA) coastal sites along the west and south coasts. ...
> >
> >
> >
> > **Possible cognitive implications of shellfish gathering as a reflection of modern behavior are also suggested.**
>
> **
>
>
> [http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248410001296](http://http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S0047248410001296)
>
>
>
> This cave is at Pinnacle point:
>
>
>
> At PP13B the evidence for symbolic behavior comes in the form of scraped and ground ochre (a naturally occurring bright red rock) that may have been used to form a pigment for body painting. This is similar to more complex ochre utilization known from Blombos Cave slightly further to the west at roughly 70,000 years ago.\[4\] **These discoveries contradicts the classical hypothesis that the modern behaviour emerged only 40,000 years ago and was reached through a "large cultural leap".**\[1\] The harsh climate and reduced food resources may have been why people moved to the shore at Pinnacle Point, where they could eat marine creatures like shellfish, whale, and seal**.\[5\]

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinnacle\_Point](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pinnacle_Point)

# Thread: low vit D = longevity.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vit-d-longevity.4712/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-07T01:29:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vit-d-longevity.4712/post-62449>

[http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/11/121105130355.htm](http://http://www.sciencedaily.com/releases/2012/11/121105130355.htm)

Previous studies have shown that low levels of vitamin D are associated with increased rates of death, heart disease, diabetes, cancer, allergies, mental illness and other afflictions. However, it is not known whether low levels are the **cause or a consequence** of these diseases.

### janagram — 2012-11-07T02:02:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vit-d-longevity.4712/post-62460>

Oil™ contains nothing but 100% pure sea buckthorn from the Himalayan mountains. Unlike most other products, it ---

I copied this from an ad....just wondering: what is sea buckthorn, and what is it doing in the Himalayas? there are no beaches up there.

### ruby — 2012-11-07T02:37:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vit-d-longevity.4712/post-62472>

Hippophae rhamnoides is a native plant throughout Europe, including Britain, from Norway south and east to Spain and Asia to Japan and the Himalayas. It is grown as an agricultural plant in Germany, France, Finland, India and China. China is the largest agricultural producer. The origin of the plant is Nepal and it migrated to other parts of Eurasia after the last ice age.

The fruits of sea buckthorn are used in a wide variety of products. Due to difficult harvest conditions and long ramp-up time of 6 to 8 years buckthorn is a relatively expensive raw material. Especially in France (southern Alps) sea buckthorn is commonly sold as fruit juice or as an ingredient in non-alcoholic and alcoholic mixed beverages. Other uses include the berries to be processed as fruit wine or into liquor as well as jam. Buckthorn tea is also made out of the fruits and originates from India.

The fruits have a very high vitamin C content, on average exceeding the vitamin C content of lemons and oranges.

# Thread: Raw Fish/Meat for beginners (me)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2012-11-07T02:35:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62471>

I want to start eating raw fish/meat. Sadly I've only had raw oysters (once), and I know I need to move more in this direction. And I'm ready/willing. But I want to start with easier things (no lamb's heart in a jar just yet for me). I want to start with foods I can source easily to get me started (I often stop before I start when the initial step is a big one (like sourcing fresh/unfrozen raw grassfed lamb's heart). I want choices that are less likely to make me sick, because I know my gut probably still leaky (given the long list of foods I still react to).

Things I am considering:

Ceviche - can I make this at home, with wildcaught sockeye salmon (previously frozen) from the grocery store?

Steak tar tar - anyone made this? I have frozen grassfed ground beef in the freezer...

Anything else? I need beginner choices...

### kathylu — 2012-11-07T02:59:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62473>

You don't want to use previously ground beef for tartare, too much risk of bacterial contamination. I would get a piece of tenderloin or eye round (actually more flavorful than tenderloin) and hand chop, grind or mince it (food processor) myself, shortly before eating it. Here's a good recipe, just skip the french fries:

INGREDIENTS

14 oz. trimmed center-cut beef tenderloin

1 Â½ tbsp. Dijon mustard

2 egg yolks

Â¼ cup canola oil

6 tbsp. salt-packed capers, rinsed, drained, and minced

2 tbsp. minced, pitted green NiÃ§oise olives

2 tbsp. minced parsley

1 Â½ tsp. Worcestershire sauce

Â¼ tsp. hot sauce, such as Tabasco

4 cornichons, minced

1 small yellow onion, minced

Kosher salt and freshly ground black pepper, to taste

French fries, for serving

Mixed salad greens, for serving

INSTRUCTIONS

1\. Place beef in the freezer to firm, about 30 minutes; this will make it easier to chop finely. Meanwhile, whisk mustard and egg yolks in a large bowl; while whisking constantly, slowly pour in oil to create a mayonnaise. Add capers, olives, parsley, Worcestershire, hot sauce, cornichons, and onion, and season with salt and pepper; refrigerate flavorings until ready to use.

2\. Remove beef from freezer and cut into Â¼" cubes. Transfer beef to bowl of flavorings and stir to combine. Keep beef mixture chilled until ready to serve.

3\. To serve, divide beef mixture into 4 equal portions, and shape each into an oval disk on a serving plate. Serve immediately with fries and mixed greens, if you like.

### kathylu — 2012-11-07T03:02:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62474>

Ok...I'm still afraid of E. coli O157, I admit it! So, I'd rather put a little extra work in and remain safe.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T03:15:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62475>

Bigknitwit, do try the seviche. The smaller you cut up the salmon, the faster it will be ready. Just squeeze limes, add seafood, chill and wait. When it feels firm, like it has been cooked, it is done. If you don't like the taste of lime, rinse before eating. If the pieces are large, you can cut them in half and make sure they appear to be cooked all the way through.

I don't blame you about the raw oyster. The first time I had it, I had ... difficulty.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-07T03:17:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62476>

> **kathylu;64289 wrote:** Ok...I'm still afraid of E. coli O157, I admit it! So, I'd rather put a little extra work in and remain safe.

I agree with you, kathylu. I remember my grandmother tasting ground beef before she purchased it from the butcher, but that was another era.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-07T03:33:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/raw-fish-meat-for-beginners-me.4714/post-62477>

Something really easy to try (and you don't have to make it) is to get cold smoked fish. Because it's been cold smoked and no heat used, it has all the properties of raw (but still has the smoky taste). We use this regularly, often wrapped in nori. Yum.

I also do the ceviche techniques with the raw meat... I eat raw heart weekly (but it is really easy to get here - in every supermarket and very cheap)... I add lemon juice and spices/herbs (along with garlic, onions, etc) and let it sit for a couple of hours or even overnight.

# Thread: Despite the superiority of low-carb we still promote low-fat
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/despite-the-superiority-of-low-carb-we-still-promote-low-fat.4715/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-07T04:23:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/despite-the-superiority-of-low-carb-we-still-promote-low-fat.4715/post-62481>

[http://www.doctorslounge.com/index.php/news/hd/33379](http://http://www.doctorslounge.com/index.php/news/hd/33379)

Study finds both low-carb and low-fat diets help overweight people reduce inflammation.

**The people on the low-carb diet, however, lost more weight**

On average, they shed 28 pounds compared to an average loss of 18 pounds for those on the low-fat diet. The study also showed the low-carb group had a more significant drop in BMI and belly fat.

Despite that finding, both diets helped reduce inflammation.

### ruby — 2012-11-07T12:00:58+0200

### ruby — 2012-11-07T12:27:05+0200

### ruby — 2012-11-13T06:31:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-fast-and-live-longer.4717/post-63964>

"It should be known that the three-meals-a-day custom is really a modern one, and is not universally practiced even today. So far as history records none of the nations of antiquity practiced it. At the period of their greatest power, the Greeks and Romans ate only one meal a day. For more than a thousand years the one-meal system was the rule in two countries that could raise armies of men every one of whom would have made his fortune as a modern athlete--men who marched for days under a load of iron (besides clothes and provisions) that would stagger a modern porter. The Romans of the Republican age broke their fast with a biscuit and a fig or two, and took their principle meal in the cool of the evening. Among the many things that have been offered as an explanation for their physical, mental and moral decline has been their sensuous indulgence in food which came with power and riches.

Herodotus records that the invading hosts (over five millions) of the Persian general Xerxes, had to be fed by the conquered cities along their lines of march. He states as a fortunate circumstance the fact that the Persians, including even the Monarch and his courtiers, ate one meal a day.

The Jews from Moses until Jesus ate but one meal a day. They sometimes added a lunch of fruit. The Hebrew scriptures say: "Woe unto the nation whose princes eat in the morning." In the oriental world today extreme moderation, as compared to the American standard, is practiced.

During the zenith period of Grecian and Roman civilization monogamy was not as firmly established as the rule that a health-loving man should content himself with one meal a day, and never eat till he had leisure to digest, i.e., not till the day's work was wholly done. The evening repast--call it supper or dinner--was a kind of domestic festival, the reward of the day's toil, an enjoyment which rich and poor refrained from marring by premature gratifications of their appetites."

The average English family adopted the habit of eating three meals a day during the reign of Queen Elizabeth. Andrew Borde, a physician who lived during the reign of Henry VIII, wrote that: "Two meals a day is sufficient for a rest man; and a laborer may eat three times a day; and he that doth eate ofter lyveth a Beestly lyfe. Breakfast as a regular meal is little heard of, though probably most men started the day with a draught of ale and some bread.

Barely two centuries ago, the first meal of the day in England was taken about noon. Breakfast was an unrecognized meal and it originated in the practice of ladies taking an early dish of chocolate before rising. The ancient Greeks--the finest of people, physically and mentally, that ever lived--ate but two meals a day. The same was true of the ancient Hebrews and it is the custom of some of the best fighting races in India today.

From "The Hygienic System: Orthotrophy", by Herbert M. Shelton

Read more: [http://chestofbooks.com/health/natural-cure/The-Hygienic-System-Orthotrophy/Two-Meals-A-Day.html#ixzz2C4c60yss](http://http://chestofbooks.com/health/natural-cure/The-Hygienic-System-Orthotrophy/Two-Meals-A-Day.html#ixzz2C4c60yss)

### caroline — 2012-11-13T09:11:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-fast-and-live-longer.4717/post-63974>

I have found that calorie restriction is really working for me all of a sudden. I am not sure that that is what I should call it and I don't think it is quite IF but it is working great for the first time in my life. What I am doing is.... Breakfast is 2 Bullet proof coffees with butter and CO and sometimes i eat extra butter and sometimes add in a little bit of protein \[fish /MHS\] if there is something left in the fridge. I wait until I am a little hungry so it might be around 10;30. I then have dinner around 3:30 or 4:30 - Almost always fish. I really find I don't like meat anymore or digest it very well. I seem to be eating quite a bit less and feel very satisfied altho I eat until full. I throw in raw oysters and shrimp and Inger's salmon a few times a week. I have always eaten alot of eggs but I had some today and they really didn't agree with me - I was bloated and felt kind of sick and uncomfortable. I have had an intuition lately that i shouldn't have eggs any more - turned out to be right. This has sort of come together from bits and pieces I have learned from all of you! Thanx!!

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-13T18:10:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-fast-and-live-longer.4717/post-64043>

Ruby - awesome post. I think the behaviors of the successful peoples are where we should look for inspiration. Note - food was a pleasure though, and has been for most societies. I suggest trying to remove that from the equation may be a human nature mismatch.

### ruby — 2012-11-14T01:47:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/eat-fast-and-live-longer.4717/post-64145>

Thank you. Yes, pleasure by all means but the good thing is it seems this pleasure is amplified after a fast, a point collective wisdom has been driving home for quite some time

So there are two ways to boost pleasure - by having more and more food, or by having less of it. And one seems more healthy than the other

The best sauce for food is hunger - Socrates

The best seasoning for food is hunger; for drink, thirst - Cicero

A hungry stomach rarely despises rough food - Horace

Hunger sweetens beans - Latin proverb

Hunger is a good kitchen to a cold potato - Scottish proverb

Hunger is the best cook - German, Polish proverb

Hunger is the best sauce - English proverb

There's no sauce in the world like hunger - Miguel de Cervantes (1547-1616)

Make hunger thy sauce be thou never so nice, For there shalt thou find none other king of spice.

\- Alexander Barclay (c. 1476-1552)

Appetite is the best sauce - English proverb

Hunger is the best pickle - Benjamin Franklin

# Thread: Seaweed and the Sick Dog

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4

### fitness@home — 2012-11-08T02:25:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-and-the-sick-dog.4728/post-62782>

Walked into quite the mess tonight. It appears our lovely Jack Russell decided to have a little snack today of dried seaweed. Must have jumped up onto the island in the kitchen and snagged the entire bag.

He obviously ate some and then just as obviously expelled it from his stomach. Anyone know if seaweed is "bad" for dogs?

The irony of it all...just had new tile installed on the entire first floor last week. Also hired a service for a "deep clean" on Monday.

Little bugger is lucky we love him!

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T04:20:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-and-the-sick-dog.4728/post-62811>

ASPCA runs a poison control hotline you could try if you're really worried. They may charge you a fee, though. Here's their list of foods not to feed your pets: [http://www.aspca.org/pet-care/poison-control/people-foods.aspx](http://http://www.aspca.org/pet-care/poison-control/people-foods.aspx)

### fitness@home — 2012-11-08T12:41:14+0200

### Lyndra — 2012-11-08T19:32:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/seaweed-and-the-sick-dog.4728/post-62946>

I remember reading something about a chemical called geranyl-geraniol awhile back. Geraniol is said to stunt tumors and have some health benefits, and is a constituent of roses and plants containing citronella. Hmm.. that candy might keep mosquitos away, but it is also a bee attractant.

Geranylgeraniol, part of the melavonate pathway, is a diterpine and an intermediate in the synthesis of vitamins E and K, and geraniol is a monoterpine.

# Thread: Undernourishment and overexercise in women
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/undernourishment-and-overexercise-in-women.4731/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-08T08:50:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/undernourishment-and-overexercise-in-women.4731/post-62852>

I listened to Sean Croxton's Underground Wellness podcast yesterday and was surprised to find an item on this. Hot on the heels of the _Fat Burning Man episode on the woman who pushed herself into a dangerous and dysfunctional state over food and exercise comes this.

> Did you know that exercise, along with undernourishment, was can do serious -- and sometimes irreparable -- damage to a woman's body?
>
>
>
> Female Athlete Triad Syndrome -- disordered eating and/or undernourishment, loss of menstruation, and bone loss -- is a growing concern in athletic programs and gyms across the country.

[http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/11/07/female-athlete-triad-w-dr-kelly-austin](http://http://www.blogtalkradio.com/undergroundwellness/2012/11/07/female-athlete-triad-w-dr-kelly-austin)

Sean himself says that as a personal trainer he found that many women were deeply envious of very thin women in the gym whose abdominal muscles were visible through the fat layer. I've always thought it a little strange how some men go on about this on paleo boards, but _women_ wanting this for themselves! The girl on Abel's show had said this was what she wanted. The interviewee says that any woman whose fat percentage is that low is probably already doing damage to herself, because it would lower the amount of estrogen available, which would affect bone health. She says often the first sign of a cascade of unfortunate phenomena that many women notice is stress fractures; however, they often don't connect these to anything else, and neither sometimes even do their doctors. This ought to be an immediate sign to be asking questions about diet and exercise and taking a hormone panel.

I can't think of any photographs of male hunter-gatherers that would show them as having a very low fat percentage.

And with women - look for example at the Theodore de Bry engraving of seed-planting. This would be among Virginia Indians who would have done some hunting and gathering, some fishing, and dabbled a little in agriculture. I should think these women would be living about as healthy and natural an outdoor life as any woman would be likely to be. Their forms are soft and rounded, and I doubt any ever thought, like Coco Chanel, that one could "never be too rich or too thin".

[http://www.encore-editions.com/florida-indians-planting-seeds-of-beans-or-maize](http://http://www.encore-editions.com/florida-indians-planting-seeds-of-beans-or-maize)

What ought to be slightly disturbing for the ancestral health movement is that it ought to be a route out of these unhealthy practices and dysmorphic body images, but for some women it seems to be a route in_.

### caroline — 2012-11-08T09:04:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/undernourishment-and-overexercise-in-women.4731/post-62854>

Michael - I just love reading your posts - I can't figure out how you come up with all this stuff! You really are a star fish!

### Michael — 2012-11-08T09:25:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/undernourishment-and-overexercise-in-women.4731/post-62855>

# Thread: New research on autism
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-research-on-autism.4732/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-08T10:45:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/new-research-on-autism.4732/post-62857>

> The study found a protein which helps brain cells transfer data through neurological pathways called synapses was mutated in autism sufferers, offering a likely explanation for their cognitive and behavioural difficulties.
>
>
>
> Principal investigator Johanna Montgomery, from Auckland University's Centre for Brain Research, said the mutated protein, called Shank3, provided exciting possibilities in the search for autism treatments. ...
>
>
> [http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/healthnews/9663181/Genetic-mutation-discovered-in-people-with-autism.html](http://http://www.telegraph.co.uk/health/healthnews/9663181/Genetic-mutation-discovered-in-people-with-autism.html)
>
>
>
> What's a bit disappointing is that there's nothing in this short piece suggesting the problem is anything other than genetic. That's reinforced by the sentence at the end that puts the spike in numbers down to "more efficient diagnosis".
>
>
>
> She said the condition was becoming more prevalent, partly due to more efficient diagnosis, with studies estimating it affects about one-in-82 children.

the Telegraph doesn't link to any other resources. It say the paper was published in The Journal of Neuroscience.

EDIT:

Rather more information given here:

[http://www.nzdoctor.co.nz/news/2012/november-2012/08/research-gives-clues-to-autism-mechanism.aspx](http://http://www.nzdoctor.co.nz/news/2012/november-2012/08/research-gives-clues-to-autism-mechanism.aspx)

# Thread: Hafting microliths
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hafting-microliths.4733/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-08T12:23:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hafting-microliths.4733/post-62860>

The hafting arrangements for these may have been quite complex. Here's a nice blog article on some Scandinavian examples. At least sometimes it seems microliths may have been embedded further down a missile shaft to act like barbs. They may also have been used for tiny chisels - microburins.

[http://hazelnutrelations.wordpress.com/2012/01/31/hafting-microlith-scandinavia-alps/](http://http://hazelnutrelations.wordpress.com/2012/01/31/hafting-microlith-scandinavia-alps/)

### Michael — 2012-11-08T21:50:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hafting-microliths.4733/post-62979>

Here's something else you might use microliths for - a harpoon/fishing-spear:

[http://www.gov.im/mnh/collections/archaeology/mesolithic/microliths.xml](http://http://www.gov.im/mnh/collections/archaeology/mesolithic/microliths.xml)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Ancient Irish Fish Trap
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-irish-fish-trap.4734/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-08T16:07:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ancient-irish-fish-trap.4734/post-62878>

This one is built of timber and said to be one of the finest in Europe. It only came to light relatively recently, but will probably be washed away and may not even be recorded fully owing to lack of funds:

> One of the finest examples of a medieval fish trap in Europe is going to be washed away before its secrets can be recorded, scientists have claimed.
>
>
>
> A 700-year-old giant wooden weir, once used by monks, cannot be fully preserved because it is exposed to the forces of nature.
>
>
>
> And with budget cuts hitting the Heritage Council there is no money to properly analyse and record the huge structure. ...

[http://www.independent.ie/national-news/ancient-fish-trap-will-be-lost-to-the-tide-2546317.html](http://http://www.independent.ie/national-news/ancient-fish-trap-will-be-lost-to-the-tide-2546317.html)

There's a photo of a wooden Irish fish trap here - near the bottom of the page. Just a few stakes now. Not sure how old this one is, but they seem to have uncovered five substantial fish traps in Dublin alone, going back to Stone Age times:

[http://irisharchaeology.ie/2011/05/dublin-the-prehistoric-city/](http://http://irisharchaeology.ie/2011/05/dublin-the-prehistoric-city/)

### Michael — 2012-11-08T17:18:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orang-utan-fishing.4736/post-62892>

> Orangutans living in Borneo scavenge fish that wash up along the shore and scoop catfish out of small ponds for fresh meals, anthropologist Anne Russon of York University in Toronto reported on April 14 at a meeting of the American Association of Physical Anthropologists. Over two years, **Russon saw several animals on these forested islands learn on their own to jab at catfish with sticks, so that the panicked prey would flop out of ponds and into a red apeâ€™s waiting hands.
>
>
>
> â€œIf orangutans can do this, then early hominids could also have practiced tool-assisted fishing,â€￾ Russon said.** ...
>
>
>
> Fish, even more than red meat, contains fatty acids essential for human brain growth. ...

[http://news.discovery.com/animals/orangutans-fishing-primates-fish-110418.html](http://http://news.discovery.com/animals/orangutans-fishing-primates-fish-110418.html)

I tried searching YouTube under "orang-utan fish" and it looks like there may be some film of this, although haven't checked it out yet.

Try the same search in Google under "video" (the general "video" link not the "YouTube" one and there's what looks to be film of an orang-utan using a stick to fish with.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-08T17:30:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orang-utan-fishing.4736/post-62896>

I've seen clips of this on discovery channel - regarding the orphaned orangutans. I wonder if the deforestation we're causing in their natural habitat is driving them to adapt to other food sources???? what happens if orangutans survive that devestation, and uses shellfish/seafoods from their surrounding water ways? could we see another great ape arise, to compete for resources 250K yrs from now? very Planet of the apes....

### Michael — 2012-11-08T17:41:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orang-utan-fishing.4736/post-62902>

You've seen it!

It's a real clincher, isn't it? If they can do it, why not a hominid? It becomes ridiculous to put "the beginning of fishing" at the date of the earliest fishhooks.

EDIT:

Does this video show it? I can't get it to load:

http://current.com/entertainment/wtf/88923772\_orangutan-hunts-fish-with-spear.htm

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-08T18:51:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/orang-utan-fishing.4736/post-62931>

I can't see video at work... but this was the show I saw it on...

[http://animal.discovery.com/tv/orangutan-island/conservation/adopt.html](http://http://animal.discovery.com/tv/orangutan-island/conservation/adopt.html)

one of the older - less socially acceptable orangutans was the one who did it... and he actually went about it like people do for sport here in the US called Noodling... [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noodling](http://http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Noodling)

soon some of the younger males and one female started emulating him...

# Thread: THIS is how you fight our enemies.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2012-11-08T17:43:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-62904>

Not saying these are our enemies, just illustrating a point. Just give your enemy a rope.

[http://edition.cnn.com/2012/11/08/world/meast/kuwait-obesity-fast-food/index.html?hpt=hp\_c4](http://http://edition.cnn.com/2012/11/08/world/meast/kuwait-obesity-fast-food/index.html?hpt=hp_c4)

### Glamazon — 2012-11-11T01:40:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63480>

Terrorism, american style. Our food culture is deadly.

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-11T05:41:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63537>

Today in the supermarket I had to visit the aisle of death. One side is soda floor to ceiling facing chips floor to ceiling. 80 ft long. I had to buy chips for a party. I stood at one end in awe and said, "The aisle of death!"

### nonchalant — 2012-11-11T23:58:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63692>

Nope, that aisle is the baking aisle. Sugars & sweetners of all types, various flours and wholegrain flours, and lots of processed oils...

Used to be my favorite area of the store.

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-12T00:01:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63693>

So, I see lots of peoples kitchens and look in their pantry. . .even "healthy" seeming people. I've yet to find anybody that has any quantity of actual food. Even the healthiest have larders full of death.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-12T00:49:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63716>

Lol larders full if death. I love that!!!

### Lyndra — 2012-11-12T01:44:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63733>

> **Cavemam;65604 wrote:** So, I see lots of peoples kitchens and look in their pantry. . .even "healthy" seeming people. I've yet to find anybody that has any quantity of actual food. Even the healthiest have larders full of death.

You won't find much food in our pantry. Some canned seafood, coconut & olive oil, olives, capers, and a few canned veggies for emergencies but that's about it. Our fridge, on the other hand, is crammed full. I wish we could get a bigger one but we are space-limited there. One positive note - I recently recovered some cabinet space when I realized I could toss the toaster - never gonna use that puppy again.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-12T01:48:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63734>

> **Dali Dula;65410 wrote:** Today in the supermarket I had to visit the aisle of death. One side is soda floor to ceiling facing chips floor to ceiling. 80 ft long. I had to buy chips for a party. I stood at one end in awe and said, "The aisle of death!"

50% of the "food" aisles are that way. Look at the cereal aisle - how many different processed sugar & grain products can they produce and still manage to differentiate & market them? Don't forget the frozen food aisles, filled with ready-to-eat junk and a myriad of differently prepared potatoes. And last, there's always the bakery section - filled with cookies & cake of all kinds & colors and more bread products that you can imagine. It's really amazing how much space is dedicated to junk. It does make it easier to shop, though. I used to go up and down every aisle, but now I hit the produce & fish, maybe pickup some eggs and frozen veggies and I'm outta there.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-13T08:16:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-63969>

Ever notice the hot food case at the truck stop? Everything they display has been deep fried and dark brown in color! Just point out which brown shape you want, the long skinny one, the square one, or the bumpy round one.

If its your lucky day, the attendant might give you 2 shriveled brown pieces for the price of 1 cause they were "cooked" more than 6 hours ago. MMMM MMMM GUUD!

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-13T18:13:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-64046>

My food shopping now is not the entertainment it used to be for sure. I kinda miss it!

### Inger — 2012-11-14T09:34:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-64191>

> **Cavemam;65999 wrote:** My food shopping now is not the entertainment it used to be for sure. I kinda miss it!

Hmmm.. To me shopping have turned even more entertaining. Now I know what I want.. and it is like going hunting! Sometimes I find stuff that makes me feel like I fouln gold..lol

It is more challenging now but that is what makes it fun too!

### Destiny — 2012-11-14T14:15:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/this-is-how-you-fight-our-enemies.4737/post-64221>

> **Glamazon;65352 wrote:** Terrorism, american style. Our food culture is deadly.

Glamazon,

Love it !

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-labs-please.4741/post-62973>

I am a 40 yo female. I have Hashimoto's and was on synthroid for 7 years up until 3 days ago and now I am on Nature throid 1 grain (so far). I am exhausted, very overweight, terrible periods (Gyno put in an IUD 6 weeks ago and my period has pretty much now disappeared). Hair is falling out a rapid rate, muscle aches, irritability... I take 50,000 vit d 2 x a week.

Glucose, serum 79 mg/dL (65-99)

BUN 15 mg/dL (6-24)

Creatinine, Serum .69 mg/dL (.57-1.00)

eGFR 109 mL/min/1.73 >59

BUN/Creatinine ratio 22 9-23

Sodium, serum 137 mmo1/L 134-144

Potassium, serum 3.8 mmo1/L 3.5-5.2

Chloride, serum 101 mmo1/L 97-108

Carbon dioxide total 23 mmo1/L 20-32

Calcium, serum 9.6 mg/dL 8.7-10.2

Protein, total, serum 7.6 g/dL 6.0-8.5

Albumin, serum 4.3 g/dL 3.5-5.5

Globulin, total 3.3 g/dL 1.5-4.5

A/G ratio 1.3 1.1-2.5

Bilirubin, total 0.2 mg/dL 0.0-1.2

Alkaline phosphatase,s 56 IU/L 25-150

AST (SGOT) 23 IU/L 0-40

ALT (SGPT) 20 IU/L 0-40

Iron Bind. Cap. (TIBC) 391 ug/dL 250-450

UIBC 315 ug/dL 150-375

Iron, serum 76 ug/dL 35-155

Iron saturation 19 % 15-55

Vit d 25-hydroxy D2+D3 45 ng/mL 32-100

Vit D2 28 ng/mL 32-100

Vit D3 17 ng/mL 32-100

MTFHR single mutation A1298C

Thyroid Peroxidase (TPO) Ab 82 IU/mL 0-34

Antithyroglobulin Ab 22 IU/mL 0-40

Thyroxine (T4) free, direct 1.30 ng/dL 0.82-1.77

Reverse T3, serum 37.3 ng/dL 13.5-34.2

DHEA sulfate 144.4 ug/dL 60.9-337.0

Testosterone, serum 18 ng/dL 8-48

TSH 165 UIU/ML 0.450-4.500

Triiodothyronine (T3) 100 ng/dL 71-180

Vit B12 1003 pg/mL 211-946

Ferritin, serum 14 ng/mL 13-150

Free T3 2.8 pg/mL 2.0-4.4

Magnesium, RBC 5.1 mg/dL 4.2-6.8

(plasma not separated from cells)

thank you for reading

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-25T16:13:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/lab-hack-please.4754/post-65576>

> Your billirubin is high probably due to Gilbert's.Â  Your direct billirubin is ~20% of the total, so that's probably what that is.Â  Very common and does no harm.Â  Just that doc's wont' explain to you why.Â  I'd watch Globulin but it doesn't seem to be a major issue.Â  You're not hypothyroid, right?Â  Retest, if your direct billirubin once again is >=20% of Total Billirubin, then Gilbert's; you will always test high.Â  But no issue.Â  If Globulin is still low, I'd watch that and your BUN, unless you have other issues like liver, anemia, autoimmunity, or GFR function decline.Â  You don't seem to.Â  It could be due to moderate overconsumption of proteins, which shouldn't be an issue unless you have pre-existing kidney damage.

# Thread: Upping fat, pain in right side!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/upping-fat-pain-in-right-side.4756/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### jonnyh — 2012-11-09T18:23:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/upping-fat-pain-in-right-side.4756/post-63138>

Hi

So I've been paleo for 2 years, low(70-120g)carb most of the time. Off and on in the 2 years I get a nagging pain in my right side, juts below and under the lower rib cage, it feels sometimes as though someone has there hands digging into the underside of my ribs, sometimes a dull ache a bit lower. It's been manageable, in that time though.

So in the last 2 weeks I've re-read the art and science of low carb living/performance, and have lowered my carb intake to around 50g day, I've increased my training, slow steady stuff fasted in the morning.

As a consequence of the lower carbs and more training, I've upped my fat intake, mainly more EVOO, Coconut oil, raw cream and macadamia nuts.

In these last 2 weeks the pain under the ribs has gotten worse, almost a constant pain, but worse in the evenings after tea.

Had a recent blood test, all ok, liver function test ok etc. vit D 59, below 1 for CRP.

Saw doc again today, she said it maybe an inflammation on the cartilage in the ribs, and said to take anti inflammatories.

Also had a chest x-ray, but will have to wait 10 days for results.

Sorry for the long post, but does anyone have, or had similar symptoms? any ideas what it may be?

My first thoughts are maybe I dont handle too much fat, and should lower the fat and up the carbs more, any thoughts.

Many thanks for any advice!

### ealachan — 2012-11-09T18:30:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/upping-fat-pain-in-right-side.4756/post-63143>

Sounds to me like your gallbladder. I had mine removed way before I ever went paleo, so I have no useful advice in terms of trying to make it feel better, unfortunately...but I DO know that the line most docs like to feed you about how "you don't actually NEED your gallbladder" is complete bull.

Good luck, and I hope you feel better soon. I remember that kind of pain, and it was miserable.

### jonnyh — 2012-11-09T18:51:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/upping-fat-pain-in-right-side.4756/post-63147>

Hi Ealachan, thanks for reply, your right about needing a gallbladder, I think there was a blog on this site recently about it.

And the pain is making me miserable, it's been getting me down in the last week.

I did speak to the doc today about whether it maybe my gallbladder, she said my gallbladder was lower down and probably wasn't causing the problem.

I'm thinking it maybe the gallbladder, anyone know what tests they do to determine if it is the gallbladder or not?

### jonnyh — 2012-11-09T19:00:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/upping-fat-pain-in-right-side.4756/post-63151>

Oh, I forgot to say, while I've gone lower carb/higher fat, apart from the pain in the side, I've felt really good, high energy levels, training going well, sleeping good too.

### ealachan — 2012-11-09T19:15:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/upping-fat-pain-in-right-side.4756/post-63154>

> **jonnyh;64998 wrote:** Hi Ealachan, thanks for reply, your right about needing a gallbladder, I think there was a blog on this site recently about it.
>
> And the pain is making me miserable, it's been getting me down in the last week.
>
> I did speak to the doc today about whether it maybe my gallbladder, she said my gallbladder was lower down and probably wasn't causing the problem.
>
> I'm thinking it maybe the gallbladder, anyone know what tests they do to determine if it is the gallbladder or not?

The thing about gallbladder pain is that there are a lot of nerves in there, and the pain can actually refer to a bunch of different places. Sometimes mine hurt just like you describe, but sometimes it hurt in my back and shoulder, or up between my shoulder blades. Sometimes it felt like someone was stabbing me right under my right breast, and sometimes it hurt directly under my sternum.

If all your blood tests came back ok, then probably the only way to tell if there's something going on in there is an ultrasound. I am not a doctor, but I will say - if you get really pale stools at any point, like grey or white, you need to go to the ER. A stone caught in the common bile duct can severely damage your liver and pancreas, at which point you're in a hell of a lot more trouble than if you just have the gallbladder out. My uncle nearly died from pancreatitis due to a stone in the CBD. He's now diabetic because part of his pancreas actually died from the severity of the inflammation.

That's not to say that there aren't non-surgical ways of dealing with it, as I'm sure there are...just be mindful that it's a condition that has the potential to turn ugly quickly, and should not be taken lightly.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-09T19:23:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/upping-fat-pain-in-right-side.4756/post-63158>

Sounds like gall bladder to me also. But it is surprising since you have been eating paleo for a long time. Usually I'd expect these problems at the beginning. But you have just recently reduced your carbs to 50g/day, so that might be it.

You might want to try some ox-bile salts, to see if that helps. If they do, I'd say definitely it is your gall bladder. But like Ealachan said, don't let them hack it out.

Coconut oil doesn't need the gall bladder for digestion, I believe. So you might want to continue to rely on it.

### jonnyh — 2012-11-09T19:39:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/upping-fat-pain-in-right-side.4756/post-63170>

Thanks Nonchalant, I will carry on with the coconut oil, maybe up that and lower the raw cream.

I will try to source some ox-bile salts this weekend.

Ealachan, I do get pain in differant places around my right chest back area sometimes, so maybe it's more pointing to the gallbladder!

Stools ok at the moment, will keep a check, thanks for that.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-09T21:21:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/upping-fat-pain-in-right-side.4756/post-63192>

I had some gall bladder pain, before trying low carb. It was sharp and breathtaking, then it would go away totally, for a week or so. And the pain also traveled to my back. When I went low carb (30 g per day) I got quite a bit of nausea, diarrhea, and a couple times, vomiting. But after 4-6 weeks, everything settled down. I probably could have used some ox bile myself.

> **seanb4;65199 wrote:** Hi guys,

> My problem is bloating after eating or drinking anything, plus lots of other symptoms (constipation, excema, dizziness, and many more). I have tried diets/supplements to treat sibo, candida, dysbiosis, overacidity, underacidity, H pylori, and many more with no success.

Estrogen dominance symptoms include:

Water retention and bloating

Symptoms of Menopause

Weight gain and bloating

Symptoms of Premenstrual Syndrome

Water retention: Bloating, heaviness, weight gain (2-4 Kg), breast soreness and swelling, generalized rheumatic pain, abdominal bloating and heaviness.

///////////////////////////////////////////

Common causes of abdominal bloating are:

Overeating

Gastric distension

Lactose intolerance, fructose intolerance and other food intolerances

Food allergy

Aerophagia (air swallowing, a nervous habit)

Irritable bowel syndrome

Partial bowel obstruction

Gastric dumping syndrome or rapid gastric emptying

Gas-producing foods

Constipation

Visceral fat

Splenic-flexure syndrome

Menstruation, dysmenorrhea

Polycystic ovary syndrome and ovarian cysts

Alvarez' syndrome, hysterical or neurotic abdominal bloating without excess of gas in the digestive tract

Massive infestation with intestinal parasites, such as worms (e.g., Ascaris lumbricoides)

Diverticulosis

Celiac Disease

Certain Medications such as Phentermine

Occurs in some due to Salivary hypersecretion and dehydration.

Intestinal worms.

//

On offer at Monash Children's Hospital in Victoria, Australia, will be:

> Six dishes which do not contain milk, eggs, soy, fish, shellfish, peanuts, tree nuts, wheat or sesame

### Michael — 2012-11-10T10:58:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scottish-scientists-think-theyve-proved-that-fat-makes-you-fat.4767/post-63335>

I think we saw this one before a couple of months ago in the _Times_ of London.

> SCOTTISH scientists have discovered that ... high-fat foods damage the \[brain\] ...
>
>
>
> The research shed \[sic\] light on how dieters will be able to lose weight without yo-yo dieting and \[will\] help tackle Scotland’s burgeoning childhood obesity crisis. ...

In your dreams ...

[http://www.express.co.uk/posts/view/357225](http://http://www.express.co.uk/posts/view/357225)

The article is illustrated by a picture of a Scotsman eating "high fat" food.

### Stavity — 2012-11-10T14:37:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scottish-scientists-think-theyve-proved-that-fat-makes-you-fat.4767/post-63347>

Sure if it is bad fat, mixed with a bun, American cheese and FF's. I remember those days of eating poisonous fats and what they did to me. I don't miss bloating, my liver enlarging with pain, swollen ankles, neck and shoulder aching, and sleepless 2 hrs at a time with acid reflux.

WHY DO THEY REPORT MEDIOCRE SCIENCE? They really do want to keep people confused.

### Michael — 2012-11-10T15:25:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scottish-scientists-think-theyve-proved-that-fat-makes-you-fat.4767/post-63361>

> **Stavity;65217 wrote:**
>
> WHY DO THEY REPORT MEDIOCRE SCIENCE?

"Mediocre science"? The professor at the University of Aberdeen will already be taking his ancestral Claymore from the wall and asking for your address ...

I guess the papers run these stories, because they have column inches to fill, and "researchers", knowing that and wishing to justify their existence, send them press releases.

I think another reason why such a story might "have legs" is buried in that report: the dietary establishment likes it. It gives them a rationalization for NOT reassessing what they've been saying for the past half-century while accounting for the increasingly obvious - and terribly embarrassing - fact that low-carb diets work. As a dietitian what you claim is that while these "crash" diets work "in the short term", in the long term they fail. No evidence is, of course, necessary for the latter assertion, but you can gesture vaguely in the direction of a rat study, or what the University of Auchtermuchty has said, or might have said, and look knowing.

### shilohman — 2012-11-10T17:08:43+0200

# Thread: The misuse of neuroscience
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-10T13:22:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/post-63340>

Here's a letter to London's _Daily Telegraph_ signed by several psychologists, neuroscientists, and social scientists that's implicitly critical of the current trend for illustrating newspaper articles with images of brains, implying that neuroscience has demonstrated things it hasn't, and forgetting that we are not merely brains but whole persons and, beyond that, moving in a whole social environment:

> SIR â€“ Failed educational attainment, persistent unemployment, poverty, addiction and crime are problems associated with politics, economics and morality, not neuroscience.
>
>
>
> The picture illustrating Alisdair Palmerâ€™s article showed a large and a small brain, but did not explain that the small brain followed â€œsevere total global neglectâ€￾: an infant caged in a cot for 23 or more hours a day, physically and mentally isolated, malnourished, and often cold and sick.
>
>
>
> In contrast the large brain probably belonged to an infant whose mother provided normal amounts of responsiveness and care to her baby. Parents providing intermittent food, shelter, warmth and comfort, according to the demands of normal life, create children with normal brains.
>
>
>
> Neuroscience has not demonstrated that parenting with heightened sensitivity will resolve social ills. But we understand why some might wish it had. Then there would be no need to restructure the economy, develop good jobs, revitalise blighted areas, and do the other hard work necessary to improve society. Instead we could just blame bad mothers for creating bad people.
>
>
>
> Dr Stuart W G Derbyshire
>
> Reader in Psychology, University of Birmingham
>
>
>
> Dr Ellie Lee
>
> Reader in Social Policy, University of Kent
>
>
>
> Professor Raymond Tallis
>
> Emeritus Professor, University of Manchester
>
>
>
> Dr Caroline Di Bernardi Luft
>
> Research Fellow, Cognitive Neuroimaging Laboratory, University of Birmingham
>
>
>
> Dr Pam Lowe
>
> Senior Lecturer in Sociology, Aston University
>
>
>
> Dr Joe McCleery
>
> Lecturer in Developmental Neuroscience, University of Birmingham
>
>
>
> Dr Jan Macvarish
>
> Research Fellow, Centre for Health Services Studies, University of Kent

[http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/letters/9652341/Problem-children-soon-reach-an-age-when-they-are-beyond-help.html](http://http://www.telegraph.co.uk/comment/letters/9652341/Problem-children-soon-reach-an-age-when-they-are-beyond-help.html)

### Thor — 2012-11-13T04:33:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/post-63948>

crazy stuff out there

### nonchalant — 2012-11-13T04:47:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/post-63959>

Maybe THAT's why we are following Jack? Because of the 3-inch brain at the top of this page? Perhaps it's not all the science he reveals, it's just the logo?

### Michael — 2012-11-13T10:26:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-misuse-of-neuroscience.4769/post-63985>

> **Nonchalant;65907 wrote:** Maybe THAT's why we are following Jack? Because of the 3-inch brain at the top of this page? Perhaps it's not all the science he reveals, it's just the logo?

LOL

I guess Jack has some title to put one there, since he deals with them professionally. Still, I suppose it's a kind of oblique proof of the iconographic value of the brain. Otherwise, he'd have put a stethoscope or something there instead.

### Michael — 2012-11-10T14:32:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nice-quote.4770/post-63345>

Here's one from one of my favourite poets:

[http://mobile.brainyquote.com/quotes/quotes/w/williambut133313.html](http://http://mobile.brainyquote.com/quotes/quotes/w/williambut133313.html)

### Michael — 2012-11-11T09:55:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nice-quote.4770/post-63558>

There used to be a full-page advertisement for Jameson's Irish whiskey with the line "smoother than the Scotch" accompanied by some particularly spiky and difficult to understand Robbie Burns and Yeats's _The Wild Swans at Coole_ ... "The trees are in their autumn beauty,/ The woodland paths are dry, ..."

Unfair to Burns or Scottish whisky, of course, but quite clever. Amazing how advertising gets into everything.

They've not yet used "He wishes for the cloths of heaven" to sell anything. Maybe a shoe company could make use of that one?

[http://www.online-literature.com/yeats/776/](http://http://www.online-literature.com/yeats/776/)

### caroline — 2012-11-11T11:01:14+0200

"The trees are in their autumn beauty,/ The woodland paths are dry, ..."

> Unfair to Burns or Scottish whisky, of course, but quite clever. Amazing how advertising gets into everything.

I love, love, love,that. This is all very new to me.... any suggestions on where to start?

### Michael — 2012-11-11T12:13:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nice-quote.4770/post-63565>

I'd buy the collected poems. Paperback copies are ridiculously cheap - Amazon UK does them for about the price of a pint of beer:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/gp/aw/d/1853264547](http://http://www.amazon.co.uk/gp/aw/d/1853264547)

Probably the same in Australia.

Some of the poetry is online, but I don't think it's all out of copyright yet. Project Gutenberg may have some. Librivox has some recordings people have made - of variable quality, since it's just amateur offerings by people. About a year ago they did _The Fiddler of Dooney_ which is fun:

[http://librivox.org/the-fiddler-of-dooney-by-william-butler-yeats/](http://http://librivox.org/the-fiddler-of-dooney-by-william-butler-yeats/)

Librivox has also sponsored readings of "When you are old and grey and full of sleep ..." which is a rather beautiful poem.

There must be something by Yeats for every mood.

I don't think I ever absorbed anything sublingually. I wanted to chew off my right arm almost the entire round no matter how I changed the dose. This is so awesome! Down 4.1 lbs day 1! All water I am sure but I will take it! I don't even feel withdrawal from carbs (yet). Maybe I will skip that altogether?? I usually get a horrible headache, and exhaustion. This is so easy!

# Thread: gender bias as an evolutionary advantage
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gender-bias-as-an-evolutionary-advantage.4777/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Entelechy — 2012-11-10T21:41:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gender-bias-as-an-evolutionary-advantage.4777/post-63416>

interesting thread ealachan had started and gave me pause for thought.. some random ideas coalescing, but could not post before the thread was closed.

the arguments presented there (other than opinion) assume that homosexuality is evolutionarily disadvantageous.

There are numerous examples of species being able to sway the sex of their offspring based on environmental conditions.

There were undoubtedly periods where resources were limited and too many pregnancies would be disadvantageous, Limiting their numbers would ensure that the fewer pregnancies in the tribe maximized their likelihood survival.

Belonging to a group was essential to survival, and all members of the tribe were invested in each other's success. Perhaps homosexuality could have evolved as a species survival trait, allowing close social bonds to be created by sexual attachment even though those bonds would not result in offspring.

Studies about altered brain gender possibly causing homosexuality have shown that androstendione blocks receptors of the fetus from receiving testosterone. Androstendione is produced during periods of stress, and correlations have been found between maternal stress and homosexuality of their offspring.

thoughts??

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kuwait-worried-about-fatty-food-too.4782/post-63552>

Isn't nonsense tenacious?

[http://news.kuwaittimes.net/2012/11/06/taking-the-first-step-towards-a-healthy-future-for-your-child/](http://http://news.kuwaittimes.net/2012/11/06/taking-the-first-step-towards-a-healthy-future-for-your-child/)

Apparently, the "correct" diet and "exercise" can solve everything.

Exercise? Generally, good for one, of course. But never mind the recent sophisticated experiment with the Hadza, or what Steve Phinney found with people on treadmills in locked metabolic wards.

It's amazing just how tenacious and just how immune to evidence these fixed ideas are. And apparently, believed all the way from Aberdeen to Azerbaijan.

# Thread: Gene testing help

**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### msclman — 2012-11-11T08:53:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/gene-testing-help.4783/post-63554>

Hi Guys. Had a dna ancestry test done years ago to see my family tree. Wondering if there is anything that can be drawn from the results with respect to SNP's but I just do not have the knowledge base to figure it out. Can someone tell me if any health advice can be drawn from these results??

# Thread: Trials: supplementation with omega 3s and iron
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-11T09:12:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/post-63557>

They tried this with schoolchildren in South Africa with mixed results. In some cases the omega 3s appeared to **un**helpful. The patchy results could indicate that the samples were too small. However, it has been found in rats deficient in o-3s and Fe that giving either alone "seemed to worsen ... memory performance".

[http://my.chicagotribune.com/#section/-1/article/p2p-73228323/](http://http://my.chicagotribune.com/#section/-1/article/p2p-73228323/)

Seems to me it's likely to be best to eat fish and shellfish and thereby get an abundant supply of o-3s and Fe ... and I, Se, Cu and Zn.

An interesting trial in any event.

### Michael — 2012-11-11T18:58:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/post-63622>

More sources picking that up:

[http://www.emaxhealth.com/11306/iron-and-omega-3-fatty-acid-deficiencies-impacts-childrens-brains](http://http://www.emaxhealth.com/11306/iron-and-omega-3-fatty-acid-deficiencies-impacts-childrens-brains)

[http://www.foxnews.com/health/2012/11/08/iron-omega-3s-tied-to-different-effects-on-kids-brains/](http://http://www.foxnews.com/health/2012/11/08/iron-omega-3s-tied-to-different-effects-on-kids-brains/)

And here's the original resource:

[http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2012/10/23/ajcn.112.041004.abstract?sid=d8a6e39b-a1bf-41e1-bdcc-80be6c1a5719](http://http://ajcn.nutrition.org/content/early/2012/10/23/ajcn.112.041004.abstract?sid=d8a6e39b-a1bf-41e1-bdcc-80be6c1a5719)

See also:

[http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01092377](http://http://clinicaltrials.gov/ct2/show/NCT01092377)

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-11T19:58:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/post-63649>

Wonder what their diets are really like. If they're eating sugar and grains, then taking one little O-3 pill isn't going to help much. The results could actually show to be worse because people can sometimes think, well I took my "healthy little pill", now I can go eat more cake.

### Michael — 2012-11-12T09:08:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/trials-supplementation-with-omega-3s-and-iron.4784/post-63792>

I think they're probably not making decisions about what to eat - just eating whatever the parents give them which will be mostly determined by what's available, what's cheap, and what's culturally normal, and probably never enough. That's probably unchanged whether they take anything or whether they don't.

I think the ineffectiveness in some of the cases probably really is down to these nutrients needing to work synergistically. Why performance can actually get _worse_ I don't know, though - but then I guess no-one does.

It's a little sad.

# Thread: calories shmalories
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calories-shmalories.4786/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-11T15:05:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/calories-shmalories.4786/post-63576>

"It had been a year, at least, since Ghanshyam last ate meat, eight months since he was able to catch fish in the village river, and six since he had had an egg, he told me.

This diet, heavy in cereals and other carbohydrate-based calories, is what most rural Indians eat. In 2010, 64 percent of the calories consumed by villagers came from cereals, about 9 percent from oils and fats, less than 5 percent each from sugar and pulses like the lentils we ate. Fruit and vegetables, meat, eggs and fish together made up about 2.5 percent, according to studies of meals across rural India by the statistics ministry."

[http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2012-10-22/hunger-stalks-my-father-s-india-long-after-starvation-banished.html#disqus\_thread](http://http://www.bloomberg.com/news/2012-10-22/hunger-stalks-my-father-s-india-long-after-starvation-banished.html#disqus_thread)

[http://www.keepandshare.com/doc/5003658/p-thyroid-globular-test-jpg-195k?da=y](http://http://www.keepandshare.com/doc/5003658/p-thyroid-globular-test-jpg-195k?da=y)

[http://www.keepandshare.com/doc/5003659/p-cortisol-saliva-test-jpg-272k?da=y](http://http://www.keepandshare.com/doc/5003659/p-cortisol-saliva-test-jpg-272k?da=y)

[http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/](http://http://www.stopthethyroidmadness.com/rt3-ratio/)

It won't let me put the measurement in for this combination. The units on T3 are ng/mL and the units for the RT3 are pg/mL.

Scratch that I converted with a calculator...and I have a number but its crazy high.....

With 1070 T3 and 221 RT3 it gives me a number of 4841.6....with ideal being Total/RT3 being above 10....that is way above 10 but not sure what that means.

Is that super ideal...

Did I miss or mess something up?

She has had WLS...I can't remember which type but will ask and wants to get to optimal. Shes currently 15 pounds away from goal after the surgery. I don't remember where but I think Jack has said something that was perhaps not good having to do with WLS....if anyone could remind me that would be great.

Thanks

# Thread: Scary Seafood of the Week....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kathylu — 2012-11-12T06:09:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63784>

I have vowed to try a new seafood item every time I shop at the asian grocery fish counter. This week's entry: flounder roe! Pink, veined, lobular sacs of fishy wonderfulness. I have no doubt that the nutritional value is outstanding, but how to prepare them? Most recipes say to dredge in flour (that's out of course), salt and pepper and saute in butter or bacon fat. I'm having them tomorrow morning for breakfast. Will let you know how they are...with pics!

What are you doing to expand your seafood repetoire?

### Dali Dula — 2012-11-12T16:16:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63813>

Flounder roe! I have been looking for this since I discovered it in a fish market on Long Island last spring. It is delicious and oh so nutritious. All roe is a choline party. It was inexpensive, but seasonal. Where are you located? If the season is on I need to know. It is firm enough to slice into 1/2 in thick medallions which I pan fried and spiced. I have been using cooked frozen lobster roe as a staple ingredient in my fish broths.

### kathylu — 2012-11-12T17:12:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63820>

Ok, here it is! The lobes remind me of a pancreas. Not very appetizing to look at, but they were actually pretty tasty! I sauteed them in bacon fat, seasoned with salt and pepper and squeezed fresh lime over them. Success!

Attached files [ ](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/65761=569-DSCN2050.jpg)[](http://http://www.jackkruse.com/forum/convert_files/attachments/65761=570-DSCN2051.jpg)

### kathylu — 2012-11-12T17:15:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63821>

> **Dali Dula;65754 wrote:** Flounder roe! I have been looking for this since I discovered it in a fish market on Long Island last spring. It is delicious and oh so nutritious. All roe is a choline party. It was inexpensive, but seasonal. Where are you located? If the season is on I need to know. It is firm enough to slice into 1/2 in thick medallions which I pan fried and spiced. I have been using cooked frozen lobster roe as a staple ingredient in my fish broths.

Dali, I'm in Maryland. The roe looked quite fresh, but to be honest, I didn't check the origin. I was just so excited to try something new, I forgot to look. So, I suppose they might not have been local.

### shah78 — 2012-11-12T22:25:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63864>

> **kathylu;65761 wrote:** Ok, here it is! The lobes remind me of a pancreas. Not very appetizing to look at, but they were actually pretty tasty! I sauteed them in bacon fat, seasoned with salt and pepper and squeezed fresh lime over them. Success!

Compared to the desocketed-eyeballs I've been eating lately, it looks quiet tame.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-12T22:31:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scary-seafood-of-the-week.4794/post-63865>

Kathylu, it is really good to eat raw, too. If you buy it fresh, just cut it up and eat it. If it's not fresh (as in caught same day), I freeze it in cubes and eat it that way. It is super good for you and when raw tastes of almost nothing. (If it has a fishy smell at all, I freeze it.)

# Thread: That was before sugar

**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
