# Thread: When something is difficult, be thankful
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2012-11-14T22:30:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/post-64325>

This is a really thought provoking article on how things that are difficult actually make us more productive, more creative while creating a deeper and longer lasting understanding.

I see this in reading through Jack's posts. They certainly aren't "easy" for me - but because I work at it and go back and re-read, I value what I get out of them more. And they really make me think, unlike these pop culture magazine ads of "do this and get this".

> Our brains respond better to difficulty than we imagine. In schools, teachers and pupils alike often assume that if a concept has been easy to learn, then the lesson has been successful. But numerous studies have now found that when classroom material is made harder to absorb, pupils retain more of it over the long term, and understand it on a deeper level.
>
>
>
> Also think this is a great read for those of us who think that getting our health back is "hard". Because if it's hard - we're going to be more creative about problem solving, and it will be more likely to be lifelong!
>
>
>
>
> > The researchers also found that when people are forced to cope with unexpected obstacles they react by increasing their "perceptual scope"â€”taking a mental step back to see the bigger picture.

[http://moreintelligentlife.com/content/ideas/ian-leslie/uses-difficulty?buffer\_share=7da8f](http://http://moreintelligentlife.com/content/ideas/ian-leslie/uses-difficulty?buffer_share=7da8f)

### caroline — 2012-11-14T22:46:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/post-64329>

>
>
>
> I see this in reading through Jack's posts. They certainly aren't "easy" for me - but because I work at it and go back and re-read, I value what I get out of them more. And they really make me think, unlike these pop culture magazine ads of "do this and get this".
>
>
>
> [http://moreintelligentlife.com/content/ideas/ian-leslie/uses-difficulty?buffer\_share=7da8f](http://http://moreintelligentlife.com/content/ideas/ian-leslie/uses-difficulty?buffer_share=7da8f)

Thanks Diane for posting that. I, like you struggle to understand Dr. K's blogs at times but I keep rereading and it is getting easier - I just wish I could explain them but I guess in time that will come

### nonchalant — 2012-11-14T22:52:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/post-64330>

Interesting article, Diane.

I still remember a science lesson from ... 6th grade? The teacher presented a practical problem, and absolutely refused to tell us if we had the correct answer or even if we were on the right track. This was not for a test, or homework. She never answered that question, even after the school year. It was quite intriguing to us, and we discussed/debated the problem amongst ourselves.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-14T23:00:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-something-is-difficult-be-thankful.4851/post-64331>

> As a poet, Ted Hughes had an acute sensitivity to the way in which constraints on self-expression, like the disciplines of metre and rhyme, spur creative thought.

Yes! I've experienced this.

I'll write something out in prose, and then convert it to a traditional poem, and I'm delighted at the results. I'm forced to use different words or phrases, because they just won't work in the poem, and I end up with something much better. More concise, and descriptive.

# Thread: Egg Free, Dairy Free, Grain Free, Soy Free Cookie
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-15T00:31:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64351>

Well, I had been making egg muffins for the kids in the morning but with DD's enterolab results I had to set out in search of something that they would enjoy having with some meat. Veggies for breakfast aren't at the top of their list... yet. While we work on all that I thought I'd try to whip up something not too bad for them. After looking at several recipes on the internet I blended them and here's what I came up with:

9T Coconut Flour

6T Coconut Oil (you could use butter or ghee here just fine I think)

2T Honey or Maple Syrup

1/4 t Celtic Sea Salt

3T dried wild berries mixed with soaked nuts

1T chopped dark chocolate bits

Mix all together, drop onto cookie sheet (I used a silpat liner, not sure how they'd do straight on the pan), pat down a bit, and bake at 365 degrees for 8 to 10 minutes or until browned on bottom. Let them cool all the way after you take them out of the oven or they'll crumble.

If you don't like salty sweet reduce the amount of sea salt...

Here's a close-up

[](http://http://imageshack.us/photo/my-images/835/cookiehk.jpg/)

If anyone else has good ideas that are free from the items in the subject line I'd love if you shared! Thanks!

Edit: This makes 12 cookies... One baking sheet full

### nonchalant — 2012-11-15T01:00:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64356>

Sounds yummy, thanks River. With the holidays coming, I'm looking at baking again. It's not something I do anymore, but for a special occasion I can make an exception.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-15T01:49:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64367>

If it weren't for the little ones I wouldn't even think about something like this - probably even for the holidays. But, yank milk and eggs away from someone who doesn't really fully understand what's happening (because they're 3) and put something that looks like dinner in front of them for breakfast with a glass of water and it's a shock, and they're in a fasted state, and well... they're 3 so it seems like a BFD when you're used to having a glass of milk first thing in the morning. Every one of their favorite meals has been impacted so I am being a little flexible on breakfast so I can be hard on the other meals. Some 3 year olds love trying new things and experimenting with food but mine are adventurous in other ways... not so much with food.

### bigknitwit — 2012-11-15T02:27:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64371>

These look like a great transition food - thanks for the idea. I could use something like this to pack for dd's snack for church (why the 3 year old class needs to have snack during their brief sunday school meeting is beyond me, but I digress...)

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-15T06:10:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64419>

bigknitwit,

They need to have a snack because even at 3 they can't go a couple hours without their carb fix to stave off the hypoglycemia.....

I mean who hasn't seen a toddler these days go into full meltdown because they've got the shakes and need their juice!

Okay, maybe I'm being dramatic.... and cynical.... but I'm in a mood and I'm claiming perogative. I realized this afternoon that there is some sort of grieving/coping process when dealing with food issues and children.

### bigknitwit — 2012-11-15T14:00:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64454>

> **RiverNeighbor;66416 wrote:** bigknitwit,
>
>
>
> They need to have a snack because even at 3 they can't go a couple hours without their carb fix to stave off the hypoglycemia.....
>
>
>
> I mean who hasn't seen a toddler these days go into full meltdown because they've got the shakes and need their juice!
>
>
>
> Okay, maybe I'm being dramatic.... and cynical.... but I'm in a mood and I'm claiming perogative. I realized this afternoon that there is some sort of grieving/coping process when dealing with food issues and children.

And in the nursery (for the children under 3), where I volunteer every few weeks, we feed the babies goldfish and apple juice. We never feed them anything different because of allergy issues. Goldfish and Apple juice. I've had to pack my dd her own snack since she could start eating solid foods (and not scream in the nursery). The way we handle food with our children in sickening, IMO. It's even more sickening though, as a mother, when we realize just how our child is being damaged by these practises, and how they no longer have the option to participate, even once in a while. There is some definite grieving to be done here, I agree. Last week, 20 minutes after snack, one of the 14 month old little boys asked for his juice filled bottle...

Sorry I'm completely pulling away from your great recipe idea again.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-16T06:12:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64658>

Not pulling away at all, bigknitwit

Cookies were a big HELP today, but not a big hit. I'd say between the two of them they ate 1 cookie. Having it as an option really made breakfast go more smoothly. Son asked me first thing this morning if he couldn't have milk could he have an extra big cup of water. He's having a harder time than daughter because he likes fewer meats and vegetables and he was more into the milk/yogurt/cheese and egg. They're adjusting and this is turning out not to be as hard as I thought it was going to be.

Daughter stood at the grocery store this afternoon and asked me why so many things have things in it that hurt people.... at first I thought she was saying it because she was sad she couldn't have the foods that looked appealing. Turns out she was concerned that other people were buying it and getting hurt. It was sweet.

### vkiernan — 2012-11-17T15:46:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-64931>

Son asked me first thing this morning if he couldn't have milk could he have an extra big cup of water. He's having a harder time than daughter because he likes fewer meats and vegetables and he was more into the milk/yogurt/cheese and egg. They're adjusting and this is turning out not to be as hard as I thought it was going to be.
>
> Daughter stood at the grocery store this afternoon and asked me why so many things have things in it that hurt people.... at first I thought she was saying it because she was sad she couldn't have the foods that looked appealing. Turns out she was concerned that other people were buying it and getting hurt. It was sweet.

You are apparently doing a great job with this! I couldn't imagine being in your shoes.

### RiverNeighbor — 2012-11-19T03:57:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/egg-free-dairy-free-grain-free-soy-free-cookie.4854/post-65257>

It is really interesting to watch the family adapt....

DD is feeling so good. She jumped up and down and yelled "I feel great" into the phone when my MIL asked her how she was feeling. Her behavior has improved dramatically (she had been having some strange behavior).

Tonight she ate both a sardine and some seaweed with her dinner.

DS went from doing well for several days into meltdown mode today and refused to eat anything after breakfast. Tomorrow's a new day. I'm sure he'll eat when he's hungry enough.

# Thread: The Potato Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 35
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AKMan — 2012-11-15T02:10:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64369>

I think the paleo blogosphere is onto something, but they don't know what. The so-called "Potato Diet" is lighting up all the forums and blogs from PaleoHacks to Free The Animal to Mark's Daily Apple.

This diet was started by Ray Cronise as a simple trick to prove that carbs weren't the cause of weight problems and in fact could be used to lose fat.

On his blog, Ray propositioned overweight folks to eat only potatoes, as plain as possible, for a period of 7-14 days. Everyone that did this lost 1/2 to 1 pound per day. It's as he says "predictable and repeatable".

I'm thinking the timing of this led to it's success. Fall is starchy tuber time.

Maybe this is one of those ancestral pathways Jack talks about, like the Leptin Reset. Our ancestors would have surely faced times of starvation when all that was available was starchy tubers, since these grow underground and are impervious to drought, frost, insects, and animals. During times of starvation, and when eating only starchy tubers, it would have been prudent to go into fat-burning/muscle sparing mode. The insulin surges from eating starchy tubers without any additional fat or protein maybe create a signalling pathway to burn fat.

So what do you all think? A seasonal way to lose a few pounds of fat or just straight-up calories in-calories out?

### Huck — 2012-11-15T02:33:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64374>

The potato diet was 1st done by Chris Voigt, head of the Washington State Potato Commission, as a protest against a government proposal to restrict potatoes in some government food programs.

In 60 days of eating just potatoes, he lost 21 lbs, blood glucose dropped 10 points to 94, cholesterol dropped 67 points to 147, and triglycerides dropped 60 points to 75. His BP at the end of the diet was 112/70.

[http://20potatoesaday.com/index.html](http://http://20potatoesaday.com/index.html)

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T02:55:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64382>

So maybe while there are no 'safe starches', the potato gives us an ancestral inroad to fatloss with no hunger--like HCG. Not for long-term use, but as a quick fat loss strategy to get our fat levels down to where they are manageable with leptin, sunlight, epi-paleo and all that jazz.

### nonchalant — 2012-11-15T02:59:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64383>

Say what you want, yes it's tuber season, but my hip joints really don't like potatoes. They complain every time I indulge. And they complain at night. Quite effectively.

### hellojtm — 2012-11-15T03:15:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64389>

Hey AKMan,

I have to say that I think Ray is a nutritionist. I.e. a person that subscribes to nutritionism. One who believes that we know what's in food and can therefore decide what we need to eat. As such I have a distaste for where he is headed. I think that once he abandons that then he will become a great resource. OK, that caveat aside, I kind of followed the potato diet thing. From my understanding he's doing it to foster the growth of a specific gut flora ecosystem. My question is this, we've seen the potato diet before, we've seen the banana diet, we've seen the raw meat diet, etc... but those are all just paleo diets. \*MY\* opinion, starchy tubers are not an evolutionarily novel food. As such your body knows what to do with them when they are a staple. Eliminate \*all\* the other bull\*\*\*\* and just have a staple of paleo food, in this case a monocrop of potatoes, and your body will know what to do with it. Do it with broccoli. Do it with blackberries. Sure, some might be better than others, like in this case his nutritionism mind tells him that a potato has aminos in it so it's better than other fruits or what not. But I think my point stands. Eat a paleo diet and you will get the benefits of a body and a meal that are in sync and you will be on your way to optimal. Although I don't subscribe to the religion of nutritionism I have a gut feel that these potato dieters are incurring deficiencies, probably not ones that become clinical in months but they would in years.

jesse

### hellojtm — 2012-11-15T03:17:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64390>

Also, to address the tuber season point, as we know most people's bodies have no clue what season it is so I don't buy that argument for the general population.

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T03:25:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64395>

> **hellojtm;66386 wrote:** Also, to address the tuber season point, as we know most people's bodies have no clue what season it is so I don't buy that argument for the general population.

Thanks for those two thoughtful posts. Very astute observations.

It seems to me this is being used a as very short-term, 1-2 week, way to lose 5-10lbs of fat without hunger. I would definitely agree with you that much longer than that would lead to deficiencies. I always get excited when I see people losing weight without hunger, like hcg users, and not resorting to surgery. The gut flora comment intrigues me also. What if a short term diet of starchy tuber clarifies the gut flora creating a signalling situation as we saw in the BG series that leads to quicker than normal fat loss?

The folks I see talking about the potato diet are saying 5-10 lbs in 1-2 weeks, that's better than any other fat-loss hack I've seen people trying like IF, CT, spot-icing, etc... Granted those aren't supposed to be quick-fix fat-loss tools, but so many people would love to have a way to lose 5-10lbs in a couple weeks. Just kinda hoping this is a good way to do it.

### Huck — 2012-11-15T03:31:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64398>

Many, many, many, many years ago I did the Diet Center diet, which was a high protein, low fat, low carb diet. The 1st 2 days, you ate a baked potato with dinner. If I remember correctly, it was supposed to help stabilize blood sugar. Maybe the idea was to keep from cutting out all sugars and starches cold turkey and eat a specific amount of starch during the 1st 2 days.

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T04:05:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64402>

> **Huck;66394 wrote:** Many, many, many, many years ago I did the Diet Center diet, which was a high protein, low fat, low carb diet. The 1st 2 days, you ate a baked potato with dinner. If I remember correctly, it was supposed to help stabilize blood sugar. Maybe the idea was to keep from cutting out all sugars and starches cold turkey and eat a specific amount of starch during the 1st 2 days.

[http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2012/10/protons-zero-fat\_3.html](http://http://high-fat-nutrition.blogspot.com/2012/10/protons-zero-fat_3.html)

Peter at Hyperlipid said this of an all-starch diet:

"But the physiology of weight loss on ultra low fat diets is basically comprehensible, especially once you look at lipids and superoxide at the ETC level, and what the body needs to function effectively. Running your metabolism on pure glucose would induce, theoretically, an infinite glucose sensitivity and low fasting insulin. If we do reductio ad absurdum you would end up with no fat stores and experience death from hypoglycaemia if you ever depleted your glycogen stores. Mitochondria like (saturated) fatty acids. Fatty acids keep them in control."

In the comments, someone we know and love said this:

"Jack Kruse said...

I know that sounds sounds counterintuitive to some, but it is precisely how the brain is wired in humans to couple food and light into our metabolism and sleep. The body collects that data from the eye (SCN) and skin for the brain and the brain senses and correlates that data to set the the metabolic rate and growth cycle by going up or down with dopamine levels at the HPA. Dopamine interacts with the leptin receptor and its pathways directly as mentioned above and this is why women and men are different at a central Nervous system level. This is why sleep, metabolism and the cell cycle are all linked to the dopamine pathways tied to light and not just food. Food reward is a good idea not looked into deep enough by those who buy it 100%. I love your series Peter. I feel like you are digging the same hole I am from different ends of the earth and soon we will meet. Our only difference is our perspective and our digging tools."

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-15T04:42:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64403>

OK, AKMan, I am now intrigued. Can you please translate the exchange between Peter and Jack for me? I've read it but I don't fully understand. How is what Peter suggests true? And how did Jack go from what Peter wrote to discussing light?

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T05:40:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64410>

> **KiwiLauren;66399 wrote:** OK, AKMan, I am now intrigued. Can you please translate the exchange between Peter and Jack for me? I've read it but I don't fully understand. How is what Peter suggests true? And how did Jack go from what Peter wrote to discussing light?

Maybe this is the portion of the article Jack was referring to, more specifically:

"How about scaling this up to a massive dose of potato induced insulin and limiting dietary fat? Severely limiting dietary fat. And never mind pussy footing around at 40g of mixed carbs and protein. There is a limit to how low FFAs can be driven, and it seems safe to assume that a baked potato or three might just inhibit lipolysis maximally and keep it that low for rather a long time. But if you deprive beta cells of free fatty acids you blunt their ability to secrete insulin. Very, very high carbohydrate diets really ought to be able to inhibit lipolysis to the point where the knock on effect is the inhibition of insulin secretion, provided you don't supply exogenous fat. Look at the nicotinic acid treated rats...

Once you get FFA levels low enough to inhibit insulin secretion you will start to move in to the sort of territory where insulin secretion might be blunted enough to allow hyperglycaemia. But the feedback effect of reduced insulin levels is also the re commencement of lipolysis. This will restore enough FFAs to maintain functional insulin secretion and so avoid potential hyperglycaemia, which the body tries to avoid. Of course you have to throw in the increased insulin sensitivity of muscles deprived of exogenously supplied FFAs too.

So is it possible to eat an ad lib, calorie unrestricted diet based on near pure carbohydrate and lose weight? Working from the premise that lowered insulin is a pre requisite for hunger free weight loss, as I always do, the answer is possibly yes. We all remember Chris Voight on his all potato diet (plus 20ml of olive oil, low in palmitate, per day) who lost a great deal of weight over a few weeks, the rate of weight loss accelerating as the weeks progressed? I had a think about it here, well before I had any inkling as to what might be happening in the electron transport chain."

OK, AKMan here, again - What I think Peter is saying, is that a massive dose of carbs, like potatoes only for 2 weeks, would greatly effect insulin and hunger. This isn't the 'safe starch' line of thinking that rice is OK every day, but a deeper biologic response to a massive dose of starch and nothing else. Since the body NEEDS fat, it will strip it from fat stores in the body. Since potatoes are so satiating, it is nearly impossible to eat enough to slow the stripping of fat from our fat supply. I think I am looking at this from a 3000 ft view, not 30,000 as Jack is, but maybe not 30 like most people talking about potato diets today.

### Owl — 2012-11-15T05:58:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64416>

I tried the potato diet, and posted about it on MDA. The first day of eating nothing but potatoes and salt, I did pretty well. Wasn't hungry in between meals.

The next morning, I decided to measure blood glucose, with breakfast of about 1 lb potatoes with salt.

Fasting 87

1/2 hr. 136

1 hr. 181 !!!

2 hr. 94

That was too high for me, I stopped the experiment. I lost nothing from the one full day of the diet.

I normally eat about 75-100gm carbs/day, so I'm not super low carb. From what I've read, BG over 140 can damage nerves and organs. I don't want to do that to lose a little weight.

### hellojtm — 2012-11-15T07:13:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64423>

thanks for the clarification on the mechanism AKMan. Very interesting and possibly this is where Ray is going with it too, but I'm sure his participants have been doing it for months, not weeks, as I've seen on his facebook page he is showing someone losing a bit over 100lbs on a 7 month trajectory. For the love of .. .um.. a diety, I hope that person is eating more than just potatoes!!

maybe you can see this:

[https://www.facebook.com/photo.php?fbid=10151146531563697&set=a.10150186504768697.304120.658518696&type=1&theater](http://https://www.facebook.com/photo.php?fbid=10151146531563697&set=a.10150186504768697.304120.658518696&type=1&theater)

### Michael — 2012-11-15T09:50:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64429>

> **AKMan;66365 wrote:** I think the paleo blogosphere is onto something, but they don't know what. The so-called "Potato Diet" is lighting up all the forums and blogs from PaleoHacks to Free The Animal to Mark's Daily Apple.

Doesn't that speak volumes about the fatuity of the movement?

There's always some fad sweeping it.

Sometimes I feel that if I ever hear the word "paleo" again it will be too soon. Rather Epipaleo, WAPF, low carb, JERF, Mediterranean or _anything else other than paleo and its very patchy archaeological/anthropological knowledge and attendant idÃ©es fixe_ and fad-of-the-moment.

You can lose weight on virtually any regimen, however foolish (though not all are as efficient, particularly for some individuals). That doesn't make it a good idea to do so.

I think the paleo movement should get over the obsession with weight-loss (and body image). Abel James has had a couple of podcasts on the dangers of this recently. He's dead right.

### AKMan — 2012-11-15T17:58:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64505>

I didn't mean to imply anyone should try it, I was just wondering about the mechanisms behind it. The 'Potato Diet', while seeming simplistic, silly, and even dangerous at first glance, maybe warrants a further look.

It is all-starch, calorie restricting, (possibly) seasonal and ketogenic. Yes, ketogenic.

Cold Thermogenesis got the same treatment in the paleosphere: Get cold, burn calories, lose weight! But upon closer examination, we know there is way more to it than that.

### hellojtm — 2012-11-15T19:37:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64534>

agree with Michael! But then again, you can find what you are looking for, for example Art DeVany doesn't spend much time talking about weight loss. Also agree with AKMan that figuring out why something works, even if it's something you are not too concerned about, can be useful and informative. It just depends when you want to cash out on your learnings. If I had cashed out as soon as something worked for me it would have been 2 weeks after reading Why We Get Fat by Gary Taubes. And... I would have had a lot of wrong ideas that were reinforced because the approach worked for me but I didn't truly realize why at the time. So I didn't cash out and I'm happy for it.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-16T05:18:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64645>

a place to look for the health effects of long term potato only diet would be the 250 years prior to the Irish potato famine.

### Owl — 2012-11-16T05:24:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64647>

You can eat sweet potatoes, which are not nightshades. Ray Cronise prefers them. Re the Irish, this is a 1-2 week diet, not a lifelong plan.

### AKMan — 2012-11-16T06:42:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64660>

Lots of evidence you can live on potatoes for a long time, but not what this is about--I'm just trying to sort out in my mind if a short-term, high starch eating spree might trigger something primal that cues the brain to release a bunch of fat without hunger and maybe even 'resets' something.

Much like shortened daylight and cold cues a hibernation response, maybe a complete lack of exogenous fat and animal protein with ample starch triggers an 'anti-starvation' response that results in hyper-insulin sensitivity and release of ffa from adipose stores.

### AKMan — 2012-11-16T07:00:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-64661>

My "primal senses" tell me there's something going on in the areas of leptin, T3, SCN, and BG signalling...

Another gem from Peter at Hyperlipid:

"Without fat, bulk calories are stored as glycogen, excepting that there is a little de novo fat synthesis from glucose in the liver. Hepatic glycogen does not cause hepatic insulin resistance. In the near absence of FFA supply the liver maintains insulin sensitivity and the ability to degrade insulin. Nothing like as much insulin reaches the periphery as is produced by the pancreas in response to 2-3000kcal of potatoes.

The...effect of shutting down free fatty acid supply from adipocytes and diet is the loss of fatty acid intermediates in muscle. Insulin sensitivity increases, the amount of insulin needed to facilitate glucose uptake by muscles decreases. Insulin secretion from the pancreas will then decrease but hepatic extraction of insulin continues while ever carbohydrate adaption continues.

The ultimate determinant of weight loss is fasting insulin. This determines how much lipolysis occurs during the period before the next meal. No one expects to lose weight during the 4 hours immediately after any meal. The following 8 hours, especially overnight, is when weight loss occurs."

What if, instead of a big protein breakfast within 30 minutes of waking for leptin reset purposes, we instead, ate approx 2lbs of plainly prepared white potatoes for 2 weeks.

This would create a cellular environment that supports infinite insulin sensitivity at the liver and muscle level, and, in turn, supports leptin sensitivity at those same levels.

After 2 weeks of this, then switch immediately to a standard epi-paleo, big-ass breakfast within 30 minutes of waking. This will complete the trifecta of insulin sensitivity, leptin sensitivity, and circadian rewiring via SCN...with the added bonus of a loss of 5-10lbs!

I don't know how I would know that. I love sweet potatoes maybe I will try it for 2 days and see what happens. ### Stavity — 2012-11-17T14:44:38+0200

> **Nonchalant;66861 wrote:** Mee too, Stavity. And now you're making me wonder about my left ankle. It has given me big problems in the past, and occasional twinges now. I'll see if there is a correlation.

My left ankle seems to be the worst and the first one to swell. Please do look into it and you will never eat a white/purple potato again. NOt sure about anyone else, but one food I did abuse is the potato. I was the potato chip kid--oh and FF's, twice baked, mashed, cheesy, must I go on. Potatoes were the hardest food I ever had to give up. I still crave them, even knowing what I do today.

But, now I can walk without excruciating pain, and I do not have to feel embarrassed by limping. I am probably older than you, and being totally crippled was just around the corner for me because it just worsens with age. Since giving them up there is, absolutely, no swelling or pain. YOu know, if I relied on doctors, they would have had me on pain pills, and steroid shots or worse surgery that would have complicated things more because I still would have eaten potatoes.

We have an advantage over those who rely on drs and medicine to try and fix us. Instead we are determined to fix ourselves and take responsibility for learning our own bodies and what is good for us. Epi-paleo is for me and I notice good things happening all the time--have a long way to go because I didn't get to the shape I am in overnight.

### Glen PDQ — 2012-11-18T18:47:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-65160>

After X amount of days in ketosis, the muscles switch to using fat as fuel, and the brain switches from glucose to using ketones as fuel. On a potato only diet do the brain and muscles switch back to burning glucose as fuel? Or due to the available potato starches, does the brain switch back to burning glucose and the muscles begin using ketones? Do glycogen stores in the liver increase on a potato diet?

I remember about the Potato Council guy eating only potatoes. According to his statements it seemed his biggest problem was food boredom, not hunger.

### AKMan — 2012-11-19T05:51:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-potato-diet.4857/post-65271>

> **Glen PDQ;67165 wrote:** After X amount of days in ketosis, the muscles switch to using fat as fuel, and the brain switches from glucose to using ketones as fuel. On a potato only diet do the brain and muscles switch back to burning glucose as fuel? Or due to the available potato starches, does the brain switch back to burning glucose and the muscles begin using ketones? Do glycogen stores in the liver increase on a potato diet?
>
>
>
> I remember about the Potato Council guy eating only potatoes. According to his statements it seemed his biggest problem was food boredom, not hunger.

My impression is, if you are in ketosis to begin with, and you start eating nothing but potatoes, you'd remain in ketosis due to the calorie restricted nature of the diet. The glucose is quickly cleared by your supremely insulin sensitive liver and muscles and you return to burning free fatty acids and using ketones for brain fuel. If you totally pigged out on potatoes, you would probably revert back to using glucose for fuel, but from what I've seen, it is nearly impossible to eat potatoes at more than 50% your normal calorie intake.

If you are a sugar burner to begin with, an all-potato diet should put you in ketosis in a few days as you use up available glycogen from liver and muscles. Again, assuming you are eating at a huge calorie deficit, burning ffa as primary fuel.

All speculation on my part, based on minimal available research...

# Thread: who likes toast?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/who-likes-toast.4884/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-16T05:26:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/who-likes-toast.4884/post-64648>

Language reflects nutrition trends

"As we know, toast is browned bread. People began eating toast, or at least they began writing about it, in the 15th century. The first reference to it in print is in a recipe for a ghastly sounding concoction called Oyle Soppys (flavoured onions stewed in a gallon of stale beer and a pint of oil) from 1430.

Toast wasn't part of the recipe as such and to say that our mediaeval ancestors ate toast isn't quite correct. **Like the batter on fried fish and the pastry on Cornish pasties, which were originally just casings that were thrown away, toast was discarded rather than eaten** after it was used as a flavouring for drinks.

As well as being a flavouring, toast was used to warm drinks and most of the early citations refer to toast being warm or hot. Even as late as the 17th century people didn't eat toast but put it into drinks. Shakespeare gave this line to Falstaff in The Merry Wives of Windsor, 1616:

Go, fetch me a quart of Sacke, put a tost in 't.

\[In Shakespeare's day, as now, sack was fortified wine similar to sherry, so a quart seems a generous measure even for the notorious trencherman Sir John Falstaff.\]

It isn't difficult to imagine the scene in which some 18th century culinary innovator, having a piece of toast and a drink served together, decided to eat the toast rather than submerging it. Jonathan Swift appears to be the first to have recorded this novelty in print in the poem Panegyrick on Dean, 1735:

Sweeten your Tea, and watch your Toast.

The phrase 'the toast of the town' came about at the exclusively male drinking clubs of the early 18th century. The 'toast' was the woman who was regarded as the reigning belle of the season. The chaps were invited to flavour and heat their wine with hot spiced toasts and drink to 'the toast of the town'.

Later in the 1700s it became the norm for any celebrated person, male or female, to be applauded by a toast.

Coming more up to date the term toast has taken a 180 degree change of direction. To 'be toast' is now hardly a state to be desired. The usage 'you're toast' = 'you're as good as dead' derives from the 1984 film Ghostbusters. The scriptwriters wrote the line 'I'm gonna turn this guy into toast' but what Bill Murray, in his role as Dr. Peter Venkman, said was "This chick is toast". It is quite likely that the expression was US street slang that was taken up by the Ghostbusters' writers, but the film is what propelled it into the popular consciousness."


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### Michael — 2012-11-16T18:00:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybodys-dead-dave.4886/post-64748>

I'm sorry. I didn't mean that at all.

What I meant was that I thought the statement they printed there rang a bit of a warning bell for me:

> "If you look closely, you'll find that humor has very little to do with it \[laughter\] "

It seems to me that a special kind of response that involves humour is exactly what people _mean_ by laughter in ordinary speech. So if someone's saying "humor has very little to do with it" (i.e. laughter), they've actually re-defined laughter in a special sense of their own. I'd see that as a kind of sleight of hand.

But I'm sure it would be worth listening to. Thanks again for the link.

### N~Doro — 2012-11-16T19:14:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybodys-dead-dave.4886/post-64758>

> **Michael;66750 wrote:**
>
> What I meant was that I thought the statement they printed there rang a bit of a warning bell for me:
>
>
>
> It seems to me that a special kind of response that involves humour is exactly what people _mean by laughter in ordinary speech. So if someone's saying "humor has very little to do with it" (i.e. laughter), they've actually re-defined laughter in a special sense of their own. I'd see that as a kind of sleight of hand.
>
>
>
> _

_

No worries. Your radar is spot on. In general they are defining laughter in its sociological function of alleviating tension, mediating gender situations etc.

The \*infectious\* laughter clips are pretty enjoyable though - perhaps closer to what you refer to.

I think I am probably missing the subtlety of your distinctions. ie maybe we are talking about different things altogether.

After watching your clips again, this was probably the wrong thread to post in. I guess I just need to lighten up!

I added this book to my reading list a couple days ago so I guess humor has been on my mind lately.

Inside Jokes: Using Humor to Reverse-Engineer the Mind_, by Matthew M. Hurley, Daniel C. Dennett, Reginald B. Adams Jr.

[http://www.amazon.com/Inside-Jokes-Using-Humor-Reverse-Engineer/dp/026201582X/ref=sr\_1\_4?ie=UTF8&qid=1352842871&sr=8-4&keywords=dan+dennett](http://http://www.amazon.com/Inside-Jokes-Using-Humor-Reverse-Engineer/dp/026201582X/ref=sr_1_4?ie=UTF8&qid=1352842871&sr=8-4&keywords=dan+dennett)

### KiwiLauren — 2012-11-16T19:42:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybodys-dead-dave.4886/post-64763>

> **N~Doro;66730 wrote:** You may enjoy this episode of radiolab:
>
>
>
> [http://www.radiolab.org/2008/feb/25/](http://http://www.radiolab.org/2008/feb/25/)
>
>
>
> The rats being tickled is worth the listen alone.

I love Radio Lab... I listen from New Zealand via podcast. Did anyone hear the show where the examined what makes us alive? There was a case study of someone in a coma who was 'clinically dead' and they assumed was therefore not alive. He still had his sense of hearing, though, they determined and through experimentation in observing brain activity when spoken to, figured out that he was still having the full range of neurological responses as a 'normal' alive person. It was fascinating as it called into question what we know and don't know about the human brain and it's capacities even when all other body functions aren't working (except via the machines that allow them to go on).

### Michael — 2012-11-17T12:12:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybodys-dead-dave.4886/post-64911>

> **caroline;66909 wrote:** I did start watching - Mrs. Brown's Boys - I think that is the title. it is so funny! I came to it rather late tho - missed alot of episodes. I hope it comes back.

I don't know that one. I'll have to see if YouTube has got any clips.

You really should catch _Father Ted_ if you get a chance. It's screamingly funny. Father Ted is an Irish priest who's been sent by his diocese to a parish on a remote windswept island (Craggy Island) on account of financial irregularities - "The money was just resting in my account," he says. It's a painful exile for him. He lives in a clergy house with a dim-witted curate (played by Ardal O'Hanlon) and a drunken retired priest, who wakes up periodically to shout "DRINK!" or "GIRLS!" or a four-letter word.

There's an episode where, owing a series of improbable mishaps and misunderstandings, everyone on the island thinks Father Ted is a racist and "has it in for" the local Craggy Island Chinese community. Here's a short clip:

[http://www.channel4.com/programmes/father-ted/video/series-3/episode-1/bunker-mentality](http://http://www.channel4.com/programmes/father-ted/video/series-3/episode-1/bunker-mentality)

Here's Ardal O'Hanlon doing a stand-up routine:

[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wSnsqjUD6b0\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;wSnsqjUD6b0\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=wSnsqjUD6b0\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;wSnsqjUD6b0)

The second one - Adral O'Hanlon -- OMG - that is soooo funny. I can't wait to watch the other clips. Do you know - Does he ever come to Australia? haven't laughed that hard in a long time - thanks

> The second one - Adral O'Hanlon -- OMG - that is soooo funny. I can't wait to watch the other clips. Do you know - Does he ever come to Australia? haven't laughed that hard in a long time - thanks

Yeah, he's funny, isn't he? Apparently, the other bloke - the one that plays Ted - was absolutely hilarious. They say he wouldn't stop clowning on set. Most of these people would be sitting there between takes, keeping quiet and trying to stay "in character". But this man (Dermot Morgan) would go into a stand-up routine, or tell Ardal O'Hanlon that he was in love with him, or any one of a number of crazy things. Unfortunately, he died very suddenly and while still quite young, despite his grey hair.

I don't know where Ardal O'Hanlon tours. I had a look and found what seems to be his website. He hasn't got his own domain name - it's just some pages hosted at his internet service provider. It's also not going to work on the device I'm using at the moment. But he may have a tour itinerary posted there or a contact email where you could ask:

[http://myhome.iolfree.ie/~iamappalled/](http://http://myhome.iolfree.ie/~iamappalled/)

EDIT

Found this - he toured Australia in 2011:

[http://www.abc.net.au/commercial/events/content/event/Ardal.html](http://http://www.abc.net.au/commercial/events/content/event/Ardal.html)

### Michael — 2012-11-18T13:52:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/everybodys-dead-dave.4886/post-65113>

Thanks. I'll have a look.

I don't know whether anyone out there will enjoy this, and one really shouldn't encourage advertisers, but ... this is a really old advert, long thought lost, but now resurrected on YouTube. Would real food _need_ advertisements? Anyway, the old TV advertisement was selling potato crisps, and did so in a pretty wacky and entertaining manner.

A bearded man, dressed in black, who seems to be a witchfinder, bursts into a modern suburban dwelling, where a man, clad in a singlet, is lying on his sofa, watching telly, and eating the crisps. The witchfinder turns on him, hissing, "The food of recidivists" ...

\[video\]\[video=youtube\_share;m8hi5cLHKoM\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=m8hi5cLHKoM\[/video\]\[/video\]

Women are at the highest risk of depression during their childbearing years, and the birth of a child may trigger a depressive episode in vulnerable women. ...

[http://www.news-medical.net/news/20121116/Low-levels-of-omega-3-may-be-behind-postpartum-depression.aspx](http://http://www.news-medical.net/news/20121116/Low-levels-of-omega-3-may-be-behind-postpartum-depression.aspx)

# Thread: truth about paleo diet :-)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ruby — 2012-11-17T14:37:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-64919>

"Meat-eaters "easily cheat, lie, forget promises and commit sex crimes", according to a controversial school textbook available in India.

New Healthway, a book on hygiene and health aimed at 11 and 12 year-olds, is printed by one of India's leading publishers."

[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-india-20354669](http://http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/world-asia-india-20354669)

### Michael — 2012-11-17T14:53:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-64922>

Hindu POV (although not all Hindus are vegetarian, only high-caste ones, and Jainism is the only totally vegetarian religion).

My guess - FWIW and may be totally wrong - this was an urban civilisation early. If you've got a lot of people living in close proximity, life is easier if it's generally non-violent. However, that's going to generate a lot of emotion surrounding that and that emotion can get transferred onto the animal kingdom. Once that happens you've got problems, because humans aren't really built to eat just plants.

### ruby — 2012-11-17T15:31:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-64930>

An attempt to reinforce the taboo, perhaps, which BTW is nicely laid out in Marvin Harris' "Good to eat: riddles of food and culture".

The textbook suggests a Christian slant -- "The strongest argument that meat is not essential food is the fact that the Creator of this Universe did not include meat in the original diet for Adam and Eve. He gave them fruits, nuts and vegetables," reads a chapter entitled Do We Need Flesh Food?

### Michael — 2012-11-17T16:08:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-64934>

Oh, good grief, people want to give Christians hang ups about this, too? Peter's vision in Acts aside, I'd have thought making disciples of fishermen and eating a Passover lamb - not to mention a couple of thousand years of actual practice - would've settled it for _them_. Why people want to make problems for themselves needlessly ...

Still, it is interesting that whatever editor had re-worked the traditional material in Judaism (at a pretty late date, I should think) had a bad conscience about eating animal food. Maybe it's the fate of many urban societies for the reason I suggested?

Plato has the original human population living off acorns, and the Stoics were outright vegetarians.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-17T19:47:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-64968>

> **Michael;66937 wrote:** Plato has the original human population living off acorns, and the Stoics were outright vegetarians.

your reading of Stoic philosophy must be limited to Seneca. He was a notable exception.

Stoics were emphatically anti-vegetarian,

### Michael — 2012-11-17T20:51:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-64982>

How would you know what I've read? And I'm not interested in disputing the point.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-17T20:59:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-64986>

I don't "know" what you have read.

I made the assumption based on your statement of fact "Stoics were outright vegetarians"

As this is not only incorrect, but in direct opposition to basic tenets of the philosophy, the only way I could understand you having come to this conclusion is if the exposure you had to Stoic philosophy was limited to reading Seneca's works, as he was a notable exception.

does this adequately answer your question?

P.S. if this is not the case, and you have material that says otherwise, I'd be very interested in reading it.

I'm always interested in expanding my knowledge base.

### Michael — 2012-11-17T21:37:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-64990>

Are you still here?

I have no "question".

I thought you wanted to dispute about what I'd read. Or was it what colour socks I'm wearing?

Can you believe I really don't _care_ whether Epictetus said, "Dine off bread and figs," or, "Vegetarians should be hung from the city wall by their thumbs"?

Substitute some other group of people in the original sentence, if it pleases you.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-17T21:46:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-64995>

I'm afraid I do not understand your hostility Michael. as a practising Stoic, I take interest in discussions of the philosophy.

not so much your socks colours.

Your "question" was " how do you know what I have read" and i attempted to answer it as succinctly as possible. I am open to being wrong.

If I substitute another group of people in the original sentence... then I change the intent of the sentence, no?

ie. Stoics were outright vegetarians. becomes Carnivores were outright vegetarians. or Vegetarians were outright vegetarians.

Am I missing some point in your logic here? you seem to be quite intelligent, but I can't follow this line of reasoning at all.

I mean no ill will.

If my comments are unwelcome, please feel free to ignore me.

regards.

“Choose not to be harmed and you won’t feel harmed. Don’t feel harmed and you haven’t been.”

\-Marcus Aurelius

### ruby — 2012-11-18T03:46:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/truth-about-paleo-diet.4902/post-65074>

ha, ha, ha, Entelechy, you, as a meat eater (Stoics - Seneca = meat eaters if I understand correctly?) have just managed to disprove the tenets of this Indian manual  well done

# Thread: Medical Doctor Fired For Speaking Anti-ObamaCare... Shocking
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/medical-doctor-fired-for-speaking-anti-obamacare-shocking.4915/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-18T03:50:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/medical-doctor-fired-for-speaking-anti-obamacare-shocking.4915/post-65078>

.

Medical doctors be careful if you speak out against ObamaCare... One doctor found out the hard way - she came into work and was told she was fired! Rated as one of the top docs at the hospital where she worked and gave lectures.

The Hospital stated employees are not allowed to speak out against ObamaCare. Dr Beller, who was fired, just so happen to have been the President of the hospital's own ethics board. Dr Beller is a former Nun, Attorney, held the rank of Major in the Army, and owned a small successful business. Wow!  you go girl...

[http://www.ijreview.com/2012/11/22553-doctor-terminated-for-posting-viral-anti-obamacare-rant-on-youtube/](http://http://www.ijreview.com/2012/11/22553-doctor-terminated-for-posting-viral-anti-obamacare-rant-on-youtube/)

**Dr. Barbara Bellar** joins the ranks of the unemployed under President Obama, as she has now lost her job for posting a viral anti-Obamacare rant on YouTube. The video garnered nearly 3.3 million views.

### Entelechy — 2012-11-19T02:35:07+0200

Darleen, I don't get excited. This is just bemused curiosity on my part as a disinterested observer.

Why you would think I don't research before I post, I don't know. I never post without an interest in the subject, and as such if I have an interest I've researched it. Every point in that "humourous" clip was something I have come across before.

Out of respect for your opinion, (thinking there may be some angle I might have missed) I've read the articles you posted., two blogs and a newspaper clipping. what a disappointment.

being as anyone can write a blog, and it doesn't mean there is a grain of truth in it...I looked at the nwherald piece first.

**Forced to purchase, fined if we don't.

H_ealth care experts say this is correct, but if you already have insurance you won't be forced to buy it. That is the rebuttle? seriously? if you are already doing what we want you to do... we won't force you to do it. _So the video's claim is "true to some degree," said David Howard, point to Beller.

**at least 10 million more people, no new doctors the herald says this is bs because the number is wrong, it's 30 million. I guess they missed the "at least" part of the sentence, and did leave it out in their article. This just bolsters Bellers point. the article then goes on to point out that the 30 million people won't be a new burden on the health care system, because _they get some emergency room care now, and the cost is borne by other people. I think it's apparent that the are not intending to recieve LESS care under the new plan. point to Beller, albeit her math was't pessimistic enough.

**no new doctors. .._.The Kaiser Family Foundation estimates it will add 15,000 new providers by 2015, but there is nothing in place to ensure this, other than they intend to 'bribe' doctors who _go into primary care with larger Medicare payments, with further incentives to get them to go to underserved areas. Yeah that sounds cheap. although eventually they will be forced to _volunteer_ if they want to even get accepted into medical school.

then even after rebuttle it says still, expanding coverage will put some new demands on the health care system._ so point to Beller. and

predicts lots of doctors will retire and lines will lengthen at emergency rooms and doctors' offices as people seek more care._ extra credit to Beller for her point above.

• 16,000 IRS agents**.... Factcheck.org calls this claim "wildly inaccurate." the critics assumed the added cost would only go to salaries meaning 16,500 new employees. Then some people began calling all those employees "agents," overlooking any need for auditors, secretaries, computer programmers and other types of staff._ So point to Obama. They are not all 'agents' being added, but the money is being spent nonetheless.. Beller should have just given the cash number, rather than attempting to identify where it was being spent, as that is the salient point anyway.

• Committee chairman doesn't understan**d. Sen. Max Baucus, D-Mont., said that he hadn't read the actual legislation because it was written in technical language that he and most other senators wouldn't be able to understand._ so... is't that what Beller said? Goldsmith was disappointed that Baucus didn't wade through the bill himself._ this was a point in the sentence, hereby further validated.

• Congress exempted.** this is untrue now, hence the reference to "out of context points" I made in my post. Congress are now _" specifically required to buy their insurance through the exchanges created by the Affordable Care Act." The original vote EVERY Democrat voted to reject'[vote](http://http://www.senate.gov/legislative/LIS/roll_call_lists/roll_call_vote_cfm.cfm?congress=111&session=2&vote=00071)' so for absolute accuracy, Bellers sentence should have read "WANTED to vote themselves out of"

• **Four years of taxes before any benefits** the listed benefits that are kicking in early are listed as _"The pre-2014 benefits include letting people stay on their parents' insurance plan until age 26 and giving tax credits to some small businesses. Senior citizens are getting money to fill the "doughnut hole," a gap in their Medicare drug coverage. Coverage is available for people denied normal health insurance because of pre-existing conditions. none of these are substantial 'benefits' by any definition.. even in the rebuttle, Howard said such changes are "pretty small potatoes"_ and Goldsmith said they were basically regulatory changes._

no other points were addressed in the article, so I will assume Obama does smoke, as well as the rest, whether they are relevant or not are ceded by the author(s)

full of BS? I don't think so,

The other blogs just repeat the same points, which as you can see are incredibly weak attacks not even addressing the intent of the points made.

as it really bothers you that people post crap they have NOT researched and expect everyone around them to swallow it hook, line and sinker, I would suggest you at least read for yourself the content of what you are posting before you post it.

regards

p.s. to the greater point..........agreed or not.

# Thread: kidneys
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### chocolate — 2012-11-18T16:50:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/post-65132>

[http://www.hindawi.com/journals/ijn/2011/315879/](http://http://www.hindawi.com/journals/ijn/2011/315879/)

The Impact of Fructose on Renal Function and Blood Pressure

> Fructose intake can also induce a central leptin resistance in **rats, leading to increased food intake and the development of visceral obesity \[35\]. Interestingly, rats on a high-fructose diet may not show an increase in overall body weight unless it is associated with diets high in fat \[36\]
>
>
>
> > 2.4. Hypertension and Vascular EffectsFructose intake from added sugars is also associated with elevated blood pressure in humans \[37\], and diets low in added sugar have been reported to lower blood pressure \[38\]. Furthermore, the acute ingestion of fructose (60 g) can increase systolic blood pressure in humans, and this is not seen in subjects given the same dose as glucose \[39\]. In addition, in one study, overweight men were administered 200 g fructose daily for two weeks and sustained a significant increase in ambulatory blood pressure \[26\]. Studies in experimental animals have confirmed that fructose can raise blood pressure. **Interestingly, the increase in blood pressure in response to fructose is better observed by tail cuff measurement as opposed to intra-aortic telemetry \[40, 41\]. However, blood pressure rises in response to sucrose or fructose diet by telemetry during the first hours of feeding \[12, 42\]. The mechanism of hypertension in response to fructose is complex but appears to be mediated by increased sodium absorption in the intestine, by inhibition of systemic endothelial function, and by stimulation of the sympathetic nervous system \[14, 43, 44\] (Figure 1). In addition, some of the effect of fructose to increase blood pressure may be the consequence of fructose-induced increases in intracellular and serum uric acid. First, fructose-induced hypertension in rats is largely ameliorated by lowering uric acid levels \[40\]. Second, in one study in overweight men, the rise in ambulatory blood pressure in response to 200 g of oral fructose per day for two weeks was blocked in those subjects concomitantly administered allopurinol \[26\].
> >
> >
> >
> >
> > > The administration of fructose to rats with reduced renal function (the remnant kidney model) can accelerate the progression of renal disease, resulting in worse proteinuria, glomerulosclerosis, and tubulointerstitial fibrosis \[47\]. Fructose intake also impairs calcium absorption and reduces 25-OH Vitamin D and 1,25-dihydroxy Vitamin D levels in this model
> >
> > **
> >
> > > \[48\]. Furthermore, the intake of sugary soft drinks in humans is associated with increased prevalence of albuminuria \[49\]. Our group has also recently administered a low-fructose diet to subjects with stable chronic kidney disease for a period of 6 weeks. While we observed no effect on renal function during the period of the study, we did observe a reduction in inflammatory markers and a fall in blood pressure in subjects with the “dipper” physiology (i.e., those subjects whose blood pressure spontaneously falls at night during sleep)**
>
> > > \[49\]. Clearly further studies are needed to determine if limiting added sugars may benefit subjects with kidney disease.
> >
> >
> >
> > 2.6. Role of Natural FruitsWhile much work has focused on fructose as driving obesity, insulin resistance, and cardiorenal disease, not all fructose sources may be the same. Thus, natural fruits also are rich in antioxidants, ascorbate, polyphenols, potassium, and fiber that may counter the effects of fructose \[13, 50\]. Indeed, Forman et al. \[51\] reported that fructose intake did not correlate with elevated blood pressure in a population in which much of the fructose intake was from fruit, whereas Jalal et al. \[37\] found a strong association of fructose intake with blood pressure when the fructose content from natural fruits was excluded.
>
>
>
>
>
>
>
> Fructose effects are also potentiated by glucose \[61, 62\], and most studies have only examined fructose alone. Most studies also show, both in animals and humans, that the effects of fructose are greater on postprandial lipids, fatty liver, and insulin resistance rather than on weight gain per se. Indeed, the effects of fructose may be more on inducing leptin resistance, and it may require the addition of high-fat diet to show the weight gain

### chocolate — 2012-11-18T17:16:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/post-65136>

> Dietary fructose, salt absorption and hypertension in metabolic syndrome: towards a new paradigm.
>
> Soleimani M.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Center on Genetics of Transport and Epithelial Biology, Department of Medicine, University of Cincinnati, OH 45267-0585, USA. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> The worldwide increase in the incidence of metabolic syndrome correlates with marked increase in total fructose intake in the form of high-fructose corn syrup, beverage and table sugar. **Increased dietary fructose intake in rodents has been shown to recapitulate many aspects of metabolic syndrome by causing hypertension, insulin resistance and hyperlipidaemia. Recent studies demonstrated that increased dietary fructose intake stimulates salt absorption in the small intestine and kidney tubules, resulting in a state of salt overload and thus causing hypertension. The absorption of salt (sodium and chloride) in the small intestine is predominantly mediated via the chloride/base exchangers DRA (Down Regulated in Adenoma) (SLC26A3) and PAT1 (Putative Anion Transporter 1) (SLC26A6), and the Na(+) /H(+) exchanger NHE3 (Sodium Hydrogen Exchanger3) (SLC9A3). PAT1 and NHE3 also co-localize on the apical membrane of kidney proximal tubule. Luminal fructose stimulated salt absorption in the jejunum and kidney tubules, responses that were significantly diminished in PAT1 null mice. These studies further demonstrated that Glut5 (SLC2A5) is the major fructose-absorbing transporter in the small intestine (and kidney proximal tubule) and plays an essential role in the systemic homeostasis of fructose. Increased dietary fructose intake for several weeks upregulated the expression of NHE3, PAT1 and Glut5 in the intestine and resulted in hypertension in wild-type mice, a response that was almost abolished in PAT1 null mice and abrogated in Glut5 null mice. This article will discuss the interaction of Glut5 with salt-absorbing transporters and review the role of dietary fructose in enhanced salt absorption in intestine and kidney as it relates to the pathogenesis of hypertension in metabolic syndrome.**

pretty interesting

### chocolate — 2012-11-18T17:25:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/post-65138>

> Fructose consumption and the risk of kidney stones.
>
> Taylor EN, Curhan GC.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Renal Division and Channing Laboratory, Department of Medicine, Brigham and Women's Hospital, Harvard Medical School, Boston, Massachusetts 02115, USA. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])
>
> Abstract
>
>
>
> Fructose consumption has markedly increased over the past decades. This intake may increase the urinary excretion of calcium, oxalate, uric acid, and other factors associated with kidney stone risk. We prospectively examined the relationship between fructose intake and incident kidney stones in the Nurses' Health Study I (NHS I) (93,730 older women), the Nurses' Health Study II (NHS II) (101,824 younger women), and the Health Professionals Follow-up Study (45,984 men). Food frequency questionnaires were used to assess free fructose and sucrose intake every 4 years. Total-fructose intake was calculated as free fructose plus half the intake of sucrose, and expressed as percentage of total energy. Cox proportional hazard regressions were adjusted for age, body mass index (BMI), thiazide use, caloric intake, and other dietary factors. We documented 4902 incident kidney stones during a combined 48 years of follow-up. **The multivariate relative risks of kidney stones significantly increased for participants in the highest compared to the lowest quintile of total-fructose intake for all three study groups. Free-fructose intake was also associated with increased risk. Non-fructose carbohydrates were not associated with increased risk in any cohort. Our study suggests that fructose intake is independently associated with an increased risk of incident kidney stones.**

Fructose is just not so good. I had to quit eating apples.

### chocolate — 2012-11-18T17:30:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kidneys.4922/post-65139>

> Characteristic clinical and biochemical profile of recurrent calcium-oxalate nephrolithiasis in patients with metabolic syndrome.
>
> Rendina D, De Filippo G, Zampa G, Muscariello R, Mossetti G, Strazzullo P.
>
> Source
>
>
>
> Departament of Radiology, Hospital Universitario Insular de Gran Canaria, Spain. [[email redacted]](mailto:[email redacted])
>
> Abstract
>
> BACKGROUND:
>
>
>
> Metabolic syndrome is a risk factor for nephrolithiasis. This study was performed to evaluate the clinical and biochemical profile of calcium-oxalate nephrolithiasis in stone formers with metabolic syndrome.
>
> METHODS:
>
>
>
> A total of 526 recurrent stone formers, 184 of them with metabolic syndrome, and 214 controls were examined on a free diet and after a sodium-restricted diet (sodium intake < 100 mmol/24 h).
>
> RESULTS:
>
>
>
> On free diet, stone formers with metabolic syndrome showed higher sodium excretion \[mean (95% confidence interval), 196 (176-218) vs 160 (150-168) mmol/24 h; P < 0.01\] and lower citrate excretion \[2.23 (1.99-2.58) vs 2.84 (2.51-3.17) mmol/24 h; P < 0.01\] compared to controls, whereas stone formers without metabolic syndrome showed higher calcium and oxalate excretion \[5.43 (5.01-5.82) vs 3.58 (2.84-4.19) and 0.34 (0.32-0.36) vs 0.26 (0.20-0.31)m mmol/24 h for calcium and oxalate, respectively; P < 0.01\] and lower citrate excretion \[2.18 (1.98-2.38) vs 2.84 (2.51-3.17) mmol/24 h; P < 0.01\] compared to controls. The ion activity product of urinary calcium-oxalate salts was similar between stone formers with and without metabolic syndrome \[1.41 (1.31-1.59) vs 1.40 (1.35-1.45); P > 0.05\]. After the test diet, this index was lower in diet-compliant stone formers with metabolic syndrome compared to diet-compliant stone formers without metabolic syndrome \[1.15 (1.10-1.21) vs 1.39 (1.31-1.45); P < 0.01\].
>
> CONCLUSIONS:
>
>
>
> **The biochemical profiles and responses to the sodium-restricted diet were significantly different between stone formers with metabolic syndrome and those without. Dietary habits play a central role in the pathogenesis of nephrolithiasis in stone formers with metabolic syndrome.**

Here, they are blaming the salt instead of the fructose.

It stops the glucose, which isn't fructose exactly, but maybe it stops that, too. I'll just put up with the boobs. Its only for a few months.

The take away I get from all of these articles in regards to fat is that fat won't make you fat unless you eat fructose with it. And fructose won't make you fat unless you eat fat with it. I'm physically not able to do both. Freaky things happen.

### PrimalPam — 2012-11-18T17:01:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-without-twinkies.4923/post-65133>

Is anyone else bothered by the amount of media attention this is getting? My FB page has been blowing up all week. Executive chefs are posting Twinkie recipes. The news stations seem to have a story every newscast about empty shelves; sellers on Ebay are getting $100 a box.

This was a business. The union said no to compromise and the company said, well fine, then we have to close. Don't want to negotiate? Then you don't have a job, 'cause we just can't afford it anymore.

I think the bigger issue is why....did their healthcare costs rise? Did their manufacturing costs rise? Did they anticipate higher costs and realized they just couldn't keep up? What does this say about unions and the power they have over American companies?

No one seems to care about the reasons WHY this business failed.

All they want is their d\*mn Twinkies. I never liked 'em anyway. Now Ding Dongs, that's a whole 'nother story.

### Michael — 2012-11-18T17:15:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-without-twinkies.4923/post-65135>

It's a kind of mini sponge cake or swiss roll or something, isn't it? Wasn't the policeman was eating them in _Die Hard_?

I've never seen one. I'm sure we have things as bad round here.

I saw a poster for some product that consisted of two biscuits (crackers) with a processed cheese filling a couple of days ago, and the poster said it was "all natural". Depends how you define "natural" I suppose. I guess if it is "naturalness" is an irrelevant criterion when it comes to food choice.

### Lyndra — 2012-11-18T18:09:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-without-twinkies.4923/post-65150>

Definitely the nostalgia thing. Those of us who grew up in the 60s and 70s found Hostess products in our lunch boxes quite often. I was more fond of Suzy Q's rather than twinkies. I had a twinkie a few years ago when I saw them in a convenience store at the end of a long ride. I was disappointed - it didn't taste anywhere near as good as I had remembered. Kind of stale and icky. Either my tastes changed or quality went downhill (or maybe both?).

Much of the commentary I've heard about the decline of the business focused on their inability to adapt to changing consumer tastes & preferences. I think they could have capitalized on parents' fond memories while innovating and come up with a product that was perceived to be more healthy. Instead, they were stuck in a time warp, making the same old product for years on end.

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-19T02:15:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-without-twinkies.4923/post-65241>

I copied the following post from another forum I sometimes check in on. I figured if I put in the link no one would bother to read it:

> Hostess Brands/Interstate Brands Business Practices from a person who lived it (My mom)
>
>
>
> From 2001 to 2004, my mom worked for Interstate Brands (aka Hostess Brands) at their facility on Route 23 in Wayne, NJ. My mom like many of her co-workers were used as part-time on call employees. She was promised regular hours and full time status but never received such. Instead my dad, my brother, or I would take my mom to work at 2 am or 4 pm depending on when they called her in.
>
>
>
> My mother explained that she and her fellow employees were expected to transfer hot cakes as they came out on assembly line from the ovens with gloved hands onto other smaller lines where the cakes were to be shrink-wrapped by automatic machines. Bad cakes were to be discarded and a high quality product was to be wrapped up and delivered to happy consumers. Such was not the case.
>
>
>
> My mom, having worked in quality control position before, knew something was amiss the first week she began working there. She shook her head and realized this company would not last, not with what they were doing on the production lines.
>
>
>
> Her and her group working a specific line (be it Twinkies, Yodels, Apple Pies, or HoHos) could transfer 300-600 cakes a minute onto the packing lines, but the baking machines were set to 1,000 cakes a minute leading to incredible waste. My mom and her workers tried hard as possible to keep up with his hectic pace but to no avail, 100's of cakes were lost a minute. You would think that someone would find an optimal speed to let the ovens produce just enough that workers can quality check the product and package at a comfortable pace. But not here.
>
>
>
> My mom described at the end of her shift, she would find 10 large bins (5'x10'x5') packed full with cakes on her line. Employees were encourage to consume as much as possible at work but not to take any home.
>
>
>
> Management looked the other way when it came to waste and when my mom and fellow union workers complained about aches and pains resulting from this rapid, ridiculous pace, the machines were slowed down for the union inspection and then ramped up several days later.
>
>
>
> Production mattered more than anything, including employee well being, waste of raw material, and quality of product. The more the managers produced and packed, never mind the waste or employee injuries/burns, the more managers were rewarded.
>
>
>
> My mom would often come home and consume Tylenol and painkillers to ease the pain. She was also injured twice on the job, a severe high ankle sprain and tearing of the muscles on her right hand and thumb which still bother her to this day and limit mobility.
>
>
>
> In my opinion, this corporation knowingly and willfully drove this company into the ground on the backs of their workers and in spite of worker protest. Having set the building ablaze the robber-barons/arsonists are grabbing as many valuables as they can, all the while screaming fire, fire and pointing fingers, as they run out of building first leaving others to fend for themselves.

Again, this is not MY post, I simply copied it from another forum I belong to to give another perspective.

### chocolate — 2012-11-19T04:28:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/life-without-twinkies.4923/post-65259>

> Her and her group working a specific line (be it Twinkies, Yodels, Apple Pies, or HoHos) could transfer 300-600 cakes a minute onto the packing lines, but the baking machines were set to 1,000 cakes a minute leading to incredible waste.

I don't know if the spent cakes were fed to pigs to finish them or not, but that's what happens with the bread pulls at grocery stores. (It makes them sick to raise them on it. They start peeing weird and get stiff joints and can hardly walk)

I also worked as a cake decorator and would do pulls while the first batch was in the oven in a small grocery store and the donut fryer was working. The bread was to go into a locked dumpster but I handed it off to a well recognized character actor that fed street people and birds with it years ago. The stuff is garbage and we all know it. You can feed broilers the stuff, also. They are cruddy quality meat, but... you are what you eat.

.

If this doesn't knock your socks off nothing will. America is importing food that is so radioactive it's been marked "unfit for human consumption - do not eat". I'm beyond words. This story will never make the local news or newspapers. Now more than ever it's a matter of life & death to make sure you know your food. Even cattle, fish, chickens and other sources of food in the US; is radioactive after Japan's Nuclear Fallout Cloud hit.

US is importing food from Japan thatâ€™s considered unfit to eat there

Americans are the NUCLEAR DUMP for the world...which includes your stomach.

We learn that Japan is selling the contaminated foods to their dear friends in the USA. Once again, Americans are paying for the privilege of recycling waste products through their bodies. IS THAT EVIL? Yep.

At 4:30 in the video - [http://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player\_embedded&v=xkXt93-Qghw\[/video\]\]\[video=youtube\_share;xkXt93-Qghw\]http://www.youtube.com/watch?feature=player\_embedded&v=xkXt93-Qghw\[/video\]](http://[video=youtube_share;xkXt93-Qghw)
