### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T21:32:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/us-imports-radioactive-food-unfit-to-eat.4925/post-65199>

just some perspective on the numbers, as North America normally uses REM instead of Bq, both measurements are derived to represent actual ionization damage, instead of total 'energy'. The idea being you only care about the energy that gets deposited in the body.

A Becquerel is a very tiny measurement. One banana has about 15 Bq due mostly from potassium40,

Brazil nuts are higher due to radium, at about 200Bq per pound.

What isn't fully taken into account with Bq or REM is the 'biological half-life" or the human body's constant homeostasis, any excess absorbed compensated by the elimination of an equal amount. The faster turn over in the body, the reduced Biological Half life, and reduced dose.

From Fukashima, the greatest risk would be in radioactive Iodine, for two reasons. One, iodine is a major fission by product, and makes up a large component of the total releases from the fuel failures, secondly.. as most people are deficient in iodine,it tends to be readily absorbed and concentrated in the thyroid.

This is why the KI (potassium Iodide) pills where in such high demand at the time. the idea is to top yourself up with 'clean' iodine, to reduce the uptake of potentially contaminated environmental iodine.

### Thor — 2012-11-19T00:14:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/us-imports-radioactive-food-unfit-to-eat.4925/post-65220>

.

KI kills your thryoid

### Thor — 2012-11-19T00:22:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/us-imports-radioactive-food-unfit-to-eat.4925/post-65225>

.

Actually this is serious - several 100 fish caught in August 2012 bore the highest levels of radioactive particles seen to date—25,000 Becquerels per kilogram! that's scary....

Radioactive cesium (what's in the fish) decays by emitting what's known as a beta radiation, a negatively charged particle that is easily blocked by metal, plastic or wood—but not skin. In particular, ingesting beta-emitting radioactive elements is "a concern," according to the NRC. "Beta particles released directly to living tissue can cause damage at the molecular level, which can disrupt cell function." Plus, beta particles are small enough to travel far in the body, causing damage far and wide.

Radioactive cesium Inhibits potassium current in myocardial cells, increases intracellular pH may affect erythrocytes, liver, and central nervous system.

Symptoms of Cesium Toxicity:

Fatigue, muscle weakness, palpitations and arrhythmia

_Therapeutic Drug Monitoring. 2003 Feb;25(1):114-6_

# Thread: How Insects Domesticate Bacteria
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-insects-domesticate-bacteria.4928/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-18T21:10:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-insects-domesticate-bacteria.4928/post-65194>

> Two years ago, a 71-year-old Indiana man impaled his hand on a branch after cutting down a dead crab apple tree, causing an infection that led University of Utah scientists to discover a new bacterium and solve a mystery about how bacteria came to live inside insects. ...

# Thread: Prostate - PSA and Cancer
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 14
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2012-11-18T21:52:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/post-65203>

I'm one of those guys whose age and family history makes me a likely candidate to get prostate cancer. I had a biopsy at age 53 that was negative, but have had some additional spikes in PSA the last few years, plus BPH with resulting urinary problems. In all cases, treatment with antibiotics relieved BPH symptoms and lowered PSA.

But I need a better alternative to antibiotics because when I take them, they kill off gut flora and I start having cravings again and weight loss stops. I tried colloidal silver instead of antibiotics and have been gaining weight.

I have listened to Jack's Prostate Webinar which was very informative. I was already taking at least half of the recommended supplements and ordered several additional ones mentioned in the webinar. Maybe that will be enough, maybe not.

I do still have reservations about not getting a PSA exam. I don't trust a government sponsored committee (USPSTF) to make medical decisions for me. As I understand the argument, even if an older man has prostate cancer, something else will most likely kill him before the cancer does. At 63, I probably have about 20 years still left in me. If cancer is detected when I'm 65, I don't want the government telling me that they no longer pay for treatment, which is what I think this recommendation by USPSTF is leading up to.

The American Urological Association (AUA) has rejected the USPSTF recommendation against PSA testing. Here's the statement from AUA Presdent Suhil S. Lacy, MD.

The AUA (American Urological Association) is outraged at the USPSTFâ€™s failure to amend its recommendations on prostate cancer testing to more adequately reflect the benefits of the prostate- specific antigen (PSA) test in the diagnosis of prostate cancer.

It is inappropriate and irresponsible to issue a blanket statement against PSA testing, particularly for at-risk

populations, such as African American men. Men who are in good health and have more than a 10-15 year life expectancy should have the choice to be tested and not discouraged from doing so.

There is strong evidence that PSA testing saves lives. The randomized trials used by the USPSTF do, in fact, show a benefit to patients. The PLCO Trial, imperfect by the pre-screening contamination of the control arm, nonetheless showed that, in a group of young men with no comorbidities, there was a significant reduction of prostate cancer death rates after a median follow- up of seven years (JCO 2011;29:355-361).

Additionally, the Göteborg Trial also showed a substantial 44 percent relative risk reduction in prostate cancer mortality occurring in men 50-64 years of age after a median of 14 years.

Importantly, the risk reduction occurred in a setting where many of the patients were not aggressively treated for prostate cancer, indicating that the harms of PSA-based screening can, in fact, be minimized by good clinical practice (Lancet Oncol 2010;11:725-732).

Furthermore, we have seen a 40 percent reduction in prostate cancer- specific mortality in the United States over the most recent 20 years of PSA-based screening. This has occurred without substantial change in how men with prostate cancer were treated (primarily with surgery and radiation therapy).

Models have suggested that more than 50 percent of this reduction is due to early detection (Cancer Cases Control 2008;19:175-181). Additionally, updated data from the European Randomized Study for the Screening of Prostate Cancer (ERSPC) has demonstrated that there is a 21 percent risk reduction in prostate cancer related death associated with screening (up to 29 percent after accounting for non-compliance). The number of cancers that would need to be detected to prevent one death has now dropped to 37.

Rather than instruct primary care physicians to discourage men from having a PSA test, the Task Force should instead focus on how best to educate primary care physicians regarding targeted screening and how to counsel patients about their prostate cancer risk.

The PSA test has allowed us to move beyond a time when men presented with high-grade, metastatic disease for which there were little or no treatment options other than palliative care.

In its earliest stages, most prostate cancers cause no symptoms; to say that only men with symptoms of prostate cancer should be tested will potentially result in a return to such a time.

Disparaging the PSA test when newer tests and diagnostics are not yet widely available does a great disservice to American men.[http://epubs.democratprinting.com/publication/?i=116007](http://http://epubs.democratprinting.com/publication/?i=116007)

The majority of the (Summer/Fall, 2012) QUEST, published by [Dr William J Catalona](http://http://www.drcatalona.com/) and the Urological Research Foundation, addresses the problems with the USPSTF recommendation. The article on p 8 discusses the use of PSA, % free PSA, and Phi score for detecting prostate cancer. According to this article, the diagnostic accuracy of Total PSA is 55%, % Free PSA is 65% and Phi is about 75%. Phi hasn't been approved by FDA in US, but is approved in Europe.

I found the % Free PSA lab earlier this year when I was looking for the best price for a PSA lab. It wasn't that much more expensive so I bought it and was glad I did. My PSA was elevated, but my % Free PSA indicated I had less than a 10% chance of cancer and the high PSA was most likely from inflammation.

So I'm questioning everything. The AUA makes a good case for dismissing the findings of the USPSTF. On the other hand, I guess the income of some members of the AUA would be effected if the PSA test is not used as a diagnostic tool.

For me, I will continue to have a yearly PSA including %Free and Phi when it becomes available. Even a biopsy, although not a fun day at the beach, is not that bad. When I had mine done, the doc used Novocain to dull the pain. He did it in his office and I drove home about a half hour after it was over. So PSA and biopsy if necessary.

The problem for me is choosing what to do if biopsy shows cancer. My preferred treatment, if it should become necessary, is proton beam treatment, something rarely presented as an option. Quite expensive, but it is painless and seems to have the smallest incidence of side effects. More info on proton beam can be found [here](http://http://www.protonbob.com/proton-treatment-homepage.asp).

Thoughts? Any other "older" guys going through this? If so, what is your plan? If you already met the robot, what were the side effects?

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-18T23:36:50+0200

The PSA is a 33-kDalton serine protease inhibitor made by the Ascomycete fungi, Aspergillus flavus, Aspergillus fumigatus, Aspergillus oryzae, Ophiostoma piceae, and Scedosporium apiospermum. An elevated PSA is seen not only in men with prostatic disease, but also in women with breast, ovarian, pancreatic, and colon cancer; and even in women during pregnancy.

The antifungal drug, NizoralÂ® (ketoconazole) lowered PSA levels in men with prostate cancer

high PSA signals a fungal infection gone awry, then the antifungal effect of ketoconazole should be the obvious reason for the lowering of the PSA.

### Thor — 2012-11-19T00:24:42+0200

### colleencoble — 2012-11-19T04:37:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/post-65260>

A routine PSA caught the huge spike in my husband's PSA (from 1.5 to 9) and a biopsy showed VERY aggressive prostate cancer. By the time he had surgery it had moved from the prostate to the seminal vesicles. Right now he's cancer free but there is a 30% chance it could come back. I tell every guy I know to make sure they have a baseline and get tested. It could save their life. Yes, it can go up for something other than cancer just like the CA-125 can go up for things other than ovarian cancer. But it's the best screening tool we have right now. At least you can get tested further to find out what is the cause. And please, the biopsy is not that bad. I was with Dave in the room when he got his and he didn't even wince. We girls get poked, prodded and squashed every year.

I will never ever quit urging guys to get checked. I lost a dear friend to prostate cancer the same year Dave was diagnosed. When it strikes younger men, it is deadly.

### Thor — 2012-11-19T07:28:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/prostate-psa-and-cancer.4930/post-65280>

.

Colleencoble - did you read my post above? PSA test has nothing to do with cancer. ugh  sad that doctors brain wash people.....

Even if you have cancer there is only a 25% or less chance the biopsy will find it and if the needle does catch cancer cells...there is something you should ask your urologist about:

**"NEEDLE TRACK SEEDING".**

It will be a VERY uncomfortable subject for the doc...since there is a considerable chance the biopsy needle will drag cancer cells out of the prostatic capsule and "seed" them into the rest of your body. Consider the information that follows:

"In The New England Journal of medicine Dr. Fritz Schroder and Dr. Ries Krause from the Erasmus Medical Center in Rotterdam, Netherlands were asked if prostate biopsies really save lives. Their answer was, "No."

Only 1 out 4 of these biopsies was able to even detect cancer. The reason for this is: The way a prostate biospy is performed is a needle is inserted into the prostate gland. Then, very small sections of prostate tissue are extracted from 6 - 45 different areas within the prostate gland.

But, the needle may not even go where a cancer exists. Some call it the "needle in a haystack search".

### Entelechy — 2012-11-18T23:44:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-the-state-pays-for-your-healthcare.4933/post-65213>

[Should Canada introduce a smoker's licence](http://http://www.besthealthmag.ca/blog/post/debate-should-canada-introduce-a-smokers-license)

Chapman's recommendation is for a type of smart card licence which would be designed to limit access to tobacco products. Proposed details include:

\- an annual cost (depending on the number of cigarettes smoked),

\- reissue each year,

\- daily limits for the number of cigarettes bought, and

\- completion of a test of knowledge about the health risks of smoking.

for those with no sympathy for smokers... replace tobacco with your personal lifestyle choices.

# Thread: What Doctor Remembers This ??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-doctor-remembers-this.4937/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2012-11-19T07:47:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-doctor-remembers-this.4937/post-65283>

.

I wonder how many Doctors remember or even heard of "banana babies?" Sensitivity to gluten based food was first noticed during the 1930's. A bright Doctor thought gluten based foods must be the source of certain sick children showing up for office visits. Children were taken off all food & given only Bananas and Buttermilk. Interesting history in medicine from so long ago. Doctors started to see carbs could make kids sick.

**Banana Babies**

The parents were asked to leave their child at the hospital for six months. (Yes, 6 months!) While at the hospital, the children were fed nothing but bananas. Thatâ€™s right. Bananas and only bananas for six months. Some of them got better, others didnâ€™t make it. They became known as the banana babies.

How the Gluten-Free Diet Began

It was a Greek scientist who first described this disease of the gut and gave it a name, but it wasnâ€™t until after World War II that the cause of the problem was found. A pediatrician in the Netherlands noticed the mortality rate among children affected by celiac disease dropped from more than 35% to almost 0% during the war. At the time, there was a shortage of wheat so corn flour was used instead. When the war was over, and wheat was used once again, the doctor observed that the mortality rate rose to its previous height. It was later discovered that gluten was the offending part of the grain and was also found in rye and barley.

### Michael — 2012-11-19T09:01:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-doctor-remembers-this.4937/post-65289>

> **Thor;67290 wrote:** .
>
> I wonder how many Doctors remember or even heard of "banana babies?" Sensitivity to gluten based food was first noticed during the 1930's. A bright Doctor thought gluten based foods must be the source of certain sick children showing up for office visits. Children were taken off all food & given only Bananas and Buttermilk. Interesting history in medicine from so long ago. Doctors started to see carbs could make kids sick. ...

I wonder if this would be Dr. Sidney Haas, upon whose work the Specific Carbohydrate Diet (SCD) was based, Thor.

[http://scdiet.com/haas.html](http://http://scdiet.com/haas.html)

The date would be about right, and he certainly did use bananas, although I don't think that was all everyone had.

My understanding is that, before Haas found what he did, the only treatment for coeliacs was to put them on a ketogenic diet. (Just as this was the treatment for diabetes before insulin, and for epilepsy before anti-seizure medications.)

I think in those days low carb and ketogenic diets were not thought of as anything very strange or dangerous at all and the kind of thing a doctor might have tried with a patient. They obviously found coeliacs did better on them. I think Haas observed almost by accident, but obviously because he was keeping his eyes open, that people (possibly babies) who would be ill with bread seemed OK with bananas. After this, he began experimenting with different foods. What I believe Haas thought was that although a ketogenic diet would work, it must be a bit boring for people; so what he would do was find the carbohydrates they _could handle.

His conclusion was that the problem was some people had difficulty, at least temporarily, in digesting disaccharides and polysaccharides. This was why he thought the bananas were OK - because most of the sugar in fruit is in the form of monosaccharides (glucose and fructose). The bananas had, of course, to be fully ripe.

At this time no-one knew anything about gluten as a cause.

Interestingly, Elaine Gottschall, who developed and popularised the SCD, based on Haas's work, felt that people fixing on gluten as the problem was actually mistaken, and although it was often part of the problem it wasn't the sole factor. She felt that Haas was right to conclude that some people are better off just off _all_ starchy foods and all table sugar (sucrose) for extended periods.

I'm inclined to think there might be something in what she says. I understand that large numbers of people who are diagnosed as coeliac and put on "gluten-free" diets don't get much healing and relief of their symptoms, although some do. Maybe for people with a damaged gut large amounts of rice and tortillas and all those cakes and biscuits made from mixed gluten-free flours and lashings of sugar really isn't the best thing.

The GAPS Diet is based on the SCD and is similar, but adds a lot of bone-broth and fermented foods and other foods thought to have gut-healing properties.

Interestingly, and totally independently of the SCD/GAPS, but getting to rather the same place, a lady from New Zealand came up with a low-starch diet that's now used with IBS patients and for arthritic pain:

[http://www.amazon.com/The-IBS-Low-Starch-Diet-hazardous/dp/0091912865/](http://http://www.amazon.com/The-IBS-Low-Starch-Diet-hazardous/dp/0091912865/)

It's difficult not_ to believe that carbohydrate, particularly starch, isn't problematic for many people. Not to say that everyone should avoid them at all times and in places, but do carbohydrates really deserve the halo they've been given in very recent times?

I think part of the problem is the demonization of fat that began with the broken diet-heart hypothesis. (And this is why Gary Taubes puts that whole story at the front of his book.) Once you start looking askance at fat, what's left to eat? There's only three macronutrients, and most people (outside low carb circles) don't eat more than ten to fifteen per cent of calories as protein. That really only leaves carbohydrate as your major source of calories.

### Optimalbound — 2012-11-19T11:57:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-doctor-remembers-this.4937/post-65293>

> **Michael;67296 wrote:**
>
> It's difficult _not_ to believe that carbohydrate, particularly starch, isn't problematic for many people.

It's difficult not to believe that there isn't a negation too many in that sentence.

# Thread: Scotland's oldest house
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-19T11:17:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/post-65290>

A new discovery - what's probably the oldest dwelling in Scotland from 10,000 years ago.

This would be what "the movement" would call Paleolithic, but archaeologists Mesolithic. IOW, it pre-dates farming, but is also characterized (among other things) by particular lithic material used for smaller, fleeter game. (They would have the bow and arrow.)

Plenty of charred hazelnut shells and, people will not be surprised to learn:

> Proximity to the rivers would have allowed its occupants to exploit the abundant aquatic life.

The dwelling was probably occupied seasonally.

[http://www.scotsman.com/news/environment/archaeologists-unearth-stone-age-dwelling-on-the-banks-the-of-new-forth-crossing-1-2643154](http://http://www.scotsman.com/news/environment/archaeologists-unearth-stone-age-dwelling-on-the-banks-the-of-new-forth-crossing-1-2643154)

### caroline — 2012-11-19T11:52:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/post-65292>

so interesting - thanks Michael. I have always wanted to go to Scotland - It is so close to you - I suppose you have gone many times. My Husband was born in Nova Scotia and his ancestors were from Scotland.

### Michael — 2012-11-19T13:58:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/post-65298>

I've only been to Scotland once, and then no farther north than Edinburgh.

Some of the scenery is spectacularly beautiful. I sometimes think that I would like to spend a long stretch of time living somewhere quite remote. The southern part of England has its charm - Sussex is actually one of the most wooded counties and has some beautiful flint buildings - but it's just too crowded. You notice this driving north in England - the further you go, the more everything opens out. And you can be sitting in an urban conurbation in the North Country and still be in somewhere like the Peak District in minutes.

Here you're never far off the beaten track. Go to cross a country lane, and there's probably a car coming along.

I don't think it's so much a natural/man-made distinction, as the sheer shoddy quality of the way we build and the way we live in the landscape nowadays. That and the sheer overtcrowding.

I think the west coast of Ireland is probably as remote, unspoiled, and spectacular as Scotland.

A woman said to me once that she wished that she could just be in a cottage and go for long walks and be with me, which I suppose was a nice thing to say to someone. I don't think it was a thought that lasted very long, though.

### caroline — 2012-11-23T22:01:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/post-65481>

hmmmmmm........I think that is quite an extraordinary thought .......amazing thing to say to someone I think ....or - I guess - to be the recipient of that thought. lucky you

### caroline — 2012-11-23T22:15:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/scotlands-oldest-house.4939/post-65483>

# Thread: The hand
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-19T12:21:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65294>

Here's someone else who isn't really known in the paleo movement, but whose work, it seems to me, deserves attention: Raymond Tallis. I did see him mentioned once at MDA, but that would have been a rare encounter.

Tallis, a former professor of medicine, now retired to become a full-time writer, is a useful corrective to the "evolutionary psychology" which is quite popular in the movement. This often seems to offer some very simplistic, and occasionally ludicrous, explanations of phenomena in human life. Part of the problem seems to be that people fail to understand the importance of self-consciousness and misdescribe human life in terms that would be equally applicable to animal life. Tallis says this is not Darwinism but Darwinitis - and something the great man himself was generally free of. For example, Darwin only says that facial expressions have their _origin in those observable in animals. And, of course, everything is not simply equal to its origin - or there would be no change in life.

Tallis has an interesting explanation for self-consciousness, which whether it be right or not is worth reading. He thinks the human hand is key here. He says that a large brain is a necessary but not a sufficient criterion for human mental life. What is needed is a particular mode of consciousness - namely self-consciousness. Some primates can pass into this state episodically, but they don't live in it. The human hand, Tallis thinks, enables that barrier to be crossed, because it creates a kind of reflexive awareness. He goes into quite a lot of anatomical detail, noting that humans are the only creatures with a _fully_ opposable thumb, also that the fingers can move independently (not totally unique but important), and much else. Furthermore, once you have an upright posture, the hand can come into relation with the eyes. Also, with the head up from ground smell becomes less important, so it has less chance to overwhelm awareness of the hand-eye relation.

He thinks pointing_ is of particular importance.

This is obviously a ridiculously quick and bare sketch of some important ideas, but if anyone thinks any of this may be of interest to them, one place to start might be looking at the Amazon reviews of his book Michelangelo's Finger:

[http://www.amazon.co.uk/Michelangelos-Finger-Exploration-Everyday-Transcendence/dp/1848871201](http://http://www.amazon.co.uk/Michelangelos-Finger-Exploration-Everyday-Transcendence/dp/1848871201)

### prAna303 — 2012-11-19T13:30:10+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65296>

Come to think about this post... [http://blog.exuberantanimal.com/smart-hand/](http://http://blog.exuberantanimal.com/smart-hand/)

### ruby — 2012-11-19T13:52:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65297>

"Some primates can pass into this state episodically, but they don't live in it".-- lucky primates, so envious

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-19T14:30:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65301>

Interesting topic...

I would posit that consciousness would have to stem more from the reaction to stimulus, rather than the stimulus itself.

Wouldn't proprioception, pain, hearing, or any other sense have done this? why the emphasis on vision feedback awareness?

Also, lets not forget the importance of head balance... Uprightness came first, then the sophisticated use of the hand.

What allows for the brain chemistry? (I think we have a pretty good idea).

Were we upright before or after we went to water for food. Or more likely that would have been the transition. Just like the other swamp/aquatic primates.

Can bonobos get into this limited awareness more frequently than less upright primates?

Wish I had more time to look into this today...

### Michael — 2012-11-19T15:03:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65309>

> **prAna303;67303 wrote:** Come to think about this post... [http://blog.exuberantanimal.com/smart-hand/](http://http://blog.exuberantanimal.com/smart-hand/)

Oh, yeah - Frank Forencich's site. He would be the sort of person to discuss that sort of thing. I do get his RSS feed; but I haven't seen this. Must have a look.

### Michael — 2012-11-19T15:17:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-hand.4940/post-65315>

> **Tom;67318 wrote:** Wouldn't proprioception ...

It's feedback through touch that he sees as important. But specifically through the hand, which with us allows different grips and has a lot of nerves in. The toes, for example, might be "aware" of the sides of each other, but it seems to him that there might be relational hierarchies within the hand - thumb to fingers and so on. I think he thinks the relation of the eyes to the hand, which is possible in an upright posture, reinforces that, but that it begins with the hand itself. But it's a quite complex discussion and I'm only part-way through and could be introducing misunderstandings of my own.

He doesn't think animals _really_ have tool-use, despite the way people sometimes speak, because they give no sign that they have any grasp of underlying concepts related to tool use, don't re-purpose an object used in one context into another, and while they can learn seem not to teach. He thinks the stick or stone or whatever is totally embedded in its context and probably to them more like an extension of their own body than a tool. He also makes the interesting suggestion that since a tool proper has this separate and rather abstract nature it is itself something like a "sign". Hence we may have one of the roots of language here.

# Thread: Greenland's viking settlers gorged on seals
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greenlands-viking-settlers-gorged-on-seals.4950/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Michael — 2012-11-19T20:19:14+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greenlands-viking-settlers-gorged-on-seals.4950/post-65391>

A nice Epipaleo news story:

> "Our analysis shows that the Norse in Greenland ate lots of food from the sea, especially seals," says Jan Heinemeier, Institute of Physics and Astronomy, Aarhus University.
>
>
>
> "Even though the Norse are traditionally thought of as farmers, they adapted quickly to the Arctic environment and the unique hunting opportunities. During the period they were in Greenland, the Norse ate gradually more seals. By the 14th century, seals made up between 50 and 80 per cent of their diet." ...

[http://news.ku.dk/all\_news/2012/2012.11/greenland\_norse\_gorged\_on\_seals/](http://http://news.ku.dk/all_news/2012/2012.11/greenland_norse_gorged_on_seals/)

### [email redacted] — 2012-11-19T21:38:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/greenlands-viking-settlers-gorged-on-seals.4950/post-65415>

Scholarly stuff never leaves room for sheer chance and serendipity. Perhaps a significant portion of the young women were won in a battle that a majority of Norse participated in. The women were drug off as slaves/concubines etc and assimilated into other cultures and perhaps one bad winter dwindled the rest.

Could be there was a great battle and through "an act of god" all the ships sank, etc.

Sometimes \*\*\*\* just happens.

I have read some really interesting observations about the Norse/Viking reproduction habits. I was noticing one day that going back on the fraternal side of my family (my clearly a viking overloard uncle has a passion for this) would not reproduce much. Only every 3rd or 4rth person would reproduce, and almost exclusively single children not multiple.

Seems when you notice these trends over multiple generations, there may be some reason that is material.

If this "trait" went in the way-back machine, perhaps a combo of happenstance and low reproduction just left them too small to rise up again.

Chaos isn't so sure. But she does like smoked oysters. And octopus & squid are still he favorite seafood!!!

### NaturalHealthForLife — 2013-08-07T06:15:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/havoc-likes-raw-oysters.4963/post-91725>

I am getting my autistic 5 y.o. son to swallow 1 raw oyster per day for lunch. I don't know if he "likes" it, but he is so used to taking a variety of meds, that he didn't put up a fuss getting these down. I do it with him. I'm wondering if I should try to get him to take more than 1/day, but not pushing it for now...

Oyster shots!

Love it!

Thanks Dr. K!

# Thread: Improved eyesight
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2012-11-25T08:39:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-65544>

I was at the optician yesterday to check my eyesight because I do my drivers lisence right now. I was checking them last time about 8 years ago maybe? I have been without glasses all these years and wear them only when seeing movies or at school or such things. I used my old glasses maybe last time one year ago? I do not care if I see a little less. I just do not like wearing glasses at all. They are in my way. And no way would I put any contact lenses on my eyeballs, they need to breath for sure! I had -2 dioptrines on both eyes so not too bad. Now, yesterday it come out I have only -1,5 on one eye and -1,25 on the other! Yay! So I will get new glasses. Because I still want to use them when driving.. even if my doctor said I do not need to use glasses when driving but it is better for sure.

I have no idea what have helped or if I am just getting older..lol because sometimes eyesight improve when getting older too. Or it might be good nutrition and some facedunks? Or lookig in the sun in he mornings? Anyways, this made me really happy! Ok I will get some nice looking new glasses too.. at least the two women in the shop and me choosed them togehter and we agreed they looked nice.. I do look like quite a strong lady in them so I better only use them when driving..lol

### LinD — 2012-11-26T11:32:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-65627>

> **Inger said**
> I was at the optician yesterday to check my eyesight because I do my drivers lisence right now. I was checking them last time about 8 years ago maybe? I have been without glasses all these years and wear them only when seeing movies or at school or such things. I used my old glasses maybe last time one year ago? >
> I have no idea what have helped or if I am just getting older..lol because sometimes eyesight improve when getting older too. Or it might be good nutrition and some facedunks? Or lookig in the sun in he mornings? Anyways, this made me really happy! Ok I will get some nice looking new glasses too.. at least the two women in the shop and me choosed them togehter and we agreed they looked nice.. I do look like quite a strong lady in them so I better only use them when driving..lol

Great news!Â  I love to see clearly.Â  Surely the nutrition has something to do with it.

I do sleep in Ortho-K lenses and take them out in the morning.Â  The overnight correction helps me to see clearly the whole day and fairly well the next if I don't wear them one night.Â  I like the extreme clarity so tend to regret not wearing them that one night, but I like the little break.Â  If I happen to awaken in the night, I am aware of some dryness.Â  I keep eye drops close by to use, if needed.

My DD has awful vision--- both eyes contact lens prescription is -7.Â  Really bad.........Â  She started wearing contact lenses around age 11---when she was on the dive team.Â  My word, how could she see the end of the diving board without them!Â  Eeek.Â  She is no longer diving, but still wears the lenses.

### Awainer1 — 2012-11-26T20:11:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-65661>

I think my eyesight might be getting better too.Â  I dont know though.Â  I know last time I went to the the eye doctor it had fiinally stopped getting worse.Â  That was just being paleo an not epipaleo/ct.Â  Things seem a little less fuzzy when I wake up in the morning but it could be my imagination.

### Inger — 2012-11-27T04:04:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-65680>

Yes, it is nice.. and I have done no exersices or such what I know one could do to improve ones sight! If I did it might get even better? I need to start doing them because this made me just happy!

LinD.. I have a friend that has -7 too and he need his glasses with him everywhere.. That is pretty much! That is weird that you need to sleep with lenses? How can they correct when you sleep anyways and are not looking? Are they doing something to the eyes, like forming them in another shape? Because from what I have read, that is what makes one see worse... when the eyeball get formed in another shape because of the muscles around it when they get stressed and not relaxed... and that is why certain exercises should help to relax the muscles and form the eyeball in its right shape again.

Caroline, I promise I will make a picture from my new glasses on my face and put in this thread when I get them in about 2 weeks.

But promise not to laugh..ok? They are big and they are black...lol..

### prAna303 — 2012-11-27T04:59:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-65681>

> **Inger said**
> Because from what I have read, that is what makes one see worse... when the eyeball get formed in another shape because of the muscles around it when they get stressed and not relaxed... and that is why certain exercises should help to relax the muscles and form the eyeball in its right shape again.

Back in the days, many painters got really bad eyesight for periods of their life. I think it was their work that was to close for their eyes, got no workout for the muscles. When they did travel to the countryside they had to change perspective and watch the horizon, thatÂ´s perhaps why they got better efter a while and could continue their work later on. Today i get really sad because we give our children iPadÂ´s and smartphones and later on we wonder why they canÂ´t go thru a room without crashing into stuff AND other people.

### Inger — 2013-03-01T11:40:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-78482>

Okay I said I'll post a pic when I got my glasses.. so I took a pic today me wearing them, here it comes..

I do not know but I think I look real old in them..lol I actually hate the feeling looking through lenses, the whole world feels so very different.. argh! I am going to wear them only when driving.. when I finally get my drivers lisence.
I actually do not mind seeing a little less, makes everything more relaxing for sure..lol I am so used to it as I stopped wearing glasses when 15 yo. I see less and feel more.. HA

I can see and feel this, the beautiful sunrise from my balcony.. that is about enough

Now, off to bike to the shop to look after my oysters I ordered for my weekly energy boost!

### Rachelle — 2013-03-01T13:05:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-78485>

lol those are in your face glasses and I like them on you. I just bought two pairs of glasses and stuck with the style I always wear--boring. Those are the style of the day and I wanted to try a pair but didn't have the nerve.

I started wearing glasses in my late 40's and I do blame the computer. We really need to constantly move our eyes and look around us all the time instead of staring into a screen. Since eating seafood and CT my eyes are getting better and I find myself taking them off more frequently. I can't watch TV without my eyes hurting so I should just get rid of it. Ugh!!! That will be hard for me. I had become oblivious to what was happening around me and while I welcome change it can be scary.

P.S. you couldn't look old even if you tried.

### nonchalant — 2013-03-01T17:05:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-78499>

> Back in the days, many painters got really bad eyesight for periods of their life. I think it was their work that was to close for their eyes, got no workout for the muscles.

Another factor is that artists can have some lean times with poor food. I know my eyes got worse when I got an office job, but they also got better with a better diet, with no change in work.

### Inger — 2013-03-01T17:05:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-78500>

Yeah Rachelle.. cut the TV.. just throw it out.  That is what I did and I never regretted it. You will get so much more time for life! Life without TV is so much more cool, I promise you.
I can watch movies on my computer if I want but I have not watched one in a year or so.. I always have so much else to do, right now I am reading about quantum theory it is more interesting than any movie made I assure you..!

### caroline — 2013-03-02T01:50:05+0200

### David Moorefield — 2018-09-08T04:49:54+0300

For Orthokeratology, I wounder if the compression of the cornea and epithelial cells (according to wiki) would interfere with the photon gathering functions of the eye... because you would be able to gather more light without wearing lowered glasses, only allowing sun to come in from the edges.

### Pablo — 2026-01-06T20:23:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/improved-eyesight.4972/post-364873>

Hello..

A friend of mine has been asking me a lot about orthokeratology (using ortho-k lenses at night to improve daytime vision). He is a policeman, so he wants to work without glasses (in case of fights) but also he is worried about daytime use of contact lenses, since I have warned him about dr- Kruse work, saying they are bad cause they block UV in the eyes and also oxygen.

What do you guys think about these Ortho-K lenses at night??? I´m also quite interested for myself, at least to use them ocasionally when I wanna to in daytime some sports that require it, like some competition sports, or some water sports (surfing, etc.)... If they were safe for human health, they would be so cool!...

I haven´t found much from Dr. Jack online abouth these Ortho-K contact lenses... Do you know what he thinks? (Or if he sees this and wants to comment would be so good...). I found this brief comment on a blog, seemingly talking positively about these contacts, or at least being less bad than regular daytime contacts:

### [TIME #4: HOW CAN MAN HELP OUR EYE CLOCK IN A TECH WORLD?](https://jackkruse.com/how-man-helps-the-eye-clock/)

BLOG TAKE HOME 1: WHEN WE COVER OUR PUPIL WILL ALTER THE LIGHT IT SENSES???? WILL THIS SPEED UP TIME SO THAT WE CAN GET SICK AND DIE EARLIER? BLOG TAKE HOME 2: Hopes are like Purple light. Fears are like blue light. Now stop and ponder that and then ruminate on this:

 jackkruse.com

**"_CONTACTS OR GLASSES?_**
_
There are two primary issues, oxygen transmission and UV transmission that we need to concern ourselves with. The contact industry has always worked towards increasing O2 transmission by mainly by adding silicone to the lens. The O2 transmission has a massive effect photoelectrically because of the electronegativity of oxygen. This can create some comfort issues. Some people cannot wear silicone lenses. Wearing glasses, of course, fix this issue. Y**ou could consider orthokeratology where you wear a lens at night and your eye breaths normally all waking hours. This is done with a rigid gas permeable lens with a very high DK (O2 transmission/permeability)**_".

What do you think guys??? Otho-K contact lenses could be a decent option, or are they unsafe/unhealhy long term? Could they be ok for ocasional use at night, for doing some kind of sports the next day with good vision, or risky thing??


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Let's hope this post actually shows up!Â  I've been having trouble with the forums since the upgrades.

Â 

Yesterday I came down with what I assume is the flu . . . fever, aches, chills, minor cold symptoms, etc.Â  Normally, this isn't a huge deal.Â  I believe in letting my body ride things out and take care of the germs on its own.Â  However, I leave tomorrow for Florida for a week.Â  The first 3 days are for business and the rest is a personal vacay and DH will be joining me.Â  I've flown while sick before and it was pure he!!.Â  Any suggestions on what I can do to try to speed up my recovery?

Â 

I've been increasing my D and fermented cod liver oil.Â  I can't take vitamin C due to oxalate issues.Â  I'm trying to force lots of water and bone broth, but honestly today I don't want to eat a thing!Â

### Rachelle — 2012-11-25T16:22:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flu-how-to-get-over-it-quickly.4974/post-65578>

If you have any ginger root, cut it up and make tea. drink 3 times a day. Always works for me.

### Inger — 2012-11-26T03:23:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flu-how-to-get-over-it-quickly.4974/post-65610>

Try some CT... { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. I am honest! I am waiting to get a flu too to try this out... a Russian guy told me his mom that grew up in Sibiria always took a icebath when getting a flu. The next day she was healthy again. That was a traditional way of healing oneself in a place where you got no medicines and doctors around..

I am very intrested if this really works. Sadly I am not getting sick.. so no way to try it out.

Â 

Sliced raw organic ginger left in a glass of water for some hours.. and then drinking the water should be good too as Rachelle says. That is what a friend of mine is always doing and he says it helps.

### BJ — 2012-11-26T07:52:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/flu-how-to-get-over-it-quickly.4974/post-65614>

Thanks to you both.Â  I had some ginger tea last night before bed and did actually do some CT yesterday morning and am hoping to again this morning before I catch my flight.Â  I feel a bit better today, but now whatever it is has settled into my chest and throat with of course the dreaded congestion that I hate to have when flying

Â 

Â 

Hopefully a nice cold bath and some more ginger tea this morning will knock it out of me.Â

### janagram — 2012-11-27T15:25:45+0200

# Thread: Bone Broth temp question
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### raikenreiner@gmail.c — 2012-11-26T13:36:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-65634>

The bone broth I'm making is around 190s...is this alright?Â  The slow cooker descriptions I've seen all say to set it on low....but is meant to be low or the lowest setting on the slow cooker?Â  I'm thinking some are different than others and the directions never mention other settings.

Â 

I read that you can start with a boil...but than go down to a simmer on the stove or set it on low for a slow cooker with no mention of other settings.

My slow cooker has High, Low, and Warm which my mom said is the lowest setting.Â  Should I keep it on Low....or Warm?Â  I switched it to warm and it hasn't seemed to go down in temp but it did have bubbles and now it doesn't so...it seems to have gone down with the visual but my thermometer isn't saying its lower....weird

Â 

I was wondering if I should use the High temperature for a set amount of time and than switch to low or warm as well?Â  I started with high for about an hour and a half.Â  Would it be ok to start with warm or low?Â  I've heard its better to boil some first...

Â 

Thanks

### diane — 2012-11-26T13:45:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-65637>

I put my slow cooker on "low" for at least 24 hours. I don't put it high first - just start it right out from low and leave it there. To me, warm is after something is cooked.

Â 

I know that many people on here cook it for 48 - 76 hours on low - so you can definitely go longer than 24 hours.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-11-26T16:19:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-65647>

I start mine out on high to get it to a boil and then turn it down to low and let it cook for about 2 days.Â  I have noticed my slow cooker seems to run a little hotter than I think it should be but "warm" is way too warm.Â  I'm making some right now! We are drinking this twice a day now and loving it.

### raikenreiner@gmail.c — 2012-11-26T17:15:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-65652>

Anyone with experience using Feedlot bones...conventional raised stuff?Â  I read something that mentioned it being better than nothing at all...everyone agree?

### furface — 2012-12-10T18:51:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-67049>

> **raikenreiner@gmail.c said**
> Anyone with experience using Feedlot bones...conventional raised stuff?Â  I read something that mentioned it being better than nothing at all...everyone agree?

If a person is going to slow cook it for at least 24 hours, it would probably kill any impurities the conventional raised beef, lamb, pork, or whatever may have in it anyway. Esp since grass-fed is so hard to find, and soo much more expensive...esp if it's just for bone broth. Some may feel other wise, but this is JMHO.

### kathylu — 2012-12-10T22:19:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-67081>

I try to use GF bones...but if I don't have any, then conventional it is! Remember to add the vinegar...very important to solubilize the minerals. I use 2 tsp ACV per quart of water.

### furface — 2012-12-10T22:57:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-67085>

> **kathylu said**
> I try to use GF bones...but if I don't have any, then conventional it is! Remember to add the vinegar...very important to solubilize the minerals. I use 2 tsp ACV per quart of water.

I never knew that about the vinegar. Thanks, Kathy.

### Patty Cakes — 2012-12-11T15:20:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-67136>

> **kathylu said**
> I try to use GF bones...but if I don't have any, then conventional it is! Remember to add the vinegar...very important to solubilize the minerals. I use 2 tsp ACV per quart of water.

Hey Kathy is that much vinegar needed?Â  I used around 2 Tbsp for my whole crock pot, which is the large size of course. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### kathylu — 2012-12-11T16:04:54+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-67145>

> **Patty Cakes said
>
> > kathylu said
>
> **
>
> > I try to use GF bones...but if I don't have any, then conventional it is! Remember to add the vinegar...very important to solubilize the minerals. I use 2 tsp ACV per quart of water.
>
> Hey Kathy is that much vinegar needed?Â  I used around 2 Tbsp for my whole crock pot, which is the large size of course.

Probably not, but I find that all the vinegar tasted disappears after a few hours of simmering. I just assume you get more minerals out of the bone matrix with more acid present.

### Rachelle — 2012-12-11T18:16:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/bone-broth-temp-question.4982/post-67152>

I never allow my bone broth to come to a hard boil. Also, I cook mine much less hours than most do. Chicken broth - 4 hrs, and beef about 5 - 6 hours. Mine comes great and it gels.

Â 

Here is the recipe I have always used.Â http://www.wholetraditions.com/blog/2009/04/making-broth-for-gaps.html

### DarleenMB — 2012-11-28T18:18:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/immortality-via-a-jellyfish.4995/post-65793>

> After more than 4,000 years â€” almost since the dawn of recorded time, when Utnapishtim told Gilgamesh that the secret to immortality lay in a coral found on the ocean floor â€” man finally discovered eternal life in 1988. He found it, in fact, on the ocean floor. The discovery was made unwittingly by Christian Sommer, a German marine-biology student in his early 20s. He was spending the summer in Rapallo, a small city on the Italian Riviera, where exactly one century earlier Friedrich Nietzsche conceived â€œThus Spoke Zarathustraâ€￾: â€œEverything goes, everything comes back; eternally rolls the wheel of being. Everything dies, everything blossoms again. . . .â€￾
>
>
>
> Sommer was conducting research on hydrozoans, small invertebrates that, depending on their stage in the life cycle, resemble either a jellyfish or a soft coral. Every morning, Sommer went snorkeling in the turquoise water off the cliffs of Portofino. He scanned the ocean floor for hydrozoans, gathering them with plankton nets. Among the hundreds of organisms he collected was a tiny, relatively obscure species known to biologists as Turritopsis dohrnii. Today it is more commonly known as the immortal jellyfish. Â 

Rest of article here: Â http://www.nytimes.com/2012/12/02/magazine/can-a-jellyfish-unlock-the-secret-of-immortality.html?hp&\_r=0

Your trigs are elevated, you have inflammation (hsCRP), your cortisol is low across the day, your E is high (wondering if your T is low), I'm guessing your melatonin is also low (though I didn't see a lab) and your DHEA is low. Â So it looks like hormone imbalances, cortisol-melatonin axis issues and overall inflammation. Â As Gretchen said, JanSz can hack a lab better than most, but those results would seem to suggest both some sort of leptin resistance and other systemic issues.

### Shijin13 — 2012-11-30T11:35:46+0200

when did you have this set of labs? Â  What types of foods were you eating?Â 

KiwiLauren hit the big ones.Â 

Â 

Here's what I'm seeing:

your APO B:A ratio is high. your Trigs are way too high and indicates your eating grains - you want your Trigs under 50 if you're eating paleo, your HDL is Low - which is also an indication of eating grains and excess carbs out of season. you vitamin D is low you want it above 70. Â your HS Crp is high, as is your homocyctine - you've got serious inflammation. Â 

your TSH looks high - but since there's no ranges can't be for sure. Â your T4 is low, you T3 is ok but isn't in the top Quartile and your free T3 looks good - you might have a Thyroid issue. Â Additionally a high TSH coupled w/ LH/FSH issues can be an indication of pitutary problems in men - I'm not well versed in reading LH/FSH in men - Quelsen is your guy for that... PM him.... and he'll take a look when he can.Â 

your Testosterone, looks good, you're bumping into the Â low end of the top quartile - but you're going to want to keep an eye on it. b/c your DHEA is low, as is your IGF-1. I'd bet your IL-6 and TNF are elevated. Â your Estradiol is high and that's something to be concerned about.Â 

your Brain Gut axis is off. Â you need to go back re-read the BG series be 100% compliant eating the Leptin Rx with an Epi-Paleo template that incorporates the EPCOTRx. Â You also need to get your butt in some cold baths - this coupled with the Leptin Rx and eating Epi Paleo with a focus on ketosis will bring down your inflammation as well as your FBG/Fasting Insulin and HbA1C. Â Additionally b/c you're HDL is below 60 as a man - you want to ditch all nightshades until the HDL comes up - so you need to ditch any peppers/tomatoes untill that happens.Â

# Thread: Hack My Adrenal Fatigue Symptoms
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 16

### TheKid — 2012-11-29T12:00:43+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/post-65834>

I am hoping someone can help me sift through some symptoms.

Basically, I am trying to determine whether what I am experiencing is due to adrenal fatigue, mitochondrial disease, or both.

My problems starting back in 2009 when I began to get what I believe were panic attacks. I would have a pounding heartbeat, chest discomfort and sometimes my heart would race suddenly. I would go to the ER only to have docs tell me everything was OK.

These seemed to resolve, but my health has never really improved. I am afraid that instead of actually resolving, I simply moved on to the next stage of adrenal fatigue.

In late summer I did a 4x salivary cortisol that came up 12, 0, 0, 0. Jack was extremely concerned about the goose eggs I put up. But I don't know what stage of adrenal fatigue that puts me at.

These days, I am all over the map. When I try to exercise, I get knocked on my backside for sometimes 2 weeks. THe fatigue is crushing.

But what worries me more than anything, is chest pain and shortness of breath. Two weeks ago, after doing some high intensity interval training, got really serious chest pain. It honestly felt like I had a rock in my chest instead of a heart. Of course I rushed to the cardiologist, and after a EKG and echo, I was once again told I was NOT having a heart attack.

That rock hard feeling is gone, but my days are up and down and literally all over the place. Chest pain is frequent, as is mood. Sleep is erratic. And I have these terrible adrenaline rushes. The rushes used to come once a night, but then increased to twice a night and even crept up during the day. I also am nearly always short of breath with any kind of physical activity, even walking. Since the cardio told me I surely don't have congestive heart failure, I am at a loss.

One thing Jack has me looking into is mitochondrial dysfunction and disease. But with all this overlapping symptomology, I don't know which it would be.

My endo wants to start me on hydrocortisone and DHEA. I am also planning to start some D-Ribose as well.

Last 5 days, I have basically been unable to leave the house. So I'd appreciate any words of wisdom from folks who've already navigated this territory.

My main concern is that hydrocortisone is a damn powerful hormone, and notoriously hard to come wean off; and that's IF your adrenals kick back in. I am thinking to hit the D-Ribose first to see what it's effect would be. Any help would be appreciated. Thanks.

### Birdy — 2012-11-29T12:24:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/post-65837>

Sorry I can't help you more.Â  I'd say if anyone even thinks that they have adrenal fatigue, they shouldn't be exercising.Â  Lucky for me I was always borderline AF, but the best thing I did for myself is to quit any kind of cardio (I still kept active with my day-to-day activities) until things got better.

Check out Dr. B G on the blog Animal Pharm, for her experience with AF and the leptin reset.Â  She disagrees with Dr. K on the order of things, but I guess it really matters if you have all the testing and expertise at your disposal at the outset.

Other experts, such as Schwarzbein, recommend no exercise and no ketogenic diet.

Your endo suggested cortisol, which I guess is a crutch, then again, crutches are handy when you break your leg.Â  When a doc recommends crutches, they don't intend for you do use them for life, do they?

### vkiernan — 2012-11-29T14:34:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/post-65851>

[http://www.jackkruse.com/brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/](http:///brain-gut-16-adrenal-fatigue-rx/)

Â 

I would say read the link if you haven't already and follow the protocol.Â  Do not exercise.Â  You already know it wipes you out.Â  I have had af for years but didn't really know it or understand it.Â  I cannot help you with the other stuff.Â 

I would think your last ASI would have told you the severity of it.Â  If you are tanked at all 4 times and your DHEA is tanked that is the last stage.Â  Jack says that it is not the adrenals unless Addison or Cushings(?) but the PVN according to theÂ blog linkÂ I posted above.Â  So if that's the case then you need really need to follow the light rules morning and evening.Â  You can look up af and find charts.Â  Some have 7 stages and some have 4.Â  My particular lab had 7 it showed my results and charted them so I had a clear visual.Â  I would say I still have it but I am slowly getting better.Â  Still really hard to stay awake in the evenings past 8 if I can even make it that long.Â Â 

How is your thyroid?Â  There is a protocol thatÂ calls for taking some form of t3 or t3/t4Â (NDT)Â except for slow release in the early hoursÂ when your body is making cortisol and this seems to help a lot of people right out of the gate.Â  The book is Recovering with T3 by Paul Robinson.Â  I can tell you it is helping me.Â  Slowly but surely. Â There is some info on the STTM website

I took bio-HC for a few months and at one point it did help but then that went away quickly.Â  I was taking between 27 and 32 a day.Â  I gained weight with it too.Â  Wasn't too happy about that.Â  I went off it to have some tests done and I was exhausted for weeks afterwards so I knew it was stopping my own adrenals from working.Â  I would think twice about starting it.Â 

I wish you the best in fixing yourself.Â  I understand where you are coming from and it is no picnic for sure.Â

Then, I want you to think about the number one killer of all humans today: Heart disease. How does heart disease tie into this theory of whole body physiology?

If you begin to alter your blood glucose because of dietary or any other epigenetics signal (light or temp) and cause inflammation at a cellular level by eating badly or not being mindful of circadian cycles, you start secreting large amounts of insulin from beta cells to deal with it. This alters your Magnesium levels which activate the metabolically efficient Na/K ratio because of DHA as laid out in BG 13. When we have an altered Na/K (low) ratio, this in turn, simultaneously cause brain metabolism to ratched down because its function is directly coupled to the efficiency of the Na/K ATPase for our species. As the brainâ€™s metabolism falters, it begins to shunt energy to itself at the determent of other organs. It robs Peter to pay Paul. This physiologic bio energetic â€œstealingâ€￾ of energy alters functions everywhere in the body to satisfy the brains needs. As other organs fail slowly under this physiologic directive this further alters brain function from the excessive 60% energy boon created by the DHA/ Na/K ATPase, which results in your feeling less well with less energy and you have a chronic cognitive haze. Is this sounding familiar to anyone?
It is the modern human condition that most of us exist in. As this goes on chronically, brain function is eroded (the real source of adrenal fatigue in the PVN) because there is an excess of Na ions, which alter our homeostatic brain controls of the renin angiotensin system and this alters our Blood Pressure. The renin angiotensin system target organ is the kidneys. The kidneys control the electrolyte balance of the serum and ultimate of the CSF (ultrafiltrate of the serum) to further alter the function of our brain.

This slow erosion of our kidney function goes on undetected and slowly undermines our electrical systems balance. This implies that our declining kidney function, directly efffects our electrical system of our heart (conduction system AV node and sinus pacemaker) to become less optimal or â€œfoggyâ€￾ in its function too. The relays in the hearts electrical system is sticky and this cause all kinds of unusual heart rhythms. **This shows you why declining brain function always correlates with declining heart function. Modern medicine has never made this link but they know it exists. These organs are chemically and electrically tied together by the action of the Na/K ATPase, DHA and iodine at a foundational level physiologically in humans in our cell membranes.** This all results in the many types of cardiac dysrythmias we see clinically. This is an early sign of major bio-energenic shifts in cells all tied to oxidation.
When this goes on further brain function erodes and we can not sense it well. We, in essence, lose our primal sense of well being and eventually we develop an inability to sense vascular trouble before it happens clinically. This leads to a heart attack/palpatations/or heart failure.

### TheKid — 2012-12-02T07:06:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/post-66117>

Thanks for the continued advice.Â  Jude, that piece from Dr. K is excellent.Â  I did read it before, but it was a while ago. So the re-read was helpful.Â  And indeed, when I was having the worst chest pains, I rushed to the caridologist.Â  He told me I was OK, despite the fact that there is a definite abnormality in the EKG.Â  The technician suspected it was a slight arryhthmia (non-conducted PAC, SA block, AV block or sinus pause).Â  But what I am getting from Dr. K's explanation is that there is no such thing as a normal arrhythmia. They may be commonly seen, but they ain't normal.

Â 

KiwiLauren, I am wondering what your experinece with D-Ribose has been.Â  I've seen you post in Ask Jack on it.Â  Jack is concerned about my mitochondria and D-Ribose can really help with that.Â Â I have a shipment on the way.Â  I just don't know if it, along with the CoQ10, L-Carnitine and Magnesium I am chewing like candy, will resolve an adrenal issue.Â  So your experiences would be insightful.

Â 

Inger, I like your style.Â  It takes a classy lady to knock back mackrel head smoothies and fermented heart.Â  In fact, Jack has shared with me that lamb heart (and other organs and meat) are filled with CoQ10.Â  So I'm off to the butcher tonight.Â  In terms of giving some more detail, I can tell you all this:

Â 

I have always struggled with recovery from exercise.Â  I was a seriously competitive swimmer for years; and despite my work ethic, I would never perform well when it counted.Â  In fact, when everyone would rest and do well at the end of a season, I would crash and fail to live up to my potential. Strangely enough, I would consistently perform quite well at the early competitions of the NEXT season, which would be like a month and a half later.Â 

Â 

Additionally, I have had one strange nagging problem all my life.Â  I get winded climbing stairs.Â  In high school, depsite being an athlete, I would climb 2-3 flights of stairs and be breathing quite heavily.Â  A classmate once actually asked in astonishment how I could be winded when I was a swimmer.Â  This problem has persisted into adult life and worsened.Â  High intensity exercise is hard for me.Â  I do better with long, low-level warm up exercise.Â  And as I know, this is a sign of mitochondrial dysfunction.Â  I also get overly fatigued by exercise in general.Â  It can take as long as 2 weeks to recover, depending on intensity.

Â 

What happened 3 weeks ago was that I did a week of exercise, which damn near killed me.Â  I had chest pain that felt like I a rock inside my chest instead of a heart.Â  My ability to think was gone; it was like I was living on another planet. And I would get shortness of breath simply walking short distances.Â  It was truly scary.Â  But with no indication of a heart attack after seeing the cardiologist, the only thing the doc could tell me was to take a pain killer.

Â 

I have done a 24-hour urine, and all the adrenal values came back in normal range and my T3 was normal.Â  The only thing that was off was my 17-Hyroxy.Â  It was elevated.

Â 

What I can't figure out is whether what I have been experiencing is an acute adrenal crisis that then slowly resolves, or a mitchondrial crash that slowly resolves.Â  And when you layer on all of this the GERD I have, the Hashis and antiphospholipid syndrome, well you're looking at co-morbidities that could all cause the same symptoms.Â  Heck, I could be just having a cytokine storm of sorts.Â 

Â 

My plan of action is to start the D-Ribose and see what happens.Â  If no resolution, my doc is preparing to put me on Hydrocortisone, and I'll take that. If still no resolution, then I'll have to hire Inger as a real estate broker so that I can find a quiet fishing village up north where I can be an acquatic ape the rest of my life.

### shah78 — 2012-12-02T12:18:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/post-66140>

In May of 2008 I started taking HCL(in additon to armour thyroid). I was up to 22.5 grms a day by June 2008.Â  IÂ  tried to get off it by substituting powdered licorice . Over a three month period I foung that three teaspoons (one tablespoon) equalledÂ  22.5 grms of HCL. I felt the best on one tablespoon for about a year . Since February of 2012 I have tried a slow(very slow) wean. After two hundred and eighty cold baths, I'm down to one teaspoon of licorice powder. I could push the wean, but I have no problem with taking licorice daily. I'd like to be done with licorice by June of 2013. I expect pollen season (Mid Jauaary-mid April in Florida)Â will prevent me from finishing up "the wean" much sooner. Licorice was a lotÂ  better choiceÂ  than the HCL. Its good for digestion, anti-viral and you don't have to go into the gross pharmacies to get it and you don't have to deal with the equally gross MDs to get it. And it tastes great. YMMV

### KiwiLauren — 2012-12-02T14:02:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/post-66148>

Hi The Kid. Â I've only been taking d-Ribose for about 3 weeks now, but I'm happy to share my experiences. Â (I also take Mg and I eat raw lamb's heart weekly... my supplement budget is necessarily tiny so this is currently the best I can do.) Â The d-Ribose has changed my digestion (made it much faster) and I don't like that but I have an intuitive feeling that it is okay, if not good for the moment. Â The only AI symptom I constantly have is swollen hands and they have become significantly less so since starting the d-Ribose (they are only swollen upon waking). Â It has also improved my energy. Â I am only on a very low dose of compounded thyroid and only once a day (currently the best I can get my doc to prescribe) and I think the d-Ribose helps with the energy fall-off that was happening come 3pm. Â Because cell signalling is under everything, I've decided to stick with this supplement for a long while and see what happens. Â It's not magic, but the literature seems pretty clear of its efficacy.

Â 

This is not what you asked about, but I'll add it because it's free and I also find it very helpful for energy, stress and (theoretically) lymphatic stimulation and pain. Â And that is diaphragmatic breathing. Â It is sometimes called 'belly breathing.' Â It is more than just your tummy rising, but actually fills your entire spinal area and it is create for enervating the entire body. Â I try to practice when I'm driving, when I'm working, whenever I can remember. Â I believe proper breathing is akin to proper cell signalling, it underpins health.

Â 

Good luck to you. Â It can be overwhelming to have so much on your plate health-wise. (At least I find it so.) Â I hope you find a way to breathe deep and enjoy your life every day, even as you climb the big hill of returning to health.

Its good for digestion, anti-viral and you don't have to go into the gross pharmacies to get it and you don't have to deal with the equally gross MDs to get it. And it tastes great. YMMV
>
> Shah, is your powdered licorice deglycyrrhizinatedÂ or just 'normal'? Â Thanks!

### shah78 — 2012-12-02T18:46:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/post-66168>

> **shah78 said
>
> > **KiwiLauren said
> >
> >
> > > shah78 said
> >
> > **
> >
> > > > It's got to be the "real thing " toÂ  stop the reactivation of the cortisol.Thus allowing for recycling of whatever dinky amount of cortisol you can produce. Worked like a charm.
>
> **
>
> > > In May of 2008 I started taking HCL(in additon to armour thyroid). I was up to 22.5 grms a day by June 2008.Â  IÂ  tried to get off it by substituting powdered licorice . Over a three month period I foung that three teaspoons (one tablespoon) equalledÂ  22.5 grms of HCL. I felt the best on one tablespoon for about a year . Since February of 2012 I have tried a slow(very slow) wean. After two hundred and eighty cold baths, I'm down to one teaspoon of licorice powder. I could push the wean, but I have no problem with taking licorice daily. I'd like to be done with licorice by June of 2013. I expect pollen season (Mid Jauaary-mid April in Florida)Â will prevent me from finishing up "the wean" much sooner. Licorice was a lotÂ  better choiceÂ  than the HCL. Its good for digestion, anti-viral and you don't have to go into the gross pharmacies to get it and you don't have to deal with the equally gross MDs to get it. And it tastes great. YMMV
> >
> > Shah, is your powdered licorice deglycyrrhizinatedÂ or just 'normal'? Â Thanks!
> >
> > That's deactivation, not reactivation.

### KiwiLauren — 2012-12-02T23:31:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/post-66197>

Brilliant, thanks, Shah. One more question if you don't mind: what do you make of the data that shows licorice upregulates estrogen? Any cause for concern?

### TheKid — 2012-12-05T03:55:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hack-my-adrenal-fatigue-symptoms.5000/post-66423>

KiwiLauren,

Â 

Just saw your post in Ask Jack.Â  I'm interested to see the answer, and I wonder if any increase in estrogen would be the same in men as in women.Â  Did you see anything about that in the research you'vew looked at?

Â 

With regard to my symptoms, I think I am zeroing in on some things.Â  It is a real maze though.Â  My main concern was that I have experienced some troubling chest pain.Â  I believe now that this is coming as a result of reactive hypoglycemia, and caffeine allergy, which I have dealt with for some time now.Â  I've tested a couple of times now and I think the reaction I have is pointing to that.Â 

Â 

My main problems started months ago when I could not maintainÂ tight restrictions on diet.Â  Whenever I get really restrictive on carbs and caffeine, cravings eventually become overwhelming.Â  That's when I fail.Â  Over the summer I was able to stay away from carbs, but I drank a great deal of coffee.Â  At the time, I did not have an ASI.Â  But later I found that I was not producing hardly any cortisol from 10:00 am onwards.Â  So the caffeine was doing me no favors, for sure.

Â 

But recently I did an N=1 to see my reactions to things.Â  On two occasions, separated by about 5 days, I ate some larger amounts of unsafe carbsÂ at dinner.Â  Within 30-45 minutes, I was having awful reactive hypoglycemia that complete with adrenaline rushes and chest pain.Â  This persisted through the entire night with GERD mixed in with it.Â  Same thing happened with a caffeine test in the morning.

Â 

When I stay away from these things, within a few days, my symptomology subsides to more common adrenal fatigue symptoms such as brain fog, lack of energy, etc.Â 

Â 

I am having trouble getting ahold of hydrocortisone where I am.Â  But that has allowed me to start a trial with D-Ribose.Â  I just got a truckload of the stuff from Life Extension along with some Acetyl L-Carnatine.Â  So I am interested in seeing what happens after taking it for a while.Â  And of course, I'm back to restricting carbs tightly and avoiding caffeine.

# Thread: Brenda Watson on PBS
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brenda-watson-on-pbs.5005/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Huck — 2012-11-30T10:40:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brenda-watson-on-pbs.5005/post-65954>

I watched part of her special on PBS and it was pretty good.Â  She is big on probiotics and reducing inflammation.Â  One tip she gave that I found helpful is to count sugar as teaspoons instead of grams.

She recommends a person have 8 to 10 teaspoons of sugar (max) a day.Â  The formula she uses is (carb grams minus fiber) divided by 5.Â  That works out to 40-50 net carbs/day.

I looked at a box of white rice and 1/2 cup (1 serving) is about 45 grams.Â  My brain doesn't understand grams very well, but when I divide by 5 and see that 1 serving of white rice is equal to about 9 teaspoons of sugar, my brain easily understands why I need to stay away.

I didn't listen to the entire program, but what I did hear supports what Jack has posted.Â  The biggest difference is her audience is the average lay person and she uses lots of metaphors and examples and very little scientific jargon.

This is one of those yearly fund raising specials, so if you want to see it, you need to check out our local PBS schedule now.
