# Thread: metformin
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-09T18:15:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70093>

Anyone have any information, theories or thoughts on why metformin would cause loose stools/diarrea? This has been my experience after about a month+ taking it twice daily. I have since read many accounts of this happening to diabetics taking it (and who have found it so intolerable that they refuse to take it any longer as it was meaning they could never be far from a toilet). I am not diabetic. I was taking it for cancer-prevention, inflammation-reduction, etc (all the mechanisms Dr Kruse has talked about). I am trying to evaluate if I should continue taking this (I have currently stopped). My FBG is good, I have done huge work on gut healing with EPCOT Rx principles, my hsCRP is low, my HDL is good, my Pg:E ratio pretty good. My DHEA isn't great and I am hypothyroid. I am also taking d-Ribose.

I am wondering if the side effect means the medication is no longer needed? Or if there is some other mechanism(s) at work. What are your thoughts??? For those of you on metformin, any similar experiences? Thanks!

### Thor — 2013-01-09T18:29:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70096>

.
Better check to see if you're positive for the genetic hickup called MTHFR - if you're positive then metformin can be deadly. Metformin sucks up all your B-12 which causes a number of problems. I use berberine as a better alternative that's safe.

Metformin-related vitamin B12 deficiency

http://ageing.oxfordjournals.org/content/35/2/200.full

also check out Dr Ben's Website - www mthfr net

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-09T19:00:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70103>

> **Thor said**
>
> .
> Better check to see if you're positive for the genetic hickup called MTHFR - if you're positive then metformin can be deadly. Metformin sucks up all your B-12 which causes a number of problems. I use berberine as a better alternative that's safe.
> Metformin-related vitamin B12 deficiency
> http://ageing.oxfordjournals.org/content/35/2/200.full
> also check out Dr Ben's Website - www mthfr net

Thanks, Thor. I don't know if I have MTHFR nor when I would be able to get tested. But the B12 deficiency fits the symptoms. From the article you linked to it seems that one could become B12 deficient on metformin even without a methylation issue. Sounds like berberine doesn't cause the same problems. I'd rather take a non-pharmaceutical product anyway.

One thing I wonder about this theory... if the symptoms stop completely the day I stop taking metformin, can B12 respond so immediately?

### Thor — 2013-01-10T00:27:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70122>

.
That's where it gets tricky - if you're not positive for MTHFR then you'll be fine on Methyl-B12 However there are a zillion genetic hickups that could be a problem. Which means you'll need a doc to help dial in which B-12 (Hyrdox or Methyl) and how much. If you're just low on
B-12 then it should hit fairly fast. I was getting admitted to hospice care for end of life treatment - within two doses of Dr Kendel Stewart, MD product for MTHFR I was almost normal - which blew my socks off. www Neurobiologix.com

Here's a partial list of other meds you should avoid if you're positive for MTHFR

Antacids, Protein Pump Inhibitors and Histamine Blockers
Many antacids contain aluminum. With the MTHFR gene mutation we have trouble clearing metals so aluminum is not a good thing to put in our bodies.
We also have trouble maintaining adequate levels of B12 (methylcobalamin not to be mistaken with cyanocobalamin). Aluminum can inhibit the absorption of B12.
Protein Pump Inhibitors decrease B12 and Folate (not to be mistaken with folic).
Histamine Blockers reduce the absorption of Folate and B12.

Colchine
Reduces the absorption of B12.

Oral diabetic medications
Metaformin
Causes malabsorption of B12, Folate and possibly B1 (thiamine)

Ethionamide
Inhibits B6 (P5P)

Cycloserine
Inhibits the absorption of Folate and inactivates B6.

Aminosalicylic acid
Reduces the absorption of Folate and B12 (methylcobalamin)

Antibiotics
Cephalosporins, Fluorquinolones, Isoniazid, Macrolides, Penicillan, Sulfonamides and Tetracyclines
Destroy intestinal gut flora
B1, B2, B5, B6, Folate and B12 all have decreased absorption.
People with MTHFR sometimes have problems clearing sulfates. Molybdenum seems to help people with MTHFR clear sulfates and sulfites as well.

Synthetic B vitamins
Many people I speak with who have MTHFR have problems with synthetic b vitamins. It actually jams up the active b vitamins from doing their job. I avoid vitamins with synthetic b's and foods fortified with synthetic b vitamins.
Synthetic B6 contains formaldahyde
Synthetic Folate known as Folic contains coal tar residue
Synthetic B12 known as cyanocobalamin contains cyanide phosphate 3'
These are not the active forms and with MTHFR you clearly need the active forms of b vitamins to manipulate this enzyme deficiency

Phenytoin and Fosphenytoin
Decreases the absorption of Folate and B12

Cholestyramine
Decreases the absorption of B12 and Folate.

Nitrous Oxide
Will cause B12 (methylcobalmin) deficiency in people with an MTHFR gene mutation that can possibly be severe. Prior to surgery and/or a dental visit it is important to let your doctor and anesthesiologist know that you cannot have nitrous oxide. Nitrous oxide can poduce hyperhomocystenemia and high homocysteine which in turn can cause a life threatning clotting event.

Pancreatic enzymes
Here is the deal with pancreatic enzymes. You can have low production of pancretic enzymes due to lack of nutrition and what pancreatic enzymes you have in your system can go into override and be depleted due to lack of nutrition. Pancreatic enzymes are needed for the methylation process. Medications and poor diet contribute to this. MTHFR and b deficiency is a clear marker of hypomethylation.

Phenobarbitol
Interferes with absorption of Folate

Cobalt
Can interfere with the absorption of B12

Chlorpromazine
Interferes with B2-riboflavin

Methylprednisone
Decreases Folate

Statin Drugs>Reduce glutathione levels. Many people with MTHFR already have compromised glutathione levels. Low glutathione levels can cause high homocysteine levels which in turn can lead to a clotting event.

Antidepressants/SSRI's
Signs of a Folate and B12 deficiency are sometimes anxiety and depression. If you know that you have an MTHFR gene mutation and suffer with anxiety and depression, shouldn't you try B12 (methylcobalimin) and Folate? Also BH4 is doing wonders for people who have the MTHFR gene mutation, anxiety and depression. TMG also helps with this. No one should ever stop taking their medication without first consulting a physician.

Cyclosporine
Cyclosporine and the MTHFR gene mutation has been found to elevate cardiovascular risk factors in the C677T gene mutation.

Methotrexate
Methotrexate is used to treat psoriasis and rheumatoid arthritis. It is known to cause Folate deficiency.

Vaccinations
People with the MTHFR deficiency sometimes have problems clearing metals. There are many toxic preservatives in vaccinations. Thimerasol and aluminum are found in many vaccinations. They can cause serious problems to someone with an MTHFR gene mutation. Thimerasol (mercury) and aluminum are known to deplete b vitamins. A person with MTHFR has trouble clearing metals. Mercury and aluminum absorb what b vitamins are in the body.

Amalgam Fillings
Amalgam fillings are made up of the toxic metal mercury. Someone with the MTHFR gene mutation who clearly cannot dump metals can have many problems with amalgam fillings. Anytime you chew food or put hot liquid into your mouth, a toxic mercury gas is let off in your mouth. Guess what is right above your mouth? YOUR BRAIN. Mercury depletes your B vitamins.

Fluoride
Fluoride has been linked to lowering glutathione.

SAM-e
Some people with MTHFR are not able to tolerate SAM-e
Side effects are dizziness, gas and headaches. SAM-e is unstable and can be inactive by the time you take it. TMG and BH4 work well in it's place.

Acetaminophen
Acetaminophen and glutathione deficiency
Many people with MTHFR have lower than normal glutathione levels.
B12 (methylcobalamin) and NAC (N-acetylcysteine) help to elevate glutathione. NAC does have some side effects. People with leaky gut syndrome, asthma and pulmonary hypertension should not use NAC.
In my opinion acetaminophen should be avoided by people who have low glutathione and the MTHFR gene mutation. There are studies out that show acetaminophen lowers your glutathione by 84%.

Diuretics
Diuretics have been linked to causing Thiamine (B1) deficiency.

Antifungals
Antifungals such as grisefulvin and fluconazole are hard on the liver. Glutathione is needed to clear out toxins and to maintain a healthy liver. With MTHFR and low glutathione it is best to avoid these drugs. Oil of oregano, coconut milk and a low carb diet are healthier alternatives that help keep yeast and candida away.

Fish Oil
Mercury tends to suck up our methylcobalamin (B12).
It is best to try other alternatives such as flaxseed oil and nattokinase (should not be taken with coumadin). Remember that many people with an MTHFR gene defect have problems clearing heavy metals and there is only one other heavy metal deadlier than mercury. It is plutonium.

Asthma medications
People with MTHFR sometimes need asthma medications. B12 (methylcobalamin) helps improve asthma symptoms. B12 is known as an antisulfite agent. Sulfites can cause asthma in people with MTHFR gene mutations because they have trouble converting sulfites. B6 promotes ATP and helps relax bronchial tissue.
Theophylline, albuterol and prednisone have been found to make us B6 deficient.

Anticonvulsants
Can cause B12 and B6 deficiency which can lead to anemia an fatigue. It can also elevate homocysteine and lead to stroke.

Neuroleptics
Neuroleptics can cause B12 deficiency and neuroleptic malignant syndrome.

Levodopa
Levodopa is used to treat Parkinson's.
It also decreases levels of B12 (methylcobalamin) and Folate.
This can cause polyneuropathy.

Antihypertensives
Antihypertension drugs interact with NAC (N-acetylcyteine). NAC helps produce glutathione. So if you are taking an antihypertensive drug, it is best not to put NAC in your MTHFR protocol.
Hydralazine increases the excretion of B6.

Oral contraceptives
Oral contraceptives have been found to drop folate by 40%. A person with the MTHFR gene mutation already has trouble keeping adequate amounts of folate in their body. Low levels of folate can elevate homocysteine and lead to a clotting event.

### Destiny — 2013-01-10T07:43:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70137>

I have been taking metformin 500 mg twice a day in the morning and evening. I had a lot of running problems at the beginning. I backed off and took just the morning dose for a month. Then, I added it back and I have no problems now. I also inject B12 once a month.

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-10T14:53:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70179>

Destiny, are you doing B12 injections because of metformin or for another reason? I guess I am feeling increasingly wary about a pharmaceutical drug that has the effect of driving down B12, given how critical it is for the body. It makes me wonder what else it is doing errantly. I admit I am already skeptical of any pharmaceutical product... and when I read about the clinical trials on berberine vs metformin, berberine out-performed metformin, so I'm even more skeptical now!

### Patty Cakes — 2013-01-10T18:30:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70211>

Thanks for all that Thor! Good info for folks just getting into the MTHFR mutations. Oh my, there is so much to learn.

### Destiny — 2013-01-10T22:17:56+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/metformin.5406/post-70249>

Lauren, I was taking B12 before metformin. My husband has to take it every month and I piggy back every second months. My test showed me in 1500 and that looks ok.

I have to read on berberine. I tried that for a month at the beginning but then jumped on metformin since the dx.

# Thread: Liverwurst basic recepe
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liverwurst-basic-recepe.5430/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Charlotte — 2013-01-11T15:00:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liverwurst-basic-recepe.5430/post-70319>

2 kg Liver (pork, game, beef, chicken, whatever you like or have)
2 kg Pork bacon like you use for breakfast bacon (not treated though or treated for different taste)
400 g porkmeat or any other meat (fatty)
400 g Porkfat or any other fat
4 onionsw
80 g salt, pepper, any other spice you like
majoram, garlic

Put the liver shortly in hot water to be able to remove skin, inside it should still be raw and bloody, Meanwhile cook the 400 g porkmeat in boiling water (makes a nice broth).
Cut all in small pieces and put through the grinder twice making it as fine as possible. Cut the onion (and garlic) in small pieces and sautee in the porkfat until soft and sweet. Then mix it all together thoroughly and use spices as you like. We have tons of different ranging from mild to very spicy, so suit your own taste or divide it in batches and give each one its own taste for variety.
Then fill in glasses and put them in Water, covering except for the lid. Let it steep there at app. 95Â°C for 2 hrs.
Take out, put the lid firmly on and you can keep it. Let it cool slowly so the fat does not collect at the walls of the jars.

Important is, to have it real fatty as otherwise it is difficult to spread. You can also experiment with other meats or less liver.

Just found a recepe for making rolls from eggs and psyllium husk flour which I will try with the liverwurst.

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-11T17:19:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liverwurst-basic-recepe.5430/post-70334>

Can't wait to try this...all the liverwurst I've bought just doesn't taste that great to me, this sounds yummy. thanks!

sorry, I guess what I meant was not cured as breakfast bacons are. But I think you might try cured or not cured. In Germany we can now buy also the American breakfast bacon, which I like a lot, but it is not typical German. We usually get our bacon just as it is. We call it "Wammerl". It is also used for cooking in Sauerkraut.

I think maybe pork belly would be a great substitute?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/liverwurst-basic-recepe.5430/post-70486>

There is still this mingling of politics and religious affairs here.

# Thread: Going Raw
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### DarleenMB — 2013-01-11T16:49:32+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70328>

OK, I've been edging in this direction veeerrrrryyyy slowly. Inger is a stunning testimony to the power of raw food so I'm thinking it might be time.

I asked for, and received, the food grinder attachment for my Kitchen Aid mixer for xmas with the notion I would try my hand at steak tartare. Even have a recipe downloaded.

Today I went hunting for info on a raw meat diet for humans and I found this blog: http://theprimalparent.com WOWZER. bookmarked!

Snagged a recipe for ceviche from food.com and am now thinking that sea bass that's been hiding in the freezer is destined for a bowl of lime juice along with with a handful of scallops.

So anyone else doing raw and do you have tips, tricks, recipes, suggestions. I am NOT ready to do high meat nor am I ready to eat lamb heart (I guess I'm not as predatory as I think because the thought of all those spring lambs cavorting out in the field just keeps me from eating it) raw or otherwise. Baby steps here folks.

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-11T17:30:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70336>

Darleen...my approach has sneaked up me. I didn't really set out to eat raw, but found myself ready to pop a few slices of this and that into my mouth....then I would cook the rest. Really thin slices is best for me! Steak in larger amounts is now no problem, but for the organ meats, I slice things when they are still partially frozen, that makes it easy to control the thickness.

I love horseradish and pickles, capers etc. They also have a lot of histamine, so I have to control myself, but the taste of something spicy really makes the raw stuff easy to handle.

The other day I bought some octopus and inspired by a documentary on the best sushi master in Japan....I ate it raw with some lime juice and wasabi. It was yum.

So...have fun and see what happens! Just the fact that you can think about it represents a lot of change doesn't it??

### caroline — 2013-01-11T18:51:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70346>

The thing that really works for me as far as raw meat goes......I buy small pieces of rump steak -with no fat on it and then set it on a wooden board and just leave in the fridge - as I use it I just keep opening more packages. I am eating some now that has been sitting about 5 days I guess and it is nicely drying and is somewhat like salami. I can't even eat a big steak anymore - seems really gross to chow down on that much meat.....

### DarleenMB — 2013-01-11T19:17:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70349>

MMM octopus with wasabi sounds kinda tasty, actually! Of course, good luck finding it here in Wyoming.

Caroline that's what I was looking for ... I'll try that too. Sounds like a nice little nosh about midday since I'm not real hungry then but if I don't eat a little something then by 4 I'm ready to eat everything in sight.

### Destiny — 2013-01-11T19:25:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70351>

I need to try Caroline's "salami". I made steak tartare this week and it was yummy. My husband was just asking me if there was any leftover and I said nope, you are too late { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

!
I also buy sashimi for lunch twice or three times a week. That is my raw stuff so far. Yes, baby steps.

### caroline — 2013-01-11T19:52:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70353>

That is exactly right Darleen - a few thin slices seems to go along way - that is what I have for breakfast and 2 cups of BPC .... but a few slices for lunch would be perfect too. If I am more hungry I just add what ever leftovers I have.. Maybe at first - let some sit in the fridge for a few days and let it dry a bit - it gets dark and kinda looks like salami - not raw at all. Good luck!

### Priscilla — 2013-01-11T20:31:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70358>

"let some sit in the fridge for a few days and let it dry a bit â€“ it gets dark and kinda looks like salami â€“ not raw at all."

Caroline, do you just leave it in there on a wooden board uncovered??? (that sound you hear is my father rolling in his grave)

.

I'm eating raw fish and oysters, scallop ceviche, and I tried some lamb heart but I think I would do better eating it partially frozen. I don't like the texture of raw shrimp. I guess I have some more experimenting to do. But at least now i prefer my fish raw rather than cooked. With wasabi and a bit of organic, GF tamari (I know, I need to find something else).

By the way, I looked at the ingredients of my wasabi: it is basically horseradish with a thickener like corn starch and some green food coloring. Yuck. Good thing I like horseradish, I'll mix it with some lime juice and see how I like that.

### diane — 2013-01-11T21:40:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70363>

I made salmon ceviche and loved it. Of course I included a generous helping of cilantro - and I love it! I have also made steak tartare, but would love a really good recipe for it. I've also done raw steak with just a bit of balsamic vinegar (which had nothing added) and olive oil on it. Or raw fish with some wheat free tamari sauce (i use just a little bit) and pickled ginger.

### DarleenMB — 2013-01-11T22:09:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70364>

Fixed steak tartare tonight and made way too much, LOL. As cooked meat it would have been a good portion but raw ... only got about half down and I was done. I will say it was delicious. Ground sirloin, dijon mustard, capers, balsamic vinegar, olive oil, minced onion, egg yolk, salt and pepper. I'll cook the leftovers for breakfast with some bacon and an egg.

### endless — 2013-01-12T05:26:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/going-raw.5432/post-70376>

I'm definitely leaning towards more raw all the time...this morning I had 1/2 lb raw elk meat for breakfast. I would like to be able to eat raw meat at least once week...if I had the money I'd eat it everyday!

### caroline — 2013-01-12T16:24:35+0200

I have read this story awhile ago - I loved it. I often talk to a Greek man when I go to the rock pool at the ocean. Oddly ...he is one of the few people who is really interested in C/T ...altho has never heard the term of course ... and I can talk to him about cutting out sugars altho he loves Baklava! and he is so interested in being as healthy as possible. I first met him at the rock pool in the winter - he tries to swim everyday. I have convinced him to try ice baths for some very sore leg muscles. His Doc told him to use heat. It was like a revelation to him when I said to follow your own instincts and get more than one opinion when necessary ....\[Doc wants to operate\]. I talk to him alot about what his parents and grandparents and extended family ate in Greece ..... a much simpler life and much more healthy - a lot of seafood and wine { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. # Thread: Banana Bread??
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/banana-bread.5435/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 15
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2013-01-12T16:00:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/banana-bread.5435/post-70430>

I just made a banana bread from Abel James' site. It was quite nice - maybe not as great as he says - but he is a guy! I was looking for things to make because my grandsons were coming for the weekend and I have decided not to participate in their unhealthy eating anymore. They are very healthy and very active but I want to see them stay this way..... so I bought lots of fruit \[in season here\] and dark chocolate and this banana bread ....they loved it and ate quite alot of it. If anyone wants it I am happy to post it...

### vlynnb — 2013-01-12T17:22:05+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/banana-bread.5435/post-70437>

Yes, I want it. Dealing with my grandchildren's usual diets graciously is a big problem for me. I have one set of grandchildren who live just downstairs from me so we eat together often. Another set I take care of on a regular basis in their home as their mother is on chemotherapy right now and I would really like to help all of them eat better but it is so hard to facilitate any change.

### janagram — 2013-01-12T20:29:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/banana-bread.5435/post-70451>

I need that recipe also!! (another grandma)

### Kiera2 — 2013-01-12T21:00:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/banana-bread.5435/post-70453>

http://archive.aweber.com/fatburningman/OqeQs/h/The\_Best\_Banana\_Bread\_of\_Your.htm

### caroline — 2013-01-12T21:00:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/banana-bread.5435/post-70454>

Banana Bread recipe

4 ripe bananas - mine were kind of average size I think \[this is aways a problem with banana bread recipes -they should specify weight\]

4 eggs

1/2 cup Almond butter or cashew butter

4 TBSP GF butter

1 tsp vanilla

mix all above together well in a cuisinart or some such appliance

mix together all the following dry ingredients and then add to wet stuff and mix well

1/2 cup of almond flour

1 tsp each of baking soda and baking powder

1 pinch of sea salt

1 TBSP cinnamon
\[could fold in some chopped nuts if you want\]

grease a 5" x9" loaf tin

add mixture and smooth and then bake at 350 for about 45 minutes if using a metal tin ...maybe 60 min if using glass \[or someone on his site made muffins\]

This goes together really, really fast and doesn't seem to be tempermental at all. I bet it freezes beautifully too

I am so impressed with my self - I have been having a lot of brain fog this past year or so and it turns out I remembered this recipe with no problem and I have only made it once!! If that isn't a win I don't know what is!!!! thanks yet again to J.K. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

If I wasn't explicit enuf please let me know!

### Claudia — 2013-01-13T00:01:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-and-women.5438/post-70467>

How was the weight loss and women webinar? I'm pretty much struggling with a lot, one of those being financial, and was hoping it might be discussed on here. Are the webinars discussed ever?

Would love some insights?

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-13T14:49:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/weight-loss-and-women.5438/post-70531>

I am still trying to make sense of that webinar (partly because I was seriously jet-lagged when I listened). I think my simple take-home message was that women are much more complicated hormonally and also much more sensitive to the environment (we inherit all of our mitochondria from our mothers, so survival depends on women being super-sensitive to the environment, encoding that and passing that down), which means a more complex journey back to healthy homeostasis if our previous ways of eating/living have created health problems, imbalance and/or disease. I think my major take-home was to keep going with what Dr Kruse has laid out, not to give up, and to expect time for changes to happen.

# Thread: Question about Reverse T3 Labs.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-reverse-t3-labs.5446/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2013-01-13T13:13:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-reverse-t3-labs.5446/post-70514>

11/4/11
rT3 24 (range 11-32 ng/dL) ratio with Free T 3-11.3

6/19/12
rT3 28 (range 11-32 ng/dL) ratio with Free T 3-10

12/17/12
rT3 13 (range 11-32 ng/dL) ratio with Free T 3-21.5

Without knowing anything about me, why would my reverse T3 improve? My TSH is 4.45 (range .35-5.50 uIU/mL). I have hypothyroid symptoms and yet my reverse T3 went down. I am not understanding it. { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### vkiernan — 2013-01-13T15:51:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-reverse-t3-labs.5446/post-70548>

Maybe you have corrected the reason you had a high rt3 in the first place whether it be too much stress, surgery or low ferritin. Sometimes happens inside you that makes a high rt3 to slow your body down naturally. The tsh is telling your body to make more t4 which is supposed to make t3 and the fact that your rt3 is reducing maybe means you are trying to make more t3 to get into your cells. That is my .02 cents and that is about all it is worth.

### [email redacted] — 2013-01-13T15:55:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-reverse-t3-labs.5446/post-70549>

vkiernan, thanks for the .02 cents! It's very much appreciated! I am the caregiver to my 92 year old aunt and 91 year old mother. Both went into assisted living at the end of July and I have been very much less stressed.

### vkiernan — 2013-01-13T17:50:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-about-reverse-t3-labs.5446/post-70574>

That darn ole' stress wrecks havoc on the body and does things you would not believe. I hope you can do something to reduce your stress and keep up with the family issues!

# Thread: Thyroid confusion
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-confusion.5447/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lyndra — 2013-01-13T13:32:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-confusion.5447/post-70516>

I've been on low-dose Armour thyroid for several months and have been feeling progressively better (more energy, better mood). When I saw my doctor in mid-November, she thought I was going hyper (was feeling warm a lot and was losing weight without trying) so she had me cut my dose in half (down to 1/4 grain). At that time, my labs were:

TSH 1.67: range 0.45-4.50
Free T3: 2.6, range 2.0-4.4
Free T4: 1.0, range 0.82-1.7
RT3: 17, range 9.2-24.1
FT3/RT3 (calculated at STTM): 15.3 (goal is >20)

A couple of weeks ago, I decided I might be getting hyper because I my pulse was too high, especially at night, and I was too hot and sleeping poorly (though sleep is a constant problem anyway). I stopped taking my 1/4 gr Armour on a January 1 and then ran thyroid tests on January 4. Results were:

TSH 2.67: range 0.45-4.50
Free T3: 2.1, range 2.0-4.4
Free T4: 1.21, range 0.82-1.7
RT3: 9.2, range 9.2-24.1
FT3/RT3 (calculated at STTM): 22.8

So, the good news is my FT3/RT3 ratio is much improved but FT3 is still very low and TSH is fairly high.

As an experiment, after being off Armour for 10 days, I started up again 3 days ago. Ugh. Too hot during the day, waking up hot at night, and high pulse again, so I quit again.

Does it make sense that I would be hyper with low FT3 and high TSH?

I've measured thyroid antibodies 3 times since and they have been stable each time: TPO 9 (0-34) and Antithyroglob <20 (0-40) so I'm thinking it's not Hashimoto's.

I plan to check in with my doctor but am wondering if you all have any ideas?

### vkiernan — 2013-01-13T17:48:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-confusion.5447/post-70572>

I am wondering if you have a low cortisol problem. Hence the reaction to more thyroid med and poor sleep - too much for your system too handle.. Looks like your ft4 is turning into rt3, not ft3 like it is supposed to. Might be a good time to try t3 only since you won't convert that to rt3 once you clear your t4. Got to figure out why you are making rt3 in the first place. Maybe read the All Things T3 thread. Lot's of good info in there.

### Lyndra — 2013-01-13T19:42:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-confusion.5447/post-70600>

Thanks for the response - I know you've been through a lot with thyroid and have done your homework. I have been battling adrenal fatigue for a while now. My cortisol was at the bottom of the range throughout the day when I first tested early this year but was better when I tested a few months ago, though still too low in the AM and too high at night. I tried T3-only early on but it gave me migraines; I'll talk to my doctor about trying another formulation to see if that is better.

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-13T23:32:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-confusion.5447/post-70618>

Stop the Thyroid Madness people also suggest looking at ferritin and iron levels if things are not working with increased thyroid.

### bigknitwit — 2013-01-14T11:23:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-confusion.5447/post-70661>

I am dealing with high pulse too (especially in the morning after breakfast, and sometimes at night just as I'm starting to fall asleep), despite being on a lower than optimal thyroid dose. I think it has to do with cortisol, somehow. I used to think it was low cortisol, however, during episodes of elevated heartrate, I've also noticed that I have far less blood sugar control than otherwise. This leads me to think that in fact my cortisol is elevated (which would inhibit insulin from doing it's thing). Just to compound things even further, on really bad days, I've taken 10mg of hydrocortisone, and it usually helps stabilize my heartrate... So I'm not really sure which way to call it. On days when I sleep poorly, I struggle much more with higher heart rates (I think cortisol would be higher on those days..?).

I will also mention that from my own experimentation, I do not tolerate Armour thyroid, no matter what the labs say. I am doing much better on a combination of T4 and T3 (cytomel). I think it probably has to do with the fact that I can't eat pork without a reaction...

One more thing to mention: I few times I saw d-ribose mentioned on these forums, so I decided to do a little research/reading about it, which lead me to the book, "Fatigued to Fantastic." I've been reading the book, which mainly tries to address issues people with CFS deal with. His perspective on the issues resonates a great deal with the things Dr. K talks about (like the hypothalamus being suppressed, and energy crisis within the cell). So I started taking d-ribose at the dose recommended in the book, and also started acetyl-l-carnitine at the LEF dose recommendation (2000-3000mg/day). I think they are making a real difference for me in dealing with all of this. I have tried countless different supplements over the years, and very rarely can I say that I think I can "feel" a difference taking one. The heart rate issues are diminished (not gone), sleep is a little better (I still struggle, especially if I'm woken up during the night), and my head feels much more, well, normal, as opposed to feeling like I desperately need so kind of stimulation.

I'm not sure if any of this helps, and I apologize if this is coming across as "all about me." I thought sharing about my experiences may help you figure out yours a little...

### Lyndra — 2013-01-14T11:44:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-confusion.5447/post-70664>

BKW - thanks so much for your thoughts. I am also wondering about my cortisol levels and hearing your experiences is very helpful. I thought my adrenal fatigue was doing much better so I started exercising more a couple of weeks ago. It went well for a few days and then big crash - lots of fatigue and poor recovery so I backed off. You and V may both be right about the cortisol situation. Based on 23andme results, i have methylation issues (waiting on a consult with Tim Jackson to learn more) and have a few mito Rx supps on order. I'm thinking this is a complicated situation and there is no one simple answer to improvement. What a way to work on becoming more patient...

### SeaHorse — 2013-01-14T13:11:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-confusion.5447/post-70678>

Bigknitwit...I was wondering how you were the other day...good to hear from you and great news that the supps are making a difference.

# Thread: Low Vitamin D Linked to Headache
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vitamin-d-linked-to-headache.5464/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Thor — 2013-01-14T15:20:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vitamin-d-linked-to-headache.5464/post-70695>

.
Nonmigraine headache is associated with low levels of serum 25-hydroxyvitamin (OH) D, according to results from the sixth survey of the Norwegian TromsÃ¸ Study, TromsÃ¸ 6.

Marie Kjaergaard, MD, University Hospital of North Norway, TromsÃ¸, Norway, and colleagues found that the incidence of nonmigrainous headache was 20% higher in participants who had the lowest levels of serum 25(OH)D than in those with the highest levels.

In contrast, no association between serum 25(OH)D and migraine was identified in the same large population sample.

"\[T\]he VDR \[vitamin D receptor\] and 1-alfahydroxylase, which hydroxylates 25(OH)D to the active metabolite 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, are found in areas in the brain which are thought to be involved in the pathophysiology of headache and migraine, thereby indicating a role of vitamin D in these tissues," the authors write. "We found that low level ~of serum 25(OH)D were associated with non-migraine type of headache, and the association remained significant after adjustment for possible confounders."

The study is published in the November/December 2012 issue of Headache.

Few Case Reports

As investigators point out, only a few case reports have described the effect of vitamin D on headache, and no adequate intervention studies have been done.

Because others have reported a high prevalence of headache in association with high latitude, the authors felt these findings suggest a relationship between headache and vitamin D.

They subsequently explored this potential relationship in 11,614 participants in the sixth survey of the prospective TromsÃ¸ study.

The TromsÃ¸ Study began in 1974. This epidemiologic, prospective study of health problems and chronic diseases serves as a resource for the surveillance of disease risk factors. The study initially focused on cardiovascular disease but through the years also included several other chronic diseases and conditions, such as vitamin D status and chronic pain.

Participants were asked in a questionnaire whether they had experienced headache in the last year. If the answer was yes, they were asked to specify whether the headache was migraine or another type of headache, along with the average frequency, duration, intensity, and type.

A total of 725 participants indicated on the questionnaire that they suffered from migraines, although only about half of these fulfilled the group's criteria for migraine.

Another 3336 participants reported that they had another type of headache, but of these, 1051 fulfilled the group's migraine criteria.

The data were also stratified according to smoking status because the method of measurement used in the study was found after the study was completed, to overestimate serum 25(OH)D in smokers. "To adjust for this, we chose to divide the study population into current smokers and non-smokers," they write, with former smokers included in the nonsmoking group.

"In model 1 with adjustment for age, gender, BMI \[body mass index\] and chronic diseases, the non-smokers had a significantly higher OR \[odds ratio\] for non-migraine type of headache in the lowest quartile," investigators report. "This finding remained significant in model 2 when additional adjustment was done for the possible confounders physical exercise, alcohol consumption, and educational level."

In contrast, no association between headache and serum 25(OH)D was found in the current smoker group.

Risk for Headache Across Serum 25(OH)D Quartiles in Nonsmokers

Serum 25(OH)D

Migraine Headache (Nonsmokers)

Nonmigraine Headache (Nonsmokers)

Model 1

/>4th quartile

Reference

Reference

1st quartile

1.14 (0.76 - 1.71)

1.31 (1.14 - 1.49)

Model 2

4th quartile

Reference

Reference

1st quartile

1.03 (0.67 - 1.57)

1.20 (1.04 - 1.39)

As investigators note, it is important to consider confounding variables in a study such as this one because serum 25(OH)D levels reflect lifestyle and nutritional habits as well, both factors that can affect or be affected by headache.

Physical exercise, alcohol consumption, education level, and some chronic diseases also influence serum(OH)D levels.

The study was supported by the North Norway Regional Health Authority. The authors have disclosed no relevant financial relationships.

Headache. 2012;52:1499-1505. Abstract

Pam Harrison â€¢ Medscape Medical News Â© 2013 WebMD, LLC

http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1111/j.1526-4610.2012.02250.x/abstract~

### Huck — 2013-01-14T15:57:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/low-vitamin-d-linked-to-headache.5464/post-70706>

I started having a headache about 18 months ago and vitamin d was in 50's. It went away in the Spring and stayed gone all Summer. In October it started back up again and I still have it now (January). Vitamin D is now around 85, but over the summer was as high as 110. If I remember to, I will check vitamin d at the point the headache goes away again.

### vkiernan — 2013-01-15T00:01:33+0200

If I remember to, I will check vitamin d at the point the headache goes away again.

Huck, low thyroid causes headaches too. Didn't I just read that you have a rt3 problem? You might have a t3 problem in the winter and thus the seasonal headache. Just a thought from my own n=1. I really don't have headaches anymore since I am on enough thyroid med. I use to have them daily. Not saying it isn't from low D as my dh said his headaches became less when I was having him supp with D3, A & K2. Also mag at night though so we aren't sure which helped.

Headache came back later but not as strong as usual.

# Thread: Earthing with humidified air
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-with-humidified-air.5468/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### bigknitwit — 2013-01-14T18:37:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/earthing-with-humidified-air.5468/post-70742>

Dr K has mentioned the importance of earthing - having contact with the ground as a source of electrons needed by the body. So I started looking at different options for grounding one's bed, etc. Then Dh said that if I'm after being electrically grounded, what we need is a proper humidifier in the house, and possibly an ionizer to improve it. And this is why static electricity is not a problem in the summer (sufficient humidity in the air), compared with the winter (inside anyways). So, I am wondering would air that has a reasonable humidity level be sufficient as an electron source for the body? It's like the WiFi of earthing devices... What I need is a voltmeter, my humidifier (which needs cleaning), and a little bit of experimenting. Thoughts?

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-14T20:54:42+0200

So, I am wondering would air that has a reasonable humidity level be sufficient as an electron source for the body? It's like the WiFi of earthing devices... What I need is a voltmeter, my humidifier (which needs cleaning), and a little bit of experimenting. Thoughts?

Interesting theory. I'd like to know the answer (I haven't a clue)... especially because there is no static electricity in my house in winter that I have noticed. But then, it is uninsulated and unheated. Maybe instead of a humidifier you can just turn off your heat and open your windows { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. **Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/find-a-spring.5470/post-70860>

Found this through Dr Mercola's site. Search for a spring near you and go fill up your jugs. http://www.findaspring.com/springs-by-region/

I'm thinking of taking a drive to Mt Airy (Mayberry), NC. Here's a description of the water (from comments)

> Calcium, sodium, sulfate and magnesium are the predominate minerals found in the spring water. Each of these is considered beneficial for various aliments and maladies. Mineral waters vary in their medicinal effects depending on the various water components and the configurations of those components. Today, these essential nutrients are all too often lacking in the industrialized nations where there has been a high increase in the consumption of refined, fat rich and processed foods.

Sounds Epi-Paleo to me. Nice drive in the mountains, time for a good hike, plenty of outdoors time, and some great water.

# Thread: Something I've noticed about body odour. (Possibly TMI)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-ive-noticed-about-body-odour-possibly-tmi.5475/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### furface — 2013-01-15T14:06:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-ive-noticed-about-body-odour-possibly-tmi.5475/post-70877>

I've been on the Leptin Rx (Leptin Reset) since December 3rd of last year, and one thing I've been noticing is that it seems as if my body odour (esp in the armpits) is not nearly as "strong" as it was before I began the Leptin Rx. I also don't seem to be sweating as much as I was...and I used to sweat alot! I would sweat while standing in a freezer!

I mean I still sweat/perspire of course, but not nearly as much. Maybe this has to do with the fact that I've lost about 30 lbs. But I'm still obese, maybe it has something to do with my new eating plan/regimen, or a combination of all of these factors, but I'm wondering if all of this is "normal" for someone on the Leptin Reset?

I'm not complaining mind you, just curious. Esp about the body odour not being as strong as it was before. Is this a sign that I may be starting to slowly inch toward becoming Leptin Sensitive? I know I still have a long way to go before I am, but is this the start of that happening?

Thanks.

And sorry if this was TMI.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/something-ive-noticed-about-body-odour-possibly-tmi.5475/post-70991>

### Glen PDQ — 2013-01-15T23:41:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/any-sleep-technicians-here.5480/post-70985>

I'm curious if there are any sleep techs on the forums because I recently became interested in possibly pursuing a career in this field. It would be great to hear what others have to say about it. One of the concerns I have is how often during a week does a sleep tech work the night shift. This might sound like a "duh" question, but are there sleep techs that specialize in monitoring shift workers? If so then perhaps I could pursue that and avoid the danger that comes with working the night shift. I'm in Texas and I gather that sleep techs here must be certified. I checked around and a school in TX has a sleep tech program and charges over $3000 for the 10-day course. Is this the best way to get into the field or would it be better to begin working as a sleep trainee somewhere? I'm not exactly sure how one would go about that except perhaps find some local sleep clinics and apply for entry level positions there. Hope someone can give some insight into this field.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-are-invited-free-new-years-qa-with-dr-jack-kruse-tonight.5485/post-71215>

> **Anne said**
>
> That really was extraordinarily generous of the both of you, Misty and Jack.
> Thank you both so much.
> Anne.
> (freesia)

You said it, Anne. So generous to answer all those questions, in such detail. I left after 3.5 hours, way past my bedtime. Thanks so much for your time!!

### Rick — 2013-01-17T11:38:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/you-are-invited-free-new-years-qa-with-dr-jack-kruse-tonight.5485/post-71220>

I concur, thank you Jack and Misty! Logged on at 10 min to kickoff but then had connectivity problems and missed the first half-hour, so I wanted to ask a couple questions of the attendees:

1\. I totally get the concept that athletes should be burning fat for fuel primarily rather than re-feeding/fueling on fruit and sweet potatoes (ala Robb W. and crew), but Jack answered this question and I totally missed what he was saying, something about getting the mitochondria to burn lipid fuels more efficiently with D-ribose? Can anyone elaborate please on Jack's answer? I do understand that lipolysis generates far more ATP than gylcolysis; I was most interested in what Jack said about how long this takes and why many athletes fail on ketogenic Paleo templates (aside from not doing epiPaleo) and how D-ribose improves mitochondrial fat burning to let athletes become the most efficient fat burners?

2\. The second reply to my question on chromium picolinate I did hear, but just wanted others' opinions on Jack's answer: so he said that Cr3 picolinate can release -OH ions which in the literature has been shown to damage DNA (while other literature, RA Anderson was one researcher I believe, says Cr3(pic) is perfectly safe even in mod to high doses so Jack seemed to be saying the conflicting results have more to do with the test subjects' context. High level athletes that have great serum Zeta potentials and excellent mitochondria will be able to clear the hydroxyl ions and avoid oxidative damage. But, since Cr3(pic) is helpful in activating the low molecular weight chromium binding molecule (LMWCr) which amplifies peripheral tissue insulin sensitivity, if you use the picolinate to help diabetics control serum,glucose, they could be at risk for DNA damage at mod to high doses because they already have poor mitochondrial function and Zeta potentials?

So I'm wondering if using Cr3(pic) for diabetics should keep dosing to <= 200 mcg/day and also would it be wise to add L-arabinose with the chromium for its synergistic effect on carb metabolism?

Anyone have any thoughts on either of these points please chime in and I thank you in advance for your input!

Rick

# Thread: Nutrition for Myelin in Infants  Adults
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-for-myelin-in-infants-adults.5492/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2013-01-16T16:27:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-for-myelin-in-infants-adults.5492/post-71094>

The past few weeks I have been turned on to the significance of myelin to brain function and development. I have a 4 month old baby girl, so on top of trying to get to optimal myself, I have been trying to learn as much as possible about the development of babies and especially how their brain develops as they grow older. While my current understanding of all of the topics involved with these areas is limited, I feel I have a decent start thanks in large part to the articles on this site and books like "The Other Brain" which I have been devouring.

While there is a lot of information saying that the brain stops myelinating around 20 years old, the evidence provided by Dr. Wahls and others that have reversed MS and other brain degenerating disease suggests that this isn't exactly correct. I was wondering what people here think about this subject and if anyone can point me to any information they found valuable in learning about brain development in children as well as repair / maintenance in adults? What specific properties of food aid in this process? What types of exercise and learning methods work the best for children in regards to this? I know that brain development is a very broad and intense subject, so any other topics that you think I might find interesting are welcome also.

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-16T17:07:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-for-myelin-in-infants-adults.5492/post-71107>

> **[email redacted] said**
>
> The past few weeks I have been turned on to the significance of myelin to brain function and development. I have a 4 month old baby girl, so on top of trying to get to optimal myself, I have been trying to learn as much as possible about the development of babies and especially how their brain develops as they grow older. While my current understanding of all of the topics involved with these areas is limited, I feel I have a decent start thanks in large part to the articles on this site and books like "The Other Brain" which I have been devouring.
> While there is a lot of information saying that the brain stops myelinating around 20 years old, the evidence provided by Dr. Wahls and others that have reversed MS and other brain degenerating disease suggests that this isn't exactly correct. I was wondering what people here think about this subject and if anyone can point me to any information they found valuable in learning about brain development in children as well as repair / maintenance in adults? What specific properties of food aid in this process? What types of exercise and learning methods work the best for children in regards to this? I know that brain development is a very broad and intense subject, so any other topics that you think I might find interesting are welcome also.

For social-emotional development as it relates to mylenation, HPA axis and optimal brain development, my fave is Dr Allan Schore.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-18T08:22:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-for-myelin-in-infants-adults.5492/post-71369>

hi byboozian, for now I think that your milk is the best. When I imagine paleo-people, I am sure that after stopping to nurse, they have started to chew real food for their babies (probably even while nursing to adapt them to real food).
And then the children ate the same as their parents, so if you show the good example and you keep the baby away from all that neolitic food, its brain will develop best. I think that vaccination might pose a problem too, so avoid it if you feel confident enough. I have heard terrible stories here about children who developed severe problems after vaccination.
You might want to look into Montessori Kindergarten and School. I prefer this to the Steiner Schools, as children can decide themselves what they want to learn and at which pace and how deep they want to get into a subject. (my youngest son went there)
Then there is a book I read when my children were small which is from a couple in Russia who made an interesting experiment with their 7 children. It is called "the Nikitin children". They were both teachers, tried to have the government let them do an experiment with school children which they did not get permission for. From the first day of birth, they implemented what they considered best and one of the things they did, was keep the babies from day No. 1 in a very cold room, only with sparce clothing. The babies did not mind, but became very active kicking to keep warm (shows, how intelligent the body is). They were not rich, and one of the parents always stayed with the children as grandmothers, neighbours and others thought they were crazy and tried to sneak around to "save" the babies from their parents. I think they grew most of their food themselves (its been a long time I read it).
Okay, if you are interested, just get the book. I only give you the quintessence of it. These children were extremele sociable, very intelligent (the house looked like a school with chemistry-physics and other tools), very early independent. One son skipped several classes and went to university at 15 yrs. Two daughters were among the world class gymnasts, and so on. Depending on their particular innate capabilities, they developed extraordinary skills. Its a book worth reading and at the end, they give details about games they developed and played with the children to enhance their skills. Important to say was, that the kids were never forced to do anything, but for example when the dad saw, that one of the children showed interest, he immediately went and asked if he could participate.

### Charlotte — 2013-01-18T08:36:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nutrition-for-myelin-in-infants-adults.5492/post-71370>

tried to find the english version on amazon, but no luck. Either it was never translated or amazon does not have it. I need to dig deeper. Will let you know, if you are interested. Could only find the book in German.

### [email redacted] — 2013-01-21T13:31:34+0200

I have also noticed our baby is very active and when she is awake is moving around almost constantly, it makes sense that she is doing this since we keep the house fairly cool in the winter.

# Thread: The Trivium Method of Education
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-trivium-method-of-education.5495/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2013-01-16T17:14:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-trivium-method-of-education.5495/post-71110>

I haven't seen this topic mentioned in the forum, although judging from most of the people posting here I would guess that the majority of you already practice it whether you know it or not. In a nutshell, the Trivium is a method of learning that consists of grammar (who, what, when, where) logic (why) and rhetoric (how). The order of those is very important and when practiced it allows one to become auto didactic or, a self learner. It teaches people how to spot logical fallacies and dissect arguments systematically so you don't get caught up with emotional or unrelated subjects.

This method of teaching and education was used in America up until around 1890's and is a major reason why one teacher was able to teach 30 different students of all different ages while getting better results than the modern school system does. The teacher would show the student how to learn a subject, which allowed that student to translate the same skills of learning to any subject he or she choose in the future so they didn't require a teacher to learn a new skill or topic.

Putting the things I've learned while studying the trivium into practice is the main reason I have ended up on this site and it has helped me more than anything else to cut through the BS you find on the subject of nutrition. Hopefully some of the other people on here will find this a useful tool in the future. The website below is where first learned about it and although some of the interviews are a bit eccentric, I found it extremely useful.

http://www.triviumeducation.com/

### ashryn — 2013-01-17T07:40:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-trivium-method-of-education.5495/post-71188>

# Thread: My labs-schnitzeljagd
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-schnitzeljagd.5504/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### AdrianaDr. Radler-Pohl — 2013-01-17T09:53:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-schnitzeljagd.5504/post-71207>

Probably you guys here are wondering what the german term "schnitzeljagd" means. Well - as kids we had a fantastic game which erveryone had lots of fun. We would choose some who would be the "treasure" we would hunt and then they would go around leaving little traces everywhere eg. on the ground, or a piece of writing with a puzzel .... leading to the next trace .... and so on - of couse they did it without the others watching often the day before ..... then the hunt would start .... and the little pieces would eventually lead to the hidingplace. It was always a lot of fun and huray. ..... But this is the way I'm feeling on my way to optimal. Bits and pieces here and there .... a puzzle here and there leading to the ..... TADA!!! ... next puzzle. OK - done complaining ... was mad this morning missing the Q&A!

I got my recent labs and would appreciate some input ... THX in advance everybody ... 53 yr. old female, postmenopausal, breastcancer 6 years ago

ASI:
6am 8220pg/ml (1800-14500). ...Jack says to be in the top quartile ... def. not!
8am. 3166pg (1300-10300)
12am 2010pg (700-5700)
14pm 2296pg (600-4700)
6pm. 1356pg (300-3300)

Now the steroids (saliva) morning sample
DHEA 542pg/ml (90-460) ??? too much?
E2. 1,5pg (,2)
Estriol 5,0 pg (<21,0pg morning, 1:200) sucks! right?
Testosteron 35,6pg/ml

... its not that but ... exept the ratio ... Or is the cortisol too low?

Thyroid panel

TSH. 2,81mIU/L ( 0.27 - 4.2 mIU)
fT3. 4,4 pmol/L. (3.1 - 6.8)
fT4. 19.3 pmol/L (12 - 22)

Antibodys Thyreoperoxidase 10.6 kIU ( < 34 kIU) .... some say Hashi when you have some ... even when low ... hmm.

VitD 25 (OH) 268nmol/L ... (62,5 - 170) ... guess I over did it ... backing off right now
Vit 1,25 (OH) 102 pg/ml. (17-53)

Ferritin 307mcg/L (15 - 150) !!! WHTF???! ... if you excuse my German language ...
CRPs. 1,14. mg/L

In a later test The Doc here suggested to test Serotonin ....
Serotonine 81.82 mcg/g Kreatinine (148 -230) !!!! ... another WHTF ....

Well - that has been a lot of typing ... and Im really bad and slow ... so thanks for your input ... it is very much appreciated!!!

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### KiwiLauren — 2013-01-17T16:03:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-schnitzeljagd.5504/post-71257>

I am pretty bad at labs, so take this with a grain of 'salz'....

You didn't appear to test Progesterone. I think you need to. You want your ratio with E2. And you want to know how you're doing with P, especially with a history of breast cancer. If you're not supplementing estrogen, I would imagine that estriol would be low postmenopausally, so I wouldn't worry too much.

Re: vitamin D, I'd try to convert those to the US units. When my Vitamin D tested 'high' and I converted it to US units (from European ones like you have) it turned out I was still below where Dr Kruse recommends! I think the reference ranges listed only reflect what that lab/country recommend.

You seem to have good FT4 but aren't converting it well into FT3. That could mean high reverse T3. Or something completely different - perhaps Patty has ideas here?

The good news with your ASI is that you've got the right curve/pattern occuring, which is super. I don't see melatonin, though. Did you test that? That would be helpful info.

I don't know about that high DHEA. Are you supplementing? If not, perhaps I'd be concerned... any PCOS symptoms?

And re: high iron... I think (if I've understood properly) that you could be one of those iron accumulators (he talks about this in one of the BG posts) and also that this can be a sign of poor blood viscosity... looks like the Mitochondrial Rx for you.

Viel Gluck!

### AdrianaDr. Radler-Pohl — 2013-01-17T17:00:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-labs-schnitzeljagd.5504/post-71265>

THX Kiwilauren ... so sweet to give me your thoughts ... actually I did test Progesterone but with my lousy typing I missed it ....

Progesteron 70pg/ml ... postmenopausal range 10-50pg/ml .... so my ratio is 0,021 and should be > 1/200! ... but in my Consult with Jack he was betting that it was higher than this .... he was right spot on ... sigh!

.... could someone give me a hint about in whitch blog of the BG series this ironcollector was mentioned .... Im only familiar with Estrogen collectors ... Im learning new things every day ...

and ..... my VitD 25(OH) is equivalent to 107 ng/ml for american standard ... divided by 2.5 is the conversion to ng ...

### Kiera2 — 2013-01-18T21:32:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/90-day-optimal-reset-labs.5510/post-71519>

I figured the first paragraph was a typo. I think the three tests are in the 2nd paragraph: HDL, DHEA, Vit D. He mentions HDL three times there. I hope so, because I can't convince my GP to do an hsCRP.

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-04T07:45:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/90-day-optimal-reset-labs.5510/post-74038>

My doctor today wouldn't order a DHEA test for me. He suggested he refer me to an endocrinologist if I wanted hormone panels done as he didn't know much about them. I don't really want to go down that road as seeing specialists is incredibly expensive. But I am getting the hs CRP and vitamin D done.

He only wanted me to have the ordinary CRP as the "hsCRP is so sensitive its meaningless. Everyone has inflammation so it doesn't tell us anything." But he did write it up. I offered to pay for the reverse T3 twice and I'm not sure if its in the shorthand version of the request for tests - he's ordered TFTs - will cover that. He was very resistant to anything that wasn't in his usual work up panel. You should have seen the shock and disgust on his face when I dared mention leptin resistance in passing in the conversation about rT3. He wouldn't order an HbA1c as it wasn't necessary unless the insulin was very high.

He had no idea what a VO2 Max was or where I might get it, nor where I could get a neuro-cognitive tests as outlined in Jack's optimising labs blog, other than, after a discussion about what it was and why I considered it important, suggesting I consult a physio for the former and a registered psychologist for the latter.

I was not impressed but he was at least polite to my face so it could have been much worse. And all these tests are being paid for by the government - so once I get the results I might be able to get the other things I need from another doctor who is likely to work only outside the government system.

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-04T07:47:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/90-day-optimal-reset-labs.5510/post-74039>

Tomorrow though I can't have my BAB within 30 minutes of waking as the blood test place doesn't open up until 2 hours after I would normally eat. But I guess that's permissible on one day.

### caroline — 2013-02-04T09:40:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/90-day-optimal-reset-labs.5510/post-74044>

I ordered a saliva cortisol panel from ZRT labs - no script

### ashryn — 2013-02-04T11:28:47+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/90-day-optimal-reset-labs.5510/post-74052>

AN, I am going to see my doctor tomorrow to ask for the same things.. But not vo2 max.. That's a fitness test. You can do one at a gym or I know Murdoch uni is set up to do them.. Dexa scans too.
You can do the cognitive tests online.. Just google them. There's some free sites.
Based on your experience I am going to say to my GP that I don't need her to interpret for me because I have the resources at home, but I can't monitor my progress and spot any changes without having the correct tests to check against in the first place. Wish me luck with that approach!
i'm not sure she will order the hbA1c because it is specifically recommended for diabetics, and the heart disease indications it used to be used for have been largely disproven now so if she's up to date in her reading she will know this, then again, if she's not, I'll be able to use the "I'm eating a load of saturated fats now and want to monitor the signs closely" line and see how that works out.. I'm also taking my DD in for the same tests because she's doing the reset as well, and I'm hoping it will add some weight because she's so young..

All this is under the assumption that the Dr will freak at the high fat mod protein no carbs concept, and want me to get all the tests that could possibly prove me wrong!

This is the doctor that gives me duramine without an appointment these days despite it being an addictive amphetamine (or analogue) with some serious side effects (stroke for one) that broke my metabolism beyond belief before I wised up and stopped taking it. Every time I go in to see her she is looking fatter and more tired.. Probably from following the CW diet advice. Maybe I'll be able to convert her to paleo when she sees my results!

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-05T07:49:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/90-day-optimal-reset-labs.5510/post-74253>

Ashryn, how did you get on with your doctor? Its my experience that they listen to you then take notice of your requests and they do as little as they can unless they absolutely agree with you. What annoyed me is that he ignored what I was saying and didn't have the courtesy to say he wouldn't be prepared to let me have the rT3.

Still waiting for Jack to get back to me with his assessments of what labs I need. Not sure how long that takes him but no doubt he will get to my history when time permits.

### ashryn — 2013-02-05T12:09:18+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/90-day-optimal-reset-labs.5510/post-74263>

Great news! My doc ordered every test I asked for, except salivary cortisol because she said the labs don't like them because they don't think they are accurate enough (suppose it depends on what you're testing for, but whatevs..) I'll bet I can get my medical scientist friend to order it for me.

she did say she isn't sure if the RT3 is covered by Medicare, but ordered it anyway.. She took a look at my list and listened to the reasons I wanted them all, and just started writing them all down. DD and I are going in on Monday morning ( she has busy work this week and can't take any time off) to get them all done.

Dr didnt even balk at the hba1c which I thought she would.
She asked about whether it is that french diet the high protein high fat one.. i said no, it is a variation on the paleo diet with additional things like observing circadian cycles to make sure you produce melatonin at the right time, and eating a large breakfast to help reset the leptin receptors that allow you to not store every single piece of carbohydrate as fat.. she said 'that all sounds pretty reasonable', and asked how I found the 'new diet' I said sheepishly, "the Internet", expecting her to do her narna, but she asked more and wrote down the name! She seemed interested and I think she might do more reading about it. How cool is that?

I can imagine it must be a refreshing change for a GP to see a patient that actually wants to take responsibility for their health.

The only thing I have ever gone to see her about in the last decade is being fat.. And to get referral to a physio after a car accident... She has watched me try everything and knows nothing has worked (at least nothing that's sustainable) so when she heard about how well this worked for me in the beginning, and how well I am feeling in general, maybe she was willing to entertain a new idea, or at least trust me that I have done some thorough research. She was probably happy that I wanted to test to make sure everything is good rather than just play around with myself and possibly cause some problems..

Whatever the reason, I am very happy with her response, now it just remains to be seen how many of them the lab will do on Medicare.

Nana, I have had that experience with every single male Dr I have been to, and one female who tried to convince me to get an abortion when I was 18. Sometimes I think all those years of studying just convince them that they are Einstein and the rest of us are monkeys.. Then again, it is possible that when it comes to medical care, most of us are monkeys, I guess it would be just as easy to get jaded in that profession as any other. Maybe more..

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-06T08:50:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/90-day-optimal-reset-labs.5510/post-74399>

That's great, Ashryn. i'll wait and see how my tests have gone and may then go and see your doc to see if I can pick up the others.

# Thread: Looking for suggestions for ways to track changes in cognition and memory
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-suggestions-for-ways-to-track-changes-in-cognition-and-memory.5515/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Optimalbound — 2013-01-17T16:10:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/looking-for-suggestions-for-ways-to-track-changes-in-cognition-and-memory.5515/post-71258>

I have at long last managed to get my 86 year old mother's doctor to take her off statins (Simvastatin). Yay!

My mother has experienced a rapid and noticable decline in memory and other cognitive abilities over the last year or so, and it has been a very disturbing and distressing process to witness. I haven't established exactly for how long my mother has been on Simvastatin (she no longer remembers...) but i reckon it has been maybe three or four years.

I am very anxious to see if any of these symptoms will be reversed too some degree now that she is off Simvastatin, and I am looking for advice on how to objectively detect any such changes. I have googled a bit, but haven't found anything useful yet. Are there good and simple tests to measure memory and cognitive skills in a manner that is precise enough to be useful for detecting progress?

Any advice is very much appreciated.

# Thread: Sea-crop - for your bath?

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 9

### freesia — 2013-01-17T23:19:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sea-crop-for-your-bath.5519/post-71332>

Sea Crop is distilled from the Pacific Ocean and is generally used as a nutrient for the garden.
https://www.facebook.com/pages/Sea-Crop/206610026133103

Back, more than a year ago when I was learning how to grow photosynthetic bacteria and some others with paramagnetic qualities, { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

I stumbled into this slightly strange, nonetheless charming, community of people experimenting with all kinds of applications.

I didn't use Sea-Crop myself, but I know it was very highly rated. I don't know for sure, maybe someone else does, but perhaps it was a good electron donor? Or the balance of minerals enhanced electron uptake? Anyone have an opinion on this?
I can't get here, sadly. But if I could...I would absolutely put some in my earthed ice bath!
As it is I use my homegrown bacteria, seaweed, and mg chloride, but it's still a LONG way from the Pacific Ocean.

If anyone does use it, check in here with a report!

I feel a bit bad after the Q and A last night. The beach is right at the end of my street. Half a block away...and I don't use it nearly enough. It's not cold enough most of the year for CT the way I like it, but I really need to take better advantage anyway and get my feet in the wet sand more.
..I don't generally make New Year's resolutions but this year I just did, and that's it!

### caroline — 2013-01-17T23:37:49+0200

This winter will be different and I'm really looking forward to it. Meanwhile my dog is really going to love some extra walks along the water's edge.

here's what I use:
Mg Malate - I use jigsaw mg malate www.jigsawhealth.com
mg Threonate - I use LEF www.lef.org or Amazon

d-Ribose - I use LEF
CoQ10 - I buy the ubiquinoal in the red bottle at costco 100mg capsules
acetyl -L Carnatine - Now brands - amazon.com

haven't ordered taurine yet, but probably need to...

At iherb LEF d-Ribose is almost double the price of another brand. so - generally speaking - are LEF products better? cheaper can be just as good but I suspect sometimes not. I have been using LEF Vit D3 and very impressed compared to supermarket brands that I would just pick up and go. On the other hand maybe my Vit D has come up because of hormones I am taking.
There must be a benchmark tho for quality? I would sooner pay more but get the best quality. Is this an impossible ask??

### Shijin13 — 2013-01-18T20:50:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brands-of-magnesium-d-ribose-etc.5521/post-71511>

I use Now brand for a lot of stuff - b/c its cheaper and has less fillers... but for dhea and pregnenolone I've found I do better with the lef brand... just my n=1

not everyone is going to respond the same....to the same supplements..

### Theta — 2013-01-18T20:55:06+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-brands-of-magnesium-d-ribose-etc.5521/post-71514>

Thor, I checked the lb of D-Ribose at

http://store.metabolicmaintenance.com/ProductInfo.aspx?productid=00621

and found it to be $87 ...not $45.

Will you direct me to the $45 link?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/grounding-shoes.5530/post-71617>

Clean room booties usually contain a grounding strap. It is fixed to the bottom of the bootie, and the best place to put it is under your feet inside the shoe. These booties are disposable, but the grounding straps can be recycled and worn with any other shoe.
