when you render tallow or lard there is no smell.

### Lyndra — 2013-02-12T20:02:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tallow-for-the-skin.5810/post-75570>

Interesting, there was a distinct smell when I rendered both tallow and lard here (reportedly pastured raised animals).

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-12T20:18:40+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tallow-for-the-skin.5810/post-75575>

I just made some with a little lavender oil and something called Peace & Calming oil. It smells good. I will try it after it chills down and has that buttery feeling to it.

### nonchalant — 2013-02-12T21:45:36+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tallow-for-the-skin.5810/post-75594>

For 3 days now I have pried off a small chunk of tallow from the jar in the fridge (I rendered it a year ago) and rubbed it on my hand with the dermatitis/eczema/whatever rash. I like the effect. I first dunk my hand in water with sea salt, epsom salts, and seaweed, let the back of my hand air dry, and then rub on the tallow. I used to do this several times a day, using olive oil after the dunk. And if I am diligent, my rash stays away. But the tallow is now allowing me to just treat my hand once/day, with no itching later in the day. I can barely tell the difference now in the skin of both hands!

Thanks so much, Rachelle, for suggesting this!

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-15T00:55:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tallow-for-the-skin.5810/post-75908>

I made the tallow and added essential oils. It stunk when I was making it (definitely grass fed bones) and it stunk when it was finished. I wish I could afford to add an entire bottle of lavender oil! In any case, the smell is mostly gone with the addition of lavender and lemon oils. I have been using it on my feet (it has a beautiful velvety consistency that I love - it's like what I imagine super expensive lotions to be like) and my feet have never been so baby soft (and that's with going most of the time barefoot, too!).

### Rachelle — 2013-02-15T19:03:11+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tallow-for-the-skin.5810/post-75990>

When I rendered my tallow and lard it did not stink much when done. I don't get it.

Anyway, I have also thought about adding some CoQ10 to the mixture next time I make it. What do you think? I hear it is good for skin.

# Thread: Thyroid antibodies - is anybody at zero?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-antibodies-is-anybody-at-zero.5836/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lyndra — 2013-02-11T01:27:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-antibodies-is-anybody-at-zero.5836/post-75304>

I've had mine tested several times and they've been very stable but non-zero. TPO at 9 with a range of 0-34 and antithyroglobulin at 20 (range of <40). I'm happy they're not rising but am thinking they should both be at zero, ideally. Just wondering how many people out there have no detectable levels of thyroid ABs...

### BJK77 — 2013-02-11T01:46:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-antibodies-is-anybody-at-zero.5836/post-75311>

I have never had any detectable levels. I've always been shocked by the results, but I suppose I'm one of the few with just functional hypothyroidism and not true Hashi's. Now my T3 (and even T4), on the other hand, is usually all over the place. I'm still working on stabilizing that, but I have been making some great progress thanks to compounded T3 and Naturethroid.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-11T04:23:00+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-antibodies-is-anybody-at-zero.5836/post-75352>

Mine are ridiculous (high) even with cutting all the AI foods, doing the Leptin Rx, Mito Rx, balancing hormones, etc . Peri-meno may have something to do with it. Started taking 400 mcg of selenium/day recently so hopefully I can test in the next couple of months and maybe they will be down a little. Oh and I bet EMF has a lot to do with the antibodies too. Maybe cutting more EMF stuff will help. Basically no help from me!

### Lyndra — 2013-02-11T19:10:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-antibodies-is-anybody-at-zero.5836/post-75405>

Thanks for the replies ladies.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-11T19:39:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/thyroid-antibodies-is-anybody-at-zero.5836/post-75409>

I just checked my labs and compared June with the October results.

TPO 17 / 5 (a little improvement)

Antithyroglobulin <20 / <20

These two labs weren't included with those ran at my physical. Going to have to request them at my next appt.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-11T04:05:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/awesome-seafood-stew.5840/post-75344>

_The family includes Datura, Mandragora (mandrake), Atropa belladonna (deadly nightshade), Lycium barbarum (wolfberry), Physalis philadelphica (tomatillo) , Physalis peruviana (Cape gooseberry flower), **Capsicum (chili pepper, bell pepper)**, Solanum (potato, tomato, eggplant), Nicotiana (tobacco), and Petunia. With the exception of tobacco (Nicotianoideae) and petunia (Petunioideae), most of the economically important genera are contained in the subfamily Solanoideae._

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solanaceae](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Solanaceae)

Ahhh....I miss tomatillos too! Used to make verde sauce all the time. Oh well, all I can say is it's all worth it as I feel so much better.

# Thread: How to convert DHEA result
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### diane — 2013-02-11T03:33:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75334>

I got my labs back, but can't figure out if my DHEA level is good or bad. These are Canadian units. Here's what it says.

DHEA-S 4.8 UMOL/L

For my age range it says <6.5

It has a note \*REVIEW RESULT\* - so I don't know if this is good or bad.

I've tried to look up conversion tables but can't seem to find one that converts the right units.

Thanks!

### caroline — 2013-02-11T06:11:49+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75367>

Diane - didn't it give you a range? Have you called the lab?
I mean - like a low end to high end range?

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-11T07:23:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75368>

As I read it. the range should be less than 6.5. Dianne's is 4.8 which is less than 6.5. However Jack may have a different range for optimal. I've made some notes that Jack would like it at the upper range for women. Women's normal ref range is 65-380 u/dL and ideal is 250-380 u/dL, only I'm not sure that my written u is the same as your umol or what I think of as mu/dL. I'll see if I can find anything.

I'm not sure about conversion tables but there is probably one out there somewhere.

### Aussie Nana — 2013-02-11T07:33:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75369>

I found the following
Dehydroepiandrosterone (DHEA) ng/mL (conventional unit) 3.47 nmol/L (SI unit)

to convert from the conventional unit to the SI unit, multiply by the conversion factor;
to convert from the SI unit to the conventional unit, divide by the conversion factor.

on website: [http://www.unc.edu/~rowlett/units/scales/clinical\_data.html](http://www.unc.edu/~rowlett/units/scales/clinical_data.html)

Now is that umol the same as the nmol? and we have one in decilitres and one in litres.

So if we divide the 4.8 by the 3.47 we get almost 1.3. Now we have problems with the decimal places, so would that make the reading 13?

Perhaps someone more au fait with working this stuff out could help us here.

### diane — 2013-02-11T15:07:35+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75379>

Hi Aussie Nana. That's the same conversion that I found - but the numbers don't make sense compared to the other ranges posted on here, because they don't even come close. So there must be something I'm missing in the conversion.

### endless — 2013-02-11T18:14:21+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75395>

I get 184 by following this example....

> Hi, My DHEA-S levels are reported in metric(?) I want to follow the DHEA supplementation guidelines that are given in the book "Healing HIV" by Jon Kaiser but I have a difficult conversion problem. My lab report says I have 8 umol/L of DHEA-S but I need to know how much that is in ug/dl. This seems to be a really tough problem. Thank you.
>
>
>
> Response from Dr. Holodniy
>
> Well, let's see if my math makes sense. To convert from SI units to ug/dL, divide 8umol/L by (conversion factor 2.6) = 3.07, multiply by 100 = 307 ug/dL.

### diane — 2013-02-12T20:25:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75577>

Thanks Krista!

Then it looks like mine is still on the low side...but on the positive side, it's up from the last time I had it tested!

### ashryn — 2013-02-14T11:53:25+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75797>

My lab gave me a range of 1.65-9.15 umol/L. If that helps?

### diane — 2013-02-14T15:00:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75809>

Ah - yes it does. Thanks Ashryn! And AUS and CA use the same units - so that's helpful!

I'm not sure why the lab here uses only being under a certain value. Then it makes doctors think it's ok as long as it's under a certain value - the same way they measure cholesterol.

### ashryn — 2013-02-14T15:05:19+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75811>

I just posted all my labs in my journal with all the ranges if you need any others for your reference.

### diane — 2013-02-14T15:17:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-convert-dhea-result.5841/post-75815>

Awesome! I was just looking at them and comparing to mine (although yours were more extensive). It's funny how the ranges are different on many of the tests. No wonder we are confused as patients!

# Thread: Reactions to Non-Organic Food?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Eve — 2013-02-11T22:03:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-75436>

Does anyone else with "leaky gut" have reactions to non-organic food? For example, I can't do butter. I've tried organic and the reaction is mild (e.g., some thirst, fatigue, sometimes mild cramping). But when I tried regular, non-organic butter (ingredients: cream -- so no additives), the reaction is three times as bad (e.g., very thirsty whole day, headache, fatigue, feel hungover for that day and most of next day).

I'm starting to wonder if these symptoms are really "leaky gut" or something else.......

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-11T22:50:24+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-75440>

if its only dairy - it could be the casein

if its all non-organic foods (Assuming real food not processed foods), it could be pesticides, it could be the hormones, and antibiotics, could be Genetically Modified....

### Eve — 2013-02-12T03:17:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-75481>

It happens with all non-organic food, not just dairy, so I think it's more than issue with casein. If I eat organic almond butter I have no reaction or symptoms, but if I try regular almond butter I have a reaction.

I understand there are toxins in non-organic food, usually a minute amount (e.g., in just a pat of non-organic butter, but I thought the body somehow accounts for this and filters it. I don't understand why my reaction to non-organic food is so extreme.

### bigknitwit — 2013-02-12T14:01:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-75521>

Your experience is very interesting to me. I am having issues with all kinds of foods (symptoms much like you describe), and my list of foods I tolerate seems to be getter shorter (just lost beef and chicken). But now I'm wondering if it was because they were "store" bought (ran out of my side of beef and can't get another just yet - same with chickens). That would make things a little easier.

I wonder if you are sensitive (and here's where I don't know whether to say allergy, or something else) to one of the pesticides, and whatever other additives are commonly used? I may experiment with this myself, but first I have to wait for my chicken reaction to resolve (often takes several days for me).

> [Eve said:](/goto/post?id=75481)
>
> It happens with all non-organic food, not just dairy, so I think it's more than issue with casein. If I eat organic almond butter I have no reaction or symptoms, but if I try regular almond butter I have a reaction.
>
> I understand there are toxins in non-organic food, usually a minute amount (e.g., in just a pat of non-organic butter, but I thought the body somehow accounts for this and filters it. I don't understand why my reaction to non-organic food is so extreme.

### Inger — 2013-02-12T16:47:42+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-75531>

Store bought chicken is poison to me. I tried some chicken soup I made for hubs from store bought chicken last winter, twice, as i could not believe it was that what made me so sick. But it was. The second time I got sick too. Herpes, sick feeling overall.. I never touch such stuff anymore. Tells me there must be something terribly wrong with it. I had not eaten chicken many years previous to that experiment because I ate raw foods and never chicken. I have tried my parents home grown chicken though, short after the store bough chicken soup episode to test, and from them I did not got any issues! So I know for sure it is something they get fed or hormones or stuff. I try to always by organic. Or better, wild grown foods.

### Zorica Vuletic — 2013-02-12T17:03:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-75532>

> Your experience is very interesting to me. I am having issues with all kinds of foods (symptoms much like you describe), and my list of foods I tolerate seems to be getter shorter (just lost beef and chicken). But now I'm wondering if it was because they were "store" bought (ran out of my side of beef and can't get another just yet - same with chickens). That would make things a little easier.
>
> I wonder if you are sensitive (and here's where I don't know whether to say allergy, or something else) to one of the pesticides, and whatever other additives are commonly used? I may experiment with this myself, but first I have to wait for my chicken reaction to resolve (often takes several days for me).

> It happens with all non-organic food, not just dairy, so I think it's more than issue with casein. If I eat organic almond butter I have no reaction or symptoms, but if I try regular almond butter I have a reaction.
>
> I understand there are toxins in non-organic food, usually a minute amount (e.g., in just a pat of non-organic butter, but I thought the body somehow accounts for this and filters it. I don't understand why my reaction to non-organic food is so extreme.

Again I shake my head and ask why (mine) as well as everyone's here immune system or whatever pathway to deal with such foods (not even processed McDonalds) sucks so bad that we can't deal with a minor infraction. I mean sure it's nice to get an 'immediate feedback' so as to help motivate to not eat these foods (headache, drunken stupor and slurred speech etc) ... But I was really assuming my gut or immune system or whatever could be strong enough to handle just a bit every once in a while. I guess I do not grasp the concept of TIME for healing etc. O well...

I want to eat WAY more raw (especially with meat too). I don't want to eat raw meat if it's not as organic/grass-fed and/or wild caught though. Any time I do eat raw (usually it's going to be seafood since that is easiest and most 'trustful' I feel much better. When I get my own place again I will switch to 100% raw. For now I will eat as raw as I can get based on what is available at the moment.

Haha....I know that by default things will get better during the summer and peak in September and coincidentally that is when I'll get to be more raw...lol. I will try not to give too much credit for raw as I was doing with heavy seafood consumption last Fall. Because I did see some downfalls despite major seafood intake. I have to be more sensitive to my needs and realize that some adjunct strategies are necessary to sustain better/as close to optimal health for myself. I can't make the mistake that diet alone can carry me if I have some ongoing epi-genetic factors going on (from birth) that is either harder and/or not possible(?) to turn on or off the good/bad genes. So yes, I shall not be ignorant to myself any longer.

Ok good luck y'all with good quality f00ds.

p.s. I am giving up coffee for Lent....OIIIIIII! lol Wish me luck on that one. haha (And I'm not even really religious). lol

### diane — 2013-02-12T19:15:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-75560>

The way I look at it (which may or may not be correct) is that the cleaner your diet and the more sensitive you are to how you feel, the more you feel when something affects you. Years and years of eating stuff just accumulates the bad feelings. I never felt the impact of wheat or grains before, other than the cumulative impact, but now since I don't eat them, when I do, I feel it right away.

Guess I'm not looking to change my immune response to be able to eat these things as I'm not sure that some foods will function properly in the body (that's just my opinion). I'm looking to change my gut so that my hormones and systems run smoothly.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-12T20:00:04+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-75569>

Some people seem to be more sensitive than others with foods. My guess is that it may not necessarily be the actual food item itself but the pesticides, other foods that the item was processed with or around (almond butter may have been processed in a peanut facility), etc. Also may be an indication of methylation issues that some of us are learning about.

### jamsrich — 2013-03-26T12:46:51+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-81835>

Hello,
Once the knowledge about non-organic food is gained in terms of its cultivation and processes, it is very important to understand the harrowing side effects of eating such food for life long. Let us look at the key factors destroying human health.

Pesticides & Herbicides , Fertilizers
, Genetical Modification , Additives , Radiation , Boosters to animals and other food

### nonchalant — 2013-03-26T15:23:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/reactions-to-non-organic-food.5853/post-81840>

well.. be careful what you wish for... Havoc disappeared for about 10mins... so I went to go find him - only to discover he answered the call of nature - but he didn't figure out #2 on the potty is different from #2 in the wild... it was all down his legs and the little guy didn't know what to do... he's all cleaned up and now having mud fights w/his sister...

DH is going to flip... but I say let them get as dirty as they can... we've got a hose...

### caroline — 2013-02-12T01:08:25+0200

### KiwiLauren — 2013-02-12T03:50:20+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-sent-chaos-and-havoc-outside-to-play-in-the-mud.5855/post-75484>

> [Shijin13 said:](/goto/post?id=75457)
>
> well.. be careful what you wish for... Havoc disappeared for about 10mins... so I went to go find him - only to discover he answered the call of nature - but he didn't figure out #2 on the potty is different from #2 in the wild... it was all down his legs and the little guy didn't know what to do... he's all cleaned up and now having mud fights w/his sister...
>
> DH is going to flip... but I say let them get as dirty as they can... we've got a hose...

LOL, Gretchen. Your post made me think of two things.

The first is my mother-in-law's motto (she raised 3 boys and a girl) which was 'there is nothing the boys can get up to that a hose can't fix'. (She required them to strip naked outside and hosed them off before letting them inside!)

The second is a story about a father playing outside on the grass with his sons. The mother comes out and says, "Be careful, you are tearing up the grass." The father responds by saying, "We are raising boys, not grass." (Always a good reminder for me about what's important.)

Hope you all enjoyed the mud puddles.

# Thread: Chocolate Pudding Recipe
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate-pudding-recipe.5858/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-12T01:46:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate-pudding-recipe.5858/post-75469>

I thought I'd share this fabulous recipe for the best chocolate paleo pudding.

1 bar of Lindt 90% Chocolate (3.5 oz)
1 can of coconut milk
Stevia to sweeten to your tastes
1-1/2 to 2 Tblsp Unflavored Gelatin
1/3 cup cool water or coffee to enhance the chocolate flavor
1 tsp vanilla

Other additions can be cinnamon, nutmeg or even powdered espresso.

Melt the chocolate with the coconut milk and sweetener until melted and mixed thoroughly. Remove from heat and mix in the vanilla and any spices. Mix the gelatin with 1/3 cup of warm water until dissolved. Add the dissolved gelatin to the chocolate mixture and stir well. Put the pudding into the refrigerator for two hours or until set and chilled.

This can be a fun recipe that is like a chocolate pie without the crsut!

### caroline — 2013-02-12T02:03:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chocolate-pudding-recipe.5858/post-75472>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=75472)
>
> what size can of coconut milk? sounds yummy - but also sounds like something I wouldn't be able to stay away from!

It's the regular size can of coconut milk which I think is 15-16oz. It's quite filling so usually a 1/4 cup goes a long way!

# Thread: Heart Attack Grill's 'spokesman' dies of heart attack
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-grills-spokesman-dies-of-heart-attack.5870/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ATL_Paleo — 2013-02-12T23:54:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-grills-spokesman-dies-of-heart-attack.5870/post-75610>

The unofficial spokesman for the infamous Las Vegas restaurant Heart Attack Grill has, you guessed it, died of a heart attack.

John Alleman, 52, was waiting for a bus in front of the restaurant last week when he suffered cardiac arrest and was removed from life support Monday afternoon, the restaurant's owner Jon Basso told the Las Vegas Sun.

The Heart Attack Grill's slogan is "Taste Worth Dying For" and it is known for it's medical theme and high-caloric menu, which features items like the **record-breaking 9,982-calorie, 3-pound Quadruple Bypass Burger.**

The restaurant features waitresses dressed as sexy nurses and has a "diet program" where they "prescribe great-tasting, high-calorie meals including the Double Bypass Burger, Flatliner Fries, Full Sugar Coke, Butterfat Shake and no-filter cigarettes!"

[http://www.nypost.com/p/news/national/heart\_attack\_grill\_spokesman\_heart\_tJ5F4LMP9eWdsh1JnDpw4O](http://www.nypost.com/p/news/national/heart_attack_grill_spokesman_heart_tJ5F4LMP9eWdsh1JnDpw4O)

# Thread: I need help with smart meter fight!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/>
**Subforum:** Factor X
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2013-02-13T09:59:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75677>

Ok folks, please help. I am fighting about the new installed smart meter, and I need some education..
I got a paper where says at what frequences the signals are, and it is in between 75 kHz - 132 kHz. In my book about radiowaves and how they destroy health, the radiowaves in between 87 MHz - 108 MHz, instead of 70 MHz as they used to be in "old times". The lower frequensies were much less bad for our health. Now, the kHz is way less than MHz, so I figured maybe our smart meter is not too bad? But I do not know enough about this to do a wise desicion! They said there are different smart meters and some have much stronger frequensies, they have a very "safe" one. Hmmm..

I am not sure if it is this but since a while (the meter is here about 2 months) our home telephone makes so weird nose, real loud, for maybe 5 seconds, every 30 second or so. It is so annoying! I wonder if it has to do with the meter. It started around that time the meter was installed I guess.. as it is a new phenomena.

They sent me papers about a filter too, that can be installed. Now I need to find out if I let them meter be and install the filter or what to do. I know way to little about those low frequensies (kHz) to make a desicion... I really do not want to make unnecessary hassle either..

### caroline — 2013-02-13T10:36:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75680>

I wish I could help Inger - but really don't know - someone else is bound to tho.

### BJK77 — 2013-02-13T15:32:33+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75695>

I wish I could help Inger, but I'm in the same position and am hoping others chime in with some good information. I contacted my electric company 6 months ago to ask about the schedule for installing smart meters and was told they were only done in select houses on a trial basis and it would be years before it reached my area. I was also told we would be notified before the meter was installed.

I just called back in again this morning to follow up and see if they had any newer information and now I'm being told we ALREADY have one!! I am furious. I told them my son has sensitivities to EMF (which I believe to be true) and I want to know how we can have it removed. The person I spoke to was very rude and said there's no such thing as being sensitive to the smart meters because they don't emit any EMF since everything is transmitted through the phone line. She also told me they no longer have any of the analog meters, so we really don't have a choice here, but she'll have someone call me back anyway.

I need to get my facts together and figure out what I'm going to say. It pisses me off to no end that we have no effin rights in this country anymore. They want to tell us what the hell to eat, how to spend our damn $, etc, etc. I am on FIRE right now!!

### shilohman — 2013-02-13T20:45:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75735>

Inger, consider your telephone the equivalent of a canary in a coal mine. Your phone can't die like the canary, but its trying to tell you there is EMF.
What to do:
1) at night have someone take an axe/hammer to the smart meter. Not recommended, but I read that in Germany after the third replacement the power company will put the old meter back up.
2) the meter is attached to the outside of your house. On the inside of your house on the wall the meter is attached to 1) cover the wall with aluminum screen, doesn't have to be big about a square meter or smaller. Now go check your telephone. Hopefully your phone is behaving properly 2) on the outside of your wall install a grounding rod and attach a wire to the rod, run the wire through the wall and attach to the aluminum screen attached to the wall. If you do not have access to aluminum screen you could use a sheet of metal.

The power company may complain if your aluminum shield prevents the smart meter from communicating with the smart meter attached to the house next door, but that is their problem.

### Jude — 2013-02-14T07:22:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75781>

I found this article quite interesting-especially this description on the smart meter.

THE DUTY CYCLE
The data from the wireless Smart Meter is sent out in short transmission bursts, which usually last only
from 12 to 120 milliseconds (ms) at a time. These transmission times are referred to as duty cycles. A
100% duty cycle corresponds to continuous operation, e.g. 24 hours/day. A 1% duty cycle corresponds
to a transmitter operation of 1% per 24 hours, which means 14.4 minutes/day.
The most controversial question in the assessment of wireless Smart Meters is how often is this
intermittent burst occurring per day? This short time sequence makes it difficult to detect and measure
the signal properly with RF instrumentation. The estimates on how often the pulse or burst occurs range
from:
Once or twice a day
Once (1) every four (4) hours
To 750 times/day
To an almost continuous transmission from utility access
points

[http://electricsense.com/wp-content/uploads/private/Smart-Meter-What-We-Know-2-6-12.pdf](http://electricsense.com/wp-content/uploads/private/Smart-Meter-What-We-Know-2-6-12.pdf)

### nonchalant — 2013-02-14T16:43:27+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-need-help-with-smart-meter-fight.5872/post-75823>

> [shilohman said:](/goto/post?id=75735)
>
> Inger, consider your telephone the equivalent of a canary in a coal mine. Your phone can't die like the canary, but its trying to tell you there is EMF.
> What to do:
> 1) at night have someone take an axe/hammer to the smart meter. Not recommended, but I read that in Germany after the third replacement the power company will put the old meter back up.
> 2) the meter is attached to the outside of your house. On the inside of your house on the wall the meter is attached to 1) cover the wall with aluminum screen, doesn't have to be big about a square meter or smaller. Now go check your telephone. Hopefully your phone is behaving properly 2) on the outside of your wall install a grounding rod and attach a wire to the rod, run the wire through the wall and attach to the aluminum screen attached to the wall. If you do not have access to aluminum screen you could use a sheet of metal.
>
> The power company may complain if your aluminum shield prevents the smart meter from communicating with the smart meter attached to the house next door, but that is their problem.

Shilohman, like the screen from a window or door? That would be easy and cheap! Most screen is plastic now, but I can find some old stuff... Our neighbor's smart meter is visible from our bedroom window. I need to see what that window screen is made of.

# Thread: Oysters, Oysters.....& hey, Oysters
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-oysters-hey-oysters.5877/>
**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Christy — 2013-02-13T20:47:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oysters-oysters-hey-oysters.5877/post-75737>

ok, so, I'm trying to add oysters to my menus, maybe even up to 8-10 times per week, **IF** I can figure out enough different ways to prepare them. (Can't do raw yet, gut too leaky) And don't want to get bored with them.

But I wanted to be able have some in the fridge already cooked so they can be a quick fix - say with paleo cocktail sauce and lime juice. except they are so delicate that even steaming mad them dry out a tad, so even a little reheat can make them tough.

So I went looking for another way and found out how to poach them - and it worked out so well!

below are links for recipes for both shucked (I buy Hilton Point shucked/raw)

[http://www.coastseafoods.com/shucked.html](http://www.coastseafoods.com/shucked.html)

and they are oh so convenient and still taste fresh! And were soooo good reheated the next day, too!
Here's the poaching recipe I used, (but next going to get creative with the poaching liquid for added flavor!)

[http://www.silverpointoysters.com/recpoached.html](http://www.silverpointoysters.com/recpoached.html)

and here's a poaching recipe if you want to poach them in the shells:

[http://www.newenglandprovisions.com/shellfishrecipes/poachedoysters.html](http://www.newenglandprovisions.com/shellfishrecipes/poachedoysters.html)

**
so I am wondering how everyone's fixing their oysters AND what kinds of sauces you are using on various seafoods, (if any) that might lend itself to oysters, as well???**

thanks!

Christy


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Can anyone help with recent salivary adrenocortex stress profile?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-anyone-help-with-recent-salivary-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5882/>

### Claudia — 2013-02-14T04:20:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-anyone-help-with-recent-salivary-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5882/post-75776>

Here are my results:

I had a recent salivary test of adrenocortex stress profile done this past January.
Morning is 11.3 (range is 6.0-42.0 nmol/L),
noon is 9.2 (2.0-11.0),
4 pm is 9.1 (2.0-11.0),
evening is 2.9 (1.0-8.0),
cortisol daily, total 32.5 (11.0-76.0),
DHEAS profile morning 13.9 (2.5-25.0 nmol/L) and
DHEAS/CORTISOL AM is 1.23 HIGH (0.20-0.60 RATIO)

I also have been taking t3 only for six months with some improvement in energy. I take 12.5mcg of t3 an hour or so before waking. If I don't wake up in time and take it at wake up, I don't feel as good. I had some weight gain when summer changed to fall and increased my t3 from 5 to 10 and then 12.5.

I don't think I'm LR but my hormones need balancing and I guess my adrenal test posted above is not normal. What do I do to improve my adrenals? I bought adrenal support by Gaia and it has ashwanganda and rhodiola and holy basil. I'm not sure if it's helping or not. The salivary test cost me $179, which is a LOT for me. I want to retest in 3-4 months but I am not sure if I will have the $ to do it. I want to avoid having to take hydrocortisone. My doc said that would be the next thing to try.

I welcome any advice, interpretation, ideas.

My diet is Paleo, not quite Epi...but heading there with shrimp and salmon several times a week, but mostly grass-fed beef, eggs, bacon, no grains for 4 years now, almost no fruit, lots of coconut oil, pastured raw butter (and not from A1 cows....the farm I buy from is A2 cows).

Thank you.

I no expert on adrenals but your morning cortisol is lowish but the rest for afternoon and night looks decent. It looks that you clock is off. I think you should try the BAB with morning sun gazing as per dr. K. This should help to turn of your melatonin in the morning. How is your sleep, Claudia?

My sleep is good in the sense that I don't ever have insomnia or trouble falling asleep. I fall right to sleep, usually very tired, and I go to sleep too late...working on going to bed earlier. I do sleep in complete darkness. I wake up and urinate, once or twice per night, which I don't think is so good....is this related to adrenals. But I am also awakened by kids sometimes. I have 2, 10 years old and 5 years old. Thank you for your response Destiny!!!

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-25T19:59:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-anyone-help-with-recent-salivary-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5882/post-77601>

Here are some cortisol guidelines:
Morning - Should be near the top of the range
Noon - Should be above middle but still closer to top of range
Afternoon - Close to middle of the range
Evening - Near the bottom of the range

Basically your afternoon one maybe a little high but I wouldn't worry about that one. Your morning one is definitely too low. Are you still doing the morning routine of looking at the sun upon waking and BAB? This will definitely help with morning cortisol production. Also look into Paul Robinson's book Recovering with T3. There is a method for taking T3 early in the morning to help stimulate your adrenals. But, as Jack states, adrenal dysfunction is a brain disease so I think this needs to be a multi-pronged approach.

I have been somewhat in your shoes and I can only tell you what helped me. I would force myself to wind down by 8pm and be in bed by 9pm because if I stayed up past 10pm I caught a second wind and could not sleep. After a while I started waking up with the sun and then I would get sun in my eyes and eat a BAB. Things have corrected over time but I still have to be mindful of this routine ALWAYS. Also, I wear my blue blockers and avoid EMF as much as possible at night.

It sounds like you are doing alot of things right. Sometimes it is the little things that make a big difference, I'm thinking light & EMF. Have you tried CT ing? Do you wake up refreshed or exhausted?

### Claudia — 2013-02-25T23:18:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/can-anyone-help-with-recent-salivary-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5882/post-77638>

I have not done the morning light at all. It seems hard to do. I have to go outside for that. Usually I am rushing to get the kids ready for school, cook their BABs, lol. I wake up tired, never refreshed but not as exhausted as I had been. I do know about Paul Robinson and I have been doing the circadian dose. I take my one dose of t3 about 1.5-2 hours before waking. It's usually timed when the kids wake up normally and I hear them, wake up, take my t3, and go back to bed. I do think I feel better when I do this. A couple of times here and there I have missed the timing. It does seem to make a difference in my day. I don't take anymore t3 though, only one dose of 12.5mcg (one pill cut in half). I am honestly not into taking medications so this was hard for me as it is to take t3. I normally won't even take a Tylenol or Advil. I'm not into taking high doses of t3, which I know people are doing for the CMT3 Robinson method. Scares me a lot and my docs aren't into either.
I have done CT. I started doing it last year in March. I had some issues with hives so it was very slow going but I did notice good changes, etc. I did some swimming at the beach in April and May (which is early for New York). Then, I stopped for the summer or just swam at my beach every day here in New York. I was going to pick it up again in the fall. Actually I did continue until early October. Then, I got chicken about it. I did walk in the snow once and lasted only 1 minuted, lol.

I actually went to the beach today and walked barefoot but didn't go in the water. I though maybe I was doing some good grounding, earthing. I don't know a lot about this but both my son and I went barefoot (no brainer for him at 5 years old ) and it felt good. It was about 50 today and there was even some snow on the sand in some spots and I walked on the snow, too. We stayed barefoot for 30 minutes. I got lots of sunshine on my face, chest and arms.
I can't seem to do the bathtub...just have a great aversion to it this winter. I am planning on a polar bear plunge this Saturday. I prefer to just do the water at the beach, the bathtub is such a turnoff to me. It wasn't so bad last year when I first read about Dr. Kruse's CT.

PattyCakes, thanks for the cortisol guidelines. That's what I was thinking that the others are ok, but the morning is way too low. But if I do the morning light, isn't that too late to raise the cortisol? Or will it start helping with future mornings?

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-26T04:35:00+0200

> I can't seem to do the bathtub...just have a great aversion to it this winter. I am planning on a polar bear plunge this Saturday. I prefer to just do the water at the beach, the bathtub is such a turnoff to me. It wasn't so bad last year when I first read about Dr. Kruse's CT.
>
> PattyCakes, thanks for the cortisol guidelines. That's what I was thinking that the others are ok, but the morning is way too low. But if I do the morning light, isn't that too late to raise the cortisol? Or will it start helping with future mornings?

I don't take meds either except my T3. I had major dental surgery last May that included anesthesia and a hospital visit but I didn't have any other choice. But I decided not to take the antibiotics because I hate the drugs so much. However, when I started taking thyroid meds my life changed so I decided that this was a replacement for what my body wasn't making. I watch my thyroid levels through labs several times a year to make sure my doses are in line and I am taking the most minimal amounts possible and getting the results necessary to live. So much of the body relies on the thyroid I for one want to make sure it is functioning well. I won't ever go back to "living" my life that way. It really wasn't living. For me the low FT3 lead to a host of other issues including the adrenal issues. But now I have so much energy to burn and such a different outlook on life after healing the adrenal dysfunction and keeping my thyroid labs at appropriate levels.

The 12.5mcg T3 dose is a really low but if that is helping you that's great!  If your FT3 is low on that dose that is probably one of the reasons you are tired. When your thyroid doesn't produce enough FT3 for energizing systems (I am simplifying here) then the body goes to the adrenals. Then there is pregnenolone steal and so on down the line.

Keep up the CT!

# Thread: My ADrenocortex Stress Profile
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5883/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Claudia — 2013-02-14T04:32:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-adrenocortex-stress-profile.5883/post-75778>

SALIVA TEST 1/1/2013
Today, I got my adrenal saliva test results. The lab's comments: Saliva AM cortisol level is below mean range and suggests adrenal insufficiency. This suggests a degree of adrenal hypo function, maladaption/abnormal pacing with abnormal HPAA. Other cortisol levels are adepquate and within range. Evening is normal and within range.

Morning cortisol 11.3 (range 6.0-42.0 nmol/L)
midday, noon, 9.2 (2.0-11.0)
4pm, 9.1 (2.0-11.0)
evening, 8pm 2.9 (1.0-8.0)

Cortisol Daily, total 32.5 (11.0-76.0)

DHEAS Values
morning 13.9 (range 2.5-25.0)
DHEAS/Cortisol RATIO AM 1.23 HIGH (0.20-0.60 RATIO)

SALIVA DHEAS/CORTISOL RATIO An increase in this ratio is found in patients suffering from panic disorders. Suspect: An abnormal physiological response to stress, with shifting of the steroidogenic pathway to DHEA at the expense of cortisol.
Consider lifestyle changes: stress reduction
Herbal support: Adaptogenic herbs: American or Korean ginseng (Panax spp.), Siberian ginsend, WIthania, Licorice to prolong the half-life of cortisol, Sarsaparilla is a cortisol precursor
Glandular support: Adrenal, pituitary, others as indicated
Hormone replacement therapy: Cortisol, DHEA, pregnenlone as indicated
For herbal, glandular, and hormones, it is important to preserve or restore circadian rhythms by dosing in morning. May give 1/3 to 1/2 of morning dose at noon. Dosing later than noon is not advised. Consider measuring testosterone and or estradiol levels and intervene if necessary.

I'm not sure how bad this profile actually is. I've always felt tired at waking. I don't have problems feeling tired at night and falling asleep. Although I get up to pee a few times.

What can I do to improve my adrenals? What exactly is the adrenal RX and does it go hand in hand with leaky gut? Can someone help me with this? THank you so much.

I don't think I'm LR and I actually did do the Leptin Reset in Nov 2011 through January and then, I stopped when I had the signs. But my hormones need balancing, my t3 is low, and I"m taking t3 only (low dose of 12.5 mcg per day), and Jack has told me that I have mitochondrial inefficiency. I have off and on fatigue, moodiness toward depression, weight gain (for what feels like no reason at all and unrelated to what I eat).

I don't do the BAB because I'm just not hungry. In fact, I'm often not hungry. So, I've been practicing intuitive eating and paying attention to my body and hunger signals. If I am not hungry in the morning, and my cortisol is on the lower side, then, am I right to think this is not a good time for me to eat A LOT of food? It would be forcing since I am not hungry. I get a little hungry in the afternoon and then, I eat. I am hungry again at dinner, and I eat. I don't get very hungry ever. I think when I do eat the combination of protein and fat keeps me satiated for many hours.

Thanks for any advice, interpretation. I posted in some other threads, too. I wasn't sure where to post .


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: how do you know if you shouldn't take sulphur?

### janagram — 2013-02-14T19:03:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-75833>

How do you know if you shouldn't take sulphur? A naturopath that I only saw twice suggested that maybe I should avoid sulphur but I cannot remember why? Any symptoms to clue us? Or is it all some test?
(I have started taking Sam-e.....)

### Rachelle — 2013-02-15T19:17:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-75991>

sulphur in homeopathic form or what?

### DarleenMB — 2013-02-15T19:43:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-75992>

If it were me, I'd want a solid reason why. I'd go back and ask for further clarification.

Sulphur is found in many foods such as eggs, broccoli, meat, etc. It's also in MSM which I've been taking but have now stopped since I got my 23andme results.

### freesia — 2013-02-15T19:58:34+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-75996>

Some people have problems making sulfate out of sulfur (related to the SUOX enzyme reaction IIRC) and sometimes people with that problem can have problems with excess sulfites as a result. This can apparently, impact glutathione levels negatively. You can google sulfite sensitivity and see if you think it might apply to you maybe?

I wouldn't avoid sulfur without a very good, clear reason either.

### springs — 2013-02-16T02:03:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-76041>

You can order sulfite and sulfate strips to test your urine, if you are concerned. Dr. Yasko offers them, but they're cheaper at CTL Scientific. The CBS mutations will also cause excess sulfite/sulfate. You can also have nutritional deficiencies, like with molybdenum, that will keep sulfites from being turned into sulfates.

### janagram — 2013-02-16T08:03:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-do-you-know-if-you-shouldnt-take-sulphur.5886/post-76090>

sooooo...Im also taking sam-e....which is a form of sulfur....hmmmm. then I read that it can prevent glutathione production....I just don't know....gonna go look up some stuff.

# Thread: I just wanted to say.....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-wanted-to-say.5895/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2013-02-15T01:50:02+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-wanted-to-say.5895/post-75928>

Since this is Valentines day - a day to appreciate others in your life ....I just wanted to say thanks to everyone who posts. You never know when something you say sticks to someone else. I have found this over and over and over again. Something that someone else says becomes actionable for you in your life that you wouldn't have considered before. Lots of times - it takes awhile and then you remember something that becomes really relevant to you ...at least that is what happens for me.
When I weighed myself this morning I remembered something that Destiny had said quite awhile ago now and I had taken it on board after I had thought about it for awhile. I think this is how it goes - Dr. K had said that she needed to stop eating fruit and she would lose the excess weight effortlessly - of course there were other reasons because of Marta's situation \[I hope I am remembering that right? apologies if I am not\]

Anyway - I decided to stop eating fruit for awhile \[I love,love.love fruit\] I have effortlessly dropped 2 Kilos and am eating like a horse \[I am embarrassed to say!\] I can't believe how much I eat and my weight is very stable and going down .....but the very stable part is what is great. I used to always feel like I could gain at the blink of an eye. Always scared to get on the scale but I knew that I had to. But there has been so many other things that have made a huge difference for me - so thanks again!

So- what you share is hugely important to all of us ...just as JK is sharing with us ....so we need to pay if forward......

### SeaHorse — 2013-02-15T04:17:15+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-just-wanted-to-say.5895/post-75946>

Great point Caroline...I too have taken things I've read here and have had them come to life for me in ways I didn't expect. All the experimentation, opinions, experiences and discourse are the living embodiment of change....and that's what we are all doing.

Happy Valentines to you too!

# Thread: TSH plummeting majorly....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tsh-plummeting-majorly.5917/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Hope — 2013-02-16T22:20:29+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tsh-plummeting-majorly.5917/post-76162>

So I got some new labs...and though sadly my FT3/4 is still in the tank (2.6 and 1, respectively), my TSH has tanked and gone down to 0.02. I have been having bad heart pounding/racing so that I had to stop my thyroid meds and some of my sea veggies to not have this happen a lot, especially at night.

Has anyone seen this?

I wonder if I am not able to absorb selenium.....and if that contributes to anything.

The crazy/good thing is that I also lost about 10 lbs since late Nov. and have started generally to feel better....more energy, better brain, from all the stuff I have been doing from the Kruse Rx including more meditation and some light yoga now and then.

But this is a bummer.....especially because I feel I need the thyroid med (Armour, half grain) to up the FT3/4....eventually.

Has anyone seen this happen? Any other insight you could share??

Thanks in advance...

# Thread: Women's experiences with CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-experiences-with-ct.5918/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### [email redacted] — 2013-02-16T23:22:09+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-experiences-with-ct.5918/post-76163>

Hi there, I'll be starting CT again for my fatigue issues and am motivated to stick with it this time. I'd like to hear experiences from other women (especially those with adrenal fatigue) who got thru all the discomfort and detox and made it to finally be cold adapted. Hearing from other's struggles turned successes would be good motivation for me!! Thank you!

### freesia — 2013-02-17T04:25:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-experiences-with-ct.5918/post-76183>

Hi,
I started with a basal body temperature average of 95.9 F and maximum afternoon temperature of 97.5 F - often lower.
10 months later - CTing every one or two days - I have an average basal body temp of 97.4 F and afternoon temperature of 98.6 F.

I got down from 20 minutes in a 60 F bath to 45 minutes in a 46 F bath over the course of about 12 weeks. Took it slowly (like a sick person, not a sportsperson) but was _very_ consistent.

Overcame discomfort. Check! Check!  You don't really want me to dwell on that bit do you?
Detox was a strange one - mostly estrogen I think. Wow..I remember getting pimples, and mood swings LOL. DIM and I-3-C saved the day!
For quite a while I used to wake up the next morning feeling slammed by the day before's bath. CT hangover...
I managed that by ice bathing every second day instead of daily until I could push it again.

I forget how many weeks in (but not too many) my body started heating up in anticipation of a bath, and I could stretch the time out to 45 minutes, from 20 - 30 minutes. In Winter when we were walking in an icy wind in the city one of my children mentioned my hands were so warm. I was always someone who had cold hands and feet and HATED Winter...feared the cold.

Particular adrenal symptoms I had were a crash mid afternoon, and waking around 3am a bit panicky. Leptin reset and CT together seemed to sort this out quickly.
Morning and evening cortisol levels were in normal range 4 months in.

Hope that helps.

### [email redacted] — 2013-02-18T03:42:28+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/womens-experiences-with-ct.5918/post-76296>

Thank you so much for telling me your experience. Super helpful. Back to it, 2nd day of face dunks... Here we go!!

# Thread: D'Adamo's Blood Type Testing and Diet
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Martin — 2013-02-17T17:27:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/post-76207>

One of my docs in the past wanted me to get tested for my blood type and then base a diet on the results. Dw and I then had a dark field microscopy test. Much to our surprise, my blood cells were happily floating and bumping and freely moving about while DW's were all clump-assed together. He suggested that we follow **The Blood Type Diet** and offered up this book on the subject.

I complied, read Peter D'Adamo's book and changed some foods. No coconut!

No help. Moved on.

I ran across a review on the Weston Price site that really confirmed changing my mind back to traditional foods, and, not to eliminate nor add things based on D'Adamo's "theories". I went on to eat more and more traditional foods according to Price and others.

One guy, one book, one theory. I learned that what works in vitro doesn't always equate to what works in vivo.

Big lesson.

[http://www.westonaprice.org/thumbs-down-reviews/eat-right-4-your-type](http://www.westonaprice.org/thumbs-down-reviews/eat-right-4-your-type)

Lots of interesting examinations of D'Adamo's flawed theories in the above link.

### janagram — 2013-02-28T07:28:31+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/post-78261>

As an O, I learned I needed meat and lots of it....really helped me since I had been a vegetarian for decades....(!) But I allowed as how I could probably eat coconut oil and be fine...and have now embraced epi-paleo.

### Martha Ray — 2019-05-28T02:49:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dadamos-blood-type-testing-and-diet.5921/post-271412>

I believe that both of the Dr. D'Adamo's added value and that the Blood Type Diet is good for many people as it stresses real food and lowers enough lectins for each Blood Type. The food lists for O and A are good not perfect. They are the only Blood Types I have experience with. I've given them a hearing and a good long trial and have noticed the trends true to myself as well as most of my family and friends that have tried it. It is just a tiny step toward individualizing dieting. The fact that we are different blood types and some are secretors and some nonsecretors is too unique to not factor in somehow... just as our own genetics factor. Much more to learn I believe. I believe that plant 'based' especially green leafy's has been reasonable food over the ages. All food is under the gun quality wise depending on how much man has tinkered 'improving' it... ha. The power of the mind to have confidence in what one decides to ingest is most likely a huge factor as well. Growing up eating the SAD that I perceived as 'healthy', then natural hygiene, zero carb, macrobiotic, carb and carb and sugar addict, vegetarian, vegan, paleo, alkaline paleo, Weston Price, blood type diets, ad nauseam.... I was pretty much a professional dieter. Some things aligned and I've failed enough to be open to what I don't know what I don't know.
I love Jack Kruse and within the past 4 months been enjoying the fresh air...I like outside the box thinkers... and I am extremely inquisitive. I've got a lot to learn.

Hi Martha

Have you read the D'Adamo book The Genotype diet? From my biometrics information plus my blood type I learned that I am more than typically primed to survive famine, wish I'd known that before I started on the chronic dieting road.  It was definitely more useful to me than the blood type diet, changed my relationship with food, and knowing my risks based on my biometrics, it has influenced my IF practice.

But it a road less traveled... What is on the horizon Dr. Peter J. D'Adamo?

# Thread: It appears that one block of sleep is a new thing...article on the history of sleep
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-appears-that-one-block-of-sleep-is-a-new-thing-article-on-the-history-of-sleep.5924/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### lee — 2013-02-17T21:58:30+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-appears-that-one-block-of-sleep-is-a-new-thing-article-on-the-history-of-sleep.5924/post-76241>

The Myth of the Eight Hour Sleep
[http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-16964783](http://www.bbc.co.uk/news/magazine-16964783)
By Stephanie Hegarty

(It's a more pleasant read if you follow the link, but just in case the link dies, here's the article.)

We often worry about lying awake in the middle of the night - but it could be good for you. A growing body of evidence from both science and history suggests that the eight-hour sleep may be unnatural.

In the early 1990s, psychiatrist Thomas Wehr conducted an experiment in which a group of people were plunged into darkness for 14 hours every day for a month.

It took some time for their sleep to regulate but by the fourth week the subjects had settled into a very distinct sleeping pattern. They slept first for four hours, then woke for one or two hours before falling into a second four-hour sleep.

Though sleep scientists were impressed by the study, among the general public the idea that we must sleep for eight consecutive hours persists.
In 2001, historian Roger Ekirch of Virginia Tech published a seminal paper, drawn from 16 years of research, revealing a wealth of historical evidence that humans used to sleep in two distinct chunks.

His book At Day's Close: Night in Times Past, published four years later, unearths more than 500 references to a segmented sleeping pattern - in diaries, court records, medical books and literature, from Homer's Odyssey to an anthropological account of modern tribes in Nigeria.
Much like the experience of Wehr's subjects, these references describe a first sleep which began about two hours after dusk, followed by waking period of one or two hours and then a second sleep.

"It's not just the number of references - it is the way they refer to it, as if it was common knowledge," Ekirch says.

During this waking period people were quite active. They often got up, went to the toilet or smoked tobacco and some even visited neighbours. Most people stayed in bed, read, wrote and often prayed. Countless prayer manuals from the late 15th Century offered special prayers for the hours in between sleeps.

When segmented sleep was the norm
"He knew this, even in the horror with which he started from his first sleep, and threw up the window to dispel it by the presence of some object, beyond the room, which had not been, as it were, the witness of his dream." Charles Dickens, Barnaby Rudge (1840)
"Don Quixote followed nature, and being satisfied with his first sleep, did not solicit more. As for Sancho, he never wanted a second, for the first lasted him from night to morning." Miguel Cervantes, Don Quixote (1615)
"And at the wakening of your first sleepe You shall have a hott drinke made, And at the wakening of your next sleepe Your sorrowes will have a slake." Early English ballad, Old Robin of Portingale
The Tiv tribe in Nigeria employ the terms "first sleep" and "second sleep" to refer to specific periods of the night
Source: Roger Ekirch

And these hours weren't entirely solitary - people often chatted to bed-fellows or had sex.

A doctor's manual from 16th Century France even advised couples that the best time to conceive was not at the end of a long day's labour but "after the first sleep", when "they have more enjoyment" and "do it better".

Ekirch found that references to the first and second sleep started to disappear during the late 17th Century. This started among the urban upper classes in northern Europe and over the course of the next 200 years filtered down to the rest of Western society.

By the 1920s the idea of a first and second sleep had receded entirely from our social consciousness.

He attributes the initial shift to improvements in street lighting, domestic lighting and a surge in coffee houses - which were sometimes open all night. As the night became a place for legitimate activity and as that activity increased, the length of time people could dedicate to rest dwindled.
In his new book, Evening's Empire, historian Craig Koslofsky puts forward an account of how this happened.

"Associations with night before the 17th Century were not good," he says. The night was a place populated by people of disrepute - criminals, prostitutes and drunks.

"Even the wealthy, who could afford candlelight, had better things to spend their money on. There was no prestige or social value associated with staying up all night."

Roger Ekirch says this 1595 engraving by Jan Saenredam is evidence of activity at night

That changed in the wake of the Reformation and the counter-Reformation. Protestants and Catholics became accustomed to holding secret services at night, during periods of persecution. If earlier the night had belonged to reprobates, now respectable people became accustomed to exploiting the hours of darkness.

This trend migrated to the social sphere too, but only for those who could afford to live by candlelight. With the advent of street lighting, however, socialising at night began to filter down through the classes.

In 1667, Paris became the first city in the world to light its streets, using wax candles in glass lamps. It was followed by Lille in the same year and Amsterdam two years later, where a much more efficient oil-powered lamp was developed.

London didn't join their ranks until 1684 but by the end of the century, more than 50 of Europe's major towns and cities were lit at night.
Night became fashionable and spending hours lying in bed was considered a waste of time.

"People were becoming increasingly time-conscious and sensitive to efficiency, certainly before the 19th Century," says Roger Ekirch. "But the industrial revolution intensified that attitude by leaps and bounds."

Strong evidence of this shifting attitude is contained in a medical journal from 1829 which urged parents to force their children out of a pattern of first and second sleep.

A small city like Leipzig in central Germany employed 100 men to tend to 700 lamps

"If no disease or accident there intervene, they will need no further repose than that obtained in their first sleep, which custom will have caused to terminate by itself just at the usual hour.

"And then, if they turn upon their ear to take a second nap, they will be taught to look upon it as an intemperance not at all redounding to their credit."

Today, most people seem to have adapted quite well to the eight-hour sleep, but Ekirch believes many sleeping problems may have roots in the human body's natural preference for segmented sleep as well as the ubiquity of artificial light.

This could be the root of a condition called sleep maintenance insomnia, where people wake during the night and have trouble getting back to sleep, he suggests.

The condition first appears in literature at the end of the 19th Century, at the same time as accounts of segmented sleep disappear.
"For most of evolution we slept a certain way," says sleep psychologist Gregg Jacobs. "Waking up during the night is part of normal human physiology."

The idea that we must sleep in a consolidated block could be damaging, he says, if it makes people who wake up at night anxious, as this anxiety can itself prohibit sleeps and is likely to seep into waking life too.

Stages of sleep
Every 60-100 minutes we go through a cycle of four stages of sleep
Stage 1 is a drowsy, relaxed state between being awake and sleeping - breathing slows, muscles relax, heart rate drops
Stage 2 is slightly deeper sleep - you may feel awake and this means that, on many nights, you may be asleep and not know it
Stage 3 and Stage 4, or Deep Sleep - it is very hard to wake up from Deep Sleep because this is when there is the lowest amount of activity in your body
After Deep Sleep, we go back to Stage 2 for a few minutes, and then enter Dream Sleep - also called REM (rapid eye movement) sleep - which, as its name suggests, is when you dream

In a full sleep cycle, a person goes through all the stages of sleep from one to four, then back down through stages three and two, before entering dream sleep

Source: Gregg Jacobs

Russell Foster, a professor of circadian \[body clock\] neuroscience at Oxford, shares this point of view.

"Many people wake up at night and panic," he says. "I tell them that what they are experiencing is a throwback to the bi-modal sleep pattern."
But the majority of doctors still fail to acknowledge that a consolidated eight-hour sleep may be unnatural.

"Over 30% of the medical problems that doctors are faced with stem directly or indirectly from sleep. But sleep has been ignored in medical training and there are very few centres where sleep is studied," he says.

Jacobs suggests that the waking period between sleeps, when people were forced into periods of rest and relaxation, could have played an important part in the human capacity to regulate stress naturally.

In many historic accounts, Ekirch found that people used the time to meditate on their dreams.

"Today we spend less time doing those things," says Dr Jacobs. "It's not a coincidence that, in modern life, the number of people who report anxiety, stress, depression, alcoholism and drug abuse has gone up."

So the next time you wake up in the middle of the night, think of your pre-industrial ancestors and relax. Lying awake could be good for you.
Craig Koslofsky and Russell Foster appeared on The Forum from theBBC World Service. Listen to the programme here.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-17T23:56:03+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-appears-that-one-block-of-sleep-is-a-new-thing-article-on-the-history-of-sleep.5924/post-76260>

Thanks for posting...it was interesting read. I usually wake up enough to be _aware_ of it about 4.5-5.5 hours in. Most the time it is easy to fall back asleep.

Occasionally my body goes to wide awake stage (not going to shut back down). That's when I get up and just go sit outside on the patio in the moonlight. Figure, why fight it? The next night I sleep extremely well.

### Rachelle — 2013-02-18T16:02:07+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/it-appears-that-one-block-of-sleep-is-a-new-thing-article-on-the-history-of-sleep.5924/post-76313>

I use to wake up every 4 hrs into sleeping and stayed up about an hour and then went back to sleep. I feel MUCH better when I sleep a straight 8 hrs.

Recently I've been waking up only about once a night, for about 15-30 minutes. Not sure about the time, since I don't look at the clock. I can't complain, because my sleep has been quite good lately, but I'd actually like to sleep like a log all night.

# Thread: Leftover Duck Fat

**Subforum:** Epi-Paleo Recipes
**Posts:** 9
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### fitness@home — 2013-02-18T03:51:26+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leftover-duck-fat.5930/post-76298>

We roasted a duck for dinner tonight and as you know, it leaves a lot of leftover drippings in the pan. I have it in a jar in the fridge because it is fat, after all.

So how do you use yours? Obviously I can use it for cooking. Any suggestions for specific usage? Recipes that utilize it?

Thanks

### janagram — 2013-02-19T21:29:37+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leftover-duck-fat.5930/post-76532>

well, not only is it fat....it's duck fat!!!! The best! So they say...my DH loves anything fried in it...of course, fried potatoes, ("french fries"), and it's a main ingredient in cassoulet, which I have never had, but it sounds pretty good. But it has a type of dried bean in it...hm, not! OK, then, fried sweet potatoes! yum. Roasted veggies....

### BJK77 — 2013-02-19T21:47:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leftover-duck-fat.5930/post-76533>

I've been hoping someone would answer fitness@home. I have a duck in the freezer just waiting to be roasted and I never know what to do with all that fat either (other than use it for frying)!

### Shijin13 — 2013-02-19T22:12:01+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leftover-duck-fat.5930/post-76537>

its great on sweet potato fries! too!

### Hope — 2013-02-19T22:22:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leftover-duck-fat.5930/post-76541>

That is great...

Fitness, where do you get your duck?

And Gretchen, how do you make your fries? In oven? Do you eat them now?

Just wondering

### fitness@home — 2013-02-19T22:33:55+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leftover-duck-fat.5930/post-76546>

Hope,

My dad is a duck hunter so I grew up with wild duck and goose on the menu.

I bought the duck we just had at 99 Ranch, the big oriental market. That's where I bought groceries at last week. Always spend more than anticipated when I shop there.

Never thought about just eating the fat. I eat coconut oil off the spoon so duck fat should be relatively easy. Will have to try some sweet potato fries soon. I like them seasoned with Cajun spices.

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-19T23:23:13+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leftover-duck-fat.5930/post-76563>

I keep a tub of duck fat in the freezer for slathering on veggies or sweet potato fries. You can also cook other things in it. It is a rich fat that is so yummy. I have done this for years so was glad to hear this bird and it's respective fat was not that foul!

### fitness@home — 2013-02-21T02:51:38+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/leftover-duck-fat.5930/post-76802>

I had saved the duck carcass and this morning tossed it in the crockpot with some leeks, a few carrots and celery stalks to flavor the broth. There was just enough meat left on it to add to the soup too. It turned out wonderfully. This is what I'll be having for lunch for the next two days.

Never throw out chicken, turkey, or duck carcasses. Freeze them for making soup later on!

### Patty Cakes — 2013-02-21T06:03:24+0200

>
> Never throw out chicken, turkey, or duck carcasses. Freeze them for making soup later on!

I do this all the time and love the broth. I really like turkey broth but try to limit it because it's foul. Haven't had it for 4 months. But I've had a hankering for duck and may have to find one and make broth with that. Thanks for the idea.

# Thread: Finally got sick, and CTed.. yay!
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 6
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Inger — 2013-02-18T11:28:41+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76302>

I got the flu!!!
I wanted it so bad all winter because I wanted to test how it feels to CT with fever.. but I never got sick. Last week I did! I started to get runny nose.. and then because we had guests and I was busy and then I left one day without going into the ice hole.. next day I woke up sick. ha.
First day I had lowish fewer, next day I had high fever, and today I feel almost perfectly fine again, no fever and only a tiny bit coughing. I did CT both days, not long but several short dips. I can tell also with high fever it felt only nice to dip in the river.. wow! I kind of craved the cold, and the warm temps in my parents home downstairs really made me feel worse.. I spent time there this weekend because we had birthday party for dad. But I cannot tell how relieved I felt when I went upstairs to my own cool 15 degree C cave and even much colder bedroom!
Weird is I felt light in my body even if I was sick... it was not bad at all! Now I know, CT is great medicine for flu.

I guess having too many candles on made it hard for my lungs. I really feel they start to suffer. From regular lamp oil too. I do not like the smell either.. I like to use the olive oil lamps much better! And not too many of them. When alone I use only 1 or 2 lamps/candles and that is fine! When too many I can tell by my lungs they do not like it... I am not bothered by the darkness at all, I like it! But when others around.... it seems to bother them big time and they need plenty of candles to feel comfortable.

### fitness@home — 2013-02-18T12:41:48+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76305>

You sound so excited to have had the flu! Glad you're feeling better Inger. At least you finally got to test CTs usefulness in fighting the flu. A cool room always feels better to me too.

I haven't had a cold or flu in so long (which is a good thing!). Stopped getting flu shots a couple years ago. Figure my immune system must be working pretty well. Have been exposed to it at work and through family members.

I'm like you and enjoy minimal lighting at night. Just one candle for use while making my way through the house is good. It actually must have rubbed off on DH. I've noted that he doesn't turn on any lights after I go to bed...he stays up at least three hours later than me.

### MamaGrok — 2013-02-18T16:49:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76317>

Do you use beeswax or some kind of modern candle, like soy? I'm wondering if it's the smoke itself or the chemicals from the wax that is bothering you.

### Hope — 2013-02-18T18:23:59+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76326>

Hi Inger,

that is truly awesome! And I am with you not the temps.....whew! And I have the same thing with candles...I don't like the smell when I sniff them out....

Thanks for sharing and all the best to you!

### BJK77 — 2013-02-18T18:38:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76327>

I also wonder if it is the chemicals present in the wax and not the candle itself that is causing you problems?? I really don't tolerate any candles other than 100% pure beeswax. In fact, they are known for purifying the air and I think they give off such a warm, natural glow and smell. I love to use them.

### caroline — 2013-02-18T22:18:16+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/finally-got-sick-and-cted-yay.5931/post-76356>

Inger - so glad you are feeling better .... you have always known that C/T is the answer!

# Thread: Day 3:  broke out in hives
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/day-3-broke-out-in-hives.5932/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### BeFearless — 2013-02-18T18:39:45+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/day-3-broke-out-in-hives.5932/post-76329>

Anyone else break out in hives when starting this lifestyle?

I dove right in and went cold turkey on carbs. Put coconut oil in my coffee. Ate a variety of proteins -- eggs, fish, shrimp, beef, bacon, and some salad greens. On the morning of day 3, just after breakfast, I discovered hives all over my chest, back, neck, arms, and hands. Thankfully, they went away with Benadryl and hydrocortisone cream, so obviously it was some sort of allergic reaction to something I ate.

I suspect I may have overdone it with eggs. In the past I wouldn't ordinarily chow down 4 deviled eggs in a sitting. Maybe it was the coconut oil?

I know Dr. Kruse is big on testing, but I'm not insured and there is no room in my budget for allergy testing right now. I have environmental allergies year round (I suspect pet dander and pollen) and take 1 Zyrtec daily. To do allergy testing, I'd have to stop allergy meds for 2 full weeks. I can't think of any time of the year when I could tough it out for 2 full weeks -- I'd never sleep.

> A brain implant which could allow humans to detect invisible infrared light has been developed by scientists in America.

> But the new study, by researchers from Duke University in North Carolina, is the first case in which such devices have been used to give an animal a completely new sense.
